title: A studio staple.
comment:
I grew up recording and monitoring with these. My ears are as tuned to them as they are to the Yamaha NS-10m's (which they are, very much hehe).

A great set of cans. Perfect for monitoring for long periods because they are also extremely comfortable.

The size conversion adapter is also included, so you can plug these into anything not Apple (grrrr! I am still mad about that because bluetooth is laggy and is no good for even mundane audio work on an iDevice so great, another expensive dongle to lose--but that is another review for another day).

The audio quality of these hasn't changed in all of the years I have been using them. My old set sounds just like this new one. That is no small feat, considering, well, you know... ummm, modern manufacturing techniques let's just call it.

They are also accurate. Not too much of anything, nor too little. Mids are clearly defined which is all important when mixing especially. I would only use them to monitor and "check in" with a session while mastering though, they aren't good enough for "in the box" mastering, of course. Not much is. Nothing is at this price point. But for what they are - workhorse studio monitors, this is the "go to" set, I believe. I've tried just about everything, and while decent Senns DO come close, I always seem to find my way back to these.

title: Highly recomended!
comment: I bought the L.R. Baggs radius pickup for my Washburn 118 mandolin and am very pleased.  It is designed for those who don't want to make structural changes to their (high end) instrument. K&K and other pickups are designed to be installed inside the mandolin which requires drilling a larger hole for the output jack, where the strap is attached.  This installs on top and can be removed, when not needed. The reproduction of the mandolin tone is true to the acoustic sound, which is what really matters, and it is resistant to feedback. I had the Myers pickup previously, which functioned well and had a built in pre-amp, but had a lot of feedback problems.  I use this pickup with my Tascam 8 track recorder for recording and am very happy with the result.  Since it is a passive pickup, I bought the L.R. Baggs Venue pre-amp to boost the volume and to EQ the signal.  I decided to buy this after seeing a Sierra Hull video where she had the same setup.  Recommended!

title: A whole new world of easy audio
comment:
[[VIDEOID:5e41a769d1a495ecfa79078b57086b2]] I've been doing video production for over 15 years. Sound is a very underrated video production value. Now, cell phones are capable of shooting 8k video! Video quality is not the limiting factor with a newer cell phone. Audio is. So now I have a way to get good, crisp audio from a rechargeable lav! I'm so happy! This single inexpensive device will elevate my video quality without a lot of effort. Perfect for vlogging.

At first I was a little let down by the fact that you MUST use their proprietary software to capture video. My phone has the ability to shoot 8k, but the Smartmike software doesn't have that option. Really though, not a deal breaker. 8k is super compressed in a cell phone. And 4k IS available in Smartmike. That's fine with me. The options within the software are pretty robust. More than I expected. For IG'ers you can shoot 4:3 or even 1:1. It also has a range of filters.

My short audio test showed the sound quality to be much better than the phone mic and now I don't need to be tethered to the phone. This really helps to open up scene options. It's amazing all of the things that open up when you don't have to be concerned about the audio. I'm really looking forward to trying out new video scenes with this little mic. I'm more impressed than I thought I would be.

If you're from a world of Sennheiser lavs attached to 100 foot XLR cables, this mic is going to make your day.

title: Very Good “BUT” not Great!,  Go Naked!
comment: I will Start by saying I don’t have any fandom towards a particular string company. What I’ve learned about most strings and things in life they can’t excel at everything. To keep it simple if your ears are familiar with the beauty of a naked set of Phosphor Bronze strings you will clearly notice an audible loss in the name of warmth, crystal clarity and chime. I want to love these strings I like them for what they are, They last longer and are silky. Is that more important than The Sound? If it is Houston we got a problem. Would you put a blanket over your speakers at the cost of altering their sound just to protect them? Or perhaps your grandmas sofa with the thick clear plastic on it sacrificing its Beaty and comfort for preservation. Same applies here. You coated the very thing that vibrates and makes the wonderful tone. I’m happy playing coated strings on my electric it’s much less evident. You have many other factors contributing to your output. An Acoustic Guitar however is naked that’s it’s purpose and it’s design to sound natural. These strings will last an extra month but that won’t make up for the unnatural sound they produce or the inspiration lost due to laziness of an occasional restring. Go Naked.

title: Low frequency pickup range
comment:
I gave these a solid try for a few weeks before writing this review, because I wanted to see how they fared with repeated use. I'm a streamer, and I'm always looking to see about expanding what I can do on stream, and being able to move around freely was something high on my list.

Out of the box, there's two mics, two extra windsocks, and a USB-A adapter for PC. They are plug and play, so no need to install external software. At first blush, they seemed to work fairly well, and have a decent range. After some prolonged use, a few issues started to show up. The sound quality degraded, making me sound hollow, and introducing a bit of unwanted reverberation. There was also some popping and crackling showing up around the 350-500 Hz range. This was consistent across both microphones.

After about three days, one of the mics started behaving strangely, showing only a red light, and refusing to be power cycled.

Overall, these didn't suit my purpose. The audio quality is rather poor, overall, and the mics don't seem to stand up to continuous usage without having fits. If you're looking for a good quality lavalier mic for any kind of recording, I'd suggest looking elsewhere.

title: Way better than the Luna Great Wave Concert
comment:
After a recent trip to Hawaii I was inspired to pick up the Ukulele. I went to the local music store and their concert sized Ukeleles were this Spalt Maple and the Luna Great Wave Concert. The Great Wave Concert was $120 and this was $200. Picked them both up, I noticed the Spalt Maple was better quality but still didn't think it would be an $80 difference. I Practiced and learned on the Luna Great Wave for a month. There was always something about the tone I didn't like. Also, I felt like the tuning pegs had a lot of play before the string would tighten and even more before it would loosen. I also had a bad buzz on my 'C' string so I always intended on getting it repaired or drop the extra money on this one. I went back to the store and picked this one up and I noticed everything felt tremendously better. You could tell right away a lot more time was spent on this Uke, it felt more sturdy/solid and all the edges were rounded and very smooth, and the tuning hardware is really good. Also, most importantly, the tone is infinitely better. Before I felt like all the cords I would play before sounded a little off and I didn't like the hum after the cord was struck, now it sounds sweet. The only downside is my wife thinks its way less cute.

I'm not sure why it's so much here. Luna sales it directly for $200.

title: Great guitar but you have to be a skilled luthier
comment: Setup was terrible. When I got it the strings were laying on the frets. After I cut the cheap strings, backed the Truss Rod off 2 turns to straighten the neck, cleaned the finger board with Lemon Oil, checked the neck again to make sure it stayed where I put it, re-stringed it with Ernie Ball Slinky's, did the initial tune, re bowed the neck for a correct string height, tuned another 3 times, adjusted the pickup height and polished the finish, it is now something that you just cannot put down. QC is a little more time consuming but it would be worth it

title: WOW! Glockespiel me, baby!!!
comment:
The sounds my almost-3yo can get out of this thing are freakin' amazeballs-- I opened the case up, left her alone for three seconds, and heard what almost sounded like music. The hammering and sliding actions both came to her immediately, and she sang her songs along right away. I've given her a ton of musical instruments, adult and kids versions alike, and this is the only one she took to so quickly. And that's not a weird parent flex-- I'm pretty sure any kid could get on this thing that quickly.

The unit is packed in a rugged, latched case that should stand up to some abuse. Not being a glockenspieler/xylophoner myself, I was surprised to find that the case held 8 individual notes to hammer, instead of a fixed rack with attached bars. I guess this so you can expand the set, but it could pose some issues if you don't want to see the pieces scattered to the wind. Still, everything is labeled by number and scale letter, just in case the size of the plates doesn't tip you off to the order they're supposed to be in. Again, I'm no expert, but the sound quality is crystal clear and pleasing.

Couldn't be happier with this, and neither could the kid.

title: Shocked at the sound quality
comment: Before trying these for monitoring while recording, I did not consider myself a headphone "snob" at all. I'm certainly not an audiophile. I've never owned any super high-end headphones - I use a pair of Beyer DT770s for monitoring in my vocal booth.  I needed a second pair of closed-backs to monitor while recording podcasts and a little bit of playback/editing. I could not believe how different these sound compared to the Beyers - the 280 Pros were all mids to my ear. Like nothing but mids at all...it was so off-putting I had to return them. I did not expect them to sound the same at all, and I know for monitoring while recording you're not doing any critical listening but still, it was just such a weird sound profile that it was distracting and I felt like I was capturing a bad quality recording. The funny thing is that the DT770s are known for being too sharp in the high frequencies rather than being neutral but man they're so much more natural sounding to me than the 280s. I have a pair of Sennheiser IEMs for travel and they sound nothing like these thank God. If the sound signature of these doesn't bother you....I did find them comfortable and if you swap out the ear pads for some softer fabric they'd probably be very comfortable.

title: Volunteer audio engineer == Thumbs up
comment:
I think this mic sounds great, and I regularly use $150 mic elements as a volunteer audio engineer. It is super sensitive even in cardoid mode. You will hear every fan and chair squeak, but that just means it is a good microphone and I need a better recording environment. title: 3.5 Stars - Decent Sound In A Tiny Package With A Few Quirks
comment:
I'll be honest up-front and say I always loved the sound of the Jupiter 8. All my favourite bands from the early 1980s used it I would have had one of my own back then if I had been able to afford it. 35 years later I must admit to being surprised to discover that the JP-08 is not the synth I remember at all. I am not saying it is a bad emulation, I have nothing to judge that by, I think it is more that the original is probably not as good as I thought it was. So my review will be about the JP-08 and what it has to offer as a hardware synth in the 21st Century.

First up, you will be shocked at how tiny this thing is. It is about 50% longer than a DVD case, almost the same width and a bit more than double the thickness. I also bought the K-25m keyboard and the whole thing is smaller than my 13" laptop. It feels well made and should hold up to a little intermittent gigging.

Having a dedicated slider, knob or switch for most functions is fantastic. One of the features I particularly like is the MANUAL button that instantly gives you the sound based on the current positions of all the controls. It makes it a breeze to create your own sounds from scratch. The layout of the controls couldn't be simpler and the LEDs in the sliders just make you want to create your own sounds as soon as you turn it on.

When it comes to sound creation, the JP-08 has great oscillators with lots of modulation and cross modulation options. There are a good range of waveforms available and flexible tuning options. It's a pity, then, that the filter is not really anything special. It gets the job done but that's about all. That is undoubtedly why the Jupiter 8 was best known for it's big leads and lush pads, sounds that don't really push the filter. The two ADSR envelopes and LFO do just what you'd expect, with the LFO offering plenty of waveforms. The presets give you a fair idea of what's possible but, surprisingly, a lot of them sound very similar.

Perhaps the best feature of the JX-08 is the DUAL mode, which allows you to stack two sounds. It feels like it doubles the polyphony, because you can still play four notes. There are also UNISON and SOLO voice modes. UNISON is a bit disappointing because you can't control it and it doesn't really add the kind of thickness you might want. There are other features buried inside the synth, like a delay effect and settings for portamento and other functions, but they are not marked on the panel so you have to read the manual to know how to get to them. The manual is pretty terrible, it's just one big sheet of paper, recreated in PDF format if you download it. There is a two character LED that is not very helpful, either.

There are some good things and some decidedly ordinary things about this synth. Overall, though, I think it is definitely worth having a good, close look at because it packs a pretty big sound into a very small package for a very good price. I also have a Korg Minilogue, which has different strengths and weaknesses. The two complement each other well but if I could only have one, I think I'd choose the Korg.

title: Quality Can Vary Widely
comment:
Despite the very low price, this is a real instrument, not a toy. Yet potential purchasers should know this is a cheap, Chinese-made beginner instrument, not a high-quality stringed instrument. Don't expect the ukulele equivalent of a Martin or Taylor guitar--or even a $200 entry-level guitar.

Quality can vary widely with this item. The first Lanikai LU-21 I ordered was defective, with problems with fit and finish, very weak tone, and major intonation* problems, rendering it essentially unplayable. The replacement also suffers from sloppy fret dressing and assembly, and the bridge seems to be made of a different wood than the first one. However, the tone is dramatically richer and fuller, and while intonation problems still exist, they are not as bad as before.

As others have noted, it will take a few days of constant retuning to stretch the nylon strings fully to where tuning stabilizes. After that, the ukulele holds its tuning quite well, and you'll only need to tune it from time to time.

This ukulele comes with a very brief instructional pamphlet covering tuning, chord progressions for a few folk and children's songs, and chord charts for a few major and minor triads and 7 chords. No fingerings are suggested, so beginner musicians will be left to fumble around, but experienced guitarists will be able to work out useful fingerings quickly.

* Intonation refers to pitch accuracy and is affected by a number of factors where fretted instruments are concerned. If intonation is poor, you can tune the open strings to the correct notes, but then the fretted notes will be sharp or flat. This ukulele offers no way to adjust intonation; you'd need a qualified luthier to work on it, which would probably cost more than the instrument itself.

CONS
1. No included power supply.

3. Fan has some noise and fans are always a potential failure point.

SUMMARY
For the price, this little baby is quite impressive. The colors and designs are pleasing, and the audio modes seem pretty versatile. title: Best entry level Ukulele
comment:
The is my first Ukulele. I've owned electric guitars for 25 years and over time I sold everything I had. A friend of mine bought a Luna Tattoo so I went online and looked at the different brands and this one caught my eye. My Ukulele arrived in a very nice case. I opened the case and I noticed the Uke was covered in foam wrapping. I uncovered the instrument and I was very pleased by the finish on the product and the overall design.

I pulled the Ukulele out of the case and I strummed it and I was surprised to hear that it was perfectly tuned. The tuning is different from a guitar so I'll have to get use to it and get retrained. There are many free, helpful videos on Youtube that will get me pointed in the right direction. I also signed up for a free group lesson at the Guitar Center that is offered on Saturdays.

There are very minor blemishes but you really have to look for them to notice. On the front it looks great. On the sides there are a couple of blemishes I noticed but they are minor. One of the blemishes is a small dimple from possibly being hit against something and the other blemish that is on the side I can't see unless I take it outside in the sun then if I look carefully at an angle I can see a drip mark from the shellac or whatever it is they use to make it glossy.

The tone and projection of the Ukulele is excellent. I was very concerned about buying a cheap Ukulele for under $100 because I knew that if I didn't enjoy the sound I would soon become discouraged and question my playing ability and give the Ukulele away.. A good ukulele player can make a cheap Ukulele sound good but a novice player will just make a cheap Ukulele sound like a garbage disposal. A beginner should not buy a cheap Ukulele because the sound it will produce will most likely discourage the player from becoming serious. I'm sure there are exceptions but it's not the rule.

The case is better than expected and it comes with a nice strap for the case. The tuner and cloth were exactly as advertised.

This is the best entry level Ukulele on the market today.

title: Very Good, Almost Great with Caveats
comment:
Very good and almost great. The build quality is great, high quality handle-rollers and cabinet and a great set of inputs and controls: just what I wanted. In addition, the wireless mic is actually pretty good with a good pickup pattern and has a noticeable, but not objectionable, pre-amp noise level.
The downside is that the pre-amp section for the two mic inputs is really, really noisy. As soon as you plug into either of those inputs you are going to get a noticeable loud hiss that cannot be eliminated. I have had two of these and they are identical. The guitar input has less gain but does NOT have that issue so if you have a high output mic or external pre-amp you are good to go. You also can use a Phantom Power pre-amp and a condenser mic into this guitar input and it will be quiet. The two mic inputs are plain out noisy and start the noise as soon as you plug ANY cable into them, even without a mic! One other fixable item: the stand pole mount plate on the bottom is NOT a standard size and you should check you speaker stand to see if it will fit or as I have done, purchase the correct/standard size pole mount and replace the one on this unit.

An idiot designed this and they didn't test it. Spend the extra cash and get something that works from a name brand.

title: Does the job
comment:
I bought the UCA202 to substitute for my computer's built-in sound circuitry to drive my office stereo system. It works very well; the noise floor is lower and the sound seems much clearer and more transparent.

Installation was easy. Win7/64 found the drivers and installed them automatically and changed the default audio output device to the UCA202. A new volume control appeared in the icon area at the right of the taskbar, giving access to a mixer and various sound "enhancements" (right-click on the icon).

Basically, the device converts the computer's digital sound output to a stereo line-level signal available via a pair of standard RCA jacks. A normal double RCA cord with plugs at both ends then connects the device to your receiver or any other piece of equipment that expects a line-level signal. There are also a pair of RCA line-in jacks that seem to be intended for other line-level signals to be mixed with the computer's sound. The too-brief manual is not entirely clear on this! There is also an S/PDIF digital output.

One note: The level of audio output from the UCA202 has to be controlled from the computer (for example, the volume control icon mentioned above). The unit has its own thumbwheel-type volume control, but this affects only its headphone output via a standard 1/8" mini-jack. There is also a small slide switch to route the input signal "directly to the headphone output." Again, I'm not clear what difference this makes but have no need to find out! For those with more complex needs than mine, a better manual would be welcome.

To clarify the product page, the unit comes with no software, not even drivers. All the software mentioned is readily available on the Internet. Custom drivers are available at Behringer.com.

In short, the UCA202 was easy to install, works as advertised, and offers good sound for my purposes. A bargain.

title: OK, Not the Best, Needs Preamp
comment:
Pros: Looks good for the most. Price was great. Can be removed via the plug while endpin jack is permanently installed. Sounded good enough when used with a preamp (BEHRINGER V-Tone Acoustic Driver is what I'm using.) Had good string balance. Can be wired in permanently or used without modification.

Cons: Very weak signal without the preamp. Was not a solid piece of wood (mine had a glue line in the middle top -- can be seen in the attached pic.) Endpin jack holder was a little floppy. Mounting screws were crooked (I noted that on another review as well.)

Conclusion: This is just OK. I wanted something for a beater guitar that could play an open mic and was hoping that it would be strong enough to use without a preamp box, it is not. Has volume to turn down need be to kill feedback. Tone seems useless. I'd recommend spending a little more and getting a Dean Markley or Seymour Duncan (both better and both I'd use without a preamp if I had to) or spending a lot more and getting a DiMarzio (the best I've used) or L.R. Baggs.

title: Fatrat's good for non-extended range guitars
comment:
Bought it for guitar and doom riffage on a Ibanez RG8.

Bass boost or "Fat" setting is great for standard tuned guitars, but it might sound muddy on extended range guitars.

The fat setting will boost the low end and cut some of the upper frequencies which can muddy up the sound a bit. The stock setting sounds better for brightening up the low strings while keeping the rat growl intact.

I ordered both the rat2 and the fatrat to see which one sounds better for doomy riffs and the cheap rat2 growls just as good as the fatrat with the stock setting.

I dont want a low rumble, I want a growl. I'm returning the fatrat and saving a lot of money.

title: Beautiful Classical Guitar
comment: This classical guitar is simply beautiful and elegant to look at and a joy to play. It also happens to be made by the legendary guitar manufacturer, Jose Ramirez of Madrid, Spain.  It has a nice, warm tone, with bright trebles, excellent bass, intonation, and good volume.  The action is set to classical standards, 4mm at the 6th string and 3mm at the first.  My only exception to the original setup is, due to personal preference, changing the original Ramirez hard-tension strings to a set of normal tension D'Addario Pro Arte EJ45's.  Otherwise, this guitar is perfect.  It is definitely an excellent choice for the advanced student.  Based on Ramirez's 'E' line of estudio guitars, it is all solid wood, hand-crafted using top-notch materials. It compares favorably to Ramirez's 4NE model but at a lesser cost. This particular model will not be made after this year.  I highly recommend it, especially for those, like me, who cannot yet afford a hand-made, custom luthier guitar.

title: Decent mic, a few design flaws, one major issue.
comment:
This is a decent microphone. There are a few design flaws, and one major issue.

1. The Blue LED light on the front is extremely bright, and this is distracting in any environment and with any placement of the mic. As a general rule, no peripherals should use bright blue LED lights, but this one is always in front of you, so this is a really big design mistake. A red LED would be fine, and it should have a cloudy cover piece to soften the light.

2. The stand is very cheaply made and has stability and durability problems. The plastic is cheap, and the tri-leg design is flawed. The microphone is heavy enough to topple the stand over when positioned to the side of the legs. This is avoidable, if the microphone is installed upside down and flipped to the other side.

3. The unit has a problem with randomly "freaking out" when transmitting audio and it garbles the sounds transmitted. This happens frequently in Skype. I have not conducted extensive tests to see if this happens in other applications.

Minor issues:
Does not come with a pop-filter. And it does need a pop-filter, if you are using this to record vocal music up close. This is not necessary for regular voice chat.

title: A third the cost of a Taylor, but not as good
comment:
At 3x the price, a $2000 Taylor does sound better than this Cort.

Cort Pros
* Looks great, even in the details and finish.
* The built-in pickup system / tuner works great. Fishman makes great stuff.
* The tuners work well.
* String action is much easier than a $200 Yamaha.
* The packing materials are recyclable.

Cons
* Strummed chords sound muddy, even with new strings.
* Tuned BtoB, the lower two strings were buzzy initially. I loosened the truss slightly and that helped some. I think a better fix would be shimming up the bridge on that end.
* On my unit, regarding the lower strap pin / output jack, the nut inside the body arrived loose. I tried to reach in to tighten it, but my forearm is too large to fit through the sound hole, even with all the string removed. Over time the nut fell off.
* No case is included but you can buy an inexpensive soft case for $30.

title: Good sound, great isolation, fair build quality, rather uncomfortable at first
comment:
The sound on these is very good -- it's a bit lesser quality than the circumaural Sennheisers, and the bass is a bit flat so bring it up in the mix (unless you're on an iPod or other digitally amplified device where "bringing it up in the mix" causes clipping at every volume level), but there's a lot of good detail and imaging for a closed ear phone. The isolation, at least 30dB worth, is wicked good, better than most active isolaters -- I often leave these phones on at work even with no sound playing through them, just to tune out the ambient noise. They work great on a plane and other loud environments.

The build quality is very iffy. I'm on my third pair (thank you very much, 2 year warranty) and all three have cracked from standard travel in my laptop bag. And they are tight! Uncomfortably so at first, now it's only a bother if it's hot.

Finally -- the cable on these is about six feet long, coiled to three or four. That, combined with the ostentatious look, means you won't be wearing these on the street (though I do wear them to mox the lawn...no tin ear!)

title: Portable Roll-up Drum Pad for Kids
comment:
This is a nice toy for kids under 15. I don't consider this to be a professional electric-drum pad at this price range. But this can be used by kids to develop their sense of timing and rhythm and to play along with songs.
Likes: Portability--this roll-up pad can be charged and used wireless. This has around 7 built-in drum-set sounds. I only liked two of them, but my son enjoyed the silly sounds of the others. It also has several songs that you can play in the background while you practice your drumming. You are able to change the tempo slower and faster. You can also change the volume and it can surprisingly loud with the built-in speaker. The sticks are short and chunky and made for kids. I also like the pedals, which can simulate using an actual bass drum pedal and hi-hat.

Dislikes: You will need a stable surface to place this on. (not glass! and not meant for road trips) The pads are sometimes not responsive and will skip beats that you intended. At this price, I didn't expect that much sensitivity! The pedals as well, are not made for adults--don't use too much pressure!
The built-in songs can be quite annoying if your kid leaves them playing continuously!

This is a great toy and beginning tool to introduce drums to younger children at a good price-point.

title: Best midi keyboard. Period.
comment:
The Novation 61SL MKII is by far the best keyboard out there for music production. I previously owned an M-Audio Axiom Pro 61 which I sold to get this. Best thing I ever did. I have owned this keyboard for about 6 months now and still absolutely in love with it!

Unlike the other reviewers, I use the Novation with Propellerhead Reason 6.5 for both production in my home studio and for live gigs. Since I have a slower macbook that can only run Snow Leopard, I have yet to fully utilize its use with Automap (their latest version is only compatible with OSX Lion and up). That being said, it can still be used without Automap software at all--simply as a midi controller via usb. The first thing I noticed about the keyboard, as others stated, is the build quality. No other keyboard looks or feels as sharp as the Novation in my opinion. The keys are amazing (and thats coming from a piano player). Semi-weighted with after-touch. Quiet. NOT STICKY (like the M-Audio Axiom Pro) and seem to be able to take a beating without any damage after 6 months of banging pianos, pads and drum beats on them! Very easy access for controls you use often, put where you'd want them to be. Plenty of assignable buttons/faders. The faders were well done compared to the Axiom Pro. I noticed on Axiom Pro when I would assign knobs or faders to the keyboard and move them there was a distinct lag if moved too quickly. It would miss that I even moved the fader--not good in a live setting! The Novation has excellent mapping and does not do this. Very real-time controlling. You move quickly, the fader moves quickly.

Honestly, I have very little bad things to say about the keyboard. It's simply amazing. Worth the extra $$ hands down. I'm going to have this thing for a very long time to come.

PRO's:
-Best quality build of any midi keyboard out on the market
-Tons of buttons/faders, an X&Y pad, Pitch/Mod stick
-Seamless use with any DAW, especially Reason 6.5
-Touch sensitive buttons and faders. When you literally just touch a fader or a button the LCD screen switches to show the values of the entire row that button/fader was on as well as putting brackets around the actual button/fader you just touched. VERY COOL FEATURE. Extremely helpful for live gigging when you want to make sure you are about to pull up the correct pad or piano sound before it's actually used or to check the midi values of something
-Excellent feeling semi-weighted keys with aftertouch, don't squeak or stick

CON's: (and I'm being nit picky)
-The 8 black "drum pads" (lower left) are extremely stiff and require quite a hard punch to get any result. It would be hard to trigger a loop (accurately) or make beats with these (especially if touch sensitivity is important for your recording needs). Not nearly as good as the pads on a M-Audio Axiom Pro or Akai competitors. This clearly wasn't a focal point of the features this keyboard offers. That being said I don't use them all that much so it does not affect my view of the keyboard
-Only 8 faders instead of 9. That 1 extra would have been nice to use as a master fader, but alas
-The mod and pitch bend is a unique set up. It works, but I feel it is really lacking only because of the mod aspect. The mod "wheel" or lever snaps back to zero when done unlike some of the other wheels on the M-audio Axiom Pro which stay put where you left it
-Automap takes some serious getting used to... a little too much in my opinion. Lots of things to assign correctly
-Your friends will be sincerely jealous

For me, the thing that sticks out more than anything, is the amazing sound.  Pick it up, strum a chord, and take in the amazing result.  I sat down at a guitar store and tried various guitar makers in the $500 to $800 range and the Martin DRS2.  That guitar felt better in my hands than any of the other 10 or so that I tried, and the sound was simply amazing.  So I was basically committed to the DRS2, but then found my CUSTOM D for a bit less, so I grabbed it, and have no regrets.  It is quickly becoming my guitar of choice.  The only problems I've come across, is that my fingers are used to soft nylon strings, and sometimes get a bit sore, and the narrow neck makes chords with fingers close together easy to miss sometimes but the barre chords are easier.  I could not recommend this guitar any higher.  Besides...it has my name on it.

title: 19" is perfect for my Fender 2x12 cabinet...
comment:
Being a musician, I constantly try to find ways to not only make my setup time a little faster, but also things that give me a little peace of mind throughout my 4-hour shows. While micing my amp, I'd been draping my Sennheiser e609 over the top of my Fender Mustang IV and securing the cable to the cabinet with a strip of painters tape. Honestly, it's worked out ok in the past, but there were a few times the tape let loose allowing the mic to move away from my preferred sweet spot right in the middle of a show. That's really not something I wanted to keep doing or worrying about every time I played, nor did I want to drop $40+ dollars on one of those amp clamps or worry about lugging around & setting up an extra stand for my instrument mic. There wasn't really a better way on the market, so I came up with one...
.
I took a 19" gooseneck, a 4x4x2" piece of stiff packing foam, some gorilla glue, and a screwdriver and made a mic support that works perfectly and cost less than $8 by using items I had laying around the music room. I used the screwdriver to punch a hole thru the center of the foam, slid the snake thru the hole, and gorilla glued it to the female end of the gooseneck. The foam fits under my amps handle and not only secures the gooseneck to keep it from moving, but also acts as a deterrent to keep low frequency vibrations from making it to & being picked up by my E609. Setup is a breeze and it saves a little time, and once I'm through, I throw it in the back of my amp for storage. Couldn't have worked out better.
.
The gooseneck is strong enough to hold my E609 in place perfectly, but unless you're running a single 12" cabinet where the handle Is directly above the speaker, I doubt it would hold a mic much heavier without positioning it and wrapping some electrical tape around it to better hold the shape. The Shure mic clip I used had no problems screwing right on this gooseneck, but I did not try the female end as I didn't need to. All in all, I'd buy it again in a heartbeat to use for the application I described, but if I were going to use it to mount something heavier, I doubt I'd buy it. I'm just not confident it would work. Hope this helps...
.
Troy
47/M/TX

This kit provides everything you need to tune a piano, except ears, experience, and expertise.
The hammer is a star shape so you can get a good angle on the peg. Long enough for good leverage. Hammer technique is important for stable tuning.
The wedges with wire handles are very helpful for muting strings. The felt strip is good too.

title: Nice upgrade
comment:
I replaced my mixer and was imediately pleased with the new one. It has enough built in FX to eliminate the need for my floor pedals. The big advantage for me is it also allows me to record on my computer! On the recording side - BE AWARE that the supplied software, Cubase LE 5, does not offer any documentation specific to this hardware, and the mixer documentation doesn't cover anything related to recording.

Once I came to understand that unlike other devices covered in the Cubase documetation there are only two buses, "In" and "Out". The "Out" is controlled by three buttons on the mixer, "USB to MAIN", "USB to MONITOR", and "MAIN to MONITOR". This allows you to record a single track in Cubase from whatever is on the 'Main' ("In") bus of the mixer. You can create unuable routing (bad echos, static, etc.) so don't be discouraged. Once you get the hang of it you can record a track or two and then go back and lay down a vocal track while listening to your mix.

Now that I have worked with Cubase LE 5 and actually accomplished multi-track recording I decided despite the learning curve the recording abilities are good enough. I have never used Cubase so I will take some of the blame for setup issues I had but feel I must say I found their licensing problematic. Installing the software is one of the biggest challenges. It isn't made clear on their website but all versions of Cubase, including the trial versions, require a physical USB-Licenser dongle [[ASIN:B0009TD5Z4 Steinberg Key License Control Device]] so I was not able to see if the Cubase upgrades even worked with this unit.

In summary the mixer itself is great and despite my setup issues with the software I still think this is a great buy.

I just finished up repacking mine for return to Amazon. There were a number of issues, I will list them (not necessarily in order of importance to me):

2) Mismatched tonewood, esp the top was very noticeable in that there were two knots on one side of it the that were not evident on the other side.
3) Way too much gloss clearcoat applied, esp evident in & around the soundhole.
4) Action was way too high. I do my own setups & I'm not even sure the saddle had enough height to sand off an amount required to get the action low enough to be playable for me.

Sound wise I guess it was OK, nothing special imo. Kind of hard to tell when it really was not playable w/ the action being that high right out of the box. I understand & expect that China made guitars all are going to require a proper setup but this one was just too far out to attempt it. I give it two stars instead of 1 because I really did like the Sunburst finish, other than the fact there was probably 5x more gloss clearcoat applied than necessary.

title: Does NOT Work for 5-String Bass
comment:
I see a lot of reviews saying this tuner works well for low-B string basses, and I wanted to write my own experience: I bought this about a week ago, and I'm already returning it.

No matter what I did, there was at least a three-quarter-second delay between playing a note and seeing the indicator change, which isn't helpful for trying to fine-tune the pitch - every time I made a change, I'd have to wait for it to register. Across five strings, that time adds up in quick-moving rehearsals and performances. Notably, this happened across three different basses, and I tried this both direct into the tuner and run this out of the tuner jack of a Grace Design Felix preamp, with the amp/tuner volume turned all the way up.

Beyond the general delay, it couldn't handle even an E-string's low pitch. It noticed that there was a signal when played, but couldn't resolve it to a note two times out of three - the B string basically never resolved. I wound up having to tune harmonics, which were unstable in their own way.

Would not recommend. All players are going to see the time-delay problems; low pitch problems will hit bassists specifically.

title: First Decent Condenser Mic I've Tried!
comment:
[[VIDEOID:3434159248c91583a5dfa8026a7d47ac]] Overall from reviewing and testing this microphone, I've found the following:

PROS:
- Matte Black Consistent theme (not sure about pink version)
- Very good weight and metallic build to it
- Gain/Volume Nob Feature
- EASY USB plug-n-play setup
- Low Background noise pickup
- Works well when you're close & a little far away
This mic overall is really great for an amateur setup, and for someone on a budget, this has worked great for me!

CONS:
- Cheap Tripod
- Gain nob sensitivity
Hey, at least the mic comes with a tripod, right? Well, it does get the job done for the most part, just don’t expect
this to be the professional mic solution you've always hoped for. It sits too low on the desk and is partly plastic, but
the mic does work on a boom arm, so I'd recommend investing in one. Otherwise, the gain nob is a little sensitive,
and you'll have to keep it pretty low, for the most part, not being able to adjust it much over 15% unless if you're
really far away. (or else you'll always be peaking)

Other things to note are that the cable is 6ft long, and is made of the typical rubbery-material, pretty standard.
Otherwise, | think this microphone is exactly what you pay for, and is pretty worth it for me! (not five stars because
there wasn't anything that blew me out of the water, but it does sound very nice!)

title: Best Monitor Stands on the Market
comment: I picked up a set of the Soundrise Pro stands earlier this year and have been extremely satisfied with their performance. They raise your monitors to the ideal listening height when coupled with most standard desks and are much more stable than I first had expected. I paired mine with a set of Yamaha HS7 studio monitors, which are notoriously on the larger side for desktop speakers. But these solid aluminium stands held their weight very well. The shape of the stands actually allows for them to flex subtly under weight, and this is especially useful when listening to music at higher volumes because they absorb a noticable amount of the sound reverberations that would normally transfer into your desktop. I’ve had my monitors sitting on top of my Soundrise Pros for almost eight months straight and there are no signs of any wear or stress on either stand.  Another subtle feature of these stands is the textured finish, which provides for good friction against your speakers and prevents any chance of slippage. The final feature of the Soundrise Pros that I especially appreciate is the cable management hole through the center. It saves me from having to use extra cable ties or velcro wraps, which can move around and often take away from the sleek look of your system. All of these seemingly subtle design elements come together to create a product that looks and feels extremely professional. I would never settle for anything less than these, and will continue to recommend them to fellow producers and music enthusiasts because of how much they have contributed to the appearance and functionality of my computer setup. Easily a 5 star product across the board.

All the cords were packed neat and secure and the setup was extremely easy. I recommend that you purchase a compact mixer to ease the stress of running your line straight through the speaker. It also improves your sound quality.

I am still waiting for some audio cords to come in (purchased separately for DJ Controller) and I cant wait to crank it up to see how much more power we are working with. So far it’s as good as promised, if not better. I highly recommend these speakers for a mobile dj or band.

title: My 140W Pioneer receiver drives harder than this NX3000D
comment:
At first power up with no cables connected (not purchased or assembled yet), there was arcing inside seen through the front vent grill. After immediately shutting it down and checking that nothing could have happened externally, I tried it again for a couple seconds and no more sparks.

So after building some cables and testing it out, it seems to drive the intended speaker ok, with no more arcing, but...

I was planning to connect a series/parallel network of these 50W speakers to equivalent of 450 to 900W, but with only one connected and driven with this AMP at full power, it seems to be barely pushing 100W. My 140W receiver drives this single speaker much harder than this AMP does.

Although I can't see any arcing damage or blown/burnt components inside, something must have limited the output on both channels.

I guess a return is in order. Hopefully I can update this review with a happier ending soon!

title: Impressive value for the price
comment:
Back when I first started playing guitars (1969), budget instruments were horrible. Warped necks, bad intonement, bad action, just everything was going to ensure a struggle for a beginner. Flash forward to now and wow!

A guitar for $129 has to cut corners. The surprising thing it is hard to see what makes this instrument so cheap. It is built in China and supposedly get a tune up and adjustment check at the Epiphone USA factory before going to the consumer. The neck and action right out of the box were really nice. I changed the strings after a couple of hours because they sounded off. After that it sounds and plays well, with the neck being as comfortable as my 1975 Gibson Les Paul Custom. Not the same, but just as good. Uses a wrap-around bridge instead of a Tune-a-matic style with end stop. The only bridge adjustments are bridge posts for height adjustment and two longish screws on either end of the bridge for intonement adjustment. While noticeable, the intonement screws haven't gotten in the way of playing. Have a pair of small needle nose pliers handy to grab the string end when changing strings on the wrap-around.

The guitar looks pretty good. I expected "worn cherry" to have raw wood in spots, but it's really a very nice matte finish over the entire instrument that has no visible flaws. Nothing exciting about the wood grain, but nothing ugly about it, either. Lightweight and easy on the shoulder. Can't believe how nice this instrument is for the price.

So where does it fall short?

The tuners - two are noticeably stiffer than the others. They do work fine, but a some point I may want to upgrade to a set of [[ASIN:B0002GJGUM Grover 102C Rotomatic 3 per Side Machine Heads, Chrome]] and [[ASIN:B003HGFRO8 Graph Tech TUSQ XL 1/4" Epiphone Slotted Nut]]

Controls - just two pots, a common volume and a common tone, and a 3 position pickup selector switch. It would be nice to have separate volume and tone control to allow mixing the sound of the neck and bridge pickups, but it is not a deal breaker.

There was no cord, pick, or strap included. There is no case or gig bag included, so pick one up for your instrument's protection. Don't worry about protection in shipment, the Epiphone factory packaging is excellent and your guitar will get to you without problems.

Overall, a very good instrument for a beginner on a budget. Experience players will find they can love one of these, as well, when looking for a different sound or a second instrument they don't want to worry over.

Keys don't feel like actual piano keys, but they do feel like a professional performance keyboard. The M-Audio Oxygen 49 has more control knobs and buttons than you can shake a stick at -- okay maybe not, but plenty to satisfy your control needs.

The included 3rd-party software left me disappointed, because both Ableton Live and ProTools First focus more on copying & pasting someone else's loops than on helping you make your own music. But since this is a class compliant MIDI controller, you can use your MIDI software of choice. I've had no problems using the Oxygen 49 with Sekaiju, Anvil Studio, as well as ACID Music Studio 10 and the latest ACID Pro 8.

title: Best bass anywhere near this price! Really!
comment:
I play bass professionally and bought this bass as a beater/practice bass/flyswatter but was pleasantly surprised that it is good enough to take to gigs (with a better pickup). It is an absolute steal. It plays well after setup and other than being slightly top heavy, feels great. As everyone else has already said, it arrives with the necks truss rod loosened for shipping - this is normal. You must either set this up yourself, take it to your bass teacher and have him or her do it for you, or take it to any good music store - it doesn't take long. I set it up in about five minutes and got the strings down as low as they would go. No buzz anywhere, frets were level and had no sharp edges. The pickup is weak but functional and just fine for someone learning to play. I replaced mine with a stock Fender P-Bass pickup, so now it sounds like a Fender and plays about as well. The stock strings are functional but are rough and will need to be replaced ASAP, but that is expected with a cheap bass.

As I wrote earlier, the bass is top heavy, but other reviews told me that before I ordered it so it was expected. This problem is fairly minor and was solved by adding a little weight to the guitar strap near the bottom of the bass. I use a 1 pound strap through scuba divers weight. It is very light to begin with, so one extra pound isn't going to strain anyone. The strap that comes with the bass is cheap and too short for me, but it's functional and will be just fine, as is the gig bag, cord and the other assorted stuff. Make sure you hang onto the Allen wrenches, you need to make adjustments every few months until the bass settles in completely and the sizes might be a pain in the backside to find. I'm not sure if they are metric, but they probably are. The neck feels smooth and unvarnished, just the way I like it.

title: Innovative Product with some Great Features and Some Disappointments
comment:
I debated between giving the Korg Triton Taktile a 3 or a 4 rating. I would prefer a 3.5, but ultimately rated it as a four, as its strengths outweigh its disappointing weaknesses.

This product is unusual in that it is a 25 key Midi controler that has 500+ on-board Triton sounds. I am not aware of any similar product, and I was excited when it was released.

First the good stuff. It is a well built, solid instrument, heavier what you would expect from a simple midi controller. To me, that is a good thing - it feels and looks like a professional keyboard. The Triton sounds are great, the keyboard action and touch are about perfect. The touch pads and there responsiveness feel and sound good. The keyboard has numerous features and capabilities allowing the user to make some sophisticated music. Overall, well built, solid, and sounds nice.

Now for the negatives. You will not learn about all of the capabilities from the scant documentation. I would recommend you watch a few You Tube videos on the product before purchase, and realize you are going to have to figure out how to do the cool stuff on your own. The documentation is poor. There is a users manual that comes with the keyboards. After the standard safety warnings there are a grand total of 6 pages. There is a note in the documentation recommending you download the parameter guide, which is 21 pages. The parameter guide is more helpful, but after reading both several times and working through the documentation I still cannot seem to get to some of the commands I need to, particularly with regard to the trigger pads. I am no expert, but I have other keyboards I have been successful with after reviewing their documentation.

There are also apparent mistakes in the documentation that are confusing. There is one Midi port on the device. On the case of the keyboard it is identified as "Midi Out" which makes sense. But in the documentation it is referred to as Midi In in one case, Midi out in another, and simply Midi port in another. In the text describing the Midi Port the reference to the port is sometimes plural, for example "these ports are used for controlling DAW software". This particular section on the Midi port is extremely confusing. I have not tried it yet, but I am assuming it is midi out as labeled on the keyboard.

Another disappointment is the keyboard's audio output. On one diagram it was labeled as a headphone output, in other places it is simply referred to as "Output Jack" I can find no specifications for the audio ouput jack in the documentation. The output jack is a stereo mini-jack, which seems non-standard for this type of device. It works with headphones, although the audio levels on some instruments are a little low for my taste. The use of a stereo mini-jack for this type of keyboard makes it seem more like a lower end consumer keyboard. I would have much preferred traditional left and right quarter inch jacks that can be directly connected to a mixer or audio interface. I am sure this connection can still be made using adapters, but the extra adapters in the signal path are not optimum. I am sure all of this was done to save space. It appeared that the design goal was to have all connections on the back right side. I think it would have been better to have two 1/4 audio outputs on the back, and a traditional 1/4 inch headphone jack on the side.

The majority of the controls on the keyboard have lit lettering when they are engaged. But many of the keys are very difficult to read when not engaged. You will need strong lighting on the board.

The documentation insists that you use the included USB cable, and the trouble shooting guide attributes all sorts of potential problems that will occur when you do not use their cable. But most of us will not, as it is only 2.5 feet long and it is not a very substantial cable. I used a 5 foot cable from one of my other midi controllers and it works well. You should note that it does not come with and AC/USB power supply. I am using the one from my IPad and it works fine.

Finally, I have never seen a keyboard that did not have the brand name displayed on large letters on the back of the device. This one is blank. I kind of like the brand on the back. I wonder why Korg decided to go without it.

I had debated returning the Korg, but the more I played with it the more I like the sounds and the other features I praised about. So I am going to keep it, but cuss whenever I cannot see the buttons or have to fool with adapters to get a decent audio hookup.

title: Great little soundcard
comment:
This thing really does the trick for a small home studio type of setup. What really sells me on it is good drivers. So many pro interfaces have crap drivers that are a never ending source of headaches. The Focusrite drivers have no issues I’ve seen. It just does its job, without complaint. Very nice mixer software too. Despite being a little unit, it has the same mixing DSP as the bigger ones so you can set up whatever sort of internal routing you need for your given task.

It has a good feature set too for the price. Two mic/line/instrument inputs with variable gain, plus a pad and phantom power, two fixed line inputs, 4 line outputs, S/PDIF I/O, MIDI I/O, and two headphone outs. So whatever gear you have, you can probably hook it up.

I’m really pleased with it, and can’t think of another soundcard in the price range that I would recommend more.

When run almost all the way to the left on the distortion (near 9 o'clock) with volume up (near 4 o'clock) and filter all the way up and switch on hyper I find it sounds a lot like a modern day blues overdrive. With the distortion cranked, the filter all the way to the left, the switch on classic and the volume where appropriate you get a nice hard clipping distortion almost near fuzz territory. You can kinda dial in anything in between with this thing. I don't think I would consider it a "metal distortion" because it doesn't have a huge scoop to the mids and even the low end doesn't have a great roll off, but overall, I think this is a GREAT budget pedal for the price.

title: One of the BEST I have used (4.5 stars)

I installed them in my basement studio for tracking guitars, bass, and sometimes drums. I can easily say that these are some of the best acoustic sound treatments I have ever used.

They diffuse and absorb unwanted frequencies beautifully. They do a wonderful job reducing reverb in large empty spaces. They are ready to go, right out of the box. You do not need to prepare them, decompress them, etc., unlike the foam counterparts.

The reason for the 4.5 stars is the corners of the frames are very sharp and some of the hardware behind it was loose but this was an easy fix.

I sincerely hope that you would find this review helpful.

title: I would recommend visiting a physical store touching feeling the wire and ...
comment: I previously own two pairs of these lines (same make and model) and bought them from physical store and have been using them for many years without any issues. I bought 2 of these lines as backups, the new ones I bought online feel cheaply made. The Fender logos at the ends of the cables smeared off the instant I touch them; when compared to the ones I've had for years and suffered much abuse and those logos are still going strong. The metal housing feel weak and brittle compared to my previous ones. The copper wire inside appears thinner and the ones I currently own. I don't know if Fender is outsourced this product to be manufactured in China or anywhere else but it sure looks and feels that way. Also there are a lot of counterfeit Fender parts and accessories out there today I can't prove this one is one of those but just watch out. These cables do work and so far no issues have occurred, but I would not use these on a daily basis for my main rig. I would recommend visiting a physical store touching feeling the wire and the cable itself before purchasing.

The Blue Ember microphone is a solidly built microphone that provides fantastic value for the price. The Ember provides a warm tone that is well-suited for vocals. Despite being a small-diaphragm condenser I did not find it lacking in capturing nuance.

One caveat: a pop filter is mandatory. The Ember does not handle plosives well and even with good mic technique this will be an issue.

Caveat aside, the Ember is excellent for voice applications.

title: I am incredibly pleased with this purchase all the way around. Absolutely no question it is worthy of a 5-star rating.
comment:
The iGear Crystal Sound Lavalier Lapel Clip-on microphone is designed for vocal or video recording. It provides clear and accurate voice recordings without the muffled sound or with. It provides broadcast quality recordings and I wouldn’t be surprise if they are actually using iGear Crystal Sound microphones. It comes with a 1.5m cable for greater flexibility and long range, meaning, you can move a further distance while recording and it will not destroy the quality of the recording. This microphone provides omnidirectional (receives sounds from all direction) pick-up pattern. This microphone is very lightweight, portable, flexible, and comfortable. It did not fall off or interfere with me moving around while it is clipped on my cloth. It can be used for indoor and outdoor recordings. The quality of my recordings sounds so much better than before. During playback, my recordings sounded more professional and crystal clear.

Now, I was very happy when I received this microphone. It is compatible with my Apple I-phone 6 Plus. Looking at the specs on the website for this microphone, I was a little confused with the term TRRS. Afterwards, I did a little research and discovered the reason why. Many people such as me do not have a clue what TRRS means. When dealing with audio jacks, there is a common family of connectors typically used for analog signals.
It is cylindrical in shape, with two, three, or four contacts. Two contact versions are known as TS connectors, the “T” stands for “tip” and the “R” stands for “ring.” With this microphone, there is a four- contact version. TRRS would mean this is a “tip”, “ring”, “ring”, and a “sleeve.” This microphone has a 3.5mm TRRS Jack. The microphone is compatible with Apple iPhone, iPad, iPod Touch, Android & Windows Smartphones.

Also it comes with a soft pouch, 2 wind muffs and 2 clip-ons. Since iGear believes in their product so much they give you 30 days money back guarantee and a Lifetime warranty.

title: Same size as the standard Tele. A lot of the ads elsewhere say, "for small hands"., Um...no. But, it's fine for the money.
comment:
Firstly, I was looking for a good "Tele" type guitar, but on like a 3/4 scale. I've been playing since the 50s and have a number of "real" Telecasters and Strats.

I must have been pretty tired when I finished up my on-line search for a smaller or 3/4 size guitar and ordered this one for that reason. Well, Duh, it's not any smaller with the exception that the neck is not quite a millimeter narrower at the fifth fret.

I adjusted the saddles for accurate intonation open and at the12th fret and it came out pretty good. I'll probably do some wiring tinkering like running the neck pickup out of phase, but...I digress. This is a nice little "hack around", Saturday afternoon garage jam guitar and when I get to spend more time with it, it might even put a little time on the recorder on my next million seller song. The next one will be...let's see....one in a row. Yep, it's ok. For $200 you won't do much better.

I also have an American strat which i played both guitars side by side to compare the sound. The Wilkinson is not as good as American pickup, however the vintage sound of these pickups are very nice and worth the investment. The wiring is very high end and easy to use.
The only issue, which other folks also noted, mismatch screws which do not fit the pickups. I'm not sure why they shipped that way, but hoping manufacturer address this issue. Lot more cheaper pickups don't have this kind of issue.

title: Super detailed sound, accurate but still pleasant!
comment:
I've been making music semi-professionally for about 10 years at this point, I've experienced everything from crappy computer speakers to full studio rigs. Previous to these, I used the KRK Rokit 6 for my home studio (I still have those, but I use them as general speakers, for which they do quite nicely ).

I've had these for about a year now and they've definitely made a big impact on my ability to mix songs effectively. They are extremely accurate in all ranges (especially if you have a decent room). The sound staging is excellent, you get a very real impression of space. The tweeter style is quite nice - there's a pleasant texture to the high end. The lows are honest, so if you've got problems down there you're going to hear them. Having power switches and additional volume controls on the front is an awesome feature!

On balance, I think these are an excellent choice in the price range. I'm super pleased with mine, and have no regrets or desires for anything else in the monitor world.

This is the first time that I tried the Harmo brand.  I like the idea of supporting a US company, especially one in Idaho as I suspect it might be the next best place as Colorado is getting too crowded.  The Harmo Polar and Marine Band play very similarly.  I feel the Harmo responds with a little less breath.  I can't be specific, but playing the harmonica part of Helpless in C sounds better on the Polar.  My Marine Band sounds a little sharp to me after playing my Polar.  I also like the white cover and styling of the Polar.  It somehow feels pleasantly retro and gives me the impression that I'm playing an ice-cream sandwich.  I bought both key of C and key of G.  I plan to buy a key of D so I can play After the Goldrush and a key of A (possibly the Harmo Torpedo model)  to play Out On the Weekend.

title: Excellent choice on vintage instruments.

One day I decided to try the Rubino in my Martin 00-28C. When I opened them a bright red glow burst into the room .. The basses were a crimson red like no one had seen before. The trebbles were of a color that emulated the cat guts used by the jugglers during the middle ages. And then I try them. When I played them for the first time, a voice emerged...a sound as clear and sweet as the lyre of the same Orpheus, many said that the resonance can be heard and felt in the room until this day.
Ok maybe I am over reacting, but really they are soft strings with a clear and unique sweet sound.
Just one more thing, first set I received come with red basses, second one with white basses.. not sure why is that.

title: Sound and playability equal to other guitars  in the price range...AND it folds for travel.
comment:
I travel to Central America several times a year and wanted to have a travel guitar that had a full sized neck length and nut width. I foolishly thought that it would have to have a small body. There are several to choose from, and I ended up with a Washburn Rover. I tried my best to appreciate it, but it sounded awful and played even worse. When the bridge started to lift it was time to start shopping again, and was I glad that I did.
The Voyage-Air OM-02 arrived last week and I can't stop playing it. Of course it does not sound like my 10 year old solid tonewood Dread, but who would expect that. It does stand up to similar guitars in the low to mid price range, AND you can travel with it. Sound and playablility way beyond the other travel guitars. I was a little worried that not having a solid top would would be a problem, but it sounds fine, and I'm thinking it was a good trade off for the added durability. It is a little bulkier than I had expected, so well have to see how well it fits overhead. Most reviews have said OK in the larger jets. Called the company with a couple of questions and they were very responsive.

I found this cable to be constructed well and they should last a long time if treated properly. The connectors are constructed well and are not loose when connecting devices. When testing the cables for sound issues I did not detect any noise issues and I had no noise added due to improper shielding.

If you are looking for a well-constructed and noise-free short adaptor cable you should consider giving this cable a try.

title: Amazing sounding budget headphones! decent comfort
comment:
I use this as my gaming headphone for my Playstation 4. It have absolutely no complaints!

Pros
-Bass is very tight and clean, not like the SOL Republic V8's I have which have looser muddier bass.
-The High are good, they come out a little strong but sound clean and mids are clean as well.
-The Sound stage aka surround sound is amazing as others said, I can say it is with confidence! Again, I use the for gaming hooked up to my Turtle Beach DSS and even when connected straight to the controller sound stage is still good.
-The wire is detachable!
-The volume levels are good but the highs are a little strong at the loudest volume.
-Comes some extra's! (baggy, velvet changeable ear pads, 1 short 1 long cable, 6.5 mm adapter)

Cons
-Like SOL Republic V8 since they don't have an adjustment band they clamp onto your ears. This can fatigue your head after an hour or two so they aren't for extended listening
-They aren't comfortable for people wearing glasses, thin frame gaming glasses wearers can get by.
-The wire is detachable! so it comes out easy if yanked on for all those clumsy people out there.
-If you plan on using these with a phone, the connector is kinda thick and might no work with all cases.
-Leather gets hot (but they come with softer ear covers so you can change them)

Overall
I really love these headphones! I haven't used them with the softer ear pads so the comfort could be a none issue. These are the best sounding headphone I have ever had! The removable cable is wonderful so no worrying about breakage (I'm not clumsy but maybe a short but I cold fix that lol) They are pretty big but I say buy with confidence

title: Very sturdy!
comment:
I'll start with what I like the least... the rubber parts that come in contact with the horn are a little tacky and give a little grab to the horn when lifting it of the stand, not a big deal, I'll be buying a bone sock for that.
On to the good stuff. I have a 40 year old Holton that has seen better days and is not trustworthy to securely hold any of my horns so I spent several weeks researching stands. The gal that play second chair recently got one of these stands and I was impressed. I asked on several trombone groups and the K&M was the clear choice. It's solid, the knobs fasten solidly although they are a bit small. My Holton had large knobs and they actually get in the way, these K&N knobs don't protrude out so I think they are fine. The locking mechanisms are well designed and lock positively. All the joints on the legs are tight and smooth. (Edited 5/24/18) Inspecting the plastic parts revealed a nice surprise. The plastic base hub is glass (fiberglass) impregnated plastic as are other main structural party's. They are engineered well and I'm pretty sure they will last a long time. I play a King 3B and a vintage 1930s Blessing and this stand is solid. I'd trust it with a heavier horn no problem. If I had a real heavy horn then maybe I'd go with one of their bigger stands. Great value for the price.

title: The Donner Sweet Juice gets an A+.
comment:
[[VIDEOID:599abb41df7d526315f237dfbd6369d3]] The Donner Sweet Juice is something that I've always wanted, but didn't know that it existed. I play through an Epiphone Valve Junior 5 watt tube amp that has a volume knob and that's it. The amplifier sounds great but doesn't offer much as far as adjustment. The Sweet Juice offers two channels that are based on the Fender 65 Twin Reverb and the Orange AD30. It's like owning two unique guitar amps in a single guitar pedal.

Channel A/Green channel is based on the Fender Twin Reverb. Channel B/Red channel is based on the Orange AD30. Green is clean and Red is crunch. The pedal gives you control of level, reverb, gain, low, mid, and high. It also offers an amp simulator that can make your tiny combo amp sound like you're pushing several 4×12 speaker cabinets! Once you have the knobs turned where you want them, the pedal stores it as a preset. This allows you to switch over to the other channel, turn the knobs where you want them, and again, leave a preset. Basically, you have independent control over the settings for each channel. I am familiar with both of the amps that the pedal simulates and I have to say, they sound very authentic. Even better, you have extra gain and extra reverb that the originals don't allow. The pedal produces sounds that are as warm as a tube amp and truly sounds analog. This is a great thing. Most guitar pedals that are not true analog sound very processed and artificial. That is not the case with the Donner Sweet Juice. The company obviously spent a lot of time and effort on fine tuning the pedal. It's simply sounds fantastic!

It would cost thousands of dollars to purchase original or replicas of the Fender and Orange amps. The downside to owning those amplifiers would be the maintenance upkeep and bulkiness in size and weight. The Donner Sweet Juice is like shrinking those two amps into one tiny pedal that fits in your pocket. The price will cost you less than $100 and not much more than $50. That is a steal! I'm very particular in how my guitar setup sounds and have gone through dozens of pedals and several amplifiers over the years. Finally, I have something that sounds great for recording and for live performance.

I suggest not to waste your money on expensive boutique pedals and vintage amplifiers until you have tried the Donner Sweet Juice. Donner has proven itself in making amazing yet inexpensive guitar pedals for several years now. They are definitely the best value in the guitar equipment market. The pedals are well-built, look and sound great, and offer you so much for such a low cost. I own 15 Donner guitar pedals and love them all, but the new king of my pedal board is the Sweet Juice. Thank you, Donner!

title: nanoKEY Studio - good controller, software bundle is lacking
comment:
nanoKEY Studio

Nicely build, lightweight, pads are stiff, faders have a smooth rotational feel, keys have a click sound (like a computer keyboard), but it's easy to play on. It uses 2 AAA batteries. I received the nanokey studio 5 days ago and have been using it since the first day (about 2 to 3 hours average per day); still on the same batteries it shipped with.

Bluetooth: connected to iOS GarageBand and Korg Gadget, no noticeable lag or latency of any kind. It never dropped connection, even when I left it alone for awhile.

Wired: I was able to connect it to my iMac without any issues, but just for testing since I own a full 88-key Roland. I was also able to assign parameters to the faders through Logic's midi learn cc and they worked well.

I was hoping the Korg Software Bundle would have included something useful. But I was wrong. The included software key only unlocked two extra Gadgets from the Korg Gadget LE app, which left the app pretty much in its very limited functionality form. In other words, be prepared to buy any apps you were thinking of using with this controller. I purchased Korg Gadget (full version $39.99) mainly because the most important parameters are automatically mapped, which makes this whole experience really nice.

I would have rated 5 stars if there had been some kind of discount for the full version of Korg Apps, especially since they made a big deal about the Software Bundle they were gonna include. The LE versions are incomplete and not very helpful when trying to compose.

comment:
These strings have changed everything!

I have a 1969 Garcia, one of few that were still made in Spain, with a cedar top and rosewood sides and back. (You can find videos online comparing it favorably with a Ramirez from the same time.) The last time it sounded this good was 30 years ago when I had La Bella Pure Silver strings on it. Unfortunately, La Bella seemed to change the formula some time in the ‘80s and they were never the same.

Since then, the best strings for this instrument have been Savarez combos. Last month I decided to try a D’Adario EJ45TT set. I was severely disappointed. They seemed competent but generic and some of the strings were distinctly “thudy”. I took them off after a week. I then put this set on and was frankly gobsmacked! Besides the full, rich, tone color, these strings also have a shining, scintillatingly bright dimension. (See! I’m already waxing rhapsodic!)

Perhaps the most surprising and gratifying thing is that they are immensely “musical”, and I found myself inspired immediately- new melodies and ideas just seemed to arrive under my fingers. In short, they are a joy to play! This is the best my guitar has sounded for decades. Now I can't wait to play and see what new things I will discover!

FOLLOW UP: I have had three sets of these strings and each time the D string snapped after 4-6 weeks of use. I kept hoping this was an anomaly but same string three times? Big disappointment. As much as I love the sound of these strings, I can no longer recommend them.

title: Best suited for big big heads
comment:
The Tascam TH-2000 are best suited for big big heads. I often have trouble fitting in other headphones but with the Tascam TH-2000 I fit in at around the halfway point of the full extension. They extend up to 13 steps. The first extension is to the 9th step point and if you pull them again, you get four more steps. The total range is around 4cm. Accommodating big heads is their biggest plus if you are having a hard time fitting into most of the headphones out there.

The headband had a very strong smell. Two weeks later, the smell was still there. About a month later, it has subsided some, but if you put your nose to the headhand, it's still there. Most of the time they were stored in an open room without any bags or cover. I don't recall having smell issues like this with headphones before. The included Tascam bag had its own separate smell as well, but that one mostly went away after leaving it in an open space for a couple of days. I am not sure what to make of this since I am not a chemist/materials scientist. While smelly, construction quality, while not fancy or polished, feels at least as good as its price.

This is a closed headphone so it has some of the usual closed vs open headphone trade offs. Overall nothing screamed foul at me, except for a couple of modern rock songs with quieter/fragile vocals sounding mismatched with their instruments (I forgot to write them down). For busier music, I did not notice this. Compared to other headphones, I haven't found a close match in sound, and a lot of it seemed like personal preference. I did not notice any issues while playing the default piano setting on a Casio music keyboard. This from an average consumer perspective using them in real world situations, not from a technical/audiophile perspective sitting on a comfy chair and listening to pristine audio sources under ideal circumstances and paying attention to every note.

The Tascam TH2000 are good at keeping the music in and diminishing outside sounds. When playing music at mid-volume at a cafe, I have to put my ear to the earcup to hear any sound. It also does a good job of reducing the outside world sounds, especially when playing music that has a constant volume output, at around mid volume. It diminishes outside sounds but does not extinguish them. For example, at coffee shops, I can still hear the blender, but not as loudly. A group sitting next to me chatting non-stop, I could only hear them in-between songs and in quieter points in the music. Helpful in concentrating better.

Comfort wise, they are causing discomfort where the eyeglass frames curve at the back. I rarely have this type of issue with headphones. This with thin frame eyeglasses. I do not have the thicker (Elvis Costello) type of glasses to see how that would work. The Tascam TH2000 earcups are round with a 9.5 inch diameter, not oval. It's not a strong clamping issue, but probably a head shape vs headphone shape misalignment (or something like that) that causes this. It got better after a month but it's still there. I haven't used them without glasses.

The cable cannot be removed and it is a thicker/heavier than average coiled cable. One benefit of the heavier coiled cable: I can wrap the cable around my shoulder and attach the Sandisk Clip Plus to it, and while sitting or moving slowly, the tiny Clip+ stays put. Good if you don't want to put things in pockets or have cables below the waist. There is always a risk it may fall or hit something, but the Clip+ is tiny and inexpensive. This is too risky with smartphones and iPods.

Each earcup structure folds inward at the pivot point to make them smaller. The earcups themselves rotate 90 degrees in one direction and maybe 45 degrees in the other direction facing you. The earcups also tilt 90 degrees down on their own axis.

Included with the headphones is a black 10x8 inch drawstring pouch, with the opening on the 8-inch side and a noticeable white/silver Tascam logo on one side. Also included is a screw-on 1/4" adapter. The front of their outside box has a "secret" page that opens up to show more info, including a frequency response chart. The outside box is 10x9x4 inches and easy to disassemble for recycling purposes. It does not look pretty for gift giving.
