BUT...It needs more sliders, more knobs, more control. With all the people using Live, Logic, Reason, etc., and all of the newer interfaces and controllers coming out from Arturia, Native Instruments, Novation, Korg, etc., to compete, you need to provide more...well, "control", if you're going to call yourself a controller. there is PLENTY of room to do this on this LARGE keyboard, so I'm sure it was to match a price point, yet, I'm still a bit underwhelmed by their absence.

If you need ONLY a full size keyboard with a built in very nice audio interface/mic preamp, then this fits the bill. However, if you need to control your DAW and/or plug-ins, this isn't really going to do it for you.

title: Took some extra work but worth it
comment:
PROS
* various finish options
* priced in the range of other strap locks
* flushmount is a nice option if your guitar can accomodate

CONS
* not easily reversible due to needing to drill a larger hole for the flush mount insert
* requires drilling about 1/4" deeper than a standard schaller straplock
* maintenance is recommended in the instructions -- cleaning with wd-40 to avoid jamming/sticking
* may have difficulty using certain higher quality/thicker leather straps
* error in instructions calls for drilling with a 0.400 drill bit (good luck finding one). They actually mean that you should drill with a 3/8 bit, to a depth of .400 - measured the part with calipers before I installed.

DETAILS
I usually use Fender/Schaller style straplocks on my guitars and have never had an issue with those, other than the nut that holds the top half to the strap can come loose - but when it does it is typically obvious before you would ever have an issue. You tighten it with a socket on one side, and an adjustable wrench holding the other side -- and it will stay tight for a long, long time. The only problem -- on an SG the strap lock is on the back, facing your stomach, and the Schallers stick out about 3/4 of an inch, and poke you in the stomach. (Or possibly worse if you like to hang your guitar more at groin height. Can I get a snare hit?) Dual design Dunlops also stick out too far, really, on the SG. I tried those first but finally came back to the Flush Mounts, and in the end I'm very glad I did. However...

I'm confident that both Schaller and Dunlop make good products that work well when properly installed. But the countersunk Dunlops are a little tricky on an SG. Mine is a '61 SG reissue, for reference. Compared to a "normal" straplock - Schaller or dual purpose Dunlop -, the screws will go about 1/4" deeper. (The actual screw length is similar but they are mounted deeper due to being in the bottom of the flush mount insert.) See photo of 2 screws and consider what is flush with the guitar body and that will make sense to you.

The flush mount Dunlop *seemed* genius and like it was the perfect solution. Unfortunately, the screws are so long that they will go slightly through the front of the guitar, although in a location that is covered by a plastic cover. I read on a website that on an SG you will actually hit the truss rod, but mine didn't - I was extremely careful to drill with a tiny pilot drill first, and "feel for it", gradually expanding until I had the right hole.I removed the front plate, felt for the pilot drill, finally put the screw in once I determined that it was no big deal, and filed the tip of the screw so it would be flush with the face of the guitar, then replaced the plastic cover that hides it.

If you use a thick leather strap like a Levy's leather strap, you may have difficulty getting these on the strap. I have several Levys straps, and on one there was no way these would ever work. On the others, I was able to actually get it to fit ok.

On the bottom edge of the guitar after drilling the 3/8" hole, I discovered Gibson had a 15/64" hole inside with a plastic anchor for the original strap button -- too big for the screw to fasten into obviously. So I had to drill that out further to 1/4", glue in a hardwood dowel, drill a pilot hole in that, and then mount the new assembly.

It has worked out fabulously well, but this was more work than any other straplocks I've installed. On my SG, the other style locks sticking out was a real pain, and this is much better -- I'm glad I did it but it took a bit of extra work.

UPDATE: I gigged with this as my main guitar for more than a year since these were installed. Still very happy with them - no issues. They also never come loose (unlike my schallers), never need tightening, and that goes for the "guitar-side" part and the "strap-side" part. In fact I bought another SG today (you know...GAS), and I'm about to order another set of these and install them on that guitar too!

title: Loves Windows 8 - automatic and perfect install!

When I opened up Hardware and Sound in Control Panel, I watched the computer automatically switch back and forth between playback devices as I plugged and unplugged the USB connection. It doesn't get any simpler than this.

Among other things, I've been using it to run a club karaoke show through a rather large sound system - complete with monitors and subwoofers. If I max the gains with no music, I can detect just a faint amount of digital noise - but at those levels it would rip my head off if I were playing a track. It's infinitely better than the computer's internal sound chip. I don't hear any background noise with headphones plugged into the Behringer here at home.

When I pulled up my DJ program (Tractor S4), the Behringer was immediately picked up in S4's Audio Manager. I played a few tracks and they sounded great - no clicks, pops, or other stuff that can sometimes plague DJ programs run through USB sound devices. All in all, just a fantastic product!

title: Close to Perfect, But Some Quibbles
comment:
The SpeedPro Chromatic Guitar Tuner won’t please everyone. It’s a very sensitive tuner. How could this be a bad thing? Well, it depends on what kind of player you are. Though not generally acknowledged, tuner designers must make choices in how accurate a tuner should be. If it’s extremely sensitive, beginners or casual amateurs can find it incomprehensibly skittish. Thus, many tuners on the market are made to be reasonably accurate, but not too accurate. The advantage is that they give more steady readings—something most amateurs prefer. The disadvantage, of course, is that you’re not getting the ultimate in accuracy. (With some tuners, you may find you can audibly change a note’s pitch, but the tuner doesn’t register the change.) Advanced players know that intonation is often a moving target. They’re aware that a note’s pitch may vary during its attack and decay. So they’re willing to sort through the sometimes jittery readings that a more sensitive tuner will give. If you’re this kind of player, then the SpeedPro may be right for you.

On the plus side, this tuner is ruggedly built and should withstand heavy use. The customer service for MAC Music Accessories is also excellent. (I have first-hand experience with this.) But there are caveats. Like many other tuners, the SpeedPro display changes color as the note approaches correct intonation. Oddly, however, the SpeedPro display changes color when the note is still significantly off. What’s the point of a color change if it doesn’t accurately reflect when the note is perfectly in tune? One might argue that the color change lets you know when you’re getting close. Seems a wishy-washy approach, though, and it makes more sense to have the display change color only when the note is dead on.

More troubling is the placement of the power switch. With the tuner clipped onto a guitar headstock in the usual way, you must uncomfortably contort your hand to reach the switch. You can overcome this by clipping the tuner on the underside of the headstock. This puts the power switch in a position that’s easy to reach. Unfortunately, it also means the display is now upside down. (It’s possible to make a tuner in which the display can be reversed, and that’s a nice option for those who prefer to mount the tuner on the bottom of the headstock.) SpeedPro should have put more thought into its placement of the power switch. Musicians, especially skilled ones, are alert to good ergonomics. When we encounter a product that displays poor ergonomics, we tend to notice.

Were these caveats resolved, I would recommend the SpeedPro without reservation. As it is, it’s still my tuner of choice. Would that this tuner, so close to perfect, could go that extra bit.

title: Great product, works as advertised.
comment: The package arrived in good time, in appropriate packaging materials.  This unit is sturdy, not made of cheap materials.  There is a firmware update available due to an intermittent charging issue, but mine came with the updated firmware already installed.  This unit eliminates the iOS popup message that says "this accessory is not supported" when you plug in ANY midi keyboard- keyboards that I previously couldn't use with the Apple camera connection kit USB adapter.  GarageBand works well with this unit, as well as every synth app I have including Sunrizer.  The guitar jack works as well, but does seem to introduce a bit of noise compared to my Apogee Jam.  I like the stereo rca outputs, which allow me to plug into an external amp, or in my case, into my Tascam 8-track digital recorder.  The sound quality is good.  The headphone jack works great, but I suspect the oversize volume button is more for aesthetics than anything else.  Doesn't detract from the functionality of the unit, though.  I gave it four stars instead of five, but only because the product support page on the website does not provide a link to register the product, even though there is a place for registered products in the 'My Account' page.  Plus the website is difficult to navigate.  Serious lack of thought on the part of Griffin Technology, even though they make great products.

title: I like it and my dog sings along.
comment:
My family is very diverse and everyone is challenged to learn a new instrument. We have selected inexpensive but not cheaply made ones to begin. The recorder appears to be an easy instrument to learn so was a definite choice. If you are far removed from the grade school education, you will need more than the included directions for proper use. We selected this particular recorder because of its color. Several of us Love Purple, but the color of the instrument isn't how you evaluate quality.
My overall impression is based on the "feel" of the mouthpiece, the ease of sounding different notes, and packaging information. If you are a beginner, you will need more instructions for playing specific notes. The included fingering chart was not helpful.
Pros:
playable & cost effective
transparent (can see the saliva build up for cleaning)
cleaning dowel included in the reusable plastic case
Cons:
poor instructions (but that does not reflect the quality of the instrument)
(in my household, the dog must either love it or hate it because he will "sing along" or "howl in pain" when anyone plays."

title: JUST SAY NO! They will break on you.
comment:
These headphones have a widely reported issue with the ear cup adjustment sliders cracking and breaking. After three months mine developed the same cracks seen in the other reviews. It had been reported that the problem went away after the initial manufacturing run when they were introduced, but that doesn't seem to be the case from my experience and other recent reviews. No, they are not "Professional" as far as their build is concerned. I would hate to see how quickly they would break being used in a recording studio instead of me sitting on the couch listening to music a couple of hours a day.

The truly frustrating part is that they sound fantastic. Here they truly are "Professional Reference". Nice sound stage, great detail, reasonably even sound across the spectrum. Beautiful but not overwhelming bass. Clear musical trebles without any sibilance. Beautiful sound actually. One of the best sounding closed back headphones I've used.

Shure needs to remember what "Professional" means and build these like they build their microphones and IEM's.

title: Make sure you tighten the nuts  well or it will fall on your equipment or drop your Macbook/Laptop.

I usually would slide the Glide Style Platform off the case & use this instead. I like how it brings your MBP up closer to you than it being on the platform slid back. However just recently I noticed that when my MBP was on the Crane Stand it was leaning forward a little. I took my Mac off the stand & attempted to tighten the bottom nut. To do this I had to pull the stand back a little from underneath my Odyssey case. As I loosened the locking tab a little the top of the stand fell forward & landed on my brand new Pioneer DDJ-SX2-R Controller, scratching the nice red finish & the Jog Wheel's silver ring! Every once in awhile you will need to tighten it. When you do make sure you do it away from your equipment & don't have your Mac/Laptop on the stand!

title: It’s an option

My iPhone 13pro connected right away. I did charge it the 3 hours. Sony has high end down but the bass will lose definition at high volumes. Do you like mids? Save your money and look for a JBL. Sony Is clueless when it comes to mids. The Sony music center app has an eq that helped a little. If you like metal and have the money get the JBL. The Sony does it’s job. I got it because of my long history with Sony. I have products that have lasted a long time. It also has better splash resistance. I cut the treble and boosted the mid and bass with the EQ. That way the mega bass isn’t overpowered, the highs aren’t so piercing, and you get a pinch of mids.

title: Fits my Córdoba 15CFM perfectly. Superb protection
comment:
Quality is exceptional — the best gig bag I've ever seen; puts all others to shame. Padding is 1.5cm thick on all sides, and the case seems to have some sort of armature or stiff structure underneath the outer fabric. Maybe it's just cardboard; I don't know. It's no hard case, but your uke is VERY well protected in this thing.

The outer fabric is extremely rugged and water-resistant. Zippers are sturdy. With a satiny charcoal gray exterior, this bag is unique enough that it's easy to spot in a pile of other cases but not so much that it calls attention to itself.

I had a heck of a time trying to find a gig bag for my Córdoba 15CFM uke — most were too small in concert size but too loose in tenor. I tried gig bags by Gator, Road Runner, Ibanez, and many other brands before finally throwing in the towel and forking out for this "proprietary" gig bag by Córdoba itself. I'm glad I spent the extra money. This one is so, so worth it, and I wouldn't hesitate to buy another if I ever needed to.

title: I like it!
comment:
I have spent some time with this instrument (albeit from a different vendor) and think it is a professional-quality instrument for an intermediate instrument's price. It is especially well-suited for modern finger-style playing.

The parlor guitar all but disappeared in the 1960s with the advent of popular folk music in club and concert venues. Most pro performers then preferred "dreadnaught" guitars for greater volume and projection in large, miked venues. The novice player looking to purchase a "better" guitar quite naturally followed the lead of his idols, not thinking about the more intimate settings in which they would perfom.

Now the 0 guitar makes its return - the machine you see in photos of Robert Johnson and Charley Patton! The Blueridge BR-3X1 is offered in either mahogany (341) or rosewood (361 - my own preference). Lutherie and quality control are outstanding. The guitars project as well as the bigger boys, and their tone is woody and ringing.

I'd never buy a guitar I couldn't play for a while, since individual instruments can vary niticeably. Look at the seller's return policy and factor in the cost of shipping if you're unhappy. Still, I expect most players who want a "guitar sound" will be very pleased.

title: I needed a preamp replacement.
comment:
I blew my original preamp so I chose this complete preamp wiring kit, which saved me over $250 in repair cost. I took to guitar center to get a diagnostic done for $27, they told me the preamp wasn’t receiving any signal at all, everything else is fine. They said to replace pickups and convert it to passive it would be over $300 out the door. This bass was a gift and I wasn’t planning on spending $300 on this rinky 4 string.

I just used my own barrel jack to wire in and that’s about it, all you do is cut and splice each of the components pickups, battery terminal, and barrel jack or whatever input jack your using. Just do each one by one and you won’t snag any issues.

NOTE: You do need to solder a ground wire from the volume pot reach all the way through the body of your bass, and take a bit of wire at the end and ground that end to the bridge of the bass by making sure the exposed wire end is touching the metal on the bridge, (any area on the backside of bridge works).

The only con I’ve encountered was putting the bridge pickup in, the tolerance was really tight it was very tough to get the pick up in the cutout, I had to remove and hand sanded the wood back a bit. Not a deal break but just an headache.

Other than that, this thing finally plays and it sounds so deep and awesome, the pickups really are not that bad actually. If you need a quick fix and aren’t set out to pay a fortune, get this preamp bundle.

Overall, I spent $59 to fix this bass and get it playing again. $27 to Get diagnostic to figure out preamp was busted, and $32 for the wiring harness replacement kit. If you know your preamp is already blown you can bypass the diagnostic. Hope this helps you decide which direction to go.

title: It's all here if you need a one-time purchase
comment:
Okay, since the beginning of the pandemic and I headed home for work I have probably used something like 20 different microphones. This is a combination of microphones I’ve been fortunate enough to have been offered the product for my review, as well as a number of them I have purchased on my own at full price!

I was intrigued by this one, because it’s actually and XLR microphone with a very small powered control panel/mixer. I hadn’t yet delved into “sound effects” and so forth on the two other standard XLR based mixers I own.

My take away is that for the price you’re getting an awful lot, and if you just want to purchase something that actually has a shockingly good sound, I would say get it. If you’re the type of person that likes to do an awful lot of tinkering and like to test out different things and especially like to combine standardized products (such as taking your XLR microphone or other microphones and using them across your various computers and devices) this is probably not the best choice. I think the manufacturer in this case has done a decent job of providing everything you need for a podcasting or recording or gaming set up for your desktop or laptop as a one stop unit.

As far as what I would consider the benefits of this device here you go:
1. everything flat out works! That’s probably the most important thing you need to know!
2. The little control panel, that provides power to the microphone and so forth and connect your computer is actually a quality little device and of everything you get in this package that’s the most impressive to me. It’s well made and easy to use, buttons all work and so forth.
3. The sound quality from this condenser microphone is quite frankly as good as most other microphones I have that are as expensive for just the microphone itself. That alone probably makes the purchase worth it, but be sure to read my cons below.
4. You get all the cables you need to get started and the instructions are passable though English (as is typical with these unknown brands) is bad and very limited. You’re going to have to do some experimentation to really understand how everything works. I would also suggest you need to do a combination of tinkering with the mixer as well as Microsoft Windows settings data sweet spot if you’re doing this with your voice.

Here’s what I’m not particularly crazy about:
1. I’ve rarely seen as much effort attempting to make stuff look metal when essentially everything is plastic. The stand, threaded connections, the outside of the XLR connection to the bottom of the microphone, it’s all plastic. Outside of the necessities of actual metal connections like the three pens of the XLR cable, virtually everything is plastic and that is annoying to me personally. But, maybe they shaved $5 or $10 off the pricing and that’s the point.
2. I don’t understand putting weird lettering and so forth on the front of the microphone. If you’re going to brand your product with something make it at least kind of legible or something that might make sense. A logo in a local loan would be acceptable, but I feel like there’s nothing but gibberish on the front of the microphone. None of the pictures show that by the way…
3. I wish manufacturers especially when they are brand-new products would utilize USB C connections moving forward. I personally am highly annoyed at this point (given the obvious advantages to the USB C standard and its rapid adoption) to see manufacturers use anything but USB-C and especially to avoid using what I consider to be the dreaded and easily damaged micro USB standard. I know, I know it’s going to be a while…. 😉

Also, just want to mention as something that is strictly opinion I think most of the voice effects you get on this little control panel are simply ridiculous. Outside of testing them I will never, and I mean never use them anywhere even as a gag. I don’t understand why that would be hardwired into the mixer panel but I guess they’re trying to offer options and make the product look kind of cool. I’ve seen the same stuff in a lot of other offerings on Amazon, and it doesn’t make any sense to me. If you’re looking for modern “sound effects” um… not here.

As I mentioned before, and I hope this doesn’t go unnoticed, you have to do some experimentation to find the sweet spot with this little set up, but it really does have a quality condenser microphone built into it. Even though it’s shrouded by gobs of plastic, it is actually quite good. I’ve plugged this microphone into my other XLR mixers and honestly this is a quality large diaphragm condenser. So, if you want to test out this little device particularly considering it has a built-in battery in the mixer and would work with your phone on the road, at least know that you have a decent little backup XLR microphone that can be used with any other mixer. Cheers!

title: Great mic set for cameras and 1/8th-inch TRRS phone, but the TRS adapter is not the greatest
comment:
This is 100% typical of the many wireless mic sets I've tested. This one is primarily for cameras, but adapts to older generation TRRS phones.

However, I have to knock off a star. The TRS camera adapter is longer than it needs to be--it could have been better engineered. See the picture to understand the issue.

Now--if TRS had been the primary, you couldn't have adapted that to TRRS, so this is how it must be from a practical standpoint. You can't conjure up an additional connection out of three, so the only way to serve two "masters" in this case is to build it as they have.

The problem is the form factor. With the adapter on, the weight is cantilevered out for a couple of inches past the phone. For a phone, the ideal form factor would be to use a cold-shoe mount and a cable.

And if you're thinking, "Well, a TRS version would be at least better," then you are right--maybe--in some cases. But it would not have worked on my Canons, because it would not have been able to insert fully.

What I will likely do is get a short cable TRRS to TRS adapter, so that the receiver can just dangle. That way the camera's jack (a far more troublesome thing to replace) won't get strained.

All of that said, this is a good tool, and it's even cheap enough to essentially sacrifice in order to get a shot. I mentioned in another review of essentially the exact same mic (different receiver) that I'd taped them to my wheel wells, in order to get the close up sound of my tires on pavement for a particular shot in a film I'm designing.

That saved me from having to extensively rig $$$$$$ mics to my vehicle, in a way that would have taken far longer and would not have gotten me the exact perspective I was looking for.

This, for me, is what these little wireless mic sets are PERFECT tools to capture. They have a nice crunchy condenser sound in those applications, and they get the job done beautifully for pennies on the dollar without risking your far more expensive mics.

title: Am I missing something?
comment:
This is a great double-pedal combo at a great price, but after setting it up and making all the adjustments, I wonder if I've missed something: it seems that the resting angle of the beater is in lockstep with the pedal, with no way to fine-tune it.

I've looked at other pedals in various rock shops, and they all seem to have one bolt that the PDPs are missing: right behind the beater, next to the cam, there's a bolt you can loosen, apparently to adjust exactly the angle I'm talking about.

Now, admittedly, with the left pedal, you can "trick" the PDP unit into having an "ideal" angle for the beater, but since you can't adjust the right pedal similarly, you end up with an unbalanced setup.

Again, I might just be missing something, here, and maybe someone will comment and fill me in.

The only other objection I have--and it's another slight one--is that the slave pedal wasn't designed to work independently if you decide to go double-bass. Seems a little odd to put the tension spring and adjustments all on the right pedal rather than making two virtually identical pedals that happen to be connected. Maybe there's something more to setting it up that way that I don't get, but it almost seems like a no-brainer to me.

Comments appreciated. I like what I bought...I just don't understand it completely.

title: Pickup Wires could be longer.

No wants to have to splice wires together especially multiple wires to get sound.  Additionally, volume output of these passive soap bars is not very robust.  If you want to seriously rock the house you are better off with active soap bars vs. passive.  I only wish there was more passives to choose from in this price range.  Not over-whelmed.

title: They sound amazing. But the best price on these strings these ...
comment:
I have been using these strings on one of my Alvarez Yairis (DY71). The guitar is naturally warm and doesn't project quite as well as my DY77 and the these strings help with tone and projection. They sound amazing. But the best price on these strings these days is $12 ($15 at the big box guitar store). Not so bad IF they prolong the life of the string. And they may if you play A LOT. But they go dead just sitting in a case as well as hard playing.

I gave it three stars because of the price. The uncoated 4100 strings are the same strings, uncoated (according to Martin) at half the price. $6 for coating? I don't think so. As others have pointed out, unlike the other brand of coated string, the coating does not add to the slinkiness of the string. If slinkiness is required then the simple solution is to use a product like Finger Ease which will work just as well on non coated strings, so why pay the extra money for the 7100s. Just sayin'. In full disclosure I have not tried the 4100's yet, but will do so next change and will edit my review if they are radically different sounding than the 7100's.

But again, on my guitar the SP 7100 strings sound amazing.

title: The wood is a great quality. The pick ups provide a great range ...
comment: I've never been a bass player. I grew up a drummer who played guitar as a hobby. When i decided to start doing solo home recordings i was looking for a cheap bass that could just get me through. It wasn't something i expected to play more than when i just needed to lay down a bass track on an almost finished song. I was shocked at the high quality for the price. I've been in enough bands over the last 20 years and screwed around on enough of our bass player's set ups to know when one is junk. This is not one of those bass guitars. The wood is a great quality. The pick ups provide a great range between the low thud and nice pop. There was no fret buzz what so ever. I've found myself just playing it for fun more than i expected. I would highly recommend this either for someone who is just starting out, a back up for live shows, or home recording. Obviously if you're an established bass player you'll probably go somewhere else but also be willing to spend much more. I've played bass guitars in the $250-300 range that this beats out for sure. So happy with my purchase!

title: Absolutely Fantastic.
comment:
When it came time for me to upgrade from my Mixtrack Pro, and Hercules MP3 E2 to a real four channel controller I researched every possible unit and came to find the G4V was the one I kept vacillating between because it is as good as it looks on paper.

Received it today and the build quality is properly sturdy, not too heavy at 11 lbs., plastic knobs on the EQ as opposed to rubberized, however.

The knobs are the best I've felt on any controller despite being plastic. They are light and nimble, with the tactile notch of the mid-point of the knobs a very subtle sensation as opposed to other units. It put me off at first but getting them moved around today I love them and how easily and confidently you can be accurate with them.

The VU meters are excellent, where have they been my whole life?

Hefty platters but not overly so, don't really care for/about the touch-sensitivity feature. Faders are the best I've felt on any mixer or controller anywhere, and are narrower and taller than the usual faders you're accustomed to. Incredibly innovative little tweak, in my opinion.

No +/- Pitch Bend buttons was probably the worst trade-off with this unit.

I considered the Numark N4 and Quad controllers, have felt and played with all of them, and this Gemini wins out on looks and build quality but doesn't have the same functionality of the Numarks. No CDJ or Phono support in this controller, despite the AUX input (it's possible to hook one up but you probably wouldn't want to because the pre-amp isn't very good).

For the price this is the knees of the bees. Buy it and never look back.

title: works great with Google Pixel 2 phone's USB-C port, must use Shure audio and video recording apps
comment:
I'm using this microphone with my Google Pixel 2 Android smartphone's USB-C port. This phone does not have a regular stereo jack, just the USB-C port. The Shure microphone comes with a USB-C cable that plugs right into my phone. I tried using the microphone with my old audio recording app and the sound quality was poor. Then I tried Shure's recording app and the sound quality is tremendous. Audio quality is very clear and dynamic; much better than the phone's built-in microphone or any other external microphone that I have tried (none in this price range, though). Unlike cheap microphones, this is a microphone that I would gladly use to record live music performances (I am a music student).

No documentation is included with the microphone, but there is a picture of their apps on the side of the box, so I looked them up and downloaded them. The apps say that some phones will not work with the microphone and the only phones that they approve of are recent Samsung and Apple phones. I can confirm that the Google Pixel 2 also works fine. The app does have links to download more detailed documentation if you need that.

Shure has separate Android apps for audio recording and video recording. The video app is still in beta release, but it does work OK. The apps have lots of settings to control the microphone's recording geometry, compression, volume, video resolution, as well as tools to edit your past recordings or save them in different formats. This is a great set of tools to have on my phone. Everything works great on my phone.

My only complaint is that the port on the microphone is micro-USB so I need an unusual micro-USB to USB-C cable to connect it to my phone. A 16" cable is included, but if I lose that or if I need a longer one, then additional cables are not that expensive, but if they used a more standard USB-C to USB-C cable, then I bet all their customers would already own a bunch of cables that they could use.

title: Best compressor I've used!
comment: I've owned a half dozen compressor pedals over the years, from squishy Dyna Comp styles to transparent "barely there" optical compressors like the BBE Opto Stomp.  The new MXR Custom Comp is hands-down the best sounding one I've tried, and very versatile.  It can range from a Nashville chicken pickin' squash to a transparent sustaining clean boost to a Byrds-y jangle, and lots of juicy places in between.  I'm blown away by how much string-to-string clarity the MXR brings out from my guitars.  I don't have any reason to pay almost twice the price (or more) for a boutique compressor; the MXR Custom Shop designed this one and it is very competitive with the high-end Ross/Dyna clone makers like Keeley.  The only negative is the attack and trim (tone) adjustment wheels are inside the box, rather than on external knobs.  This can be a drawback if you often need the whole range of versatility the pedal offers. I have only a couple settings that I use and the volume and sensitivity knobs are enough for me, but I can see how others might prefer a pedal that can be more readily adjusted (like a Wampler Ego Compressor, for instance.)  Solid 4-1/2 star rating, rounded up to 5.

title: 9 years and still going strong.
comment:
I bought one of the first TC BH250's back in early 2013 or 2012. I still have it. I've used it every single week since I've bought it.

Why is this amp special? Well, for number of reasons.

It's dead reliable. It has a mute switch!! Tuner works great. Tone print is good to a point. I just set mine for compression and have left it there for years. And it has insane power.

I bought a few years ago an Ashdown 200 watt head for cheap I planned to leave in the studio. I play through a single Aguilar 112, a killer cab. I just felt like I was struggling. Instead I added my BH250 and boom, ridiculous difference. Felt like easily double the power. Cab came alive. This thing PUMPS.

The newer models don't have what the BH250 can do. I think TC might have realized that the amp was so good, it prevent people from up buying to something more expensive.

You can gig at a big venue with this. Not kidding.

title: A good contemporary sound, at a great price!
comment:
I'd say that for the money this is a good mouthpiece. But in truth, at triple the price, it'd still be a good mouthpiece.

That said, it is a high baffle mouthpiece, and they are notorious for issues in the lower range of the saxophone. And for some, they have too bright and cutting of a sound. It took a couple days work to find a reed that worked well with it, and ended up with a Vandoren Java Red. Ligature wise, the Rico H-Ligature is perfect for this mouthpiece, as you might expect.

So the test...how does this mouthpiece play. Once you have the reed right, it plays wonderfully. Of course, I was looking for a brighter sound. and this gives you that. I was coming from playing an Otto Link metal mouthpiece, and this 7M Metalite out blows it. Funny, how after spending hundreds of dollars on mouthpieces, it is the best priced one of the bunch that wins out in the end.

This may not be right for all players, it can be hard to control, but if you want a good contemporary sound this can give you that. You just may have to work with it. The more you play it, the better it gets.

This is an update to the earlier review...I thought I'd try the M9 version of this mouthpiece, but was a little wary because of the wider opening and longer facing. But wow, what a difference. I found this M9 version a lot easier to play, and more responsive, especially in the lower register, where the M7 was lacking. The M9 version is the ONE to go with for this mouthpiece.

title: Not worth the price - too brittle and can't hold their tuning
comment:
I won't be throwing my money away on these strings again. Two of the strings didn't even make it through their initial tuning -- they just snapped (G & high E). So much for the improved sustain. The lower bass strings won't hold their tuning after being on for 3 days. I'm not sure who cares about them being "louder" (as advertised) if they can't play the right notes. In other words, I spent all that money on one mid-quality B string. This is all on a Breedlove acoustic which has not had any trouble with any other strings in 5 years. I've gone through a lot of Ernie Ball Earthwoods at less than half the price, and these Vibe strings can't even touch them as far as durability and reliability.
For a company that claims to use a higher technology, they can't break away from the "Made in China" label on the back. These are not high quality strings, as much as the company wants you to believe they are. Only the price is high. If you really want to spend that much, you might as well toss in a few more bucks and get a long-lasting set of Elixirs.

Update: Decided to keep the remaining strings on and really put them through the ringer. After 4 or 5 days, the 3 wound bass strings finally settled in. And in fairness, they do produce a nice clear tone. Not everybody has 4 or 5 days to break in strings though. If you're a gigging musician, you won't want to put these on right before a gig or as a replacement during. However, after a few weeks there is still no sign of rust or corrosion, and they get heavy use (1 - 2 hrs per day + traveling, which means more temperature changes on the instrument). This is a nice plus after a disappointing debut. The one B string is hanging in there too, but it can get a little "blatty" at times if picked too strongly. It holds up to a LOT of bending though (heads up to blues-style players).

For now, the rating rightfully belongs middle of the road. I'd feel safer recommending these to the 'at-home' guitarist who doesn't gig much and has the time to let strings break in. You might be nicely surprised -- just be CAREFUL during tuning and go slowly. But I would keep looking if you're a musician who's constantly in need of re-stringing and re-tuning.

Wow. The other reviews didn’t lie. This is a great guitar. Even for a bit more money, it would be great. The neck and shape are all really something special.
I will say the nit picks right off the bat for the one I got. My output jack area didn’t get enough paint, and there was a small bit of roughness on the underside of the edge of the fretboard. The later smoothed out quickly after playing. The former, a sharpie could maybe even fix this lol. I included a pic of the area.
I have yet to do a string change, but the strings that came with it were fresh and stay in tune great. Seems to be D’addario 10s? Hopefully it is as simple as some have mentioned above.
The good- everything else. Seriously. Frets, pickups, feel and playability are all hitting way above the price range. And very comfy to sit around and play anywhere. It fits in a mini Taylor acoustic gig bag, so if you want a bag that works, try to find a used one of those.

title: Poor Construction Ruins Its Usefulness
comment: I did not use this stool/stand intensively just for practice at home. But the screws, which were not anchored but simply drilled into the soft material on the bottom of the seat cushion, soon started falling out and could not be tightened in the soft material. The the sliding neck brace came loose, again, screws stripped. The cross brace across from the foot rest broke, a cheap braze or solder of the pot-metal material just broke. I fixed it with duct tape, but then the other end of the same brace broke as well. I now have it in a clamp hoping that JB Weld will fix it, but pot-metal is notorious for not taking adhesives well. Finally, the clip that holds the seat in place and then releases for folding up the stool is unstable and comes loose easily, resulting in the chair being unstable and can even collapse. If you are holding your expensive Taylor or Gibson guitar when that happens, the results will be catastrophic. I regret this purchase. I have never in an Amazon review used the word "junk." I won't use it here, because it would be too positive.

title: Transparent compression at a great price.
comment:
For electric guitar, I prefer the squashed sound, so I use a modded Boss CS-3 for that. The Danelectro Surf and Turf is a much more transparent compressor, though, and I use it mostly for acoustic. It is one of those pedals that you don't realize is on until you turn it off. And for that purpose, this pedal does the job admirably.

My problem with Danelectro pedals is the housing. Behringer -- another maker of cheap pedals -- also has plastic albeit sturdy enclosures that are practical, and much more pleasing to the eye. I don't know of anyone who likes Danelectro's 50s design (which doesn't even make sense for some of their effects, such as their distortion pedals. And I can't imagine that anyone enjoys the tiny knobs.

For the sound and price, I highly recommend it if you're looking for transparent compression. For the housing/design, I absolutely do not recommend it for frequent live gigs unless you leave it on the whole time.

title: A mixed bag
comment:
I received my guitar yesterday. It's a mixed bag. I've provided my immediate feedback below. I'll provide more as I spend more time with this guitar.

Pros:
The shape. This shape is cool as funk. It's like a mashup of a Strat and a Jag, with the best of both. Compared to the strat, it looks more hip and dangerous and less "grand daddy/Eric Clapton." (Hence the name Marauder!) Compared to a Jag it looks less warped and retro-futuristic. With the lower cutaway blade it looks almost like a Gibson SG. And I love the fact that this guitar was prototyped in 1965 and never saw mass production until now. What a cool story. If Fender is smart, they'll add this guitar to their line for years to come, and offer several different configurations and price points.

Headstock: I really dig the oversided headstock from days of yore. And the fact that the model is not listed looks very tidy and makes the guitar mysterious. I can't wait to show my guitarist friends and hear them say, "What the heck is that?!"

Cons:
Wood: This guitar apparently uses a wood called "koto" instead of the typical alder. I've read that these woods have the same density, but this guitar feels very light, almost too light, like it's made of balsa wood. I seriously wonder how dense and durable koto wood actually is.

Finish: While the paint is shiny, I can see raised parts where the wood grain is apparent. I would expect the surface to be completely smooth. This is more a knock on the planing of the wood rather than the finish.

Tremolo: I have to say, this guitar has poor sustain. I believe that the floating tremolo is the culprit, and possibly the wood choice. Granted, I'm comparing this to my Gibson Les Paul, so I may have overly high expectations for sustain.

Setup: I've read other comments that this guitar arrived perfectly set up. In my case, the triple-bucker pickup was partly dislodged and touching the strings. I had to push it down into place. I hope it stays put. Also, the fret buzz is quite bad. I'll need to adjust the action.

Fretboard: The rosewood feels really dried out. I suspect an application of lemon oil will help this just fine. When bending the high and low E strings, it's way too easy to push the strings off the side of the fret. I'll put on some heaver gauge strings, which may help. But this will be an adjustment.

Pickup: The triple-bucker seemed cool. When in humbucker mode it sounds good, but when you switch the triple bucker mode, there is a substantial drop in volume. I have to adjust my amp. I expected to triple-bucker option to be at least as loud as the humbucker option.

Knobs: The placement is poor and during aggressive playing I end up turning down the volume. Also, the knobs look really cheap, and will need to be replaced.

Upshot: The guitar looks great but the configuration and setup leave something to be desired. I would expect more from a Fender (this does not even have the Squier badge). I didn't realize that they sold guitars under the Fender name that were built and setup in the Far East.

My requests to Fender: Use alder wood, use two humbuckers, ditch the tremolo in favor of a fixed bridge, and use better knobs.

I'll review the guitar again after I've had more time with it.

title: Sturdy Takamine guitar case. No regrets in buying it even after 18 months.  Highly recommended.
comment:
I agree with DOUGQ's review, the case is a perfect fit for the EG444C-VV. That guitar fits well in that case. It's a snug fit but not a tight fit, in my opinion.

Pros:
The quality is above average, Very nice especially considering how much it costs.
The brown color is a pleasant change. Its very classic and vintage looking.
Soft velvet lining with extra padding surrounding the sides of the body, back and the area above the strings. Seems to the purposes of keeping cost down and minimizing the case size. I'm sure we've all seen some outrageously thick cases before.
Other extras are the storage compartment, locking clasps and Takamine logo.

Cons:
It's not a fight case that would be built better, stand up to more abuse, and only cost minimally 3 times as much?

Its a fine case that I would definitely recommend it to a friend.

Cheers.

Update (5/15/2015):
It has been about a year and a half from my initial purchase of this guitar case for my Takamine EG-444-VV. I've used the case mainly for storage and the occasional move. The winters get dry where I live so I needed to keep it in a tight case, of which this case has served wonderfully. I use this case with a D'Addario Acoustic Guitar Humidifier which is another fantastic product. The hinges, clasps and handle are still in excellent working order. The padding is still in place and still spongy. The case has evidence of protecting my guitar in the form of a 6 in scratch across the surface where the front of the body would be.
I'm not one to pay more for something because it has a brand name written on it, usually it means the manufacturers jacked up the price and have given you lesser quality. It this case it was quite the opposite. It's reasonably priced and excellent quality. If you're guitar fits, buy this case...you won't be disappointed.

One of the sweetspots of this mixer is the analog filter... it's just beautiful - knobs are super sturdy unlike it's xone92 popular brother which those knobs seem like they will break. Anywho if you buy this mixer (and it's a very flat sounding good quality sound mixer) i would advise the decksaver case (which can only be purchased from the UK)... keep in mind those deckasvers are also limited edition. Cheers!

title: Best strings I've ever heard.

I've been playing guitar in Rock and Roll/Blues/Country bands for over forty years and have been through tons of strings. My go to used to be D'addarios but I've been through many more. Because I sweat a lot when playing out I switched to Elixers. Still use them on my electric 12 strings and acoustic guitars. Love the feel and sound and they last forever without losing tone. Having been using the Cobalts on my old Strat for the past year I have three comments:

A: This is the first time I've ever switched string brands on my Strat and have heard a NOTICABLE difference in volume, tone and responsiveness. Best strings I've ever heard.

B: I don't know what the hell makes others say this string feel smoother than others. In fact I've found just the opposite: they're a little rough to the feel compared to anything else I've used. I've accepted that and my calluses have adapted accordingly.

C: They only last through one gig and then they're dead to me but their tone and cost vs Elixer makes that no big deal.

title: Worth a Try
comment:
I wanted to try the Evans EQ Pad Bass Drum Damper but I didn’t want to add adhesive strips inside my bass drum. So I decided to also get a KICKPRO Kick Drum Pillow.

For a 22”x18” bass (w 5” port cutout on resonant head), results:

(1) I found that using the combination of the Evans EQ Pad + the KickPro Pillow offered the perfect amount of damping that I was looking for
(2) The KickPro Pillow (weighted) was heavy enough to keep the Evans Pad in place (without Velcro)
(3) For a 22”x18”, the Evans Pad alone is not long enough to make contact with both heads
(4) The Evans “pad” portion contacts the batter head. Three quarters of the KickPro Pillow rests on top of the Evans Pad (holding it in place), with about one quarter of the KickPro Pillow contacting the resonant head
(5) I can insert / remove / reposition both through the 5” port (with care) without having to remove the resonant head
(6) Clean and tidy. I really like the relative compactness of the pad / pillow combination

These two products complement each other and allows you to skip adding adhesive Velcro to the inside of your bass. Both highly recommended!

title: JJ's EL84's Upgraded a Peavey Classic 30
comment: I recently replaced the old Sovtek tubes in my Peavey Classic 30 amplifier with a matched quad of the JJ's EL84's. I also replaced the preamp tubes with JJ's 12AX7 long plates tubes. I've always liked the basic sound of this amp, but the improvements made by switching tubes was readily apparent. The Classic 30 now has a much more detailed and a punchier sound than it ever had before. In addition, the lower noise of the JJ's tubes made the amplifier surprisingly quieter. Sonic qualities are always subjective, but in this case I really enjoyed the new sound of my old amp and I find I'm now playing it more than the other amps that I own. It was well worth the modest investment to upgrade to superior tubes.

title: Convoluted and confusing: like a digital recorder made for the 80s
comment:
I don't consider Zoom's recorders particularly user friendly, but you can figure them out easily and the manual explains anything that isn't obvious. TASCAM takes user unfriendliness to a whole new level with the DP-006. Nothing is particularly intuitive, unless you're used to the way you recorded audio in the 80s, and the manual doesn't make it much easier to figure out. There are just so many steps to actually getting your audio files onto your computer that I don't even know why you'd bother. There are many, many other better devices out there that require a far smaller learning curve. This thing would be really great if it just recorded to a file and that was that. Instead, it tries to pack the features of an entire studio into this tiny little box, all the while not offering phantom power or XLR inputs (you need the DP-008EX for that). Even if you learn how to use this thing and don't mind the way it works, you'll still likely find the export times frustrating. Exporting to WAV, so you can actually access your audio on a computer, takes about the same time it took to record the audio in the first place (e.g. a 25 second file takes about 25 seconds to export). That, alone, makes me not want to bother with this thing. Other recorders and portable workstations don't require conversion and export at all.

All of that said, the hardware is very nice. It feels sturdy, the knobs, dials, and buttons are all easy to operate. The screen provides plenty of information and doesn't feel cramped (despite being small). If you want an annoyingly featured-packed device that's difficult to learn and use but has great hardware, get the DP-008EX instead of this. But with either unit, there are several other better options available at similar price points.

title: The best sounding acoustic-electric bass that I found between $250-$500
comment:
I spent a good part of this afternoon playing through a number acoustic-electric basses. I was interested in an Ibanez for $250 but didn't project well acoustically. A $400 Fender had better projection and fairly good action but just couldn't sell me at that price. Deans have a really long neck. This Washburn, at $300 was also a bit more than I really hoped to pay but I fell in love with it as soon as I picked it up. It's remarkably light and it's more the size of an acoustic guitar; at age 60, that's welcome. Acoustic bass guitar is typically taught with left-hand fingering as 1-2-4 over the first 3 frets, because the frets are wider than a guitar. Not so with this Washburn. I can actually finger all four frets,
1-2-3-4, which could allow me to apply more guitar technique. It projects well acoustically and, by lowering the bass and high ends and bringing up the mellow, it's got a nice woody, strong tone amplified. You've got 17 frets until the cutaway starts to curve out, 15 to the top bout. It does not have a built in tuner, a small downside, but just slap a clip-on. It has 4 easily moved sliders (bass, mid, high, and 'presence'), a volume control and a phase button. It's only 3.5"
deep (my Yamaha acoustic-electric guitar is 4"), so it's really astounding that it provides such a rich, deep bass tone. The body is an inch shorter though the neck is considerably longer, as it would need to be for a bass. And, perhaps most importantly... it's really beautiful! The spruce top is a nice cream with a simple but striking rosette and the Mahogony sides are a very pretty , rich Mahogony red. It only has one peg for a guitar strap (at the base), which is an annoyance but, if you buy a peg, you can easily install yourself. I've easily added pegs. I'd probably add some kind of padding inside the minimal case. If my opinion changes as I play it more in earnest then I'll update but I'm giving big thumbs up. (My understanding is that you'll want an acoustic amplifier. I'm using an older 2 channel Crate Acoustic 60D and it sounds great with plenty of volume.)
NOTE TO AMAZON: The Overall rating on this instrument needs to be adjusted. It shows as '3' but there are now five 5 star ratings (including my own), two 4 star ratings, and only one 1 star rating. That's 34/40 or 85% or a 4.25 rating out of 5. Please adjust. This is a good instrument that deserves to be purchased and played. Thx.

title: The hunt for a good headset has ended.
comment:
I have been searching all over Amazon and in stores for a good pair of headsets and stumbled onto these. After going through behind the ear style, bluetooth, bluetooth behind the ear, over the ear (hook and grip that hovers over the ear) etc I finally found one I was happy with.

I prefer behind the ear style, but have been wanting one that was not bluetooth to avoid charging. However, it's very hard finding a pair of headsets that have good sound quality (bass and highs) let alone behind the ear (hooks and neck) style that use a cord. It seems everyone is going more for wireless but I prefer wired to avoid the hassle of charging or making sure it was charged before doing anything.

I test tried these on a Playstation 4, running and casual music listening (Youtube, music streaming etc) and they are pretty good. I wasn't expecting good sound quality and normally avoid any product that is not expensive and advertises "Rich Bass" since you need bigger drivers for real good bass but surprisingly the product does deliver on sound. Especially bass.

The only thing that would make these perfect for me would be a behind the ear hook style. Hopefully in the future they will make one in that style. One of the things that stood out for me was good design that did not look cheap. The wires in the headset looked stronger and better than most tiny wires i've seen on other headsets. That was the first thing that caught my eye. Glad I did.

title: Great Value - Great Looking Guitar
comment:
I was nervous at first, buying an "off-brand" electric guitar. I've owned mostly Epiphones and Squiers, and played genuine Fenders, so I was worried about quality, as I'm sure you are as you're reading this review. Just got this unit in the mail today - well packaged - no dings, scratches, etc. Frets are all nicely dressed and do not catch at all while playing. Pickups sound nice when played clean, which is mostly the way I play. They are not very noisy either. Tuners are mostly smooth and the nut shouldn't need any filing - does not catch when tuning. It intonated just fine. I do not have the tools to level frets, so I can't speak to that.

Honestly the only thing I might change on this guy would be to swap the tuners for the locking variety. Everything else is pretty squared away. I'm mostly impressed with how nice the neck and stock pups are, and I am just loving how the color looks with the maple fretboard. I may take it in later this month/year to have my local tech look at it, but honestly it's in good playing condition right now. I'm satisfied.

title: My favorite mic...period.
comment:
My Sennheiser 835s are 9 years old, so understand that this review is written well past the honeymoon period. They have been tested.

The first time I used then, we hooked these up and some Shure SM58s and compared them side by side. The Shures were louder, and that was enough for our lead singer to decide he wanted to use them. I wasn't as convinced because I was looking more for tone than volume.

My mic collection includes EVs, AKGs, Nadys (surprisingly good for the $), an SM57, an SM58 and a few others. The 835 is the best sounding dynamic mic I have ever used or heard. It is not as loud as the Shures, but it is not hurting for volume and it delivers a much, much warmer sound. It beats everything else every time.

The neck is thinner than Hofner's but I actually like that. Neck dive is an issue but the much more expensive Hofner is the same. I've gone through about three of these already since I enjoy trading instruments but I always come back to one, I like them that much. Finish has always been great, tuners are actually more solid than Hofner's and they're able to take on a heavier gauge string though not recommended. Another thing, this will not compare to the sound of a jazz or a P bass, this will give you more of a thump sound that players look for in a hollow or semi hollow body.

title: Great Turntable for Home Use, Not Just for DJs!
comment:
[[VIDEOID:567cb95e0574ce9724b0af610e21dee8]] I spent more time installing and aligning the new cartridge in the headshell than getting the turntable itself put together, wired up, and playing. But that's not a negative comment on the turntable, just a heads-up on mounting and aligning your separately purchased cartridge. The stylus has to be aligned for proper "overhang" and "tangency" so take your time and do it right.

I purchased this turntable based on not just online reviews but from audiophiles I know. I bought it for home use and not "DJ-ing" use which leads up to my only "complaint" about this turntable. I would like to see a disable switch to prevent the "reverse" button from reversing the rotation of the record if the button is accidentally touched/pressed. I will never be playing my records backwards (you must buy a stylus made for playing records both directions) and I'm trying to figure out how to place some sort of guard over the button. It's not likely I'll ever push the button since the controls I will be using are not that close to it but it would still be nice to be able to have a switch to choose between "DJ Mode" and "Home User Mode".

This turntable was about 100 dollars more than I was budgeting but there just aren't any turntables made for under $300 that have the quality and specifications of this one and to get something better you've got to spend hundreds if not thousands of dollars more. There is a point of diminishing returns when comparing the additional cost of an item and the additional benefits received for the additional cost and I think this turntable is the maximum bang for the buck.

Because it is designed for taking abuse by DJs it makes a very durable and sturdy turntable for home use. Very little plastic used in this turntable and it looks great. Yes, the lid isn't hinged but that is not a negative for me but perhaps in a future design they could make a hinge option available for those that want it for home use.

title: There were other choices but for only a few dollars more I thought the case made by the same people who made my Uke was a good c
comment: I purchased this case because the Cloth "Gig Bag" that came with my OU2 was flimsy and I wanted something more protective. There were other choices but for only a few dollars more I thought the case made by the same people who made my Uke was a good choice so I ordered from Amazon. The case I received was different from all the pictures online, I half expected that because these things happen. The package came with the box partially open because of inadequate packing material (about 8ft. of brown paper in a very large box). I also didn't expect the case to have glue on the outside in several places, or inside on the velour material, or that the padding inside was all compressed and my uke fit very loosely inside. I returned it immediately with no problems from Amazon. I have since reordered directly from an online music store after talking with one of their customer service people who assured me the UC3 should be the right one for my instrument, I was considering another brand at about the same cost. The second one was much better, my OU2 fit like a glove, nice and snug, and with no glue all over. It was still not as pictured (no plush lining, different padding with gold velour, and the bottom latch is in a different position), but so much better than the one I previously received. The case is solid and otherwise very well built and well padded. I would hope that Oscar Schmidt's quality control is better than to let a case like I first received make it to the market. It looked like a knock off, the quality was so "far off" as compared to my OU2 Ululele. Ultimately I am satisfied but was disappointed at first which is why I went to a different source for my purchase as there was no one at Amazon Direct to ask about my problem. For the cost it is a five star case but since I had to initially return it and could find no one at Amazon to talk to who would know about the product I am giving my experience a three star rating.

title: Killer little amp!
comment:
This is for initial impressions - I can't speak to long term reliability.

First off this is not even a lunchbox amp - It's almost a matchbox amp it is so tiny. However, don't let this fool you as it can put out some face meting gain. I have a serious gearhead friend who after hearing mine immediately went out and bought one. He is using his to drive a full 4x12 HiWatt cabinet and it sounds mean.

The clean channel is decent, and I have not really tried any pedals through it either direct input or effect loop.

Honestly that's not what this thing is for, and you need to spend all your time on GAIN. Dropping down the tuning on my Schecter produces some serious chug worthy of any metal fan.

I've seen forums claiming that swapping out the factory tube with a name brand (Mesa, JJ, Electro-harmonix) makes it sound even better but I'm happy as is.

If you want close to MESA BOOGIE sound on a SQUIRE budget this is your amp.

title: Better than I could have hoped for!
comment:
The morning of a concert I was supposed to record, I checked my existing ribbon mics. One of them had a serious problem and wasn't going to be usable. I really wanted to put a blumlein pair at the front (these were small ensembles, mostly string quartets, but also some brass, and piano ensembles too)

I wasn't able to find anything in town (within 30 miles at least) that had any ribbons, nor could I find any rentals on that short of notice. I did find a pair of these at a local store, so I picked a couple up. I have owned a MXL LDC before, and the sound quality was lacking (typical HF distortion and "sparkle" of a cheap condensor.) So I took a chance anyway, and not only were these clean sounding, they have a reasonable frequency response across the entire audible spectrum.

I also had a pair of Oktava's and Peluso mic's setup for this concert, and the bulk of the sound in this mix is actually from the MXL pair.

Some notes I have on these mic's:
- The low frequency response of these mic's are off the chart. The building I recorded in was a very old church with wood floors over a large basement. The 20-50hz range was a mess from people walking, the heating system, and transit busses driving by (1/2 block away!) The included shock mounts alone don't provide enough isolation, high mass stands and possibly isolation pads are a good idea for any location recordings.
- Even though this mic is rated at low impedance (250 ohms I think,) using a high impedance preamp will still help make the higher frequencies more pronounced.
- You can visibly see the ribbons through the grill. It's super easy to see if a ribbon is sagging, no disassembly required.

To summarize, I am pleasantly surprised with these mic's! I won't attempt to compare them to other high-end ribbons (like Royer,) because they aren't in the same league, but in the crowded world of cheap Chinese-made ribbons (generally with ribbons 4x the thickness of this mic,) these mic's stand out.

title: KEY9:  Overpriced, but worth trying out
comment:
First off, it’s extremely misleading to mix 4 VERY different pedals in your reviews!!! That shouldn’t be allowed, as the Key9 is inferior compared to other EHX pedals for sale, in my honest opinion.

Setup: (PRS Guitar —> K9 —> Vox Amp)
1. Dynamo: Very smooth/ ambient Keys
2. Wurli: Keys w/ tremolo
3. Suitcase: Keys w/ phaser
4. Mallets: I recommend turning “Dry sound” UP and effect volume down, so mallets can add a percussive element to your notes
5. Eighty Eight: tremolo, but recommend turning the treble down; otherwise you get this weird steel drum sound.....
6. Tri-Glorious: smooth ambiance
7. Vibes: very similar to 88, IMO
8. Organ: chorus, but make sure treble is down to play smoother/ more ambient
9. Steel Drum: high treble, bad tracking (increase DRY & use effect as accent)

Price: Definitely not worth $220+ !!!!! (At best, this pedal $80, because 4-5 of the presets are too similar to one another, and you really have to work around the pedal’s treble & tracking issues)

Overall: The pedal can be Practical, but shouldn’t be a primary part of your tone. Use first in signal chain, start w/ recommended and adjust according to your setup. If this is your primary tone, you can get away with using Dynamo/ Tri-Glorious/ Organs by itself; however, you should use the other 6 presets only as percussive accents or secondary elements with the Dry signal turned up.

Hopefully this helps future customers. I originally gave this 2 stars, because of the treble and tracking issues, but I bumped it up to 3 after doing everything I could to make the pedal work within a more complicated rig than the one I illustrated above. It’s a tough pedal to get used to, but worth checking out.

title: Wonderful Tone After a Little Tweaking
comment:
I have a concert Lanikai and have been pleased with it. When I received my baritone Lanikai, I was grimly disappointed - the strings were so close to the edge of the fret board I could not play it without smacking the string with the side of my hand. I had an old Woods Uke that I had become disenchanted with (it developed a buzz), but the notches in the nut were noticeably closer to the center and not causing the E string to nearly hang over the edge. I popped off both nuts and changed them out and had to do a tiny bit of tweaking with a shimmy to correct a buzz. The end result is a very strong, resonate baritone uke that gets lots of play time.

This isn't a uke I'd recommend to someone unless they had some tweaking skills. And judging from other reviews, my uke's issue was an anomaly. At any rate, once I corrected the problem with the nut, I was in string heaven. It has a strong mellow tone, very amenable to my picking.

title: Do not even consider this book
comment:
This book is terrible. The notation in some places is incorrect. There are exercises that do not agree with the recorded examples. There are exercises that are duplicated. He does not play the notes written in the examples. His phrasing is poor. This guy is not even a decent player. He uses distortion on his guitar so it hides the nature of his sloppy playing. This is NOT a beginners book. I would imagine a beginner would have great difficulty with the very first example. I have been playing for over thirty years. I quit after I burned out and was looking for a way to get my hands working again. Take my advice you would improve your technique and your sense of musicality much more If you got a guitar trainer that would allow you to play your favorite guitar player's CD and change the tempo without changing the pitch. (Tascam makes a great one.)Your technique will improve just by learning his solos no matter how slow. Then you can download for free anything you need to know about scales or music theory.

The fact that he expects you to learn one exercise in one day is facetious. What are you supposed to do, practice these exercises one day at a time for how long? If you want a really good exercise book try Roger Fileberto's Guitar Technic. (Mel Bay) There will be no recorded examples but the material is excellent. If you are serious about the guitar find a good teacher. If you cannot afford one it is amazing what you can find on the Internet for free. Most of the players that I have known would be happy just to sit with you and show you some things. Listen to as many guitar players as you can in all different styles. I used to tell my students 40% of learning to play is listening.
I do not mean to bash this guy but if you are encouraging young players you should not deceive them. If you practice every day you will improve your technique with or without this book. Do not be misled.

title: I liked the simplicity offered by the Ditto X4 as opposed ...
comment:
I liked the simplicity offered by the Ditto X4 as opposed to the Pigtronix Infinity Looper. The software on the first pedal that arrived froze and wouldn't load correctly- had to exchange it for a new one. Customer service was great. Once I got it working, I had two issues that made me return it. The first was that the foot switches were so close together that I hit more than one at a time. I consider my foot size to be average so this was extremely frustrating.

The second issue was that I always had to double tap to stop each loop. This is extremely difficult to do EVERY time you want to stop just one loop. There is also no button that will allow you to "play all" with one press. You have to step on the loop 1 and 2 buttons simultaneously (which coincidentally wasn't a problem because of the foot switch proximity).

I opted to return the Ditto X4 and go for the more expensive Pigtronix Infinity. This allows me to switch play/stop modes with the press of a button (even mid-song via MIDI). The pedal is roughly the same size but only has three foot switches, making it easier to step on your intended foot switch. I'm sure the Ditto X4 is great for some folks, but for me the Infinity is infinitely more flexible/programmable.

title: Great mic for the price
comment:
[[VIDEOID:d2ea8b621309557a590c5269c043b987]] My wife and I are both professional video producers.

This is not a $350 wireless set up, but it is an excellent wireless mic for the price. Watch my video and judge for yourself. We did no editing in post, so what you hear is what you get. If you do any kind of broadcasting or recording and you’re just using an onboard mic, switch to this and your productions will seem much more professional.

In the video you can see how easy it is to use. The transmitter is the only thing that needs charged. The receiver is phantom powered by the device it’s attached to. And all it took to set it up was simply turning on the mic, and plugging in the receiver.

This is great when you’re speaking at a normal volume. I did notice while recording other videos that if things got a little loud, there was a bit of distortion. And as you can hear in the video, it does cut out a lot of extraneous noise, as a good lav mic should.

title: Very good synthetic sound
comment: Personally, I like the Clanrye reed. It delivers good tone for those that don't want to bother blowing in and maintaining a cane reed. The sound isn't as warm as cane, and is slightly brighter and can sound harsh in some chanters. As expected, they will last longer than cane reeds as well. I keep one handy as a backup reed. They also play easier than cane reeds, which is good especially for beginners or those that struggle keeping the pressure up in their bagpipe. The easy Clanrye, in my opinion, is the best pipe chanter reed for beginners on the market. The medium and hard reeds have good volume, resonance, and crispness. These reeds especially work well in Dunbar chanters. These reeds require no blowing in, so you can put them in your chanter and play immediately. They also require no warming up, so you don't have to retune the drones because the reed won't sharpen with moisture and warmth. As I said, they don't have quite the warmth and purity as a well-made cane reed, but will suit the casual or recreational piper very well.

title: Literally, perfect
comment:
I was a little hesitant to order this because a few others had panned it as needlessly overpriced compared to a piece of foam. Let me tell you -- I had ordered some regular 1 inch window insulation foam and tried to cut it to height, and it is not easy to cut straight. Additionally, the insulation foam very quickly degrades and becomes "dusty". When sliding it up to the bridge, the strings themselves have a sawing effect, leaving bits of residue on the strings and on the guitar body. I also felt that the open cell foam would have an abrasive effect on the guitar finish when installing and removing it.

This tremolo mute is not like insulation foam at all, so I'm so glad I didn't listen to the naysayers. The top is more like very soft foam rubber, with a very, very soft bottom fabric that feels a bit like chamois. It slides along the finish very easily, doesn't bind and roll under the strings like the insulation foam did, and was *literally* the perfect height to fit my Yamaha GC42C. I didn't have to do anything apart from install it in place. The strings don't leave saw marks on it, and it doesn't leave residue when installing or removing it.

As a nice touch, mine came with a hand-written note and a full page of practice tips for using the mute for developing technique. Just in the first few days of using it, I noticed an improvement in my timing because I can really hear the thumps synchronize with the metronome when playing fast arpeggios.

This is exactly the tool that I have been looking for. If you are unsure, just take the leap. You will be glad you did.
