title: Awesome archtop flawed by penny pinching
comment:
First off, if this guitar had no electronics or pickguard, and a better bridge, I would give it 5 stars easily. Let me explain: other than the neck-set angle (more below), the basic construction of this instrument is amazing: solid carved spruce top, solid maple sides, and a tastefully mild flamed maple solid back; finish is beautiful and near flawless.

Beautiful 1 piece mahogany neck with a nice dark black ebony fingerboard. Fretwork is good to very good; the frets are skinny (I prefer medium to jumbo, but this is a preference thing). The guitar arrived strung with round-wound, extremely heavy gauge strings; I quickly swapped them for a set of flat wound D'Ad chromes; much better. Action was what I would call "high"; I prefer low to med-low (again a preference thing). After I bottomed out the bridge-height adjustment and tightened the truss rod to remove the excess bow in the neck, I got it into a playable state that I was happy with; the compensated bridge allowed me to set the intonation to near perfect.

The acoustic sound of this guitar is to die for; it sounds every bit as good as it looks. Full bodied and surprisingly loud acoustically. Very, very nice. I really like the neck on this thing, and the 1 3/4" nut width is a nice change from your typically cramped electric-style neck. The neck profile is very similar to my Gibson Les Paul R8; a bit on the big/chunky side, but I like it like that! This is no skinny Fender-type neck.

So now the bad: After using what I would call premium woods and construction techniques, the factory decided to save a few bucks on the wiring, pick guard mounting, bridge, and the fatal flaw: the angle that the neck is set at. These factors combined just kill all the value and potential this instrument has. Because the set angle of the neck is not far back enough, a very (non-standard) small and short bridge needed to be used to get the action anywhere low enough. And because the threaded adjustment posts are so tiny, the part of the bridge the strings pass over was bent forward about 15 degrees (still playable, but flawed nonetheless) from the string tension. I would only expect this to worsen over time. If the neck was set with say another few degrees downward angle, a standard bridge would have been able to be used, and this would not be an issue.

The other problem is the pick guard; when the guitar arrived and I took it from the case, I noticed the pick guard was at a funky angle and the rear portion has no support so it kind of flopped around; to "fix" this, I put a piece of soft foam between the guard and the body to support the guard. Because the guard is glued to the support that's screwed into the body, there's really no way to adjust it to a correct (yaw) angle.

It's really a shame they skimped a few dollars and totally ruined the execution of this piece, because this axe has awesome potential. I considered rewiring it; wiring is very cheap and delicate looking; pot is a mini alpha type pot. Also considered replacing the pick guard, and relocating the volume to the body and adding tone control as well, but at the price I paid, I should not have to put another one or two hundred into it to get it completely playable.

If this were a $500-$700 guitar, I would overlook this stuff, but at the current asking price, I expect a finished, usable guitar out-of-the-box. As things stand, it's probably going back to be replaced by an Eastman or a Peerless. Sorry Loar, you had your chance, but the extra five bucks you pocketed is going to cost you several hundred. This is what happens when a musical instrument company is run by MBA's and accountants.

title: Easy, straight forward, sounds good
comment:
The good:
the sound, the ease of placement, flexibility and fine tuning the placement to get the best sound.
The not so good:
mounting

Got a Myers mic for my Ivanovski in April and had time to use it on gigs and get familiar with it.
It sounds pretty natural to your instrument, doesn't color the sound.
It is surprisingly forward and full sounding, though the high frequencies don't extent as much.
But then again, maybe there's nothing to extend and I'm subconsciously comparing to other mics I tried that have that airiness in high frequency end which could be exaggerated . I compared it with Audio Technica Pro 70. My take is that for this style of music Myers is a far better mic. AT is a sweet sounding mic however on my guitar it emphasized lows and highs leaving midrange in the background. For a Martin guitar that's great but for Selmer style guitar having the midrange Myers provides is better. The frequencies are evenly distributed.
Worth mentioning is that I never had a bleed in noise from the crowd in some of the loud bars we play in.

The gooseneck design really nailed it, you get a big range of possibilities by moving the whole assembly around the soundhole of the guitar and when you find something you like, you can fine tune the position with the gooseneck.

Which brings me to my only drawback which is mounting: the suction cup didn't work for me. Maybe because, on this style of the guitar, the top is arched. I don't like the option of gluing the Velcro on the guitar, I'm afraid if I ever want to take if off it'll pull the finish off, and I don't want the Velcro to be permanent on the guitar top. My solution was the putty I use with other type of pick up, magnetic, and this works more of less but I have to use a piece of masking tape to secure the cable otherwise the weight of the cable will be pulling the mic off.
But I can live with this as I really like the results.
I get loud volume without feedback in loud bars but I am using Baggs Para DI and that gives me options to squelch the feedback.
One more thing, whenever I play live in different places, I never have to go to the great lengths to get the sound I like.
Greg, the owner is very personal and follows up after the sale.

This one is a keeper for me.

Edit: Gregg turned my microphone into his newer version "the feather" for the difference in price which I was more than happy to pay. Now my only drawback about this microphone is addressed. The microphone clips to the sound hole, the preamp is hooked on either my pocket or a belt loop where is nicely accessible to change the volume. At this point there's nothing else I'd like to see change.

title: This pedal works fine if you learn how to set it up correctly and follow one simple tip.
comment:
This pedal has been working great for months now. I would like to get a back-up cable just in case but it has proven to be rather hard to find. I'm going to explore alternative sources (bicycle and motorcycle shops) and see if I can come up with a cheaper and better replacement.
Two reasons that I have had almost NO trouble with this pedal:
1) I followed the YouTube video for set up. If you're not getting a straight pull on the cable where it leaves the jacket, You're going to break it, period. Take the time to set it up right and you won't regret it.
2) In an online drum group somewhere, I read that a little bit of light oil on the threads of drum hardware nuts, wing nuts, lock nuts, eye bolts, etc. will keep them from slipping and/or coming loose. I know this seems counter-intuitive but I tried it and it works. I also put a little graphite cable lubricant inside the jacket to minimize "normal" wear. That was the ONLY problem I ever had with my pedal adjustments so I tried it... and it worked! Well lubricated hardware is reliable hardware.

Even experienced left hand players, order this, even if you have what you need, you wont regret it to have an extra acoustic electric around. It plays like a dream plugged in or acoustically.
It is fairly shallow, but surprisingly that doesn't compromise the sound... It sounds full bodied and the slim nature of the body adds to its character.
I am actually a right handed player but I am somewhat ambidextrous and I can play the first position chords as a lefty. the action further down seems just fine as well. Why did I buy this? It is good to have around, you never know if your going to have a lefty over, plus, I want my brain exercise! DONT PASS THIS DEAL UP.

title: More a pick blank than a pick, horrible quality
comment:
The picks I received are awful. These are rough triangle shapes cut out of a piece of thick plastic with a vaguely mock-turtle coloration (the coloration is dull and muted, grays and light grays, and nothing like the product photo). The picks have not been beveled on the edges. The edges have not been smoothed off and there are jagged patches on several picks. The shaping of the pick - rounding off the corners - has been done in a very irregular way, leaving the picks in a very asymmetric triangle shape. About half of the picks in the pack of 12 are warped, with one corner curling up. Five or fewer of the picks are actually usable as picks, and I will have to sand and shape those five before use.

This poor quality was extremely surprising to me. I have purchased Golden Gate mandolin picks, and I have been very happy with those.

title: Over-hyped disappointment
comment: I had the Boss RC 500 for several weeks and was quite content until I saw all the videos and pre-release hype about the RC600.  Waited 2 months for it to be stocked in the U.S. and even returned my RC500.  When I finally received ithe RC600 and spent many, many hours learning it and using it as part of my recording set-up, I discovered some of the same firmware and design flaws so many other users are now also reporting (just check some of the online forums).  Tiny toy-like controls, serious MIDI syncing issues (annoying warbly sound whenever another device hosts it) , and problematic looping and timing issues that have caused some serious embarrasment to several users reporting  about their live gigs.  My own complaints to Roland are as of yet unanswered, but I wasn't about to miss the product return window while waiting for their reply. This is a good example of a product being released before the bugs and quirks were fully worked out.  Perhaps in a year and several firmware updates it will be worthwhile, but not now.  I've returned this and am re-purchasing the RC500.

Since I am out of warranty, I removed the "SET" screw at the metal head with a very small allen wrench. The screw is tiny. title: Well-made and durable pickup
comment:
I bought this for a gig on acoustic when I didn't know if I'd need to be amped or not. Ended up not, but I did practice loads with this beforehand to make sure I could get an acceptable sound. I ran this into a cheapo Crate Amp on the clean setting. I mucked with the amps settings to warm up and tune the sound - to me, pickups often sound brittle. If I was really into it, I might have also bought an eq or tube pedal with eq to add some warmth to the sound before going to amp (or PA).

The pick-up is for a standard sound hole. In my case, I was using it on a Martin Dreadnought (D-12). (It definitely did not fit the Martin Backpacker I have). The pickup is braced by dense foam along the sides which is about two and half times thicker than the soundboard. This allows you some adjustment on distance from the strings. Bring one side up closer to the low E and one side further from the high E if you want more gain on the low notes.

I can definitely recommend this pickup to anyone needing to amp a standard acoustic that doesn't have built-in pickups. The product is nicely packaged in a reusable cloth bag with an instruction book.

title: Ringing issues.
comment:
I've had it for a few weeks now, used it for a couple of jams, and it's gotten rave reviews from all my musician friends. Sounds louder than you'd expect, but not too much. If there's no drums or percussion, slip this on and just tap your foot to the beat and you're done. You can do fancier rhythms if you can handle them (presumably while playing another instrument), but a solid 2-4 accent really frames things nicely. Seems well built so far.
UPDATE: I love the idea of this product so much, and it's really well built and everything, but I'm afraid it has a crucial design flaw: the little discs don't dampen each other like they should when you play by hand because they are positioned vertically. So you get this constant ringing, around G and G#, and it does get annoying. I've been trying ways of placing it horizontally on my foot, because it sounds better when the discs fall on top of each other. If you do wear it normally, you can lean your foot forward a bit, or even bend your knee and tap the tip of your shoe on the floor, and the angle will help a bit, though it's not ideal and is less natural a movement. Just putting your foot flat on the floor and tapping will have those jingles ringing the whole time. This ringing might not matter much to some people, but I think it makes the product fall short. I'd take it down to 3 1/2 stars.

title: Line 6 G90 Wireless Receiver and TBP12 Transmitter
comment:
This is a first-class product with some user-unfriendly things about it. Functionally, I couldn't be happier. The manual that came with the unit, however, was lacking in completion. This often occurs when "rush to ship" during the manufacturing process is ahead of the tech writers. However, having an RF background, I could figure out where the 50-ohm loads should be applied. (Very nice little 50-ohm BNC loads, by the way. Very pleasing.) Online, the manual was found through another website and Revision F still contained an error on about page 5 that says: "Press CHANNEL SELECT button, then press the SELECT button." This was intended to be instructions as to how to display all available channels. I just corrected the manual by writing in "SETUP" for the word "SELECT". If you already have this unit, you've probably figured this out on your own. As I said, this unit has great features and is worth the cost, in my opinion, IF you can accept certain weaknesses (covered later). The receiver/transmitter range is very, very good and not so much as a single bit of noise or co-channel interference is evident. The fact that I live within 1/4 mile of a NASA rocket telemetry installation operating high RF levels in the 2-6 GHz frequency range was a concern before I received and tested this unit's operation. Not a problem. That was a relief and my kudos to the LINE6 designer team. However, there are a couple of concerns I have. One solvable, one not.

1. (Solvable) The transmitter comes with the instrument cable with a standard 1/4-inch plug for your guitar. The other end has a very, very unique male multi-pin, mini connector that you won't find at any Radio Shack, that's for sure. Though the cable is well constructed, and even though I repaired almost every cable type known to man (humbly stated), this is not a repair I'd want to do during intermission at a gig. Since we all know that 90% of problems we have during performances are related to cable problems, it makes sense to have spares. This unit does NOT come with a spare cable. IT SHOULD for the price class of the unit. At the very least, LINE 6 should include information about connector part numbers, spare parts, etc so the consumer can have options. IT DOESN'T. You'll find yourself going blue in the face if this cable goes bad. Believe me, it is the weak link and WILL be the first thing to likely fail on you. Good luck unless you purchase a spare. I hesitate to ruin the fun I'm having with this unit by looking up the LINE 6 price for this cable. (If you don't anticipate certain types of system or cable problems, you'll all-of-a-sudden find yourself only able to play the cow bell or tamberine at the gig!) I know that the following solution is not part of the review but if you can see a solution, perhaps the problem won't be a big obstacle for you to buy this unit. Anyway, I minimized this almost certain cable failure (depending on how much you use it) by building a "pig tail" cable that mates with the transmitter cable. I put an inline female 1/4" connector on one end (to mate with the transmitter's instrument plug) and on the other end, I put a 90-degree, 1/4-inch instrument connector. The 90-degree works better with my Jazz Bass than the straight connector that comes with the transmitter anyway. Finally, I bundled the transmitter's cable neatly and tie-wrapped it to the belt clip. I never liked the idea of clipping the unit to my belt anyway. The transmitter is powerful enough to go through walls (I tested that) so, being in my pocket makes no difference in the receiver's strength display - NO difference. I tested that theory to 75 feet with not one iota of noise or signal problem. With this setup, the transmitter's cable will NEVER flex and I won't incur the cost and frustration of repairing it prematurely. The cable I built takes all the punishment but is replaceable for about 5 bucks. I'd hate to even look at LINE6's price for that cable based upon the transmitter's replacement cost. This leads me to problem 2, which has no solution.

2. (Unsolvable) I was really enjoying my new setup until I made the mistake of looking up the cost of a spare TBP12 Transmitter that comes with this unit. Accidents happen. Rain happens. #%&^ happens. The price was an astounding $199 !! ANNOYING to say the least. Add shipping and you've paid about half the cost of a complete new system. Before I spend $199 for just the transmitter, I'd buy the whole system and then sell the main unit which is less likely to fail because you can't drop it onto the stage.

Overall, I give this unit's operation, quality, design, mounting (rack), controls, back panel, and overall appearance 5-stars. For the high cost of replacement items, I recommend purchasing this only if you can afford to support it and from a maintainability perspective, I give this 2-stars. The average is 3.5 stars and that's an honest review. If the replacement transmitter were in the $100 maximum (only with proof of original purchase) and if the product had included a spare instrument cable, then for the overall price and operation of the unit, I would have given it 5-stars. So, you decide what is important to you and extract the rating which pertains. Good luck. Regardless, you will surely enjoy this unit right out of the box. Of that, I am sure. Oh, yes, one final comment about the unit's feature of simulating the effects of instrument cables. Since using this unit means that you only have a couple of feet of cable between your guitar and the transmitter, you won't have the high frequency rolloff that accompanys ALL cables. (If you bought a cable claiming it has no frequency loss and no inline capacitance, you bought a pig in a poke. There is no such animal. But that's for another day.) Now, LINE 6 recognizes that people may not like the "brighter" sound resulting from lack of a lengthy instrument cable. So, they cleverly add internal, selectable frequency attenuation presented to the user in units of cable length. So, for example, you can choose from about 6 to 10 different cable simulations from 3 feet up to 125 feet. As the length increases, so does the amount of high-frequency attenuation (rolloff). (For my Jazz Bass, I disable this feature (zero cable length) so I get a nice brighter tone if I wish. I use the guitar's tone control to reduce that if needed.) That's about it. I'm uncomfortable not having "spare parts" because Murphy is always lurking. People I asked about wireless systems often times have owned them in the past but went back to straight cables; not because the wireless systems weren't nice but because they had cable problems or transmitters got wet, etc. It is an expensive "cable substitute" but with the availability of spare parts and lower costs of replacement components, these systems could become mainstream and we'd actually use additional RELAY units in our stage racks. Rock on!

title: Killer Sound - Best Deal Around - Pound for Pound!
comment:
KRK Rokit 6 G2 Powered Monitor Speakers

I did a lot of checking around in this general price range.
I bought these on a sale/deal after reading up and comparing lots of brands, sizes, prices, quality, customer reviews, etc.

When they arrived I was almost shocked at how heavy they are. Solid and stylish with rounded front corners and front facing slotted bottom port -a design attribute claim that they throw off a more even and accurate bass response.

Honestly, I really wanted the 8" inch Rokit 8 monitors, but they were just a little too large for the desktop and after being 90% convinced they were terrific, I ordered a pair of the 6" Rokit 6 powered monitors.

When I plugged them in and hooked them up I was completely blown away at the clarity, punch and loud volume.

I set the Hi-Freq control about halfway and boost the volume control slightly around 3-6 DB and work the rest of the Level & EQ from the mixer/source.

The highs are silky and clear.
The lows are tight and punchy. It handles high volumes very well without getting muddy, buzzy or distorted. (unless you overdrive and crank the $*** out of them!)

My mixes have instantly gotten much better knowing that these speakers are reproducing the tracks more accurately.

A good way to "hone in" on your mix is to put it up against your favorite song that has a killer mix/production. Get used to hearing what a flat professional mix sounds like and try to achieve that by going back & forth, doing the A/B comparison. This way you will know that your mixes won't be too muddy, muffled, piercing, screeching, mid-rangy or hollow when played back on most systems.

If you are like me, a home studio enthusiast but doesn't have the budget or justification to spend unlimited amounts of money on uber-expensive gear, then look no further. These are perfect. They are loud, clear, punchy and accurate.

If I was going to get speakers for a Home Entertainment System, Television or PC/Mac computer, MP3, etc., I would probably get the Rokit 6" or 8" for those too. They could definitely handle great low-end bass and spaceship/engines/bomb-type rumbling sounds, but for the really big & loud ultra-low earth-shattering sound I recommend getting separate Rokit Powered Sub-Woofer.

I love the ease of use with the powered amplifiers built-in, hookup is a snap. Just plug them in and run a cable to them. Also makes it really easy for quick portable setup anywhere. (but remember, they ARE a wee bit on the heavy side!)

3 different types of inputs to accommodate any setup.
XLR/MIC type low impedance input.
Standard RCA (stereo cable) unbalanced input.
Standard 1/4" balanced TRS (tip-ring-sleeve) input.

More than worth the asking price...better if you can get them on a special deal or sale.
If something were to happen to these I would definitely replace them right away.

As you can tell I am very happy that I bought these and highly recommend them to anybody who is looking for killer powered monitors for a reasonable price.

Check out the photos I uploaded for better views.

title: Amazing Response for the Money
comment:
I have to admit I was a bit skeptical about what you could get at this price. But my husband and I tested the JBL LSR305 on various guitars and with recorded sound and we were amazed. The sound was so clean. The highs .AND. lows were excellent. There were times, in fact, when playing some jazz that it sounded like we were sitting in a restaurant with a band playing in the room. So clear.

Now my brother is the real musician in the family, and while he's in another state and can't help test this jewel out, I've listened to some of his monitor purchases over the years and nothing he's used in this price range -- 150 to 300 bucks-- has sounded as good as this 305. Some of the monitors he used didn't have the range. The lows just weren't there. Others just sounded 'artificial' or tinny. These JBLs have great smooth sound.

SUMMARY
The JBL LSR305 Studio Monitor has a new tweeter design. If you're interested in knowing more there are some great videos on youtube you should check out. (I was curious about the design and that's where I found out about the theory behind this new design and the "Image Control Waveguide". My favorite vid and the one I would suggest you watch as it has some sound recordings is by SonicSenseProAudio.)

The JBL LSR305 Studio Monitor hosts a 5-inch woofer driven by 82 watts of power. It has balanced XLR and TRS inputs, and there are trim controls.

Obviously not for the professional, the JBL LSR305 Studio Monitor (and probably the 308 too) are a great choice for regular people.

title: Sturdy and Lots of Room, with Minor Caveats
comment:
I've been drumming for over 25 years, and have been playing professionally for over 20 years; throughout that time I have owned many cymbal cases, from bags to hard-shell cases like this one. My favorite cymbal case of all-time is the classic Zildjian Cymbal Safe, so I was excited when Zildjian finally released its own rolling hard-shell case, as I assumed that their classic no-nonsense, practical design would just now come with wheels and a handle. At most I travel with 7 cymbals (ride, hats, 2 crashes, splash, and an effects cymbal), and this case comfortably holds everything except my oddly-shaped effects cymbal.

Unfortunately, I was slightly disappointed by this purchase, though I will continue to use it because it's the most stable and secure thing in which I can transport my current cymbal configuration, which includes a 24" ride. It is a slight improvement over the SKB Rolling Cymbal Vault, but not quite the perfect hard-shell rolling cymbal case.

The good things about this case are that it's rugged, it closes securely, and it rolls. At some point during my career I had to carry my cymbals across a city to a festival for which they closed the streets (that was after several flights of stairs in a hotel with a broken elevator) which motivated me to seek-out something with wheels. There is a metal handle on the back of this Zildjian case which folds into the enclosure on the back, and the handle is extended by squeezing its sides together and rotating it upwards. (I do like the handle design on the SKB Vault better, but this one does the job.) Wheels seem solid, don't wobble, are quiet, and the case is stable while standing upright; the SKB doesn't want to stand-up unless propped against a wall in a very specific way, but the Zildjian Rolling Cymbal Vault stands freely. Another feature which gives this a leg-up over the SKB is that the knob which secures the two halves of the case goes on the outside, so it doesn't keep the lid from closing when full of cymbals like the SKB. There's also a nice bit of foam padding on the lid which compresses to keep the cymbals held firmly in-place.

However, the few negative things are hard to ignore, and are impractical design choices which I am confronted with every time I use this case. The biggest complaint I have regarding this cymbal case is that there's a strap which permanently connects the two halves of the case; the strap clips at the top (near the handle) to further ensure that the case stays closed, and presumably acts as a fail-safe in the event that the knob on the front fails. However, the strap does not unfasten at both ends, so when you open the case: the strap acts as a hinge, keeping both halves of the case together. This may not seem like a big deal -- and may be a beneficial feature to some -- but if you think about unfolding two halves of a cymbal case (handle and wheels included) which accommodates 24" cymbals being permanently-connected: that's around 4.5 feet (54 inches, or over 137cm) of space in which you need to completely unfold your cymbal case. With the classic Zildjian Cymbal Safe, or even the SKB Rolling Cymbal Vault: the lid could just be removed and placed somewhere else, effectively requiring half the working space while opened. The lid of this case doesn't really stand-up well by itself, so for that to be an option during set-up and tear-down you need something to rest the lid against. If I'm lucky: there will be a tall monitor behind the drums upon which I can rest the safe and drape the lid over the edge, but these circumstances are rare; most of the time I'm resigned to just spreading it out on the floor, and taking up a ton of space during set-up before a gig, which inevitably annoys my fellow musicians, or is difficult on stages where space is limited. I suppose this design makes the lid harder to misplace, but I've never had an issue with losing half of a cymbal case in my nearly 25 years of gigging. My classic Zildjian Cymbal Safe has always been plenty secure without a strap -- it uses almost the same screw-together design as this case -- so I don't know why Zildjian felt a strap was necessary on this model. I am considering severing the strap at the bottom, and either leaving it severed, or attaching another clip so that I may fully remove the lid.

Another thing with which I am not super happy is that the center post needs to constantly be re-tightened from the back, as it continually works itself out of the case. I will probably find a way to modify this with parts that move less, but the plastic nut on the back of the enclosure doesn't do a good job of keeping the post fully secured and extended into the case. I have never had such a problem with the classic Zildjian Cymbal Safe, so I don't know why they felt the need to change the design.

Speaking of design changes: an unconventional feature of this case is that cymbals need to be loaded the opposite of every other hard-shell case I've owned; the case should be loaded from smallest bell size to largest, and the cymbals rest playing-side-up when loaded into the case. What's great about this feature is that the cymbals are supported more evenly, and they are at no point resting on their bells. This design also doesn't seem to waste as much space as the SKB case, but there are still some areas where I feel like the case could provide more space and be just as sturdy, or at least that space could be used intelligently. For example: SKB's analogous case has a little pouch riveted to the inside of the case's negative space, and that's a feature I wish Zildjian had copied, as it's super handy to have a little pocket for extra felts, sleeves, or a spare clutch.

If you're a flat-ride user: you'll probably want a separate bag for your ride, as this case is not designed for anything without a bell. The same applies for china-profile cymbals; usually those kinds of cymbals have small bells, so it should theoretically go inside this case first, though the flange at the edge of a china cymbal would preclude proper loading of additional (larger, or similarly-sized) cymbals. If your china is your largest cymbal, and all your other cymbals fit within its flange: this case would probably work.

Overall, this product is an improvement over the SKB Rolling Cymbal Vault -- there's slightly less wasted space here, and the Zildjian Vault closes much more securely -- but the strap design was not well thought-out. Zildjian really has a winner with their classic Cymbal Vault, and if they had just found a way to put wheels and a handle on a slightly larger version of that: it would have been a 5-star, must-buy product for any working drummer seeking the maximum protection for precious cymbals. Though Zildjian missed the bulls-eye with this product: it's still the best 24" hard-shell, rolling cymbal case I've used in my many years of drumming.

title: An Excellent Digital Grand Piano, Very True to a Real Acoustic Piano ...so far. :D
comment:
So far, so good. I'm not yet completely sold on this unit, but I'm getting there. The alternative was the P-255 by Yamaha. I'm a bit concerned about the 192 limit on polyphony (the P-255 has 256). The bottom bass octave end sounds a little heavy to me on Natural Grand, but all the other octaves all the way up are very true to my acoustic pianos. I am still playing with settings, though, so, chances are it's some reverb setting that needs adjustment.

I love, absolutely love, the fact that I can have this in our upstairs sound studio and that recording me playing is as easy as plugging in the cable to Reaper on the computer or, alternatively, using a live mic or running it through the sound board and the PA. That part is awesome. My husband, who is a musician, but not a pianist or keyboardist, loves that he can compose on it, easily picking out the sounds and chord structures he wants. Back to the piano part, now.

The action is very realistic. The black keys are a little narrower than I'm used to on my acoustic pianos, and a bit more slippery than either my plastic keyed Gulbranson or my 1907 upright grand with its ivory and ebony. I may have to resort to putting a bit of black athletic tape on the keys because of finger slip, but, otherwise, it's a REALLY great machine with a very realistic action and touch. I have not yet tried playing Rachmaninoff to see if it can handle that, but, even if it fails at that, I'm still quite happy. It handles the various strikes, strokes, and lifts quite well and accurately, though, no, it's not comparable to a Steinway or Bosendorfer grand.

This will please anyone needing an instrument that they can play in an apartment (under headphones, in other words) or who needs a piano to take their child from beginner up through concert level techniques. It also will do for schools and for churches quite nicely.

*Do get the three pedal add-on, though, and the dust cover is a MUST*

SHORTCOMINGS: The Knox bench is not high enough to get you up to where you should be. The pedal it comes with is ...er. Just Get the three-pedal add-on. It doesn't come with a 2.0 USB cable, so you'll need to buy one of those, and do make sure it's 2.0 not 3.0.

Don't know anything about what's on the DVD or in the learner book. Might be useful to some.

title: This guitar blew me away and not because of its price.
comment: This is my third IYV guitar I have bought in the last few months. I was so impressed with my Beautiful Green Les Paul I bought the Mustang/ Jaguar hybrid the very next week and to my delight it too was a fantastic guitar  and I particularly like the short scale neck.  A month and a half later the reviews and the Beauty of this guitar made me have to buy yet another one. I must say it just keeps getting better because this is my favorite out of the three. But maybe I’ve been lucky. People talk about and show pics of Little minor Flaws but mine arrived 3 for 3, guitars with absolutely no flaws at at all that I can find and I have went over them even with a magnifying glass. First let me say I have been playing professionally and for a living 40+ years. I have several records and CDs out and I also work as a session musician. And I teach guitar bass and drums. I also own the big brand expensive guitars electric and acoustic. So I have some nice guitars. And their pro guitars but let me say these guitars especially this semi hollow jazz guitar that really rocks more than one might think plays and looks like pro guitars and I have already giged and recorded with them blowing people away in the studio. They can’t believe how beautiful and how wonderful they sound and record. I’ve read some reviews on here that were great reviews but I would like to maybe correct a few things that I read. On one of the videos the one with the rusty frets, He was talking about Gibson being handmade while this guitar is using a computerized 3-D cutter. I just read an article last week and discovered Gibsons finishes and does other things ising handwork but Gibson also use’s a computerized 3-D cutter on their guitars as well. The second correction I would like to make is I’ve heard a few people say this is a knock off of a Gibson ES175. The truth is the closest guitar Gibson makes to this guitar is a ES135. The ES175 has very wide sides like an acoustic. The ES135 is the same thickness as this guitar. However the Gibson is made of maple whereas this guitar is made of all mahogany. And comparing the two maybe the dimensions are close but this is it’s own guitar. Maple and mahogany do not sound the same. But this guitar even with its stock pick ups through a good amp sounds amazing. As I said I was watching the video review above and he showed Flaws in the finish around the F holes. And a few other things he pointed out. The rusty frets on his was a shame but a correctable issue. Remember Vietnam is a tropical country with great humidity and maybe some of these sit in the warehouse that may not have the best Humidity control and if it sots long enough yeah things like frets will rust, but that’s just a guess I really don’t know. Mine came with nice shiny frets and I picked my guitar as well as the other two up and looked at all the same things Plus went over the whole guitar and I was lucky in the fact that I can’t even find a scratch or bleed through on my guitar. This guitar should sell for five times as to what it sells for. But the price does go up and even since I bought mine the price has risen again just as it has on the IYV Les Paul. But I have to agree with that fellow on one thing, there are some stupid snotty people out there that pay 200 bucks for a guitar and have the nerve to complain about small things that you could find on a $3000 Gibson. The only complaint I have is the packaging of their guitars. They are just a single guitar box not double boxed not reinforced in anyway just a small flimsy box with some Styrofoam and I have to say as far as it Hass to travel to get to the US and with all the hands that it passes through I am surprised any of them get here in one piece. When I picked up this jazz box from the post office it had a big hole at the top of the box and driving home I thought this could be a big mess. But I cut the tape open the box  and took it out of this little bag I could not believe it but there was not a flaw anywhere on the guitar. I know they’re trying to cut costs but their guitar should be packed and shipped in a better way. But I can’t complain because everything arrived without a Nick or a scratch or a finish flaw plus all the electronics and knobs worked just fine. If you want a great guitar I can only speak for the Les Paul which has a beautiful green flamed maple top and the mustang hybrid and this semi hollow jazz box you can’t go wrong. And for the snobs to think they have to spend thousands of dollars to feel like they got something remember one thing, Prince bought a 70’s Honer Japanese Tele knock off that he paid used for $30 that was his main guitar that he toured and recorded with his Entire career. I know this was long winded but I hope I got a message across about this fairly new company that most have not heard of yet.

title: A Beautiful Little Soprano Ukulele!
comment:
I stumbled across the Makai LK-80W while searching for an Ohana SK-50WG soprano which I had purchased recently. It seems that the Makai has the same design and tone woods as the Ohana except that it has geared tuners as opposed to Gotoh friction tuners.

Both the Makai and the Ohana are specified to have a solid cedar top and laminated Willow wood back and sides. On close inspection however, the Makai back and sides appear to be constructed with qulit ash rather than the willow wood. Despite the difference, it looks just as gorgeous and doesn't affect the quality of the sound of the instrument.

The difference is that the Makai is considerably less expensive but yet has the same quality build, finish and sound. The other noticeable difference is the slightly sharper fret edges on the Makai.

Another thing that distinguishes the Makai from it's "twin" is the shape of the headstock. And that's about it.

Sound: Rich, Warm and smooth because of the solid cedar top. The sound of the strings are perfectly balanced and when plucked sounds almost harp-like.

Projection: Loud compared to even a Lanikai concert.

Intonation: Perfect. Rare for a ukulele this price.

Sustain: Excellent.

String Action: Amazing! The strings are set low and this makes the uke very easy to play. No buzzing issues.

Tuners: Open Geared Tuners which are precise and smooth.

Finish: Gloss surface brings out the best in the way the wood looks. A minor critique is the the frets could have been filed down for smoother action while playing up the neck.

Design: Although the shape is slightly different from the traditional soprano I really like the way it looks.

Quality and value: Excellent!

The Makai is priced just right and makes a great ukulele for the beginner because of it high quality finish and great sound but will also be a great choice for the serious player. Bottom line is this, the Makai LK-80W is a fantastic sounding uke compared to those in my collection that includes a Lanikai LQA-CCA concert, Lanikai LU-22CGC concert, Oscar Schmidt OU6 Tenor, Ohana SK-50WG soprano and vintage Harmony soprano.

Sadly, I will be parting with it soon as because I actually purchased it as a gift for someone in my family.

Highly recommended for any level ukulele enthusiast!

title: Incredible Banjo Uke
comment:
I bought this banjolele two weeks ago in the Aklot store. This is an incredible instrument. I'm a guitar player, but played the uke for years and in fact have an acoustic jam group in Oakland, CA. This Aklot banjolele got me playing the uke on a regular basis again. The tuning is like a uke, GCEA, so there was no fretting learning curve. In our last jam session, I played the banjolele with my jam group and it was awesome. I had wanted to add another flavor to the jam and it enhanced our sound signficantly. The instrument sounds just like a banjo. I mean, it's never going to sound like a full sized banjo as the banjo has a bigger drum head and resonator, but the aklot's remo skin and the open back gives it a pretty voluminous sound, but not sharp. It can actually be heard above our plugged in lead player and keyboardist, so it doesn't get lost in a jam. Tuners are good and tight. I had to retune it every so often in the first couple of days as the strings stretched, but it has been over a week now and it now keeps its tune over an hour of practice time. It's a nice good concert size instrument, but be forwarned that it is much heavier than a uke. Other reviewers said you need a strap because of the weight but I disagree. At first, I attached the strap it came with but it kept getting in the way of playing, so I took it off. Actually, the drum body traps well between your chest and elbows.

Looks? It's fine. Looks nice. Nothing fancy. I didn't buy it for the looks so it's a non factor either way. To me, it's always the way it sounds which is fantastic.

The banjolele kit came with a lot of goodies: assorted banjo picks (meh), a tuner (great), extra strings, a truss rod wrench, a gigbag and a flame strap. All for 100 bucks. The strings it came with is fine. I don't plan to change it since it sounded good enough to my banjo untrained ear.

One of our jam members was so impressed she bought one for herself immediately after the jam session. Mind you, we are not professional players—we're intermediate to advance hobbyists—but we play well enough to sound okay more often than not. Sometimes, we sound pretty darned good though we mess up a song every now and then also. I'm actually going to take the banjolele with my guitar to a new jam group jam. Let's see what happens.

I was so impressed with the Aklot banjolele that I bought another Aklot 26 inch electric tenor uke for a song. If it sounds as good as the banjolele, I would be happy. I actually haven't received it yet. UPS said it's on the way and will be delivered tomorrow. Anxious to get my hands on that uke. Will let you know how it compares with my Luna Great Wave and my Kanilea Islander.

title: Excellent sound quality but short Lightning plug
comment:
The good (actually pretty great): This device is an easy way to do stereo or two-point microphone recording to your iOS device. No drivers or apps needed despite the message on first plugging it in. I did not think the audio quality was substantially better than the built-in mic on my iPhone 11, but that microphone is monophonic.

The bad (and a deal-breaker): Because the Lightning plug on this device is snugged right up to the top edge of the panel, it requires that your iOS device not be in a protective case. That's okay if your case is one that straps in or attaches your phone, but if you're using an "armor" style shell, it's a no-go.

Thats a shame, because with a little more design thought this mic could be a really good one for mobile audio gathering.

It is suppose to operate Line 6 products with a standard patch cable however, for me, that made no adjustments at all. So then I tried using a tip ring sleeve stereo cable. It worked but, it adjusted too many effect parameters all at the same time that I didn't want adjusted on my M5. Then, there were several occasions when it worked but in reverse. I got so frustrated with setting up this device over the course of about 3 days and lack of available information on how to set it up that I gave up and put it back in the box. It may go up for sale on ebay real soon if I can't get it to do what I need it to do. I have not called Mission's customer support line as of yet for help. title: A "Medium" Sized Stand; 57-Inch Max Height; 31x31x31 Inch Base
comment:
This Pyle PSTND1 is exactly what I wanted -- a "medium" height speaker stand for a very lightweight PA speaker that's about 6-8 lbs. Stand is not too tall. Not too wide of a footprint that people will trip over in a crowded room. I wanted a speaker stand that holds my PA speaker at about head height for smaller rooms. The PSTND! works well.

This stands extends to a maximum of 57 inches in height. It's a bit wiggly at full height, but acceptable.

Its legs when extended sit on a triangle that's 31 inches x 31 inches x 31 inches. The base footprint can be narrower somewhat if desired -- say around 18x18x18. But it's less stable, of course.

Weight is 3 lb 4-1/4 oz (1.494 kg).

Folded, the stand is 31-1/2" long and fits into a box that's 5x5 inches.

The vertical tube diameter measures 34.54mm (1.360 inches or 1-23/64 inches). The tubing is very thinwall steel. It's magnetic.

There is a plastic end plug to keep the end of the tube round. I recommend gluing this plug into the tube with super glue or other adhesive so the plug will not fall out and get lost.

The stand comes with a molded plastic plate adapter you can screw to the bottom of your speaker to add a socket. The plate's socket diameter measures 34.51mm (1.359 inches or 1-23/64 inches). The hole pattern is 5-9/16 inch x 3-5/8 inch. The holes are 11/64 in (6.6mm). Overall dimensions are 6-3/4 x 4-1/2 inches.

This stand is NOT for your monster 35-lb 15" JBL Eon. It's for smaller, lighter PA speakers.

NOTE: the cross-pin holes are a bit rough and cause the center pole to move with some difficulty. If you deburr the holes with a small file or countersink, the center pole will go up and down much easier and smoother.

title: Vintage Rock Echo!
comment: Wow, the Fab Echo is one of my new faves.  I'm running it through a Brownface Deluxe; great amp with great tremolo, but no spring reverb.  I've heard of guys using slapback as a faux reverb, Dan Auerbach of the Black Keys chief among them, so I figured for a couple bucks it was worth a shot - at least until I save up for a good Spring Reverb pedal.  I am keeping the Dano on fulltime.  The delay rate is stuck at a fixed interval, but it's tuned perfectly for pseudo reverb stuff.  Crank that repeat knob up and set the mix to above 60% and it just adds the perfect amount of space.  Run it through the front end of a dirty amp and it's instant surf psychedelia.  People mod these with a potentiometer to control the delay rate, but Danelectro has actually already done that with their 600ms Delay. The adjusted cost for the 600ms Delay is less than the parts for modding the Fab Echo, so if you do want a more traditional delay pedal you'd be better off going for the 600ms.  For me the Fab Echo is an "instant sweet spot" pedal, so it's staying right where it is.

title: "Laissez les bons temps rouler" (Let the good times roll)
comment:
It's way bigger than we thought it would be. On one hand it is seemingly high quality construction, yet it is not super heavy. It has 2 wheels for easy transport with an adjustable/retractable handle for rolling, or you can use the two additional side handles on the top to carry it should you choose.

The sound quality is awesome so much so we even like to use the speaker for programming we watch on our television.

I had issues with the instruction manuals, and I found it somewhat lacking. I resorted to several youtube videos for clarification. The bluetooth function is easy to use.

I cannot really comment on the battery quality yet. We used it for what appeared to be about 6 hours on the first day, and it held strong (although it is described as lasting 1 - 3 hours). It does come with a power supply pack though (which can be used to recharge the contained chargeable battery). It does come with a microphone, and there is a possibility of buying at least 2 (maybe 3) microphones and connecting them to the machine.

On a side note, we did purchase an additional Moukey wireless microphone. If you do so, you will need alot of batteries between each microphone and the corresponding microphone receiver(s).

This machine has brought us so much joy, good times, and memories in such a short time. I would say it is well worth the money. I look forward to many more.

title: The Bau You're Looking For
comment:
Wonderful Dan Bau for the price!

Mine came wrapped in authentic Vietnamese newspaper. The body construction is strong, given its portability. Bend rod seems sturdy. Roughly 108cm in length.

I had to make two adjustments to mine to make it more playable:
-Move the bell assembly up to about 6cm from the bottom. When mine came, the bell was too low, and deep bends were impossible. Wrap electrical tape around this spot on the bend rod, to keep the bell from moving downward. When tightening the string, make sure it is affixed in the middle of the bell.
-File down and put abrasive tape on the bottom of the instrument on either end. The stock construction is very slick, and will slide around on carpet.

Also I found it helpful to indicate the location of the harmonics with masking tape.

That's it! Tune to middle C and you're ready to go.

title: Buy good batteries!
comment:
If you are going to use this keyboard without the power supply (which is oddly not included), then buy really good AA batteries. I tried using the Duracell regular gold/black AA batteries (it takes 6), and the keyboard kept shutting down after about one minute. It was fine when the power supply was used, so I suspected the batteries were the issue. I switched to using Energizer Ultimate Lithium AA (found on Amazon), and that problem seems to be gone now. So if you are playing away, and the power goes off, try more powerful batteries.

For the price, this is a nice portable keyboard. I bought it so I could have something to practice on while on long vacations. (I have a Yamaha DGX-660 at home, which is well worth the extra money if you are looking for a better piano experience.)

Note that if you are in the same boat, the NP12 keys are a little narrower than the DGX660 (and I suspect other full-size pianos as well). The measurement for ALL the keys on the NP12 (five octaves including C at both ends) is 32.5" exactly. On the DGX660, the same number of keys measures 33.25", so that's an extra .75" over the 61 keys. For vacation playing, it's not going to be an issue, but if you are ONLY playing this keyboard and then need to play in front of an audience on a real piano, you will want to practice on that piano for a while beforehand I would think. Might not be an issue if your child is an elementary stage, but something to think about if they are progressing.

Speaking of keys, I also found the black keys a little too thin for my liking, and too rounded. I feel like my fingers are likely to slip down the sides. On the other hand, the keyboard itself is pretty nice compared to the others I played at a local music store. Some of them had large gaps between octaves, and one had a distinct "spring" sound when pressed (like an old bed!). So I encourage you to visit a local store to feel the different keys. Prices are often the same as on Amazon too.

The voices are quite good though, even if the speakers seem a little underpowered for the size. I like the Strings in particular. Note that you can press the Demo button and then press each instrument button to hear a different song played using that voice. However, since I can plug this in to my laptop over MIDI, I can always play virtual keyboards on my Mac and bypass the NP12 sounds.

* Update 10/2017: I added Pianoteq Stage virtual piano software on Mac which comes with a Steinway and Kawai modelled pianos. With headphones on (the built-in speakers can't do it justice), the sound is incredible! I couldn't stop playing!

I keep it at a low volume and it still crap. I ordered on Dec 16th and it was nice that it was here by Christmas, because I wasn't expecting that. The box arrived with a hole in it. It took a couple weeks to get the new one and I was excited that it actually worked.... until Jan 26th when it started doing the same thing with the monitor outputs. title: Great value. Not a 57, but on its own merits, not bad. No clip, no bag, miswired unbalanced cable.
comment:
I must admit I didn't expect much from these, given the low price. I certainly didn't expect them to compare favorably with the microphone they're built to look like, the Shure SM-57. But I put up one of these alongside the real thing when it arrived, and while it's no 57, it's really not bad at all.

The output on this mic is higher than that of the 57 by about 3 dB with the same SPL using my voice as the criterion. Not a terribly scientific test, I'll admit, but I run a studio, not a lab. This mic seems considerably brighter than the 57, with a very open high end that's crisp but not so crisp that it sounds like a condenser. The housing is solidly built but seems a great deal lighter than the 57; there's probably a lot less metal.

The three-star rating is due to the really uncool way this microphone is sold and packaged. It comes in a simple cardboard box. A cable is included, but it's wired unbalanced with a 1/4" TS connector at the end. Not useful in the professional environment. There is also no storage bag, and incredibly, NO STAND CLIP! That's a ridiculous omission. I bought a set of two WeyMic stand clips separately and they seem to fit well. The cable is miswired at both ends, so if you're going to chop the 1/4" plug and put on an XLR male, you also need to check the wiring inside the XLR female end. it's usually wrong, too.

title: Long term review- read
comment:
I've had these 3 years. Gigged about 120+ shows with them. Yes you can drop them and they're tough. Mine still work. And at 10 bucks a deal.

What happens with these is- they rely on a rubber grommet at the base that goes into the sub box to keep it from wobbling and fit snugly. Well, these grommets don't last. And I lost one of mine. When that happens it still works but there's a lot of play and the tops wobble quite a bit. Haven't fallen but too wobbly for me. I just bought new ones with a locking mechanism to prevent wobble. We'll see what happens there.

In short, they're cheap and they work. They cost next to nothing. And they take a beating. But don't lose a grommet. If they break replace them with something or they will fit too loosely.

title: Alesis DM10/10X/10 Pro/10 Studio and Amazon
comment:
1 of 2: I have spent much time at the local music store to find the best e-drum kit for me. Prices varied between 499.99 to a whopping 8000.00. The Alesis DM10X was not available at the music store to test ride, however, I find the abundance of triggers to be very appealing. I have read numerous reviews, most of which incited much reconsideration. Most of the folks at the drum store were very adamant on procuring an acoustic kit, but what happens when we have the itch at 02:30? We surely cannot upset neighbors, and much worse,we cannot pass up on a creative urge. An electric drum kit was the answer. Compared to its competitors, or distant cousins thrice removed: Roland and Yamaha, this kit can hold its own. The module surely leaves some to be desired, as much as the rest of the kit, but for the price, you are getting a whole lot more than you paid for. Take good care of your kit, take good care of your investment, and it will surely prove most of the reviews wrong. Alesis delivered a great product, an affordable product, that rivals it's more expensive counterparts. I sure am glad Amazon chose to stock this product.

2 of 2:

A quick tangent: Have you seen the videos of airline crew throwing luggages from the cart to the aircraft? It is much more common that we realize. I have seen soft, light fabric damaged during transit at the hands of neglectful, reckless courier or warehouse personnel.

The DM10X comes very well packaged: inside, there are boxes that contain absolutely nothing to make the product as secure as possible - less shifting during transit, the product has an exponentially higher survivability rate. Outside are two nylon straps to secure the perimeter of the box. But even with great careful though and detail to packaging, it did not withstand some rough hands, dollies, truck containers or aircraft and/or its crew. From an engineering standpoint, it was almost as if I had an x-ray vision and dreaded opening my thousand dollar Christmas present: Nylon straps are torqued down for added security for the product, but when you see it other than perfectly parallel to the sides and puncturing the box, you, as I have, will feel a chill roll down your spine. The sides of the box that were punctured were "quick fixed" with layers of packing tape - this did not help my poor drum kit. Out of habit, I immediately register delicate or expensive products prior to opening it's enclosure, however, like most products, serial numbers are often hidden away with the product as an engraving or sticker. I snapped pictures as I peeled away at the layers of packaging, like a Russian nesting doll. The Nickel/Chrome dipped frame and drum heads were very badly scuffed, and the breakout cable for three of the triggers were open circuits (they were DOA). I immediately called Amazon, and as expected, they delivered the best possible service and offered a multitude of options to correct the purchase. I don't think I have ever had a single negative experience with Amazon's customer relations services. Thank you guys, you have my continued patronage and I admire your customer service team.

title: Get ready to drill if you own a Squier
comment:
I have a 92 Squier which I love. But after all this time , the old keys would not hold a tune. I play R n B mostly but love all genres and I strum pretty hard a times.
So , I read other reviews and decided to try em, hoping they would just slide in..... WRONG!!!! If it's any help, I have a 92 from Ensenada Mexico!

I knew after building Harleys and working on cars, this should be easy , though I was scared by drilling on this thing, I could break it. The holes especially the high E hole is really close to the edge.
It was not that tough, just go slow , let the drill do all the work (DO NOT force the drill into the holes)

After that it was pretty simple. I strung mine top hole to bottom so the bottom holes line up properly on the bridge. After that it has stayed in tune pretty well. I am satisfied with this product. Plus the black keys look good on her! Update May 25,2021 Stays in tune for the last month now! LOVE EM!

title: Wall piece, not a player.
comment:
1.Packaging: decent packaging although double boxed would have been better, bubble wrap around cardboard seemed to help it get to my front door safely.

2.Appearance: Nice looking (from a distance) excluding the logo on the headstock. The logo looks a bit cheesy as does the pic of Einsteins face next to the name. Excellent paint job, although pickguard and nut needed some finishing. The nut had (what looks like brown dye) on it with lots of plastic shavings coming out of the string rests. The pickguard was a little rough cut around the edges. The arrow tailpiece tends to stick up (off the body) towards the end points causing a cheap look up close. Also noticed a little piece of the fretboard missing near the 20th fret.

3.Playability: Quickly noticed at the bridges lowest setting, the action was incredibly high. Unless a shim is added, there is no way of fixing the action. Neck feels great, nice and sturdy. Tuners were a bit cheap but did the job. Small frets but strings/neck greatly aligned. Saddles within bridge mechanism wiggle with strings, which ofcourse causes a list of problems.

4.Pickups: Beginner quality, wouldnt play on stage or record with them.

Overall: Would not recommend this to anyone but beginners, I have nearly 30 guitars in my collection, this will be a wall piece not a player.

title: great cable, like the right angle for the amp connection

I could never get just the right cable, I now I have it with this one. I like an angled cable to plug into the amp so it won't be bent all of the time. I like to leave my cable plugged in, draped over the amp when not in use too.
I also prefer a straight connection to any guitar, especially my Stratocasters because the angles ones scratch the finish and just don't lay right.
I tried another one like this but it was so cheap that I got feedback and scratchy sounds from the amp when using it.... it went right in the trash.
This cable sounds clean, surprising because it isn't that thick either. I like this 12 footer so much I have an 18 footer on the way. If I ever play in a band, a longer cable would be better so you don't have to be right on top of the amp. I may look for this in an even longer length too, we'll see.
If doubt the quality, don't. I'm playing Gibson's and American Fender's through a nice Marshall amp and it sounds stage worthy, too bad I don't though.... oh well, we will keep working on it.

title: This trombone was a disappointment
comment:
At first, I was excited to play my new plastic trombone. Of course it didn't have a great sound, being made of plastic rather than brass, but I let that slide. I liked having a lightweight trombone that I could use anywhere and I loved that it came with cleaning tools, 2 mouthpieces, and a stand.

However, I soon began to realize certain flaws with the product. The slide did not slide smoothly, even when lubricant was applied. The lock ring that connects the two main parts never screwed on correctly. The high notes frankly sounded awful. The water key wouldn't seal and the water would leak onto the floor. And the trombone stand that came with it is not efficient for traveling.

As you can see I was disappointed with the tromba pro plastic trombone. I have owned it for several months now and decided to play it. It sounded like air was leaking out so I unscrewed the lock ring and tried to take it apart. I barely applied any force and the shank snapped. The shank broke off of the slide portion like it was held together with super glue. I can't repair it and I am upset that my $100+ was a waste.

title: BEEFY. EXTRA. GORGEOUS.
comment:
First off, I didn't buy this for connecting an instrument to an amp or for headphones. As you can see in the picture, I'm using it to connect an Edutige ETM-001 to my computer via a TRS female to female coupler. If I was sending an already amplified signal I would probably notice a difference, but given how miniscule a mic level signal is, the quality of the cable isn't really going to do much. I opted for this one because of it's purported durable, chonkiness, and just how friggin' extra it is overall. I MEAN LOOK AT IT, IT'S SO PRETTY, and the beefy plug neck + coupler is pretty much perfectly sized for hand mic use.

How THICC? About as thick as a ¼ inch (6.35mm) audio plug sleeve, (see pictures.)

How fit? Right angle end doesn't really fit into my phone (Galaxy S9+) with a supcase on, because of the large block and short riser (??? see pictures). The straight side fits fine thanks to it having a longer riser than the right angle end. I have issues plugging most cords into my phone because most of them don't even have a riser on the plug to to help it reach, so the fact that either end fits is a big deal.

Sound quality? No worse than anything else I have, so I can't take any points off for that. Is it better? Possibly, PROBABLY, but since I'm not using this to carry audio directly to my ears on otherwise audiophile grade equipment, it's basically impossible FOR ME to say.

Value for money? At $13.93 after the $14.66 sale price and extra 5% off WHICH I PAID, this cable is a tremendous value for the money. At the original $16.29 list price, I'd say it's just about right for what you're getting. And you need to keep in mind that this cable isn't just about the audio, it's also about the look; you're not just buying high quality equipment, but a designer accessory that looks amazing and sounds at least as good as anything else IF NOT ALSO AMAZING, (again I really am not in a position to say.) If you're not looking for something extra however, the appearance of this cable might be a con rather than a pro.

Durability? This is the first of these cables which I've ever purchase, and I've only had it for about a week, so I have no anecdotes to share and inform in that regard. It definitely FEELS durable, I would describe it as "substantial," and extremely well shielded as the cross section stock images suggest. I would be afraid of running it over with my desk chair not out of fear of the cable, but out of fear of getting thrown out of my chair when the cable stops it dead in it tracks.

title: I am having a great time with it.
comment:
It appears there are some people who have had a bad experience with this violin package. Maybe they have expectations for this violin that are too great for a $60 violin, or maybe they have a legitimate complaint. I can't say for sure which it might be if not both, but for me personally I have had nothing but great things to say about this violin.

It arrived in perfect condition. No defects on the violin or any accessories, and it really is what I expected and wanted. It is a acceptable starter instrument. It is not a good instrument. A seasoned violinist will not look at this with admiration. Do not go into this purchase expecting anything more. That being said the one I received was in great shape, and sounds pretty good. I have literally played it for at least an hour a day nearly everyday since August when I bought it.

I will say, I am inexperienced I can't say for sure, that the sound seems a tad dull compared to fiddles I listen to in music. It's not a bad sound and maybe it is just something I've done wrong, but it is not as clear or as sweet sounding as I might like (especially the G and D sting). Maybe it's the strings or maybe it's the bow. That being said it is not a major problem, and still sounds pretty good.

As others have mentioned you may as well throw out the rosin it comes with when you get it, and make sure you order some rosin with your order if you buy this fiddle. The shoulder rest is pretty iffy also. It works... just barely. Though I would recommend going to a luthier and trying out some shoulder rests to get the right fit if you decide to buy a new one instead of buying one online.

The bow is decent. A stray hair or two may be broken when you get it. It's not really a big deal. Just nip em off with nail clippers. You should still have plenty left. Not much else to really say about that. It holds the rosin pretty well.

The strings that come with it get the job done. I still am using the second set that came with it. I broke the E string on the first set tuning it when I got it. Whoops. :x So be careful and tune all the strings up at the same time in small increments. This will also prevent the bridge from collapsing on you while tuning it up.

The case is solid and looks nice. Holds stuff well?

All in all this violin package is really a steal. (In a good way.) I can think of much worse things to spend $60 on and I have something that has and will provide me with literally hours and hours of entertainment mixed with a bit of frustration.

Keep in mind that this is no doubt a factory made violin as opposed to a hand crafted one and there are likely going to be some undesirables mixed in there. Maybe you'll get one of them... maybe you won't. I say give it a go however. If you get a bad one just return it, and if you are feeling lucky try again. For me it was worth it completely.

title: For the price, if you can set it up, not bad.
comment:
I bought this as a project. I have a guitar I use to test builds. I used this guitar to transfer a build over. As such, this review is essentially about the neck and body. I did however tune and set-up the stock guitar to play it, just for posterity. It was not upsetting at all. For the price of less than $85 with a an amp, you can't go wrong. The neck was straight and amazingly there was very little to no fret sprout. The overall package is very light. Amazingly so. If you changed all electronics like I did, this would be a so simple to gig with. No heartbreak and so light. The nut however is a total joke of a piece of plastic. It is a must replace piece, novice or pro. The guitar is unusable with it. It is a 42mm nut, so it is easy to replace.

Once set up, with some mods, due to it's lightness, it is joy to play and the sound, regardless of the questionable body, is very pleasing.

While this is not my favorite guitar, I have to admit that I own a lot. This is not my least favorite, and plays well above it's price tag.

I would and will buy more.

title: DZ Strad Viola Model 101
comment:
I have purchased many entry level violas. This entry level viola to me seems a step above most other entry level violas. It is a more expensive entry level viola but I believe it is a worthwhile return on cost. It comes with a 3 star bridge, quality bow, and the finishing touches on the viola itself are not necessarily beautiful but show a concern for quality. It produces a clear, open sound and is easy to tune. I haven't had it long enough to tell you if it stays in tune better than some of the other entry level violas which often require tuning every practice. The pegs do seem to have a strong bite and don't slip like some other cheaper pegs I have seen. The prelude strings are good, not great, while the bow seems a step above entry level. The attachable rest is easy to put on and off, but the case has no internal storage for the rest, which would be awesome. There is a zipper pouch on the outside of the case to put the rest in but this seems less than ideal. The rest itself is very pretty. The fingerboard seems good, not great, but certainly good enough considering it is an entry level viola.
Overal I am very impressed. I am blown away by the bridge and the bow. The rest of it is better than average for entry level. Hard to go wrong here.

title: Excellent Quality Sound, All I Needed for Perfect Recordings
comment:
I had heard rave reviews about this microphone both from friends who own them and famous people that I talked two who own one or more of them. All of them said it produced a quality beyond expectations, so I ordered it.
Not only did the order come in earlier than expected, it was in pristine condition and sealed brand new. It was easy to set up--all I had to was screw the mic to the Ringer Shock Mount and plug it into an unused USB port--and I was quickly on my way to recording songs.
The quality is much better than I expected. When I record vocals, it picks all the parts of a voice that I need and leaves out the background noise perfectly. When I use it in live webcam sessions or video conferences, it activates when I speak and keeps the background noise to a minimum while reflecting the same quality of audio as recording.
It is the best mic I have used, and it's right in my dorm room. I just place it on my desk when I need it--no need for big expensive mounts that take up floor space. It even works better and easier than some of the mics I've seen in professional recording studios. It is definitely worth the price. I definitely recommend it to the hobby cover song recorder and to the professional-yet-simplistic audio tech alike.

title: Screaming Pedal
comment:
I'm a Golden God!!! My neighbors and my wife disagree vehemently. I don't care, sometimes you just got to crank it up and rock out. It's good for the soul.
I have an Epiphone Wildkat and a Roland Cube-01 that I play on. I like my set up. I've only been playing for about a year but I've made progress. I am committed to playing and have done so every day. I play a variety of music; funk, jazz, rock and a whole lot of blues. I bought the amp because we live in a condo and anything larger than 10 watts was just too much. I can practice and sound horrible and nobody has to suffer. Until now...

This pedal really pushes my amp to its limits. At really high volume I get feedback but I do have a hollow bodied guitar. I can tone it down and still get some really crunchy tones. The sustain is crazy. I'm really having fun. This isn't my first pedal, I have a looper, a chorus, a fuzz, a distortion, and a delay pedal. This crunchy one fits nicely in my arsenal.

The unit is metal and feels pretty good. It's on the lighter side and doesn't exude the quality that other higher-end pedals possess. But at the price point, it's adequate. The knobs adjust smoothly and the jacks are tight. The button is nice and clicky and lights up a pretty blue when engaged. The knobs light up too. There is no excessive "white noise" at full volume.

I've only been messing with it for a couple of days and I've been able to find some pretty cool and unique sounds. If it fails prematurely or if I have problems I'll update this review, but as it stands right now,... a thumbs up from me.

If you have any questions, feel free to ask.

title: A rock solid 6-channel mixer for the price
comment:
I admit, I'm often a skeptic when it comes to no-name brand music gear out of Asia. A lot of it is made by fly-by-night companies that won't exist in a few months, and the quality can be spotty.

In this case, I'm pretty happy with this 6-channel mixer. It's a good balance between affordable and functional, with all the featured you'd expect in 2023. It has a lot of what you'd expect from a trusted brand like, say, TASCAM, but at a fraction of the price -- the tradeoff being longevity, customer support, and quality of parts.

If that tradeoff is worth it to you -- for bedroom musicians and audio folks on a budget, it might be -- this is a solid piece of gear. It covers all the essentials: 3-band EQ, phantom powers/48v support, mic and instrument inputs, an FX send with separate mixers on each channel, multiple stereo outputs (including XLR), as well as USB inputs and Bluetooth support.

Getting up and running was easy, pretty standard stuff. Sound quality was good in my tests. I detected no humming, buzzing, or other noise. Just a good, clean input.

Controls feel nice and the components are decent all around. Not up to TASCAM's level, but good.

The included documentation is TERRIBLE, as is always the case, thanks to a bad translation, but if you've ever used gear like this before you'll be fine. It's all quite self-explanatory.

Overall, a 6-channel mixer with all the essential features is a bargain at just over 100 clams. How long will it last? Only time will tell. But if you're on a budget, this is a great way to get started.
