title: Great PTrain Alternative
comment:
I read the reviews, read about some of the quality issues both with the board and the case - but I still decided to take a chance. I am glad I did!! I have been using it for nearly 7 months and no issues at all. The bag has held up well. It is a little tight getting the board into the bag but it does fit and holds a fully loaded board. The outer pocket is great for extra cables and such. I ordered and installed a Truetone CS7 under it - it fit great! I had to modify the brackets by straightening out the 90 degree bends but - no issues at all and it is very securely fastened to the board. I purchased some zip ties and mounts to keep the underside neat - definitely recommend this to anyone looking for taming loose cables.

I did have a couple of areas around the welds where the grinding was a little aggressive and left a couple of divots but the velcro covered this completely.

My only complaint is that the glue on the velcro doesn't hold very well to the pedals - especially the rubber base. I did find that if the area is prepped by cleaning with a little alcohol on a cotton ball it does stick a lot better.

All in all - I would definitely recommend this to anyone looking for an inexpensive yet sturdy alternative to a PT board. The price can't be beat and it is a very sturdy and (when all is said and done) well built.

title: The Champ
comment:
I bought this amp to use at home and if needed, to mic up for smaller gigs. While very expensive, I think that it is worth it. Time will tell, but if this amp can hold up over time like the original Champs, then this will be worth every penny.

Pros:
- The amp is exactly what I wanted in terms of a very small amp that is very light weight and still produces great tone.
- While it could go either way, I like that the amp only has one knob which takes some of the complication out of finding the right tone. On other amps you can cut mids, highs, etc. on the guitar or the amp, or further down the chain in the mixer, but for me (I'm my own sound guy) I can dial everything in from the guitar.
- The finish and look of the amp is totally classic and just looks right in your guitar room. From the leather strap to the tweed, and the inclusion of the canvas cover make this amp feel like it's worth the price you paid.
- The cleans are crystal clear and chime like a bell with my strat. You can also crank the amp and find really nice distorted tones if you don't mind the volume.
- Nobody really talks about it, but this thing takes pedals well. I've used delays and reverbs (because there's no reverb on this amp), and both sound great. In addition, before I had the attenuator, I used drive pedals with some success (Fulltone OCD). But trust me, something special happens when you cook the tubes without any drive pedals. Either use your volume knob on the guitar, or a clean boost to push the amp and that distortion is better than any pedal.

Cons:
- There is only one con here... This amp is really LOUD! It has to be said that while this is a 5 watt amp, it is very loud. So those dirty, driven tones that I talked about before. Well, depending on where you're playing, you might not be able to get them. I didn't expect this to be an issue, because 5 watts, but I needed to buy an attenuator from Weber (Micromass model shown in the pictures) in order to drive the tubes at an acceptable volume level for my home.

Conclusion:
The only complaints are that it is loud, and expensive for such a small amp, but I'm sure it's loud enough to gig with (for most people) and the build quality lives up to expectations, so the premium price is forgiven. The tone is pure from your guitar and there are no knobs to adjust anything, but I argue that it allows you to focus on playing and getting to know how to coax tones out of your guitar. If you plan on practicing at home and don't want to wake the neighbors, get yourself an attenuator like the one pictured. If you will only be playing totally clean, or with pedals, then you can get away without it around 3 or 4 on the volume knob. But it really starts cooking around 7 or so, but even that is really loud, so keep that in mind.

title: Really nice mic
comment:
I'll go over things I like and things I don't like

Things I like-
- It's easy to setup, and if you need to take the mic itself out for something you don't need to take apart the whole stand to do so.
- It sounds great, it picks up sound very clearly and it doesn't have any BS built-in features that automatically reduces sounds that aren't human voice. Some mics do this, but I think that job should be completely left to software.
- It has an adjustable echo function knob right next to the gain knob. I've yet to play with it more, but you might be able to simulate being in a larger more echo-y room than you are. Might be helpful to musicians, but I have to play more around with it to test it. Overall, better than not having it I think.
- All metal construction for the most part.
- Comes with a little headphone holder you snap onto the "forearm" part of the arm. Frees up desk space, and it should be an industry standard
- The pop filter is positioned on the mic holder itself, not somewhere else on the arm where you'd have to adjust both the pop filter and the mic if you move it. Makes things more simple

Things I don't like-
- The mic arm is a little bit stiff for my taste. Of course this will get easier to move over time, but it's just a little knitpick I have coming out of the box. Keeping it in mind, I think they could have done the mic arm just a little less stiff, and I think it should still hold up well later in its years.
- I think the mic cable could be a little longer

Overall, great mic. I look at other mics for gaming and other general-purpose stuff at this price point, and this seems to have the most features without compromising on quality. There are also great small design features that stand out, such as that pop filter placement, headphone holder and ease of microphone removal from the stand that give it an edge over others.

Much has been said about the wah wah, space station, univox, whammy....and these are all great for guitarists, but for a realistic Leslie 145, this is the best kept secret out there. I'm a keyboard player, and short of spending $500 on a Neo Vent, this thing is amazing. I DO wish I could save presets, but that's a minor inconvenience. title: Turned my cheap ukulele ($24.99) into a real musical instrument!

I am wanting to learn to play a musical instrument and having been working on and off trying to learn the acoustic guitar but the guitar is big and bulky feeling.  I am a children's book author and have songs to go with all of my stories and I think the Ukulele may just be my perfect fit.  I looked online about ukuleles and saw that someone said you could put real strings on a cheap ukulele and voila excellent, or almost, excellent instrument.  I am not musically inclined (hoping to change that) and have never changed strings on an instrument before.  I was able to follow a wiki website and a you tube video and was able to change the strings out and then tune it with a Snark electronic tuner.  Amazing difference!  I am so excited now because the ukulele seems to be the right "size" for me, and I also ordered a book with a DVD, Ukulele Primer For beginners Book & DVD and this is an amazing book and DVD combo for the beginner.  I had to research online to see what kind of strings to buy and even the book/video I purchased had mentioned this brand of strings.  I feel so thankful that in my lifelong journey it has brought me to the ukulele.  I think I will really enjoying playing it and you never know how far one can go in life if you do not put yourself out there and try.  So here I go, and wish me luck!

title: Good enough for non-expensive guitars, but don't expect a premium product
comment:
The Good:
1. Product arrived as specified and measurements as illustrated, made it an easy setup for me
2. The price, about a third of what you'd pay for the equivalent Gotoh or similar quality brand
3. Locking mechanism works well enough and the posts have a low profile, which I prefer

The Bad:
1. On of the threaded sleeves used to secure the tuner head got stuck on the post. I was able to widen it enough using a pair of scissors without damaging the threading, but still shows QC problems
2. You need to be careful when tightening as the threads aren't made with small enough tolerances and if you try to screw when sleeve is at an angle you might cause it to get stuck or brake on you
3. Tuners aren't as smooth as you'd expect for after-market tuners, they aren't horrible and don't get stuck mid-turn or anything, but it feels less sturdy than what I'm used to

You get your money's worth, I personally used it as replacement on my cheap acoustic, and compared to the previous tuners I'm very pleased, definitely an upgrade. However, these tuners don't really measure up to other quality brand tuners I have installed on my more expensive guitars. Be prepared for possible adjustments that you'll have to make due to poor QC. All in all I'm quite pleased though, would give it a 3.5/5 if possible.

title: Full featured startup podcasting kit
comment:
EDIT: My son has been using this setup a lot more and is pretty happy with it. He started using the bluetooth connection to import audio stream from the computer itself and his phone, and has been doing some neat mixing. He likes having the physical buttons, especially for the mute, and the microphone has been working well for him.

The hardware seems to be rather good. The microphone and pop screen seems of good quality, as does the cable and boom arm. The foam cover for the microphone was smushed up on the packaging, but it worked just fine, stretching out once installed on the microphone. Since my son had a previous boom arm setup, we just removed the existing Blue Yeti USB microphone and installed this new 48V microphone on there, since the mounting screw was of the expected standard size. The control panel has a nice metal cover, but the rotating knobs are chrome covered, cheap plastic which wobble slightly. The sliders are better, and the buttons seem to be fine.

Operationally, there's a lot to learn about this setup. The device has an internal battery, but since we were using it with a computer, we hooked the computer to the sound board with the included USB A to C cable. The Windows 10 PC identified it properly as a device and no downloads were necessary. When hooking it up, I tried reading the micro-print on the teeny-tiny instructions that came with the device, but quickly searched the internet for something more readable. Our first operational experience was not good, as there was a weird buzzing coming from the headset and the monitor headphones after speaking into the microphone! Eventually, after pressing buttons, turning knobs and cycling the power, that stopped. I think it was some sort of voice effect that was enabled. My son wishes that the device would turn off when powering down the computer, but the internal battery means that he has to manually power off the board.

All of the buttons are backlit and there are colored LEDs under other sections of the board, which seem to be like a level meter for the microphone. It's a pretty cool looking setup for sure. My son fooled around with it for a few hours, making adjustments and using it for an OBS broadcasting session. I think he likes the features, but said he wasn't crazy about the sound quality of the microphone. I don't know how he has it setup, but I think he needs to do some more tweaking, as the sound quality of the microphone should be pretty good.

We haven't tried the bluetooth interface yet, but hopefully it works fine. We also didn't try the audio jack connection to the board, mainly because he primary uses devices and sources internally from the PC.

I think that this is a pretty nice package, with everything necessary to do some fancy podcasting and streaming, but there is a learning curve to using it.

title: can sound like some of the best amps in the world
comment:
Amp modeling is nothing new for guitar amplifiers but they have been improved a lot over the years
Fender has come out with a few "Tone Master" amps that have been reviewed to sound almost exactly like the original tube amp. This is a big plus because of the cost, reliability and weight of a tube amp are defiantly a downside.
I own some of these tube amps, Deluxe Reverb and Princeton Reverb and the GTX 50 modeling sounds almost identical. You have the feel and response of a tube amp at a fraction of the cost and it uses less power and weighs about half as much. The controls are set up perfectly and it is super intuitive to use. The only complaint I have is that the Bluetooth connectivity is poor, yes you can connect if you jump through a few hoops and the Tone app is well worth connecting to (just kills some of the inspiration getting there).I have set up and saved my preferred amp settings so I don't really need the app now. The effects are very usable and can be switched on and of easily. If you are a newbie this is a great amp with plenty of inspiring tones and effects, just don't get sidetracked with all the bells and whistles. If you have been playing a while but tired of lugging a heavy amp and replacing tubes this is a good place to jump off and get something from this century! I'm a converted Tube Snob.

title: It's Good For What It Is
comment:
I'll start off by saying that this is a solid little tuner. For the price, you can't beat what you're getting - a straightforward compact pedal tuner with an easy to read display and solid build construction. The on/off switch has a very satisfying metallic click, and the input and output hold the cables very snugly.

As I said, it has a solid build construction. If you're a bedroom shredder, then this thing should last forever. If you're a regular gigger though, I'd recommend a more serious pedal choice. This thing would probably only hold up to a handful of years of gigging.

My main gripe with this pedal is drop tuning. It has the nice feature of changing your reference pitch, which I applaud in such an inexpensive pedal. However, this little sucker doesn't like to pick up anything accurately below Drop C on the 6th string on a guitar. I couldn't tell you how it performs with a bass.

If you're staying close to standard tunings and play guitar at home as a hobby - this is your go to pedal. If you want something roadworthy, it'll work, but I'd recommend something with a bigger price tag.

title: Suggestions for improving your Aklot 8 string ukulele
comment:
According to the description, the nut and saddle are "real bone". The nut and saddle are actually just plastic parts. It's not an issue, but a clarification.

I have two suggestions for owners of the Aklot 8 string tenor ukulele.

The high C string is prone to breaking where it contacts the bridge's saddle. One way to address this problem is to protect the high C string by sheathing it in a short length of thin wire insulation where it contacts the bridge's saddle. Simply find a short length of thin gauge insulated wire, and slide about 3/4" insulation off of it. Then unravel the high C string at the tuning peg, and thread the C string through the wire insulation, and slide the insulation down over the saddle at the bridge. The small section of insulation will not alter string intonation, and will not mute the string either. It will prevent the high C string from breaking at the saddle. Please refer to the picture I uploaded for clarification.

The strings are tied together as pairs under the bridge at the factory. This is an effective way to secure the string courses, but it complicates single string replacement. An alternative method is to thread a small plastic bead onto the individual string ends, to secure them under the bridge. Suitable beads are readily available at any place that sells craft supplies. Please refer to the picture I uploaded for clarification.

An explanation for nerds:
The high C string breaks at the saddle because the string's shear strength is lower than it's tensile strength. The high C string can handle the tension required to bring it up to pitch. That's it's tensile strength. It's not being pulled apart by being tuned to pitch. What it can't handle, is the shear force at the saddle. The shear force is like a knife slicing across the string, cutting it into two pieces.

title: Really hard to beat for the money... Great value.
comment:
First let me list some GREAT things about this guitar:

-The finish is awesome. The "Tidepool" is a metal flake blue finish which is actually quite impressive at this price point.
-The pickups are surprisingly decent.
-The fretboard feels nice.
-The neck shape and profile feels good.
-The overall build quality is really solid and more than adequate for a guitar at this price point.

The negative(s), really only one and the other points are nit-picky at this price point:
-I think the tuners are a missed opportunity here. They feel like the cheapest component on the instrument. Of the 6, 3 of mine are VERY gritty feeling and jump around a lot when tuning. They hold MOSTLY stable once tuned, but when playing aggressive or using the tremolo arm more than for just flutters, I suspect the tuners are my weak point in stability. With tuner upgrades this guitar would be great.
-A few of my fret ends could have had a little attention given to them, but overall really not that bad.
-The nut slots seem like they could use a little more polishing, the cut is good, but I bet some polishing with a fret file or ultra fine sand paper and graphite lube would help with tuning and stability.
- One of my other VERY MINOR gripes are preferences, but a beginner may not know how to fix. The tremolo was basically anchored all the way down, but was an easy fix.

title: Did not work for binding pegs as advertised.
comment:
I have been struggling with binding pegs on a cello. I have bought a plethora of products and tried a myriad of solutions, to no sustained success. When I read the amazing description of Hidersine's Peg Paste, and its claim to be able to stop slippage or binding, I was literally grasping at straws, but also curious as to what sort of compound could perform two opposite functions.
I got my answer the day the package arrived. It would not stop binding, at least on the cello I had. I did as the instructions said and applied as they recommended. Nothing. I worked with this compound for several days, and it was useless for my binding pegs.
I can't tell you how disappointed I was. Maybe it works well for others, but it was a clear waste of money for me.

title: Worked better than expected with one problem
comment: I bought these for a show with difficult cable runs. They worked fantastic with one problem and a caveat. The problem was that the transmitter battery was defective so had to be plugged in all the time. I did not have time to return it so now I’m stuck with it. The batteries on the other units lasted an entire show... I would say a minimum of 2 hours on standby and 2 hours of heavy use. The caveat: unless you have a dmx splitter the transmitter has to be at the terminus of the dmx chain. If you are only using these wireless devices then you are fine, just connect it to your pc or dmx controller and away you go. But if you are mixing wired and wireless components you need to have a free dmx out to plug the transmitter into. Our last light in the chain was in conveniently located in the rafters so I ran a dmx cable from it down the side of the theatre to near a power outlet where I could plug in the dmx transmitter. I’ll use them again. There was no control lag, which was my biggest fear.

I did make a few minor modifications. The intonation was off at the 12th fret, plus the action was quite high. I just sanded down the bridge a little and moved the bridge back a bit to fix the intonation. Now the action is nice and low and it no longer hurts my fingers to play for extended periods of time.

Also, I agree with everyone else that the strings that came on this are absolute crap. I replaced with some Elixer strings and couldn't be happier. I'm giving this 5-stars because at this price it is amazing to get a decent musical instrument.

title: Good Sturdy Case, But there are Issues
comment:
First, let me say that I like this case. It is very sturdy and will protect my Tenor Kanile very well. In fact, it fits perfectly in this case.
Like others have noted, when I opened the case for the first time I was assaulted by the strong chemical smell from the glue. Mostly because the glue hasn't totally set up and the foam. I'll air it out before putting my uke in it.
The case was hastily made and there were areas of uncovered glue on the interior sides. Some of which were where the plush padding was fitted too low. I think rubber cement thinner or Goof Off will remove the glue. I am not a big fan of plush.It hides too many flaws and this case is no exception.
The biggest problem is with the fit of the center storage compartment/neck support. The foam sides weren't seated properly they are about a half inch too high. While the inner piece that makes a lip for the top of the compartment is higher still. Resulting in the lid sitting too high when closed. So the rivet holding the pull tab to open the lid may dig into the neck of the ukulele. Fortunately, the neck of my Kanile is thin so I can easily run the strap underneath the neck to protect it. I'd be concerned about my Pono's thicker neck.
The case came with TWO padded straps that attach to a single ring on the back, and a ring on each side to form an inverted "V" backpack-like configuration. Handy if you also have to hump some music, a stand and a chair for a gig.
I have read a couple of comments about the five closure latches that hold the case shut are a pain. I think they are easier and quicker than a zipper that runs around a case. All of mine are tight. The lid's fit is fine.
There is also a separate strip of plush foam to put under the bottom of your uke if it's short. The Kanile doesn't need it.
All in all, a fine case. The shell is excellent. I'd give it 4 stars because of the poor fitting of the interior foam pieces and having to clean off exposed glue.
UPDATE: It has been three weeks since I've been airing out the case and it still stinks. I put my ukulele in it for a day and my uke now smells like chemicals! Not good. I understand that out-gassing is an issue, but it should not be this bad. Downgrade to 4-stars.

title: Worth The Splurg!
comment:
I have the AT-120XUSB turntable and, like many, I purchased the VM95ML cartridge as an upgrade to the original VM95E which sounded great, however, I felt that if there was a possibility of an improved sound over the E, then I for sure wanted that and so I went for it. It all came factory calibrated so there were no set up issues.
After a month of playing, I feel like the VM95ML has broken in pretty good and I would say that for me, the improvement in sound over the VM95E is probably about 10%. But it’s a huge 10%...Of course I was after more sound separation and I got that, however, I never realized how much 3-D sound I was missing, my Polk monitor 40 speakers are pretty deep front to back and with this ML cartridge, the sound is more 3 dimensional and the soundstage is indeed wider. Another considerable payoff in quality is that the previous VM95E had some issues with inner grove distortion and with the VM95ML, this problem is now non-existent.
I chose the headshell/cartridge combo for the convenience of easy switch, the VM95ML is an investment that you don’t want to risk by having to be switching out the stylus alone.
I will say, if you have, and are currently using the VM95E, you’re fine, the VM95E Elliptical ROCKS, especially if you’re decompressing on your metal classics and for the times when there may be a lot of company around. I will keep my VM95E and use for casual listening, however, if you want to impress people with the results of the hard work (and money) that you’ve put into your system and or you’re wanting that moment of solitude to where you’re breaking out the Floyd or like Alan Parson’s Project etc., then you want the experience to go to 11 and you have to have the VM95ML in your private stock.

title: These strings give me a nice, bright tone and stay in tune all night.
comment:
My main guitar is a Taylor 210e and I have tried different types of strings but I always come back to the Martin Lifespan SP. Other strings just seem dull, easily go out of tune, and it's like strumming spagetti after a few nights of playing. Martin SPs always stay stiff and bright. Now the first time that I used Martin Lifespan SPs were the mediums. These were a bit rough on my fingers but I fought the pain and roughed it out but being a life-long bassist, it wasn't too difficult to get used to these strings but my guitar repairman at Guitar Center told me not to use mediums on my Taylor and he recommended the lights and I agree after switching. The lights are much nicer on my fingers, brighter, easier to play all night, great tone, and stay in tune.
The songs that I play are a mix from the 60s thru the 90s such as The Beatles, America, Jude Cole, Dan Fogleberg, Audioslave, Frampton, Jonathon Edwards, The Hollies, Incubus, The Zombies, Bryan Adams, etc. These strings give me a nice, bright tone and stay in tune all night.

title: Great for heavy capo use
comment:
I love this capo.

I'm not typically a fan of capos. I've played bar chords for years, so it really wasn't necessary, until I decided to learn some Simon and Garfunkel. I was used to the clamp version (or the strap version) and didn't like either design for the obvious reasons - intonation, string bending, the clamp getting in the way, etc.

Someone introduced me to this style, and it made all the difference for me. The style does take a little getting used to, and I wouldn't recommend it for quick changes, but it is a definite improvement.

As for this particular capo: The capo is well constructed and has a plastic cover. It does stow above the nut, if you're not using it. The screw works fine and the clamp part tightens easily. (As a side note - you don't need to tighten it as much as you may think you do).

title: Good speakers when the hum is gone
comment: Figured out the hum -- the power transformer is located directly behind the woofer, and is inductively coupling 60 hz (or 50 hz) line frequency, into the speaker.  There is plenty of space available higher in the cabinet.  So with some work (not the easiest thing to do), I relocated the power transformer higher in the cabinet away from the woofer, and the hum is now gone.  The speakers sound good without the hum (taking into consideration they are 4 inch woofers, so no thunderous bass).  I would give them 4 stars if the hum wasn't there in the first place.  Why did Beheringer not locate the power transformer away from the woofer?  Saving a few cents on wiring?  Regardless, a good (smaller size) speaker when the hum is eliminated.

title: Solid microphone, great deal for the price
comment:
We needed a matched pair of cardioid pattern microphones with desk stands and shock mounts for our weekly video podcast show, Camera Chat. All of the reviews said we needed to spend about $200 each for the quality we need, but we decided to take a chance and spend way less than half that for a pair. Worst case, we figured we could send them back and start over.

We are NOT sending them back!! These microphones performed far above our expectations and ended up being just perfect for the weekly video show. Sure, the rear and side rejection is not as good as a $400 microphone, but they are quiet and clear and crisp with a decent cardioid pattern. Certainly as good as many $200 competitors that don't come with a desk stand, pop filter, shock mount, or aluminum clad carrying case. And the built-in A/D converter to USB works quite well too.

But the best part of the deal way MAONO customer service. One of our two carry cases had a crack in the aluminum side plate from rough handling in shipping. One email the customer service and they made it RIGHT. Nearly unbelievable level of support considering the low price. I would definitely order again.

title: Worth the buy; but there are better options if you can afford it
comment: For the money I'd say this is a great product. I just received it today and have already shot a training exercise demonstration with colleagues and it worked well. Of course, going through the video in editing I am noticing a lot of finicky behavior with how to properly avoid white noise and sound pops, distortion for too high decibels, and lack of audio for lower decibels. Whereas the shooting we were doing was for a corporate showing, I definitely would have chosen a better option if I needed to. I was originally skeptical about this product due to its seemingly ease-of-use and low-budget criteria, but hoped for the best. It sure did deliver with decently quality audio and only minimal amount of sound errors that needed to be corrected. I would say that this would be a good product for a small business owner or maybe a Youtube creator, social media influencer, or outright entrepreneur interested in video communications. It has splitters available for use with cell phones and digital cameras. It can be used in congruence with headphones and also has a 3.5-7mm male-in-male conversion. The distance we used the equipment for worked well around a distance of 15-20 feet. We did not try it further than that so it would be inaccurate to confirm whether or not it could reach beyond that; but I wouldn't be surprised if it could. Be aware though that if you have the money to invest and aren't in a quick-decision type of mindset; then look around and maybe purchase a higher quality product which will likely cost a considerable amount more. But any investment in your own future is the best kind of investment, and pays dividends.

The quality of them feels lightweight and not incredible (which for the price you can't expect top notch). The microphones seem to cut in and out on mine sometimes, it's hit and miss. I'm not sure why. Maybe it's just mine. I just tried using then again and they didn't cut out too much this time, other times I'll test it by talking and it'll randomly cut out for not even a second, but enough to hear it take out part of your word.
I would recommend giving this a try and seeing if it works good for you, if you're looking for better quality, you can always invest a little extra $ and maybe find something better. For the price this isn't too bad.

title: A Good Value Beginners Double Bass Pedal
comment:
This a decent pedal set for the price. That being said, I would point out that in my opinion this is more fit for practicing or for beginners. The right pedal seems to have good response, the left sometimes does not seem to respond as well for quick hits. I have not tried tightening the springs yet, which may help this issue. Be sure to tighten the Allen screw well so the beater does not come loose after playing for awhile.

I will say that I am not regular drummer so I do not have a lot to compare with. The build quality seems to be decent for the price. It has a double chain and so far it feels fairly solid when playing. That being said, again this set in my opinion would not be for a touring drummer or anyone who plays out regularly. It would be good for home practice, for beginners or for a backup set of pedals. For the price it is better than I expected and will work well for my use. Just keep in mind how much other pedals go for to put things into perspective on what your expectations should be. The kit also comes with sticks.

comment:
Not long ago, my venerable Yamaha synthesizer was finally wearing out after many years of faithful service. I needed to find a portable keyboard of the same octave range that had a lot of features for not too much money -- and that had a piano touch rather than an organ touch (like my old keyboard did).

Leave it to Yamaha to live up to its reputation. This keyboard is simply incredible for the retail price (let alone as reduced at the time of sale). It has more bells and whistles (rhythms, instruments, and much more) than I will ever likely use. It can be used profitably by a whole range of musicians, from the beginner to the professional -- and Yamaha's online support (including MIDI tracks, tutorials and much more) is simply top-notch. Do be sure to get a piano pedal with this, though -- you'll need it for sustain effects (whereas my old keyboard simply had a button for those).

I was surprised to get a large folding stand along with the keyboard, as the stand is not described as being part of the package. It's probably a good thing, as this keyboard is sustantially heavier and wider than my old keyboard.

title: Great audio, decent as a camcorder, and awful as a webcam
comment:
So, as everyone else has said, the audio is amazing. You can get X/Y, stereo shotgun, mono shotgun, etc AND it still has 2 XLR inputs for your good stuff.

If you record your video, it's a really wide angle shot that's not completely sharp, but it is more than passable. It's oversampled at 3M and can be easily converted down to 1080P. The wide is very nice for recording and it has decent zoom settings that basically bring in the angle. Assuming you edit your video down to 1080p, it seems to still have more than enough resolution.

Now for the BIG downside. We tried to use it as a webcam. Used OBS and Google Meet and the video is awful. Like 2004 Real levels of awful. It looks like it's 640x480 at best even when set to 720p. And while doing all of that it's still super grainy.

Note: The file splitting at 2GB is a FAT32 limitation that anything that does FAT32 will have.

title: The Uncrowned Emperor of the World of Amp Modelling
comment:
I've used this unit since 2005 when I purchased it used off ebay for around seventy seven dollars.

I've played this unit over hundreds of shows across the Indian subcontinent which is possibly the most challenging place on earth that technology can go through given its massive temperature and humidity variations. It's performed admirably. Not bad for a used item.

I'm not going to list out its features because you can see those from the manual. What I'll talk about it why I like it.

Sound wise: I've used many other modelling units and VSTs, IMHO none of them come close to the "organicness" of the Vamp. Thats a fact. The problem is most people listen to the presets and judge the unit based on that.

What sets processors apart from amps in the real world is that amps with their analog circuitry give a lot of clean volume gain to the input signal. THAT makes a massive difference in tone and provides that tubey crunch and sag that occur in later stages inside an amplifier. For example, many Marshall amps like the JCM800 have a preamp gain knob. So when if your modeller has modeled the basic tone and response of an amp, you'll still need "functional" extensions like a line boost. I noticed this when I used my boss multi effects unit to clean boost my signal going into the vamp2. It made it sound much thicker and fuller. Just a tip. Get yourself any half decent overdrive pedal or boost pedal. Even an EQ pedal will do. Any one. and use it as a gain boost in front of whatever VAMP model you're using.

Some amp models on the vamp are so good you wont get the same sound anywhere else. The custom hi-gain is one. It's brilliant and unique sounding.

To cut a long story short, if you record with this unit, noone will be able to tell it was a processor.

I recently purchased a Digitech RP255 and although it's a high quality device with many features like looping and USB audio, its DOES NOT sound as natural as the vamp.

I think I play best using the VAMP. That sums it up for me.

I'm youtube: arjunkaul for my music and auraljunk for my gear demos.

Peace

This is one of those things that simply brightens the room, encourages the soul, and lifts the spirits of everyone.

There are more expensive "tongue drums" available, but these are of a quality and durability that surpasses most other choices within the same price point. Why pay more?

The instructions and method are easy to learn, and encourage exploration without discord.

We have given several of these as gifts and literally everyone has enjoyed playing them, musical or not, young or old. This makes just the best gift!

We plan to add a larger drum, with more keys, to our own home's instrument collection, and will then enjoy playing with the neighbors, and all of our grandchildren!

title: Like a bad relationship...weak and clingy
comment:
I screwed this boom to a shelf. So far so good. Then, I attached my condenser mic to the boom. Since the mic and boom are of the same brand I assumed this meant it would have been tested at the factory and be a perfect fit. However, things went south pretty fast after that. Once the mic was attached to the boom I noticed that, either my mic was too heavy or the boom was too weak. The combination just wasn't a good fit. So I began to release my mic from the boom at which time I realized the biggest problem with this boom. The connection that affixes the mic to the boom wouldn't let go. The release button was defective. Of course it connected just fine, never giving me a reason to think that I had just made this biggest mistake of my life. I had to use extreme force on the button and my mic to extract it. I haven't read all the reviews and maybe others are reporting this same problem. This might have been just a single defective unit. I hope the company destroys this unit when they receive my return. I wouldn't want anyone else getting this one.

I returned this mic boom after one use. There are so many problems with this mic boom that I suggest finding a different solution. Problem 1: after clamping to several different tables, shelves, etc, I never felt confident that this boom had a solid hold and wouldn't crash to the floor, destroying my mic.

title: Big sound for small budget:  My fun with the HDE pickup, by "drhanzonscience"
comment:
I am on a very low budget and I rarely play in public, so I'm just an old (76) Cajun having fun and writing some old-man songs. I purchased my first two real solid-body electric guitars withing the past six weeks, plus a really nice (old) acoustic-electric. I've relied on a couple of in-hole pickups for years, even when I was playing solo in Army Clubs in Germany in the 1970s, trying to be the next Kristofferson (I did not make it). I gave my daughter's friend one of my in-hole pickups, and purchased the Woodie Seymour Duncan from Amazon for my 12-string Jasmine. I still have other flat-tops awaiting amplification. Then suddenly, I found myself with a new Rogue resonator acoustic guitar. I ordered the HDE® Guitar Pickup Acoustic / Electric Transducer and used clear tape to attach it just below the neck. Sounded great! Next I used some crazy glue to hold it in place,but the whole deal was clumsy so I removed the pickup and set it aside. After two weeks, I moved the Woodie to my Sea Gull and slipped the HDE into my 12 string Fantastic! A luthier invited me to bring the Rogue and the HDE by and he'd see what could be done, but I'm so happy with the HDE on my 12-string that I'm not going to do it unless I find the $20+ to purchase a second HDE
for the Rogue. Meanwhile, I recommend interested people who don't have all kinds of sophisticated needs to take the plunge and try the HDE or a similar piezo-electric pickup on their acoustical instrument. You are missing out if you don'!
"drhanzonscience"

title: This is a great add-on tool for dual keyboard musicians!!
comment: This 2nd Tier is a great unit for mounting a second keyboard above a first one on the X style keyboard stands with the proper diameter tubing sized to accommodate this 2nd Tier.  If you adjust it correctly, there is room between the lower and the upper keyboard for lighting and playing.  This is a must for practicing or gigging musicians.  The 2nd Tier is packaged in a box that you wouldn't believe can house it.  Once you open the box, you can see that the unit has a telescoping back strut that is the main strength of this unit.  You are also able to adjust the slant on the upper keyboard to ease in playing.  Even though,  I have one keyboard higher, I can still play both of these using different hands comfortable.  If you have a music staff/stand, you probably have to use it on the top keyboard, as there is room to play the lower, but not enough room to place the music book. It has to go on the top tier or make some other arrangement.  This is a great unit for the price and allows you to place two keyboard in the single footprint.

I have music stands at my school with similar clamping hardware that students have destroyed by over-tightening.

When I went to set it up the telescoping part of the stand was was difficult to expand even when the adjustment knob was all the way loose. As I pulled and pulled at it, the entire clamping assembly came disconnected from the lower tube. I was finally able to get it to telescope out with some coaxing and twisting, but by then I had made my decision not to purchase this stand.

Highlights for me are:  very adjustable and usable effects, lots of inputs, two aux channels in addition to effects (one is monitor), nice nine band eq, monitor send on a fader, 6 channel inserts that can be used to send to audio interface and a built in compressor that really fattens up the vocals.  The channel eqs are also really good with a musical mid sweep.  It doesn't feel as metal and solid as a mackie and probably won't last as long but at this price its worth it if it lasts a couple of years which I am guessing it will.  Also has lots of outputs and the karaoke voice canceler works really well.  The usb audio signal isn't all that adjustable (you have to have a lot of signal in main fader) but it also works pretty well as long as your signals are up loud.

I am not 100% sure that these strings effected the tone of my bass but they do sound great.  I would have to do a blind taste test to between these and the steel Slinkys to know for sure.  I do remember thinking that they sounded better/fuller with more sustain when I first put them on but thats been awhile and I have basically gotten used to thier sound as my new norm so I dont feel comfortable commenting on thier specific sound charactistics at this point.  But I will say this, they feel better on my fingers then anything else I have ever used.  I can only discribe the feel as soft and flexable.  I love the feel so much that I will probably never even try another string set.  I use the Dunlap String conditioner cleaner [[ASIN:B0002NIRIC Jim Dunlop 6582 Dunlop Form 65 String Care]] on my strings almost every time I play (also a killer product).  Having said that the stings seem to hold up for ever.  I have never seen or felt any visible corrosion on these strings.  I love these strings.  Try a set out.  I dont think you will be disappointed. They feel great on my fingers.  I prefer to buy cheeper strings and change often.  For the money these are killer strings.

title: Great!
comment:
This was an excellent purchase and I am thrilled with its performance in every way. Though there are higher quality units out there with better transformers and LEDs and such, this is not something I need at my level of playing. If you are playing large venues for highly discerning ears, then consider those. However, if you are wanting an ABY with good options and excellent sound quality compared to other units doing the same thing, then this is your unit.

Personally, I use this for two things. First, this is absolutely ideal for jumping the channels on my '68 Fender Bassman. It gives me the option of the normal (cleaner) or bass (more driven) channel OR both of them together (my favorite). Second, I use this to be able to use my Fender Deluxe Reverb in partnership with my Bassman. I set the DRRI for amazing clean and the Bassman for nice drive and then have the option to use them together.

Personally, I do not need the LEDs. I have trouble understanding how you would not be able to hear what setting you are on. If your sounds are that close that you don't know where you are, you might not need to be using two amps or channels. I actually like that you don't need to dink around with batteries for this unit.

Bottom line: sturdy, flexible, does it's job, and much better than others I have used previously.

title: I get an amazing sound through this for my live performances and it ...
comment: I purchased this for use during live performances. I play electric violin. I plug the transmitter into my Yamaha YEV 5-String Electric Violin, and the receiver into my multi effects pedal. From there I go via 1/4 inch cable into my Behringer eq, then my signal attenuation pedal, finally into my Orange amp head. I get an amazing sound through this for my live performances and it is virtually indistinguishable from a 1/4 inch signal. One thing I noticed is that it is subject to certain frequency interference. I think that this thing may mess with bluetooth connections in very close proximity a bit. I haven't really confirmed this, so it may be something else that was responsible when I experienced this. Just something to be aware of. Overall I highly recommend this if you want to free yourself from being cabled down during live performances while still sounding great.

title: Truly EXCELLENT keyboard controller.
comment:
I know that this controller is discontinued but - IF you can find a used one, in good shape - you just might want to look into buying it.
I had been in the market for availing myself of a decent controller, for the longest time, after having had to part with my Yamaha Motif XF6. I had purchased a few different models , from manufacturers of the current offerings of keyboard controllers - took them home and played with them for a few days, only to find myself not completely satisfied. I had thought that it was going to be a failed exercise - until I happened across a used controller at the local GC. The build quality on this M-Audio Axiom Pro 49 was the first thing that interested me. Then, there were the many rotary knobs, buttons, screen and sliders which denoted great functionality. I laid a deposit on it, as it had to sit in the store for the balance of two weeks, in await for police clearance. It DID clear, and I finished off the $160 cost. During the wait, however, I did as much research as I possibly could. The only negative that I could find was the fact that it’s HyperControl feature didn’t work with Windows10. I didn’t let that sway me, though, because everything else about it was more than enough to keep me interested in integrating with it.

This thing is built like a tank. It is impressive looking, and it’s key-bed is just (in MY opinion) simply amazing. M-Audio put out a monster keyboard controller when they put THIS one out. I am not a piano player, but these keys just seem to be the ticket for what I AM able to do. Two of the other 49 key controllers that I had tried offered full integration with Reason, and cost $400, and $500. Both of them were very nice, but I couldn’t gain comfortability with their keys (they were a bit too tight, and their respective black keys were a bit more resistant than the white ones). The Axion Pro’s keys are consistent in their feel, and pressure requirements - across the ENTIRE keybed.

So, if you’re looking for a really GREAT keyboard controller, you (as mentioned above) might want to search for one of these.

title: It's all true!
comment:
Everything you read about these guitars is true. They are very inexpensive, but sound nothing like an inexpensive guitar. Are there some differences in the sound compared to much more expensive instruments? Of course, but you need high tech audio equipment to show the differences. The average listener (even above average listeners, too) will have great difficulty finding anything untoward in the sound of the Firefly. I now have four and I'm just a very average rhythm guitar player!
Now, yes, they will most likely require a setup. The necks on mine are great, but I did adjust the action to my preferences. I also set the intonation - time consuming, as it is with any guitar. I also replaced the tuners on one as well as strings on all. I have no issues with the quality other than a slight finish blem on my red one. Am I complaining? No way in h--l! For the price I paid ($139.91), this is a fantastic guitar. Don't buy on one the "AUCTION" site - you're getting ripped off! Those have been purchased on Amazon and then marked up - WAY up, not counting shipping. Wait the 4 - 6 weeks for the next shipment then buy one. You won't regret it. Sure, there can always be the "lemon", but I'm 4 for 4.

title: An IMPRESSIVE value at under $200 for professional headphones; as awesome as my other beyer models.
comment:
The beyerdynamic DT-990-Pro-250 have great, authoritative bass. The mids are not recessed, but they are flat, not forward. Treble-wise, they can get very sparkly and can be grating in the highs unless a good DAC/amp is used. Music from talented producers and engineers that aim for maximum sound quality SHINE through these, and will sound pleasant.

These are tool headphones aimed for work, not for play. Does not mean you will not like them to enjoy music with, but they are obviously voiced for music production, not music consumption.

They are treble canons because beyerdynamic designed them as such for their purpose, which is mastering duties. You need to monitor with something brighter than the material you're working with to be able to hear every single little thing, good and bad. If the treble is good on these, it will be good on everything else.

But my dude/dudette: DON'T KNOCK THEM UNTIL YOU TRY THEM. I know people write all kinds of reviews online, and Internet forums are echo chambers and parrot cages, but IT'S YOUR EARS. Use them first before reaching conclusions. Just because an Internet stranger says they're ice picks to the ear drums doesn't mean you won't enjoy them.

I don't find their sound quality inferior to other beyer headphones I own, such as the Amiron home, T90 Chrome, T90 Jubilee, and DT 880 Edition (600 ohm). The DT 990 obviously sound different, but are just as good. I promise you!

They just present more bass and treble than the others, and have a different way of imaging, but are just as revealing. I don't take these off and wear the others and go, "Wow, I hear more details now." No matter what DAC and amp I use, the DT 990 keep up with the others. Just because they're been around the block for decades doesn't mean they're less good than the newer ones.

People online love to mystify headphones that are newer or cost more, and attribute them qualities that they do not possess. These are AMAZING headphones for under $200. SERIOUSLY. You won't hear more details with the T90 or the Amiron home or any other higher-priced headphones. It's just the truth. Their value is higher than a lot of pricier headphones.

As long as you amplify them up properly, you will be impressed by their detail retrieval, sound-stage and imaging.
The bass is fast and tight, the mids are smooth and crystal-clear, and the treble is purposely revealing beyond necessity...yes...way too sharp in some recordings. Again, SOME recordings. The imaging is like listening to speakers; sounds come from outside the ears, not from inside the head. All elements are clearly positioned and layered.

When I really want to crank them up good without being bothered by the treble of some tracks, I plug them to my Schiit Asgard 2 and that amp somehow transforms these DT 990 into something else. It makes them even more revealing and bigger-sounding while smoothing them up. All the detail is there without the cringe. Potentially harsh treble is the only con of these cans, and they were designed that way ON PURPOSE.

Think about it: for less than $200, you're getting a professional headphone hand-made in Germany. We live in good times for sure! Don't let elitist snobs from Internet forums discourage you from getting something that you might like. IT'S YOUR EARS. You can read reviews, but don't let them make decisions for you. You could spend more, but don't think you will get more.

God bless you! Thanks for reading. Peace!

EDIT: [6-June-2020]
Still loving them. They still look brand new and sound brand new. My Tascam UH-7000 makes them sound amazing. If you want a taste of high-end audio, you can't go wrong with these. Do not pay much mind to reviewers or forum members with overactive imaginations that believe the higher the price of headphones, the more they "hear".

It's just not going to happen, especially if you lack trained ears. The difference will be in subjective sound presentation, not in objective sound quality. I'm trying to save you time, money and avoid you a serious case of audiophilia nervosa. Peace!

If you are on a budget or if you just want to get a great sounding mic at a great price, then buy one (or more) of these.  I have a bunch of mics (condenser and dynamic / expensive and cheap) and I was really surprised by this cheap little condenser mic.  It sounds better than mics that are much more expensive. I would not hesitate purchasing more of these microphones.  It's a little over $20 and sounds equal to (if not better than) the AT mics that I have.  I realize it's an individual preference but I really like the sound of this mic.  This thing is loud too ... but very crisp and clean.  I purchased the BM-800 too but I like this one much better.

title: Great, Great IEM system
comment:
First let me start by saying I have a PTM 10 iem system and love it!! I received the PTM 22 yesterday. Came boxed very well. I opened the box and put the rack ears on right away as I had a gig coming up in a few hours. What better way to test a new system! I really like the simplicity of the system. The only controls are on the front of the unit. You can change group and channel numbers and then sync to the receiver pack. There's no compression or eq to mess with, and let's face it, in the world of digital mixers you can do that from there. The body packs are plastic, but feel really good. They are easy to read and the lights stay on the entire time, I really like that. On the back of the transmitter are 2, 1/4 inch inputs and outputs. This is a mono unit, but you can have a second input from your own instrument or voice and be able to control it from whatever your input is for that instrument.

Now about the sound, it's crystal clear!!! I mean very very clean. There's a very small amount of white noise if you crank up the volume, but you have to really have it wide open. The much more expensive units have the same noise!! I had a sub singer with our band last night, who had just had a $1000 brand name IEM the week before, and couldn't believe the sound of the PTM 22!! She said there wasn't a difference. I had the other pac on last night and was just floored at how great the 22 sounds!! We were able to go out in the crowd and not lose signal once. Very happy about that. There wasn't any interference at all, just clean sound. I use 1.5v rechargeable batteries, they were still showing full charge at the end of the night.

I am using different in ear monitors than came with the unit. I haven't tried them yet to be honest. I use the KZ JAX found here on Amazon. 8 drivers in each ear and what I get to hear is just amazing!!

The PTM 11 and 22 is a wonderful IEM system. A very simple system to operate(I love that), with clear full sound. You just can't go wrong with this system or the PTM 10(full stereo, see my video on that page). I will be adding a video to this review very soon and will show how to operate the system, as well as showing it being used. You gotta get this!!!!

title: Hours of fun for toddler
comment:
Edit update:
I'm sorry I had to take away a star. We are not sure what happened but the drum stopped working properly after only a week or so of use. Only one of the drum pads work now. My son played with this daily and moved it around with him from room to room. I feel like if it is marketed for kids and is portable it should be built to last. How unfortunate, my son really liked this item.

I ordered this for my 4 y/o son who loves to play drums. He has a real set but it gets loud so I thought this would be a neat and quieter alternative.
He took to it immediately after we gave him a brief tutorial. It still can get a little loud because you can obviously still hear him hitting the pad but with the headphones we are no longer exposed to the sound. The drawback of the headphones is that the cord interferes with his playing.
He figured out the record feature by himself and loves to record and play it back for us. We look forward to connecting to his favorite songs for him to play along with.
The pedals don’t work with the headphones plugged in but he didn’t seem to mind not using them. My husband (a musician) says the quality of the pedals are equivalent to lower end sustain pedals for reference.
I think this is a great gift for toddlers and older kids. It’s a toy so don’t expect high quality/authentic drum sounds but they are good enough for practice.
My son told his little brother he could play with his real drum set after playing with this and that never happens. My son plays with this for hours on end. Definitely a worthy buy.

title: It's amazing.

If you are willing to invest time and money into a great guitar, it's worth the time, money and effort to get your tone right.  Don't be lazy about your tone.  You bought the guitar for what it sounds like...  Give it the respect of reproducing it's actual tone!  If you bought a cheap guitar, then don't even think about buying this.  Cheap sounds cheap.  If you invested in a great instrument, then this is it.  Put in the time and effort to reproduce you actual guitar and not just a little metal strip under the saddle. title: Misses the Mark
comment:
I had high hopes for this setup, especially for the software which promised ease of use for my many VST synths. If your workflow revolves around Native Instrument products, you're in luck. It worked pretty much flawlessly with my NI plugins. Other vendors plugins sort of worked (e.g., Korg WaveStation Legacy plugin), where the screen on the keyboard was useless but you could use the Korg interface from within the Akai software to select and modify patches. This is sort of useless, since the point is to use the board's interface. Then there were some plugins that simply don't load, some load in the 32 bit version of the program but not in the 64 bit version and visa-versa (I'm a Windows user). And then there were some that panicked the Akai software. And then there were times when everything just stopped working. The quality of the keyboard, while good, was not worth the price of this package with out a really usable software package (I though the Max49 was a lot better). So, back it goes (thanks Amazon).

Your mileage may vary, especially if you're doing live work and have a NI workflow. I'm just getting really tired of debugging software for manufacturers who promise big but don't invest in test and quality control to back it up. Akai has a great idea here, but it may be a sisyphean task to unify all VST plugins as they claim they do now. And it's going to take a lot more work to live up to that promise.

title: Bargain For Pairing With Orange Micro
comment:
I bought this speaker cab to pair with an Orange Micro Dark.
After reading a number of reviews regarding the 8 inch Orange speakers and the expense of the Orange 12 inch cab I was looking for another option that would be more affordable.

I found this Kustom cab on sale for under $80.
For a cheap cabinet it seems to be built well. It has eight chrome corner protectors. The front cover looks like the tolex on some Fender amp combos. It's just not as shiny.

What matters for me is that it's very affordable and sounds good with the Orange Micro Dark.
One caveat is that the speaker cab does not ship with a connecting cable. It's the cab with speaker only.
In my case I got a cable with the Orange amp head bundle.
I don't know that it's a solution for everybody, but it sure works for the Orange Micro amp head!

title: Zoom boosts their rep.
comment:
Handy.

This is the future. A pocket recorder that will do 24-bit recording as stereo or 16-bit as 4-way. The mics are adequate and the processing speed is suprising. Runs on 2 AA batteries and has line in and out features with soft gain controls. I use this for mastering digitally after mixing down in the analog domain. SD card limit provides hours of recording and is solid for field sampling. Transfer is fast and drivers are easy to use. The only 2 drawbacks are the button/controls/layout of the settings, and the built-in compressor/limiter...this is useless for anything but spoken podcasts....sounds awful with music. Just pre-compress/limit it and you'll be okay. This is still great for the price.

title: Very bright - no remote - music sensitivity had to turn up but looks great
comment:
[[VIDEOID:1c4cab4ce7037a1d7f69ea26ed510b09]] This is a really bright and cool looking laser. I shined mine on a screen that I use to watch movies on and it is big enough from about 25 feet away to cover a 35 foot area including floor and some of the ceiling.

I had to turn the sensitivity on the music up to 9 (I assume that is the highest) to get it to where I want it to look like.

It has an automatic mode, a DMX controller channel mode, and a sound activated mode. Each of them except the DMX has 4 different scenes in them as well. The DMX has the standard several hundred channels.

What I do not like about this is there are really no instructions to even talk about and this is a pretty complex device. I assume they think you really know what you are getting when you get one of these.

You can tailor some of the sub-scenes and set it just like you want it but I do not know how to do that. It works great just on the sound detection setting. I really wish there was a link or somewhere to go to know how to really get into the configuration of this, but I am happy with the way that it looks and crispness of the lasers.

The colors are vibrant and it is just a really well put together laser show that is just fun to watch. I took the music off the video so no one could get me for copyright violation but it is really neat and has a lot more changes than the 20 seconds that I show. I really could not capture the entire scene either with the camera because it is so big.

The main 'screen' area which it goes outside of is a 16 foot by 16 foot area but you cannot tell that from the video.

Overall, this is a great looking laser show and I am glad I ordered it.

title: Drops notes when sequenced - known issue!
comment: I purchased this thinking it would be a handy way to add drum samples and other noises & effects to performances. Yeah....not so much. When it arrived, I quickly hooked up the audio outputs to my interface and began fiddling about. The factory loaded samples aren't great, but they sounded good enough to get a feel for the functionality. So of course I had to plug in a MIDI connection from my Beatstep Pro. I pulled up a project with a straightforward synth part, and quickly tapped in a basic drum pattern. Kick...snare...kick...snare...okay, let me add some hats on the 16ths....wait...what? This thing drops notes like they're going out of style. So off to the search engines I go, only to discover that it's a well documented issue, as long as you know what to look for. The MPX8 apparently chokes on MIDI running status messages, causing it to drop notes randomly and frequently. AKAI knows about this issue, it has been reported to them by users in the past and it seems it would be a simple firmware fix. Some users have worked around the issue with additional hardware or software to filter MIDI data, but if one is to spend more money, there are other options out there that don't demand additional expense or the clutter of additional devices.  In short, if you intend to sequence this, DON'T.

title: Onboard effects, powerful two-channel design with room for improvement
comment:
This LT is Boss's attempt to combine portability and power together and I think they've done an excellent job. It is very affordable at this price considering what it does and unless you are really a pro, you don't need to go for the higher more fancy models.
Setup was pretty easy and straight forward and I liked the green setting the most tbh not the loudest but the best quality in my opinion.
It has 60 watts of power and comes in a compact easy to carry form. The tilt back for factor allows for the sound to be really clear and the lows have some good punch to them.
The biggest plus in my opinion is the bi-amp design. You have one amplifier for the big 6.5inch woofer and another amp for the tweeter. The amps send different levels of power to the speakers which makes it optimized and provides a full range solid response.
The input circuitry for the guitar and mic channels are analog and the channels come with three-band EQs that are independent. There are other nice features such as phase switch, anti-feedback notch filter and an enhance effect for the mic channel. There's also delay/chorus and delay/echo on the mic channel and also independent reverb on both of them.
The back of the device has lots of ports. There's a standard Aux input with level control, XLR DI jack and also USB. USB is specially very good since you can easily connect it to your laptop and record anything you're playing straight out of this device which is amazingly helpful. There are also another two input jacks to control mic and guitar effects with footswitches.
If you know what you are doing, you can activate the enhance function and add a reverb splash for deeper sound but that takes some getting used to and practicing a lot with the device. It took me more than 3 months to figure it out.
Overall, a great amp and I strongly recommend it at this price but if you are a pro and looking for looper or maybe harmony effects you have to pay more and get a higher end model. This model also cannot handle dual DI/Line Outputs. It should be noted that if you need higher wattage the BOSS Acoustic Singer has a 120w model that is almost twice more powerful. Don't recommend it for inside the house though.

Although do note if you have a newer galaxy with a "USB-C" this only comes with a micro USB adapter. I'm sure a converter can be found inexpensively.
Before I even received the mic I got an email from Purple Panda with a quick start guide and reassurance that they were there if any assistance was needed. The mic was EASY to set up and came packaged well. It took me less than 2 mins from first opening the package to completing a successful audio test.
I was very impressed with the quality of the mic. The lav clip is VERY sturdy. This worked great for my project. I was conducting miniature interviews in a crowded space with a LOT of background noise and the interviewee's comments came out clear and easy to understand. I'd highly recommend this mic to anyone starting out or just adding to their collections. For $30 you can't beat it in my opinion.

title: Works Well....Great Price!
comment: This item really works well for my needs.  It is not hard to install if you have a dremmel drill with the right bits.  I used a rotary cutting bit and then a rotary sanding bit with course sandpaper to cut the holes.  Don't make the holes too large or the screws won't have any place to attach.  The only reason I didn't give it 5 stars is because no instructions were included.  However I found some youtube videos to get me started.  The hardest part is filing down the bridge to compensate for the pickup which goes underneath the bridge.  Just place the pickup on the side of the bridge after you remove it and mark how much you have to shave off.  Then I used a small vise that was wider than the bridge to hold it in place exactly on the line that I marked.  Then I used course sandpaper on a dremmel drill to remove the material down to the line.  It turned out perfect.  To play it safe you may want to take off a little less (1/64") and put the strings back and test it out before removing more. It worked out so well I bought another one.  The second time I had the install time down to about 1 hour.  Good luck!

title: Beautiful guitar, set up well, marvelous tone range, great value for the money
comment:
I have been on a tear for buying new guitars lately adding to my arsenal. I own an Epiphone LP, an Oscar Schmidt hollow body, and several acoustics and have had them for going on better than 10 years. Recently I decided to expand my skills in Blues and Jazz genres without breaking the bank. I got a budget Strat (I plan to make quite a few upgrades on this guitar) and a Telecaster style (HH) thinline, but have been itching to get an ES 335 type semi-hollow style. I settled on this one, and I am grateful I did.

First off, it came quickly, especially considering I ordered it the week before Christmas.

The top is beautiful. I cannot really tell whether the top is veneer or a picture and to me it does not matter. I got the purple color and it is outstanding. The head decor (again whether inlay or sticker does not matter to me) is classy and I love the metal truss-rod cover.

The neck, f-holes, and body binding are more of the aged ivory color on my guitar rather than the bright white shown in the pictures on Amazon. I like them better in that color. I did not see any flaws on the binding or nicks anywhere.

The neck is smooth and playable, the fret ends are smooth with no fret sprout or sharp edges. The frets seemed to be polished with no rough edges or grittiness.

The only thing I noticed was the nut ends are sharp, but that can be smoothed down carefully.

I will see whether there are long-term issues with the tuners or nut holding tune but it tuned up nicely and stayed relatively close given new string stretch. The action was nice and low from the nut to the end of the fingerboard without buzz and intonation was right on. Kudos to whomever set this baby up.

The pickup heights were set appropriately, good variation between neck and bridge sounds with tone variations using the pots going from warm to bright appropriately in both positions. I am pleased with the amount of resonance and sustain. I think that is enhanced by the tail mounted bridge.

This guitar is a great value for the money and the gig bag is a nice plus.

title: Quite the deal - looks and feels great
comment:
OK this is the "honeymoon" I put the High Gloss Burgundy bench together last night, sat on it, checked it out.

THE GOOD: Fit, finish, quality of construction is very very good. Surprisingly good!
It's real wood. No trace of MDF (particle board) anywhere.
The seat cushion pad is cushy, more than sufficient comfort.
No squeaking. Sits flat. I feel confident it's not going to break. (I'm a 5ft 11 male, near 200lbs.)
I got the high gloss burgundy wood finish. IT'S GORGEOUS!!! At first when I was pressing order I was worried it would be "too too". It's not too too, it's flat out dead on classy gorgeous. I can tell you I'm so glad I didn't get walnut/brown - that would look dowdy compared to this. Note, while the burgundy is a looker, it's not flamboyant - just on the cool side of tasteful.
It showed up (arrived shipped) to my front porch 5 calendar days after I ordered it.
I had it assembled in 10 minutes. Easy peasy. At first I thought they forgot to send me the legs but they were stored in the bench itself. They give you the allen wrench to attach the legs. Once finished I put the wrench under the lid of the piano bench in one corner.
Very adjustable. I racked it out an additional 1 inch in height and it is very comfortable for my legs / posture.

I'll be using this with a large digital stage piano that is black on a Z type stand. I don't anticipate any "clash".

NOTE: Now if my intent was to pair this bench with a full size "real" wood piano, color choice / integration/ matching would be more of a concern. I just didn't want all black in my music room. YMMV.

THE "BAD" (?): If you are waist / belt size is 38" - 40" , and you sit on this bench relaxed, legs slightly akimo, and you look to either side of you, you'll find you have maybe an 1.5 inch to 2 inches of extra bench leftover / available on either of the outsides of where you sit (i.e. outside your outer thighs on either side). Some part of me longs for this bench to be 2 additional inches longer. NOTE: It's NOT that big a deal. Don't get me wrong, it's just a wish list preference. I can and will use it as it is and happy. WHAT THIS MEANS: There will be ** NO ** side by side duets of any kind on this piano bench - unless the players happened to be 8 years old and anorexic. Just forget about considering this a bench for duets.

IF IT WASN'T MADE CLEAR: The seat material is *** NOT *** any kind of leather. The seat covering is a tasteful looking leatherette of some kind. It appears durable. Time will tell, hence this starting as a 4 star review.

It's not a Jantzen but who cares? For the price it's a steal. They supply substantial lock washers for the leg bolts. I'm guessing that most users of this bench may have a routine where they re-tighten up the legs every 3 - 6 months. So what? No big deal. A big 5 minutes of your time every so often.

SUMMARY: I'm starting this as a 4 star rating, will check back in every few months and if it wears well will raise it to a 5. It probably deserves to be a 5 just being a bit cautious.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

UPDATE: 1/02/2022 I still like this piano bench. This never digs into, or fatigues, my..uh..behind. I play for an hour or so most sessions and I can say that comfort is never the reason I end a playing session. I have no complaints of this bench or the physical support / comfort it provides.

title: A Great tone and maintenance investment!
comment:
I used this product to change my HSS Fender Standard (MIM) Stratocaster over to an SSS Strat. I reused two of the old DiMarzio Area 67 pickups, and added a Virtual Vintage Heavy Blues 2 to the bridge. I had never set one of these up before but installation was easy and straight forward. You may have to lengthen some of the wires in your cavity but again I had issues with techs who had improperly installed previous upgrades and had improperly shortened key wires. (This was not the pickguard's fault.) MAKE SURE you match the wiring codes of your pickups to the Seymour Duncan color codes. I knew DiMarzio and SD use a different color code and adjusted the wiring accordingly saving time & headaches. Installation was easy, and that was with me having vision and fine motor skill issues. I was impressed.

Once I got the BYOP pickguard wired in and the pickup heights readjusted, the improvement in my tone was immediately noticeable, even with my old pickups! The BYOP pickguard uses high quality potentiometers, capacitors, five-way switch, and jack. These "little things" make a huge difference in tone and many people could avoid pickup changes just by upgrading to this harness. Its that good!

This Strat is my #1 guitar and always will be. The BYOP pickguard did three things for it: (1) breathed new life into my Strat by allowing for fairly simple pickup changes (2) freed me from being at the mercy of so called "guitar techs" who do shoddy work (3) allowed the guitar to sound the best it ever has since I bought it!

Given those considerations, the purchase price was very much worth it, and I'll be installing one in any other Strat I buy.

I just wish it came in more pickguard colors. I also wish something like it was available for my Gibson SG!

title: Working Professional Musician - Mixer works great!
comment:
I was looking for compact and cheap. Dont let the low price fool you, this mixer is fully functional. I dont worry about plastic vs metal, etc, I take care of my equipment.
I am a solo acoustic guitarist/vocalist, and have 30 years exp. doing live performance.
I needed the xlr outputs for my powered speakers, and the headphone jack works with my stage monitor. The dap effects could always be better, but they do work. I tested all channels with guitar and microphone; all work great.
Bluetooth was easy, connected my phone and played spotify flawlessly. To record upto 30 seconds, you have to use the rca inputs, but it worked and sounded good. It's compact, light weight, and it works. It's not fancy, name brand, or high end, but it is functional.

A lot of the negative reviews I've seen are from people who don't really understand how a mixer actually works...not all, but a lot. I understand due to the lack of usable instructions. The manual is lacking.

Overall, if you play small gigs, record at home, or stream audio (podcast etc.) This is a great unit to get going. I expect to get a few years out of it. Good luck, amd God Bless!!

I tried sanding one down to a thinner edge and it seems to help, but being wood, you can't make it as thins a plastic pick.

Instead, it is much more attractive and a higher quality than the one pictured above. If you type "940-edread" into a search engine, you can see the picture of what you will actually get......which is an attractive black case with brown trim and gold hardware.
It has a nice, plush, gray lining that looks and feels great. It has five gold latches (the middle one has a key-lock), a large compartment inside for picks & such, and a brown carry handle that matches the trim. My Epiphone Dove fits perfectly snuggly inside.

title: This is an incredible amp for home or garage use
comment: I bought one used, and honestly can't say anything bad about it. other than the slightly confusing learning curve when it comes to getting the most out of it, there's just so many options, and ways to set up your sound, and it's the polar opposite of what I was used to, Marshall, Fender classic style amps, etc. Once I downloaded the PDF I was spending more time practicing than I ever have, I had access to setups that cost thousands to get, admitedly there is a difference when it comes to gigging, I mean, it's a 75w amp, it's loud enough for smal room gigs, or going thru the PA system, but I think it hits it's target market with being that cool amp you keep in your room to jam on. It's pretty heavy, and build better than you'd think, teh feel of of the controls and knobs are absolute, and have a satisfying click to the notched knobs. Can be used for keyboards, vocals, guitar(not sure about bass yet), it's just ...really good, and for the price, I dare you to do better! mine is several years old, and looks brand new, nothing has worn out and I use it daily, and once again..it IS LOUD!!! but it also sound s amazing at low volumes, in fact, probably better, I've turned it up enough times to notice a slight loss of tone at max volume, but how often do you need to shake windows anyway? lol. Great amp, good quality, incredible price for what you can do with it.
