title: Lower profile straplock.  Not toolless.
comment:
This is the second set of strap locks that I've installed on my electrics and anyone can install these locks. You must use the strap posts that are included in this kit.. so yes you need a screwdriver to change these out... Toolless may be possible on some guitars, but I'm just as happy to use the ones designed to be secure. A quick installation and my strap is securely locked.

Unlike push button release strap locks, these have thumbscrews to prevent the strap from releasing -- This makes a much lower profile lock and less torque on the post screw. I find the lower profile a lot more attractive and the color options blend in with my guitar and strap. I would not think twice before getting another set for my other guitars... but first I'll test their posts to ensure I can't just use this strap on all guitars.

title: Great sound, easy to use, no problems.  Don't let the warranty be a consideration in purchase.
comment:
These strings sound great, and were very easy to put on. I am NOT a professional player, I play a bit for fun here and there. For family, too. My daughter is learning to play and she's played on these strings as well. Installation was not a problem as you might expect. I've put on a lot of guitar strings and there were no problems. I had no reason to try EB's warranty claim, which is something I was interested in.

I was concerned about another reviewer's complaints about the warranty policy. Yes, before I choose a product I see if its a decent product to begin with, I don't want to review anything that I'm sure I won't like.

The details on the warranty policy, posted on the EB website, are more or less as stated by another reviewer:
1. 90 days from the date of purchase
2. You have to return the original packaging with it (how many ppl are going to keep that?)
3. They get to decide whats normal wear and tear.
4. Maximum of 3 sets of string per customer per year.
5. You gotta return the whole set.
6. You gotta mail them to EB, and I think they don't reimburse you for that... which is kind of the kicker to me, these are only $15 strings, and between postage ($3?), USPS worthy packaging ($1?), and my time to pack it and get it shipped out at the local parcel plus place I am really not sure I would bother with the warranty even if I had a rightful claim.

Personally, I think their warranty is not really worthwhile, although I'm not angry about it.

Amazon has its own return policy, which may allow you to return bad strings without the hassle or the cost for the first 30 days, and maybe even the first 90 if you buy the strings from another vendor (not amazon) through an A to Z claim.

title: A great ukulele at a great price
comment:
Kala has earned a rep for making good and reliable instruments at modest prices. Even their entry level Makala Dolphins and Sharks with molded plastic back and wooden soundboards are respected. Still for very little more this KA-15S is really the exact same ukulele as the $70 KA-S, but without the white edging/binding or the nickel-silver frets. This is really a visual difference designed to upsell the buyer but little more.

Also better is that both of these are all wood, and are of clearly higher quality than the Dolphins. Remember, when you buy online your ukulele is not going to be "set-up" which means you may get one that has a buzz, or whose action is too high for comfortable playing. A shop set-up avoids that. Thus, you take your chances. Although most Dolphins are fine, a notable number need set-up; however your chances of getting a good one are MUCH better with either the 15-S or the KA-S.

Note too that both the KA0S and 15-S come with expensive Aquila nylgut strings (normally $10 to 12 plus ship). The Dolphin comes with cheap strings that you will soon tire of and be forced to replace, and if you are like most Dolphin owners - with the Aquilas.

Bottom line: for just $10 more than the entry level, plastic backed Dolphin, you are getting what is really a $70 KA-S with much better strings, all wood, and much better sound quality. The 15S we received was acceptable in every regard, no obvious defects, good intonation and proper action - and requiring no set-up.

More: We also have a Ukadelic by Kala, at $48 and equipped similarly with Aquilas, but with the nickel-silver frets of the KA-s. The only difference: the 15S and KA-S are make of mahogany, while the Ukadelic is "either poplar or nato (eastern mahogany)". When compared to our new 15S, the Ukadelic is a bit "brighter", but still very full while the 15S is a bit warmer, a bit more classical sounding, a matter of personal taste. To me the 15S sounds just a bit more refined. A matter of taste and still both are very similar in sound.

Good luck.

title: Fantastic first kit. It will grow with you.
comment:
For the price - the best deal out there! This is a decent kit that comes with everything you need when buying your first drum set. What I like the most about this package is that it comes with all the hardware including pedals. I've upgraded a lot of things on my Roadshow kit, but I've kept the pedals as I'm perfectly happy with them.

About the upgrades: I've replaced the heads all around (they come with clear heads on toms and bass). I'd say this drastically improves the sound, especially when you tune them properly. I also got PureSound Custom Pro snare wires recently and now I'm finally happy with the snare sound (I definitely recommend this upgrade). The bass drum hoop you see on the photos is a Gibraltar maple hoop (more about it below). I'm using my own cymbals here (the stock crash is ok, hi-hats are just bad, frankly). I also mounted a splash stack to the hi-hat stand using a Gibraltar Boom attachment clamp.

The qualifier in front of the very first sentence of this review is important though: I've noticed some surface defects in the interior of the shells. Also, one of the bass drum hoops came in bent into an oval but Amazon quickly resolved that issue. I've contacted the customer service, we looked up a (high-end) replacement hoop that would fit this kit and they sent me a partial refund to order it. Therefore, no stars deducted there.

Overall, this is the perfect first drum set purchase. I'm not playing gigs or doing recording sessions, this is my first kit, a practice kit, so for $350, I couldn't be happier. You can upgrade and modify things as you go depending on your taste and budget.

title: Really, really good for the price, and still good without considering it.
comment:
For the record I have never owned any other mixer above this budget as of writing this, if I get an interface at around the $100 range then I would be more than happy, but this was a pleasant surprise.

Given various YouTube reviews, starting with the build quality, it actually subverted expectations as it doesn't actually feel so cheap as they have over exaggerated it. Even though the casing is plastic, it's very good quality and sturdy, the knobs also feel pretty nice and have a good resistance when tuning them.

The sound quality is quite pleasant, and I recommend this over the UM2 as this has more clarity in important areas whereas that one has a muffled sound. The only disadvantage here I have come across is the Input knob has a very high gain jump from 9 to 10, it seems artificial and does not allow you to increase the volume slightly higher than before that jump.

However the volume is more than adequate and yet I have needed to turn it down further to get the right vocal volume for the music. Overall, highly recommended. Latency is great as well when it comes to the PC portion, I am able to hear both the music and my vocals at the same time in Direct mode, and the end result of the recordings have no discernible latency.

title: Best pre-built boards on the market, hands down
comment:
I own one of these in every size. The middle (or large) size GPB-BAK-1 is perfect for a moderate amount of pedals including a wah and maybe a volume depending on how many pedals you have in your fx chain. The Voodoo Lab Pedal Power 2 Plus fits perfectly in the included bracket, and has a courtesy outlet for powering one additional pedal or item, however this board will fit more than 9 pedals. I've included images showing it comfortably holding between 11 and 13 pedals (forgive the sloppy wiring, i was only testing the chain).

Most people dont run that many pedals (at least not at the same time, nor should you unless everyone of them has true bypass or a buffer), but for those of you like myself who own a rediculous amount of pedals or complex signal chains, constantly experimenting with MORE than 14 peddals at a time or with large fx looper blocks, etc... the extra large board is top notch. There are brackets for two pedal power supplies enough for 16 pedals easy, i've augmented mine to hold an aditional ISO5, for 21 channels of power included the 18v needed for the mxr EQ. The photo included shows just how many will fit on this board with a wah, volume, blackstar ht aaib, and other random stomp boxes layed out for visual representation only. Im still working on a semi permanent solution so i can toggle between boards with what i need for when i need it - as the extra large XBAK-1 is VERY heavy with pedals onboard, and really not very practical for portability, but its PERFECT for guys like me who need a large board as a more of a "laboratory", which can also be portable.

Hope this helps someone, the quality is amazing and included bag/hook and loop is a huge plus. Best pre-built boards on the market.

I have another set that's 2U more that I have network switches, cable management trays, and a 2U drawer. It's a good solution in my case as I don't want to deal with a true rack, but, would like it to look nice and not have things sliding around or otherwise being messy.

Of note: be prepared to round the ends if used in this manor; they can be sharp. Also, don't get crazy with the rack screws right off the bat; sometimes the threads will need to be cleaned a bit and the screws CAN try to cross thread. They generally do a good job with the threads, but the coating that gets put on can make a screw jam or not bite right going in.

title: You get more than you pay for -- this is a real bargain!
comment:
I purchased a this half size violin for my 8-year old daughter who has expressed an interest in the violin but isn't quite ready to start lessons through school. I didn't want to invest in an expensive instrument that might be quickly set aside, but I thought for the cost of one or two lessons (or half of one American Girl doll), I could expose her to the instrument and see if she had any lasting interest.

For the price, I wasn't expecting much. I was a bit apprehensive to open the box, wondering what broken parts I might find inside. But I was pleasantly surprised by the quality of violin I received. And by all the extras -- extra strings and an extra bridge. The violin looked nice, with no obvious problems. And to my surprise, it didn't sound bad at all. The tone was actually clear and somewhat warm. This is definitely not a toy!

On the down side: The case seemed to be cheaply made and a zipper pull was missing -- but not a big deal. And the instrument was very difficult for me to tune. And it doesn't hold its tuning particularly well. The bridge popped out three times when I tried to tune the instrument, which was somewhat startling. But then [[ASIN:B000F3JO7M The Original Peg Drops by Ardsley]], it's been a long time since I tuned a violin. And by the time I got to the G string, the A string was invariably out of tune. But I have never had a brand new instrument before, so I'm guessing that this has something to do with the newness of the instrument. The pegs were also a little slippy at first. Again, I don't really consider this to be a fault of the violin. Just want to share my experiences.

That said, the sound is fine. The bow is fine. The instrument looks great. You're not getting a Stradivarius, of course, and if your child continues with violin you will probably want to upgrade, but for a starter instrument for someone who isn't yet committed, you really can't go wrong for the price. Overall, I'm very, very pleased by this purchase and think it was well worth the money.

UPDATE: Still can't get this violin to stay tuned for ten minutes! Very frustrating to try to tune it.
UPDATE (October 2013): Got some [[ASIN:B000F3JO7M The Original Peg Drops by Ardsley]] and the tuning problem was solved!

title: Nice Mic But Horrible Arm & Pop Filter
comment: This is actually a very nice, well featured mic. Unfortunately, the arm is horrible and so is the pop filter. The arm is really difficult to maneuver because you have to loosen all the joints, move it, and then re-tighten all the joints.  So, it's impossible to just quickly shift the mic a few inches or so when you shift positions in your chair, etc.  The pop filter (especially the arm) does not stay in position and slowly sags/drops to the point that the pop filter arm will eventually hit the mic and then you'll make even worse noise trying to reposition it.  I would recommend the mic (or the same model NW-8000-USB with the 25mm extra large capsule), but you really need a better arm/stand, better pop filter arm, and a shock mount would probably be a good idea, too. I wish Neewer would sell the NW-8000-USB with 25mm capsule either by itself or with a desktop stand, but they don't. I ended up returning this one and just going with the Mercase brand mic with a simple desktop stand instead.

title: Pairs Nicely with a Blues Junior
comment:
This is the only compressor I've ever played through. I expected it to smooth out some of my pick attack. Compressors should boost the quiet playing and even out the harder playing so that each note rings at about the same volume. This pedal does exactly that.The reason I'm writing the review is that when I first got the pedal, I was playing through a Peavey Vypyr 1 amp, then through a Fender Rumble amp, and finally through a Fender Blues Junior III amp. With the first two amps, all I got was a lot of noise, hiss, and horrible feedback from this pedal. I almost threw the pedal away because it was too late to send back. Fast forward 6 months, and now with my Blues Junior I thought I would try it out again. The pedal makes no extraneous noises--it's quiet--there is no feedback, and it accomplishes exactly what a compressor should. I am now very happy with this pedal. Buyer beware that it may not play nicely with the amp you have. The only reason I gave the pedal 4 stars out of 5 is because I have not tried any other compressors and wouldn't know how it compares.

(Side note: I have the settings for Level at 1:00, Tone at 1:00, Attack at 10:00, Sustain at 1:00.)

The reason I prefer these to the 3Dio is portability. I record continuous walks in cities and in nature. These are simple and discrete. Two earbuds and a wire hooked into a Zoom H1n resulting in pro audio for 200 dollars. People don’t even know I’m recording. I’m not cayuing heavy equipment around!

If you’ve never experienced 3D audio before you can YouTube 3Dio. The sound quality from these are no less so the 3Dio will give you an idea of what to expect.

A message to the makers/sellers of these. I sincerely hope you continue to make/sell. I will be a lifetime customer.

title: Works great and delivers the goods, but spend a little time learning it
comment:
Very good machine, really delivers on its promises—but be prepared to spend a little time learning it and be patient. It is very powerful with many levels of control (most of which the average player will not use).

I also found that the harmonies seemed to improve when
1 My guitar was tuned to the correct pitch.
2 Mike levels were set correctly
3 Guitar levels were set correctly
4 Harmony levels set correctly

Until I spent some time with it the harmonies sounded like the backup singers had a bad hangover. But once it was set up correctly, they sounded great.

Once you are comfortable with it (read the manual) and the setup has been massaged a little, it is astonishing. Most of the time, it is impossible to tell these harmonies are not human on a recording.

title: It works - a fun toy!  Flexible settings
comment:
This is a fun toy to add some simple lighting effects. For just over $20, at the time of writing this review, it has a fair amount of flexibility. Non-moving, slow, medium, and fast, along with seven different color settings - an automatic setting that just cycles through the colors and a music setting. The music setting seems to just be the auto setting that turns off when the music goes quiet and comes back on when the music gets louder. The remote is easy to use and works intuitively.

The mini-tripod works fine, but seems very limited depending on your use, but it's a standard camera thread, so any tripod should do and standard clips that go on bars and lighting structures for holding camcorders/cameras should work.

Pros: it works, flexible lighting settings, fun

Cons: none that I can think of

If I could change anything about this product: put dual IR ports in the housing - in the front and back - having to use the remote from the front of this device may not be practical in some settings.

Packaging type: bulk packaging - the product comes in a plain box with no logos or anything printed on the outside

Construction Quality: from what I can tell, it feels solid - nothing was loose or jingling around in the housing

Would I buy again: if I were in the market, I'd probably buy this again - I just don't know why I'd be in the market for this again - it's a fun toy with limited applications

I received a discounted product in exchange for an honest and unbiased review of the product. I do my best to give honest and helpful reviews regardless of whether I paid full price, got the item at a discount, or on the rare occasion I got the item for free. title: Excellent guitar for beginner intermediate, minimal setup was required
comment:
The guitars fit and finish was perfect. The fret ends were polished and smooth, the action was fine, there were no blemishes.
Upon tuning the guitar, I noticed the intonation was slightly off. Upon further examination, I found the one “holiday” , the setup man went on. The bridge was pulling up, when the guitar was tuned. I took off the back plate, the tension screws were tensioned too loosely and unevenly. Two minutes I had it sorted, and this fixed the intonation.
I have included a picture of my practice setup. It included a Pock Rock, headphone amp, and effects processor(approx 40 bucks on Amazon), an old Fire tablet I had lying around, hooked into the line in, on the Pock Rock, and a pair of Vintage Sansui head phones. I have a behringer ppa200(also available on Amazon approx 350 on Amazon), that I also use for my karaoke/movie night, senior charity,when I want to Amp the setup(also through the Pock Rock).
I know quite a few chords, but haven’t played in years. Even though my next door neighbor says I’m not loud, and don’t bother her, the majority of time, I play with the headphones.
This little setup, is loads of fun, and I’m really enjoying it.

title: Incredibly clever and useful!
comment: I've used quite a few stage tuners over the years, most recently a pair of Boss TU-2's, excellent tuners in their own right.  This tuner beats the Boss units.  It offers an incredibly useful but unique feature.  Strum your guitar with the strings open and you'll instantly see a curved row of six paired-green dots (plenty bright and easy to see).  Any string out of tune will display a pair of red dots immediately below the green ones, thus indicating which if any strings are out of tune.  Go to an out-of-tune string and the display immediately changes to an LED-simulated needle display typical of an analog tuner.  Tracking is very fast and very accurate.  Tuning between songs is a breeze with this thing since you don't have to check each string one at a time; just strum and then tune the strings that need it.  When I first got it I did the strum and tune thing and then tested each string with my Peterson VSAM Strobe Tuner as a check - all were spot-on.  Add to the speed and accuracy the fact that this is built like a tank and features true bypass, and you've got the best stage tuner available.  And with all that, the price is roughly the same as any other good stage tuner.  This is one of the best guitar devices out there.

title: The 1/4" cable plugs fail to make constant contact.
comment: I used coil cords back in the 60's and have had a hard time finding one that would hold up like the old days.  Based on the reviews here I had hoped this was the solution.  I purchased this in June of this year.  The cord is very heavy so my hopes were high.  It has been a bitter disappointment.  The connectors make poor contact to the point that the cord is unusable.  I have a solid state amp and have have tried this cord in all 3 of my guitars.  In every instance any movement I make caused horrible loud popping sounds in the amplifier as if were were unplugging and plugging back in quickly.  There appears to be a slight amount of play (between 1/32 and 1/16 of an inch) after I plug it in. I also tried it in my tube amp and while the sound isn't as dramatic the same popping happens. Fearing that it will destroy my amplifiers it now just collects dust.  In the near future I will buy some new plugs for each end and repair it myself.  Based on all the other reviews perhaps mine is just a lemon but, burned this badly I won't risk more money on another one.  Never in my 54 years of playing guitar have I had a problem like this.

title: NOT A HUMBUCKER — WIRED AS A P90!!!
comment:
NOT A HUMBUCKER

Yes, this pickup is loud. It maybe great quality. But it should never be marketed as a Humbucker if it’s only got two wires!!!

You cannot coil-tap, you gain no noise cancelling advantages, as the coils are wrapped in unison and not N/S like a true humbucker.

This is false advertising and should be sold as a P90 or similar.

title: Great Preamp
comment:
This is an excellent preamp for the price.

Pros:
*The level meters are easily readable even in bright light.
*Two Independent channels with independent gain and limiter functions
*The unit is very rugged, with metal construction
*Mount points on the top and bottom
*Easily accessible battery compartment
*Independent headphone output and level control for monitoring
*12v and 48v phantom power

Cons:
*The reason for only four stars is that, if this unit is to bear the "professional" moniker, it needs to be powered professionally. It should offer a plug in or even better a USB power input so you can use a battery pack for long recording sessions.

This unit is my go-to for short recording sessions where I know I wont be needing to change batteries often.

title: Great sticks but not pitch-paired
comment:
This stick has a bit less "heft" than a Regal 5A. It's well-balanced and fast. The famous Regal Tip finish is there. All other brands of sticks, I have to coat with shellac. I ordered two pairs. One pair had two completely different pitches, even though they were weight-matched.. I guess Regal Tip doesn't pitch-pair their sticks (?). I used to pitch-pair them myself back in the day when I got them in music stores. But none of my local stores carry much of a selection, so I have to get them online.

The nylon tips get a noticeably brighter sound than wood tips on the cymbals. but they don't bring out as much fullness. On the other hand, they give you more cut-through on the cymbals.

Still loving Regal Tips after many decades! But pitch-pairing would be nice for those of us who buy them online.

title: Designed to do precisely what it's supposed to do
comment: There are a lot of files like this on the market.  After reading up on it, I took a chance, for it's not even close to the inexpensive tools offered elsewhere.  What you do get is a high quality file that is designed perfectly for fret work.  The eased corners of the file allow you to get at the fret sides from various angles without harming the fret board surface.  I like the fine pitch of the file's teeth that don't take off too much metal, allowing you to really work the the fret with some precision to get the profile just right.  The handle is comfortable too and right sized.  I'm glad I spent the extra few dollars to get this tool.  Don't skimp, get the real deal!

title: You get what you see - but questionable anchor quality
comment:
Pretty basic, you get what you see. The arms are pretty stiff when adjusting their location - so they stay where you set them pretty easily unless you have significant weight on them. I use these as a wall-mounted holder for my Saitek keyboard - as well as for my gamepad. Works perfectly for my needs.

I wouldn't overly trust the drywall anchors - they're not very robust. My keyboard spanned the width of my wall studs, so just pre-drilled into the studs and used the existing screws into those. Holds very firm when used this way. If I was forced to, I would have used some higher quality anchors. I went to use these in a different application(wall hanging) and it bent when hammering into slightly smaller pre-drilled, hole...

title: Glad I returned this!
comment:
I'm glad I returned this. I'm a tech and have been working with digital recording and audio editing for 20+ years. I read the reviews and was concerned about some issues with the drivers on the 2i2 but figured I'd try it out for myself. it turns out all the negative comments regarding their drivers are valid. I tried this unit for over three weeks working with Focusrite tech support (response usually took a few days and initially wasn't helpful but after some back and forth I got some useful info.) The issue was the drivers (both current stock and beta.

Out of the box and using the latest published drivers on their site the unit introduced a horrible distortion on the recorded file on both an older Win 7 machine and an updated win 8.1. When I switched to a standard windows driver the distortion disappeared but the latency was still there. (Fair enough on the latency, there are a lot of issue that affect this but some interfaces and drivers handle it better than others). After over two weeks of back and forth running tests and mailing results to the team at Focusrite I tried their beta drivers and got things to work on the windows 8.1 machine but things were even worse on the win7 machine. Again using standard (non -focusrite) drivers most issue went away. Clearly there is a SERIOUS issue with Focusrite drivers and this unit and it's extraordinarily delicate depending on the system you are using. I never got it working properly on the one machine and was concerned that some future tweak (or when I upgrade to win 10 or a different PC) it might not work again. You should never have to work this hard with a brand new interface to get it to do it's most basic functions. I returned it and purchased a competitor's interface (for half the price) and out of the ox that worked 100% on both of my machines.

The back and forth with Focusrite was educational but at times they clearly were pushing their agenda and not even listening to what I was explaining as the problems. It took three weeks before I got someone to admit "some" of their drivers "might" not work properly. Yet they still have the problematic drivers on the web site, haven't fixed the betas, and when you write they have you perform all sorts of maintenance and unnecessary "upgrades" pointing the finger at anyone but their own product. The 2i2, as it is today, was useless for my recording purposes. Two other interfaces worked perfectly out of the box with the same set up. YMMV but for me I'll stay away from Focusrite products now and in the future.

title: Need to cut a notch for the headstock for my JS Series Dinky JS22Q-7 to fit.
comment:
I contacted Jackson directly and asked them for the model number of the case that would fit my Jackson JS Series Dinky Arch Top JS22Q-7 DKA HT 7-String and they said this case would fit.

Well the body fits fine but the neck won’t set down in the neck groove of the case because the headstock point won’t fit in the case without first having to trim some excess foam away. I didn’t mind doing it and now the guitar fits very snugly but you would think that a case recommended for a guitar by the manufacturer should fit without having to modify it. In any case I love my guitar and the case is very rugged and will provide more than adequate protection.

I gave only four stars for my having to make a modification to get the headstock to fit.

title: Great Sound at a Great Price
comment: I have had the PreSonus Eris 4.5 BT Near Field Studio Monitors for a couple of months now.  I bought them for my home recording setup.  They have a nice, crisp sound, and good bass for small/mid size monitors.  I compared with other brands at length before making this selection, and I'm happy with my choice.  They don't take up a lot of room, so they fit well on the studio desk I purchased, while still providing the sound quality I need for music-production purposes.  As mentioned in other reviews, these are not speakers; these are monitors, so do not expect sound enhancements that speakers put out.  Monitors are meant to provide the actual sound that an audible source gives out, so a sound engineer can tailor a mix exactly the way they need to.  So, if that's what you're looking for, then these great monitors will be just what you need.  And, at a great price!

title: After 5 other slides, this one fits and works the best for me
comment:
Using it on the ring finger. I tried 234, 222, 221, 220 (too tight), and 213 (just a bit big). The two that work for me with ring size 11.5 to 12 are the 318 and 203. Works smooth and was inexpensive as compared to some fancy ones with notches. Those should be nice too. After getting this I do not feel the need to try another one for a while. The length is shorter than some other slides, which is what I like. Some models above are knuckle slides but they fit my little finger and I tried but could not figure 'em out to be useful. Not worth it to return the other ones because they were from multiple music retailers and not Amazon.
Yes is sounds good. Follow slide guitar principles, I am not expert, but a higher action will help. I am able to still use it with very low action and even on an 8 string guitar. Higher action will help along with thicker gauge strings closer to standard tuning.

title: Very good picks
comment:
I think many/most guitarists are always on the lookout for new things and picks are and easy and cheap thing to try. My favorite picks for the last ~8 years are Snarling Dogs Brain Picks - they're nylon picks that have a raspy "cat's tongue" surface that makes it super easy to grip.

I've also had a few of these Gator Grip picks for a few years now and I like them quite a bit, but they're not my favorite. I tend to use something around or just under 1 mm for electric and a bit less than that for acoustic. These picks have a matte finish which makes it easy to retain your grip on them. Sound-wise I don't find that they're significantly different from any other pick. If they ever stop making Brain Picks these could very well become my #1.

The Martin goes in a hard case for both protection and humidity management, and the Emerald goes in it's own gig bag. If I put my electrics in individual gig-bags or cases, they'd be bouncing around or taking up too much room. So this double gig bag fits my needs really well. But they're a little roomier than I'd like and there's no neck cradle for the guitars, so if I had a Les Paul or was rougher with my gear, I'd probably spend more for something with a few more features. But for my limited needs, this is pretty perfect and at a price I could stomach...

comment: I have two computers, one PC (Windows 10) and one Apple (a five year old macbook air). When I plugged it into my PC nothing happened and I got nervous. I google Behringer U Phoria UM2 and got instructions on how to download drivers. After I downloaded the driver it was pretty straight forward to install them, but getting it interface with PC DAWs was a pain so I plugged it into my macbook Air which has Logic (I'm sure garage band would work just as well) and VOILA it worked first time. The only thing I've noticed is that you have to be careful to have it plugged into a good USB source, I wouldn't use a USB HUB at least during the installation process. Other that the audio quality is good and even the cheapo headphones aren't that bad, although I don't plan on using them that much because I have much nicer ones (Beyerdynamics). I doubt anyone could beat the affordability. I'm sure PC set up is possible but it will be a bit more involved because of Windows/drivers etc, probably not the fault of the unit itself. Cables probably aren't sure high quality but its enough to get you going if your goal is to have fun recording your stuff. I will try to remember to do an update if it craps out on me and in a year if it lasts that long. Not expecting much. German designed, Chinese built.

title: Outstanding cartridge for the price but ...
comment:
This is an outstanding cartridge for the price. Like others, I noticed that the sound considerably opened up after an initial breakin period. The detail is incredible - the DL-110 effortlessly tracked even the most difficult passages with unbelievable neutral purity. In comparison on a Technics SL-1200, it made my similarly-priced Ortofon 2M Blue sound thick and unidimensional. Moreover, the cringy sibilance I heard with the 2M Blue on some loud highs (regardless of careful setup) was completely gone. The warmth of the 2M Blue was also gone, but I prefer the change on my system. I became totally addicted.

My only disappointment was that, after about 18 months of light-to-moderate use and careful handling, the DL-110 started to pop occasionally. I noticed it first on some jazz records and verified with other carts that it was the DL-110. The sound is clearly not a vinyl click but an electrical pop, as if there is an intermittent short. I understand that MCs may fail this way. Understandably, Denon will not service cartridges and I am way out of the warranty period for replacement. Given the outstanding sound for the price point, I did the illogical thing and purchased another. I’ll report back.

title: Decent
comment:
I bought this harmonica for two reasons:

(1) I had seen some YouTube reviews, hyping this up as one of the first decent harmonicas to come out of China.
(2) I needed a new case for my Lee Oskar. I saw that this was $25, and new cases like this one's are $10-12, so I figured that I'd take a chance on this for the case.

Mission accomplished, because these cases are nice and fit a Lee Oskar harmonica just fine! However, it turns out that the East top harmonica itself was a nice little surprise. Reeds are all in great shape out of the box, easy to bend. The projection is not as loud as my Lee Oskar, but overall this harp has great dynamic range. It's good couple of ounces heavier than a Lee Oskar or Special 20, and just "feels" like a really substantial, quality instrument.

There are two downsides. The first is that they go with a "tin sandwich" construction style (probably to save costs), where the reed plates stick out over the comb. On a Lee Oskar and most Hohner's, the reed plates sink into a recessed groove in the comb itself, so that nothing sticks out past the comb. This East top style is slightly less comfortable to play, but I'm not exactly enough of a virtuoso for it to be a big deal.

The second downside is that while this price is cheaper than a Special 20 or Lee Oskar, it's rapidly climbing. All of those YouTube reviews, hyping this up as an amazing Chinese harp? They were mostly recorded two or there years ago, when these things cost $15. This WOULD be a steal at that price. However, as of today the price is around $26, and that's only a notch below the leading German and Japanese instruments. I hate to be a snob, but I just think it's a little premature to start charging German or Japanese money for a Chinese instrument.

All in all, it would take a bigger "made in China" discount to tempt me away from the Japanese harmonicas that I'm used to playing. However, this is a respectable enough option for beginners looking to save a few bucks. And I could see myself buying some more of these in the future, for key tunings that I play less frequently.

title: It has "echo feature"
comment:
You can use this little amp with your phone and a patch cord. It also features the use of MP3 player with the use of SD/TIFF/ flash drive memory card. You can also record into the unit to the memory card (with use of a microphone). A "headset" microphone is included. There is also an FM radio on board with extending antenna built in.
I use this for speaking and singing practice with and without echo feature AND for an extension speaker in my kitchen so I can hear the news from internet etc thru cord. The "echo' feature adds more interest in listening. The echo is is turned on by turning the "echo" dial knob RIGHT and is digital and offers a few variations as you turn it up. This amplifier is superbly LOUD for it's size, clear and worthy. I am seriously happy with this little amp. I paid 25 dollars for GREAT quality sound. Lets also remember that this amp is 4x3.5 inches 1.5 thick. For the money, I got serious sound.
CONS: 1. I wish there was an extension speaker jack OUTPUT 2. I wish the MP3 player wouldn't start playing as I turn the unit on for regular AMP SOUND use. 3. Should be EASIER-FRIENDLY to understand for full-use. Yet, there are still some things to mention in this unit, more than I can comment here because I have two uses only, as prevously mentioned. 4. The AUX input doesn't do anything, no sound comes from it. Maybe it's ME?
In final, I have no regrets whatsoever in purchasing this little radio sized amp. I AM VERY HAPPY WITH IT. If Retekes advertised this amp beyond Amazon, they'd sell plenty. I may buy another, seriously. JIMEL

title: Disappointing and hard to use
comment:
If you're looking for a relatively straightforward digital recorder, keep looking. This is actually a four track studio in a very small, hard to use package.

I should mention I am a sound engineer and have been working withdigital audio since its inception. I was one of the earliest Porta One users and normally LOVE Tascam products. This one is a dud, unfortunately.

The problems, in no particular order, are:

1. You can't just make a recording and then export it to your PC. You have to create a "Stereo Master" first, which can only be done in real time. If your recording is a three hour jam session, you have to switch about ten settings, then mix it down, even if it is just one tack, and the process would take an additional three hours. Ugh.
2. It advertises itself as a four track recorder, but you can only record two tracks at one time. There are only two mic/line inputs.
3. The display is tiny, confusing, and worst of all NO BACKLIGHT, which is a critical flaw for a device that is probably going to be used in clubs, venues, and other dark places. Plus, get out your reading glasses. its really hard to see.
4. No AC adapter included. You gotta buy one. This device is already thirty bucks more expensive that simpler units...now its $45 more expensive.
5. The stems are not accessible from a PC. You can't even see them...they are hidden so they don't show up in File Explorer when you hook up via USB. You can only see and export the completed, "mastered" wave file.
There's probably more, but this should hopefully be enough to get you pointed in a different direction. I will repeat, I normally LOVE Tascam stuff. Not this.

title: This is an excellent recorder everywhere where it matters
comment:
This is an excellent recorder everywhere where it matters, and a bit of a pain in a couple areas that may or may not (to you).

Where it matters:

The sound quality is excellent, and it records up to 24/96. The built in mics are, IMO, at a useable professional level. They lack any kind of character, which is what separates them from expensive studio standard mics, but they are neutral, sensitive and accurate. Honestly the price for this recorder would be a fair price for these two mics, IMO. I used my Neumann and API/Neve (clone) pres into the line ins on the DR, and when that's what I need it's definitely better. But the internal mics are going to be perfect for most applications including acoustic recording in a good sounding room.

It uses cheap and spacious SD cards so recording time is not an issue and it's affordable to record tons and tons (especially when set to an appropriate quality level for what you're doing).

The sound-on-sound mode is really cool. Much like a looper pedal, you can build up an entire album just by overdubbing part after part, mixing them together as you go without a loss of quality as the recordings stack up. The basic reverb effects are good for adding some ambience to what you're recording. Balanced XLR/1/4" inputs.

Where it's not 100% perfect:

This is not what most people would think of when they look for a "Tascam four track recorder." It does NOT allow you to record 4 separate tracks one at a time and control the level and panning of them individually. Instead, it allows you to simultaneously record each input pair to a different stereo track, or overdub a new stereo track while monitoring a existing stereo track such as the sound-on-sound mode, but keeping the files separate. That said, if I had wanted a four track like one of the Portastudios I used to have, I would have gotten that. I just think it's important to point out that if you're expecting this to work like that it doesn't quite do the job. Other than the lack of balancing and crisp, modern, non-cassette sound, this is a huge improvement over the old cassette 4 tracks for songwriters looking for a sketchpad to demo ideas.

The headphone jack is weird with Apple-compatible headphones, that is to say, ones with the extra band to support a microphone, and so on. I'm not sure what it's doing but it's cancelling channels or the sum of them or something that makes monitoring a stereo signal with headphones like that (these days my only headphones) just sound super weird. When you take the files from the DR-40 to your computer and listen, it's fine. But off the machine it's weird sounding. This is a non-issue to me generally but the first time I noticed it I was very frustrated trying to determine why my source sounded so wrong when it was simply the headphone mix from the DR.

Despite being a standard memory card that will load up in a computer, you can't just pop out the card and put it into the built-in reader on your laptop. The computer will show the files, and probably let you take files off the card, but not put anything on the card and will likely corrupt the file system at this point. I'm not really sure why this happens. In any case, you have to connect a USB cable from the DR-40 to your computer and then it will work like any external HD. This is a minor annoyance because I can't just have a stack of memory cards with DR recordings and my laptop, I have to bring the actual recorder around to read what's on the cards.

So aside from a couple little things that came up in using the recorder, this is an awesome device. Great quality and ease of use. I would highly recommend this for anyone who needs to make high quality recordings cheaply and simply.

title: A Truly Beautiful Instrument
comment:
1 YEAR UPDATE: The guitar just keeps getting better. Has held up remarkably well, and still looks brand new. The sound has opened up very nicely, and I enjoy playing this at least several hours almost every day. I always look forward to practicing and playing, as this instrument is so fantastic. I've changed strings to D'Addario EJ46 High Tension, and the sound is even better than the EJ45TT's I originally used. It has been an absolute pleasure to own this instrument.

ORIGINAL REVIEW: Inspecting the instrument on arrival, it was visually stunning. The quality and build were flawless, as was the finish. Materials are of the highest quality. From the beautiful and accurate tuners to the wonderful soundboard, solid rosewood body, ebony fret board.....everything looks and feels of the highest quality. In fact, to my pleasant surprise, the spruce soundboard even has some beautiful "bear claw" figuring which enhances the appearance and just goes to show that it is high quality spruce. All 5 star. True craftsmanship. You can tell this was made with pride. Well done, Kremona.

After a full visual inspection, I was somewhat shocked after tuning up and playing. Much to my amazement, the guitar sounded flat, weak, tinny, and there was some fret buzz on the fourth string, especially at the third, fourth and fifth frets. I actually put in for a return through Amazon because I just couldn't believe the sound was so "weak". Then I changed the strings and put on new D'Addario Pro-Arte EJ45TT's. What an absolutely unbelievable transformation. Now not only was the instrument a stunner, it sounded incredible. I was really shocked at how bad the stock strings were and what a transformation took place once they were changed out.

The sound is just so much better, its depth, quality clarity and projection is remarkable. It is amazing how you can articulate the tone when plucking in various positions across the sound hole. Everything is so clear. The intonation across the fret board is excellent. This instrument just sounds so darn great. Even when changing the open tunings to play 7 course renaissance music.

The action and playability overall are excellent, as the instrument is easy to play and hold.

It is obvious that Kremona is synonymous with top notch craftsmanship and their product is of the highest quality. I feel as though I just bought a $5000.00 guitar for 1/5th the price. I contemplated giving this review only 4 stars because of the poor strings that came with the instrument. But I couldn't do it. Luckily it turns out that it was an easy fix to eliminate the fret buzz, weak sound and get this guitar to exhibit a wonderful sonorous tone I was hoping for when I read about it. It is that great of an instrument. And it will only get better as it ages.

Thank you Kremona.

title: A lot for the money, glad I bought it

The only con in my opinion is that my guitar/amp combo hums .  That's why I later went with a more expensive guitar with active pickups. I would still recommend this combo to anyone just starting out on guitar. I think the amp is fine, despite what other reviewers say. I also have a fender mustang 2 amp. And while the mustang amp sounds clearer and louder , the amp included with this kit is totally fine for someone just starting out. And its loud enough for me too . The only addition to this kit you need is a guitar stand. Amazon sells some good ones for about $13. The guitar looks well made. I can't see any flaws no matter how close I look. The rounded edges on this guitar and its light weight make it very comfortable to hold while playing. It comes set up to play. All I had to do was tweak the tuning a bit and it was ready to play. title: An okay stand if you don't need to move it often
comment: As an event DJ I've been trying to find a stand that is compact, light weight and easy to assemble. This stand is about the closest I could find to those three requirements. However, after using it for a few months I've decided to switch back to a simple folding table. CONS: My biggest complaint with this stand is the difficulty I've had in extending its legs. It has those metal buttons on the side that you push in to extend the leg. If you're by yourself it can be excruciatingly difficult and time consuming. I've also had the misfortune of pinching my finger while doing it. Instead you have to place the stand on its side to get the legs out - which can still be difficult. The stand does fold up, but again I find it a bit heavy and cumbersome to pack in my vehicle. You'll definitely get a work out carrying this thing. Also, make sure to tighten the knobs on the legs when you transport this table. They will fall off otherwise. PROS: Again this is not a bad stand. It does have a lot of utility especially the perforated top which allows plenty of room for attachments and venting. It folds up adequately which saves room when you transport it. The stand looks professional, is solid and allows for easy cable management. The thing is if you don't need to move it a lot it's a great stand. Otherwise, if you're like me with an event every weekend this stand might become a bit tedious to use.

title: One of the Firewire Greats!
comment: I've owned this for many years among a few other Firewire recording interfaces. Overall tough to beat the versatility in this price range now that it's discontinued. Especially with the updated TotalMix software and drivers...a huge improvement over the original which I can confirm runs perfect with this unit and trouble free on Windows 7 or 8. If there is one unfortunate downside, it's the 4 onboard preamps, boring, flat, lack warmth and character. Barely passable for reference audio or voice over work. If they were simply flat in terms of frequency response perhaps that would be a good thing, but they impart a bland sterility to anything I've ever tried on them. On the upside the A/D conversion direct to the 8 analog 1/4" inputs/outputs on the back is very good and uncolored. Used with outboard preamps or direct out from a mixing board, which is all I ever do, excellent results can be had. Plus the ADAT light pipe offers more expansion for ins and outs. A rock solid unit for it's A/D conversion, high resolution supported recording formats, versatility, and performance. Recommended...just skip the on board preamps...yuck.

title: Read the manual online
comment:
The video here with the girl singing a whole song and the guy holding the pedal is useless. I almost didn’t buy this pedal bc that video shows absolutely nothing. Don’t base your purchasing decision based on that video.
Better than the tc competitor, ( I use both ), just be certain to read the manual online and watch other videos because it does more than mindless one button operations.
Definitely useful if you double-down and learn everything about the pedal.
Also the output levels are not overly boosted and never a problem distorting the vocal signal prior to the mixer like the tc pedals tend to do.
Would definitely consider either brand if you are working with house systems and inattentive sound techs. It’s just safer to send what you want no matter where you go, for instance, you never see guitar players without a target tone and effects, why would you do that with vocals?
Buy this if you want a little auto tune, a little tone, and little reverb, and it will add reverb to your input instrument if you so choose.
Well worth the $90-150 price range.

title: One of the best under $500
comment:
The DT 700 Pro X (700 going forward) is an excellent headphone in the under $500 category. You're getting excellent build quality, comfort, and sound quality that's easy to drive and attractive.

The 700 has a fairly neutral tonality overall, which is to be expected for a set geared towards music production. But that doesn't mean they're lean or boring sounding. You get excellent bass extension and slam, and an overall punchy and nimble bass presentation. Highs have good air and extension but don't get sibilant. Mids have good presence, though I'd prefer them to be a touch more forward.

Soundstage is very good, with good side to side and dimensional placement of the instruments. Separation is good as well, allowing good identification of different conflicting or overlapping frequencies. Overall resolution is good, but not great. I didn't feel I was missing anything, but at the same time other headphones better resolve and isolate sounds for inspection. But the differences aren't major, IMO.

Being low impedance, the 700 is very easy to power. They scale a bit with good amplification, but it's not absolutely necessary, unless you're chasing maximum sound quality. And they're as easy to wear as they are to power. These are some comfy headphones! The velour material is even softer and more supple than the older DT 770/880/990 pads, which were mighty comfy on their own. Clamping force is a bit high out of the box but dissipates after a week or so of use. If you're really bothered by it, when you get them, leave them clamped onto a shoebox or something slightly wider than your head at night for a week or two. That will help break in the metal headband tension, and ease the clamp force.

Overall the 700 is a very well balanced sounding headphone in an easy to drive and wear package. Nothing stands out as offensive, which is excellent. Headphones usually have something sonically that stands out negatively, but these don't. They're not perfect, and might not match your preferences well, but I don't perceive any major frequency response flaws. In comparison the older DT 770 is brighter, more sibilant, with much more recessed mids, and boomy bass. All notable flaws, IMO.

There's strong competition in this market segment though. The two headphones that stand out to me are the Austrian Audio Hi-X15 and the AKG K371. Both are significantly cheaper and rival the 700 in sound quality. Let's compare, shall we?

Austrian Audio Hi-X15 - Tuning is a bit brighter, with a leaner presentation overall. The X15 is slightly more resolving, with a more dynamic response. Bass has more potential extension, and seems more responsive under duress. Treble and upper mids can be a bit intense at times though, especially right out of the box. The X15 needs a good 20-50 hours of run time to settle down. The driver is a high excursion type with a stiff suspension that IMO needs some hours to hit it's optimum operational range. I thought they were pretty bad the first 5 hours or so, but now they're a staple in my lineup. In comparison, the DT 700 Pro X is a bit darker, or less lean, if you will. Upper range extension and presence is more tame. Bass is slower and less nimble and responsive. Overall resolution is a bit less. The 700 sounds SLIGHTLY hazy in comparison. Also the X15 has more dynamic range, it can reproduce more nuance between high and low volume elements within the playback. But these differences are not huge, mind you. Do I feel I'm missing anything with the 700? Nope. I do prefer the slightly more punchy, though leaner X15 bass response, but the 700 doesn't disappoint.

AKG K371 - Tuning is very similar, with a nice even frequency response top to bottom. Like the 700, nothing about the 371's response stands out as problematic. So why spend more for the 700 over the 371? The 700 is built much better, is more comfy, and is clearer and more resolving than the 371. Bass on the 371 can get a touch boomy and indistinct compared to the 700. Also the overall sound from the 371 is slightly hazier than the 700. I think of the 371 as the poor man's 700. Very very similar overall, with the 700 just being a bit better.

If you twisted my arm and made me pick only one of the three, I'd take the 700 as it's the most even sounding of the bunch. I like the X15's higher dynamic range, it's quite impressive in such an affordable headphone, but the lean tuning is a bit annoying to me.

Beyerdynamic hit it out of the park with the DT 700 Pro X. As a longtime fan it's good to see such a great legacy company as Beyer revamp their sound to more modern standards, and do it so well. For fans like myself who soured on the older lineup with their fatiguing brightness, the 700 is a breath of fresh air.

title: Beautiful but Not Practical
comment: [[VIDEOID:b5f09f02f9a2465c48c36b51090aa705]] I believe that using ligatures that work for you is something very personal so with this review I am just going to try and give the facts and little bit about me. There is also a video review after you read this to help you further. Specifically, the Rico H Ligature is silver plated so it looks smashing on your clarinet whether you have silver or nickel keys. It also comes with a very nice Rico rubber cap which will not scratch your mouthpiece. However, it is not the most practical ligature. I was so hoping it was because it looks so beautiful. This is where the reality comes in. Ligatures do not have to be pretty to work. It took me several runs to get the ligature in the right place and if I screwed it down just a little too much I was turning blue trying to make a sound. So I re-adjusted the ligature and 15 minutes later I almost had gotten it right. By then I was too frustrated to keep trying. When you find a ligature that you like, that is what is important, not that it is shiny and looks good. I personally have 4 different ligatures. The Selmer ligature that came with the clarinet, a french ligature that came with my Vandoren B45 mouthpiece over 20 years ago, and the Rovner Ligature which I still will do a video review for you and explain further. I hope this and the video review will help you in your decision about purchasing ligatures. Again, if it is from Amazon no problem as long as you took care of it you can return it within 30 days. If it is another company they may not be as understanding so read the fine print always. If I can do anything to make your purchase easier please let me know. I hope the video review helps.

title: Strong, Sturdy and fun to play.
comment: After 2 and a half years of use my Demonator still is going strong. The feeling of the longboard is fantastic, it allowed for a strong and fast whip on the kick for fast playing. For the money it feels like a professional pedal and i love the way it looks. Its heals up over time, it has well over 5,000 hours of play time on it though studio work, live, and much more. The spring locks really do keep the springs from losing tension over time. The double sided beater allows for a larger range of sounds with the soft velvety side giving a warm thud and the plastic a hard slap. This pedal is well worth the money and will definitely last you a long time. I couldn't recommend this pedal set enough for beginners to advanced and intermediate players.

title: Excellent practice amp.
comment:
Been playing on one of these now off an on (I've got a couple other amps) for a few months now, and figured it deserved to have at least one review. I've been pretty impressed by the range of sounds it can crank out. Mainly picked it up because I wanted something that was both versatile and able to produce its entire sound range at volumes appropriate for my apartment. This thing works perfectly.

This can do anything from clean, bluesy tones all the way up to metal-worthy chugging. Separate gain and volume knobs let you play at full distortion at pretty much any output volume. The ISF knob gives you pretty decent control over your tone, though it doesn't (for me) completely replace full tone controls. It works well enough...I'm just not necessarily buying the marketing hype on it. Given that the HT-5 (and other Blackstar amps) have both the ISF and separate tone controls, I guess this is more meant to emulate different speaker configurations. But it...doesn't really do that either?

The speaker-emulated/headphone output works pretty well for recording (I ran it into a decent ASIO card), though it can be just a bit noisy. Works great in headphones, though. The MP3 input I have not used, but I imagine it does what it claims? You'll need a cable that can go from 3.5mm to 1/4" to hook that up, or the proper adapter(s).

The 8" speaker gets a bit underwhelming at higher volumes, and can sound a bit boxy at times. This is kinda par for the course for an amp this size, so I wouldn't really ding it for that, and the external speaker output gives you the option to run it into something a bit beefier. I ran it into a 12" external speaker and was definitely impressed at the difference it made. I still don't know that it would ever cut it practicing with a band...1W is still 1W. But when you don't need the portability of the built-in 8", it's worthwhile.

The lack of reverb is...annoying at times. I figured I could just make up for it with a pedal, but then your reverb is going through the pre-amp and it just doesn't work out all that well (you can reduce the level of reverb on the pedal to compensate, I guess). I'd assume that the HT-1R (and RH) has the reverb between the pre-amp and power amp stages, which will probably give you a better result...probably worth the extra $50.

Overall this seems like a solid amp, and a good value for the money (if you insist on tubes, that is). What it lacks in volume it makes up for in range, letting it stack up nicely against a lot of other low-wattage amps that may go a bit louder. It may not wind up cutting it as an "only" amp (you'll probably eventually want something larger to complement it), but at the same time it shouldn't wind up gathering dust, either.

EDIT: Bumped up from 4 stars to 5 stars. Given what it is (a 1W amp with an 8" speaker), it's pretty great. I think before I was judging it based on what I wanted it to be. But as it is, I keep coming back to this amp.

title: Great cab!  (but pair it correctly)
comment:
Great cab! Always sounds fantastic!
Some reviewers are claiming that theses weak speakers, but they're mismatching with the wrong heads! If your head overpowers the cabinets wattage rating, of course it will not sound it's best - duh! I pair it with an old GK 400RB: evenly matched (200 watt cab, 180 watt head). Always clean, never distorts at any volume (unless I purposely dial that up from the "boost" knob on the amp) AND I never need to turn my volume past 5! With a head rated at 200 watts, this cab will be perfect for anything except the biggest of stages and theatres (and on the bigger stages, you'll need a more powerful head anyway). If your head is more than 200 watts, get the 4x10 instead.

title: A Capo worth it's weight in Gold!
comment:
I would not have ever imagined paying $45.00 for a capo for my Flamenco guitar. I've owned Shubb, Dunlop and Planet Waves (designed by Steinberger) and this wonderful capo outdoes them all and is worth the price.

I use it on my Guild GAD-C2NA classical guitar which I play Flamenco on. The style is aggressive and requires a good hold on the strings. This little capo is solid up and down the neck. No buzzing and a very snug fit is attained easily. The capo can be moved and removed with the push and pull of a lever. It's simplicity is it's strength. It also comes with a lifetime warranty.

When handling the capo you will feel how heavy and solid it is. If you are looking for something light and cheap -- this is not your capo. If you are willing to make the investment for your playing, you won't be disappointed. This is a GREAT capo!!

Kudos!

title: Last resort.
comment: I thought I was getting the model with the adjustable buckle on it, but that's the pro model I realize that now. The biggest drawback with this basic model is that the tension can be either too strong so it bends the strings out of tune, or too little so there's fret buzz. It is sheer luck if the pressure is just right. But it's better than nothing, and if somebody on stage desperately needs a capo at a show, if it will be a lifesaver, I can give it away with no second thoughts. Keep it, let it remind you to bring your good capo next time.

title: One Great Guitar -So Many Great Sounds
comment: Carrying multiple guitars to a gig and worrying about damage and theft is now a thing of the past. The Variax JTV-69 has so many great sounds and plays so well. I got one of the last Korean-built models as Line 6 is moving the production to Yamaha. Unfortunately, I entered my order with Amazon but the vendor (GearNuts) claimed to be out of stock two weeks after I placed the order. Maybe it's true, maybe not. On the day I placed the order there were at least three models available from this vendor in different colors and they didn't inform me for over two weeks that there were none available--they refused to honor the order. So I bought mine at Sweetwater and got the same $748.00 price. I really love this guitar. The neck and intonation are magnificent. James Tyler has designed a very fine guitar. I used to carry a Telecaster, Gibson J-200, and Rickenbacker 360-12 to all my gigs. No more. Just take this great instrument and use a tc-Helicon Play Electric to simulate different amplifiers through my Carvin AG-300 acoustic amp. The sounds are spectacular. I recommend that you keep fairly new strings on the guitar because the modeling sounds degrade faster than a standard electric guitar as the strings lose their resonance.

title: My Pick for Fifty Years
comment: My go to guitar pick. I've been using this particular pick for fifty years. That's right. This pick has been around that long. When I began taking guitar seriously, MY FIRST guitar teacher recommended the .60 grey Dunlop nylon. I started out playing at that time my custom blue Gibson Firebird with the chrome harp engraved tailpiece and twang bar. Over the years I've started playing more acoustic guitar and have tried different picks. Even bought one of those custom picks in a 1.0 that I gave like $40.00 for. People in the acoustic genre raved about that pick brand. To me. it was like playing my HD28 with a rock. I tried many other brands. I do like the Dunlop Gel picks in a medium light gauge. But, I ended up returning to this pick, the Dunlop 44R.60 standard. I must admit, I do believe I like the Dunlop MaxGrip that I've just tried a tad better in .60 gray. Not much difference really. But the MaxGrip would probably be my choice between the two. In a blind test, I probably couldn't tell the difference in the sound. The Dunlop 44R.60 has been a reliable work horse for me and I believe it transmits the sound of the guitar strings in their purest form without adding unwanted pick distortion or pick noise. Recommend

title: Great quality at an affordable price
comment:
A while back, I went out on a whim and purchased an Akai MPK25 MIDI controller, to get my feet wet with MIDI recording on my computer. It was fun and there was a lot to learn, and I soon began to get interested in finding ways to record audio (primarily guitar and vocals) as well.

Like most home musicians who hate to spend money, I first tried the horribly inadequate technique of running audio directly into my computer's sound card. What I got was a tinny, flat, and horribly latent reproduction of all incoming audio that no amount of DAW wizardry could ever fix. This simply wouldn't do.

Biting the bullet, I started doing research on the different audio interfaces out there. What I found was a dizzying array of different brands, all with different features, reputations, ease of use, and compatibility with certain software packages. I just wanted something that was easy to set-up, reliable, good quality, and not too expensive.

I really feel like I lucked out with this unit, as it is everything I was looking for. Pop in the CD that comes with the unit into your computer (works with Apple or PC). Let the drivers install, plug in the unit to an available USB port and boom - your audio interface is set up!

The front inputs accept either standard XLR jacks from a microphone or 1/4" jacks from a guitar or other source. Setting the levels is a snap, and the sound quality is fantastic. The guitar sounds full and bright and the vocals are well-produced, too.

There were a couple of goodies that came along with the unit as well, including some Focusrite audio effects that can be called up in your DAW of choice (I'm using Ableton Live).

All in all, this is a great unit and something I would strongly recommend, assuming its features meet your needs.

And for some reason a headphone jack was important to me. I blame apple for that madness. All I needed was a $50-99 audio mixer and I could've picked ANY amp with a direct out and decent headphones. The big diff from the 350 unit is power and mid tone control. I am sure the headphone jack design is still dainty at any price point. That is my main complaint about the product. The jack design is not sturdy, its angled, and mine cuts audio from the left channel sometimes. Alternatively if the user is not planning on using the amp with a cab or not going to play for an audience, a bit more cash I'd get the Tech 21 sansamp $209, and a Focusrite 2i2 $160 (computer audio interface/2 channel mixer), to achieve IMO a similar result. Plus you'd be set up to record.

title: Pleasantly surprised, but maybe not for dynamic microphones
comment:
After trying my fair share of cheap (and not so cheap) USB microphones and having no luck, I realized I would have to bite the bullet and get an XLR microphone. That meant I needed an audio interface, and those definitely aren't cheap. I tried the Scarlett whatever it's called, but was disappointed when it didn't add enough gain to my soft speaking voice. This is for a personal project, so I didn't have much of a budget -- frankly, I only got this because I got a good deal over the holidays. I'd never considered it before since I had no use for all of the extras on this one. I was very pleasantly surprised to find this works really well. After months of searching for something that would work for me, I finally found it. I did have one technical issue (which no one else seems to have, so I'm nearly positive it was a one-off), but I reached out to Xibo and they sent me a new one within a week. Good product, great customer service. Will this work for everyone? Of course not, but for what it is and at this price, it's a solid buy and I am very happy to have found it. (The microphone it comes with is decent, but I ended up with the FIFINE Dynamic K688.)

EDIT: Removed a star because it turns out this doesn’t have true phantom power. It will power a condenser microphone, but it will NOT power a gain booster for a dynamic microphone. This cuts the versatility of it nearly in half. This can mostly be addressed in post, although I would prefer not to have to do that.

title: Best Quality for the Price.
comment:
I was going to return this for a leftie but felt too attached to it so...

5 stars. Should I elaborate?

This guitar is an amazing entry-level guitar. I've played other people's acoustic guitars yet I feel that my good ol' Rogue RA-090 just can't be beat.

Very solid build and beautiful natural color. The only aesthetic downside I have to this is that my pickguard is a little misaligned... but that's fine. The sound, price, and the overall quality for this guitar makes up for that.

The strings which come with this guitar is also durable. I've been playing this guitar for about 2 and a half months now on an almost daily basis and they're still holding up very well. Also this guitar is very easy to tune - very stable.

If I should mention anything that I consider a little bothersome is that the action for this guitar is a little high - I'm not sure if this is caused by the bridge height or anything but I've noticed that especially towards the higher frets, it takes a lot of effort to simply produce a clear note. On the optimistic side, this might help a beginner develop their callouses more quickly and get into a good habit of firmly pressing the strings.

Either way I definitely recommend this guitar. Definitely.

While I was unwrapping the saxophone from it's white covers I noticed that one of the key pads had come off (thankfully it being red was a little easier to find). This is a common issue with shellac when it gets colder, since shellac stops holding pads around 40 degrees, and that may be why some of the reviewers are saying that they can't get the saxophone to play the lower notes. Not that shellac is bad in all cases but it does explain "leaking" that the others have reported.
Otherwise it is a beautiful instrument, great tone and quality.
It does come with 10x 2.5 reeds that while they aren't bad are a little hard to play for a new player, a neck strap, pair of gloves (that never fit) a screw driver, a pair of tweezers and a few extra straps for turning the case into a backpack.
All in all a great beginner tenor saxophone.

title: Great concept.  Can be better in some respects.
comment:
Pros:
Is smallest usable guitar I've found. Great for travel and last minute stow away. Detachable neck is a big plus. Neck design is better than button type predecessors like Yamaha EZ-EG or EZ-AG series. Inexepensive. Always in tune. Good action. Seems durable enough for the elements although it might not withstand a bad drop. Customer service - so far so good. At least you get a real person (who can speak English)on the phone. Battery life OK.

Cons:
No onboard speaker. No DC input jack. No up/down step tuning adjustment. Sound and synth selection, while they might be fine for electronica focus, could be better when it comes to acoustic emulation. Not quite enough hand room at the top of the neck, although there is a socket for an optional headstock (not yet available) that might alleviate that.

Summary:
For songwriting and knock around, I still prefer my button neck Yamaha EZ-AG. But those are out of production. For midi or electric effects, this unit is better. Pity though that no company as yet has taken the risk to make a line of higher quality midi based guitars. Seems like technology is already there to produce something really awesome in the $500 - $1000 range, not so toy like with realistic feel, quality onboard speaker output,etc. Would look forward to that, although this thing is worth the money for what it is.

title: Worked like a charm.  Perfect solution when undecided
comment: There seems to as many pickguard possibilities out there as there are picks.  They come in many types of materials, colors, designs, curiosities, and potential solutions.  With that said, I simply could not make a decision.  So, instead of having to match pickguard color to my guitars, I chose to purchase these clear types.  The package comes with 2 guards slightly different in design but using the same basic overall coverage.  Laying down a clear plastic pickguard requires 3 major objectives.  The surface must be clean and oil-free, you should lay the guard in place to see your final location before removing the protection layers, and finally, remove any bubbles after application.  This product comes with the 2 guards, a moistened cleaning towel, a dry dust removal towel, and a squeegee to remove air bubbles after placement.  The directions are pretty basic but if you ever have applied a very thin layer of material to any surface, you know the rules.  Try to find a dust-free area to do the work.  Be sure to use the moistened towel to clean the surface and then wipe the surface with the dry dust-free towel.  The adhesive side protection sticker is labeled 1.  Remove that sticker and roll the guard onto the surface where you plan the pickguard to be.  Using the squeegee, move it outwards from the middle to the edges across the entire guard.  Then remove the protection layer numbered 2.  Again, squeegee from the middle outwards to the edges until you don't see any major air bubbles under the guard.  Very small air bubbles will eventually disappear.  If you see some dust or dirt under the guard, that will require you to re-apply the guard.  I was able to do both my guitars without needing a re-apply.  For the money, this kit is well worth your consideration.  Please note that after application, the only way you can tell you have a pickguard is by seeing the edges.  The guards are 100% transparent.

title: First impressions and updates, great for start-ups
comment:
Have yet to use but once I test it I will update Amazon review. Delivery was great minimal damage to the box. Products feels good quality has some weight to it. I will test the product and I will update in what I have liked and disliked.

*update*
The overall build quickly is great not the best but you know it was made with quality material due to the weight of the mixer.

Sound quality is nice, it pared well with our main sound system and did not hear any pops, static, or cutoffs. It’s important to note that you shouldn’t max any of the volumes on a sound system or mixer as that will cause the static and cutoffs to happen. All ports worked and Bluetooth connected but even though I was able to connect sound was not coming out while Bluetooth was in use, I will continue to check what it could be.

On to the knobs, button and slider. The knobs felt smooth when being turned, although most people won’t like the friction when turning knobs I for one find it useful as it will prevent human error when raising the volume. For the buttons, they had a nice tactile, click sound with a bit of a springy jump back after being pressed. Some gain/pad buttons though are not aligned at the same level as the others, meaning there is a difference in hight compare to the other buttons when pushed or left un pushed. This could be due to the use of different manufactured gain/pad switches or a bent on the circuit board. This is something minor though. The slider is good, smooth and light. The only thing I’m not liking too much is the effect knob as there is no definite stop but it works out because every time the board is turned on and off the effect counter resets to one.

Overall for the price and the quality build it is worth it for anything that you are starting be it a podcast, mixer for karaoke and even for church, just as long as you have speakers that have their own power as this mixer won’t be able to power them on its own.

title: Great Quality, Very Durable, Highly Recommended!!!
comment: Compared to some other strobe lights I've had, this Roxant Pro strobe light is great.  I bought one, really liked it, and now have 5 or 6 of them.
