They are on at night for almost the entire month of October for the last couple of years, and no issues...

title: Fender Made in Japan Marcus Miller Jazz Bass
comment:
My Fender 4 string Marcus Miller Jazz Bass is a 2012, the last year these basses were made in Japan. They are now made in Mexico. The Marcus Miller Jazz basses made in the USA are 5 strings (no 4 strings made in the USA). Marcus Miller has recently signed with another bass guitar maker to offer his signature basses. So it is unclear how long Fender will continue to offer his signature basses.

The previous reviewer stated that this bass is noisy. However, mine is dead quite. It also does not overwhelm my amp. Just turn the gain down on the amp. I did notice a scratchy noise when slapping (I have noticed this on several different Fender Jazz basses beside the Marcus). This was caused by my hand hitting the pickguard below the G string when slapping. This was an easy fix. I covered the bottom of the pickguard with copper shielding tape and the scratchy noise was gone.

The bass has excellent playability and feel. It feels and can sound just like my 1976 Jazz Bass. I have the action set low with no string buzz. I was going to upgrade the onboard preamp to a Sadowsky or Aguilar preamp, but the Fender preamp sounded so good I decided not to upgrade. This bass also has the 1970s rear pickup placement which is closer to the bridge compated to the pre 70's Fender J's or the 80 and newer Fender Jazz basses (other than the USA 75' reissue Jazz Bass).

As noted, my Marcus was made in Japan. So this review is based on the MIJ version and not the current MIM version which has some changes (battery compartment moved to the back, etc). I have bought and sold several of these bases over the years. My current Marcus plays and sounds great and is a keeper. This bass has excellent craftsmanship more like basses costing two or three times as much. Mine reads MIJ (Made in Japan) as opposed to CIJ (Crafted in Japan). It is my understanding that the last couple of years this bass was made in Japan, Fender used a new factory in addition to it's other factories in Japan due to the large demand. The new factory produced some excellent basses. Considering what other basses are available at the price point of the Fender Marcus Jazz Bass, this made in Japan Jazz bass is a great deal.

title: Make the Stool Work With Your Body Type And You'll Be Fine
comment:
I've watched these things go from being regarded as standard equipment to being blamed for all manner of back problems and even nerve damage. I think they've gotten a bum rap due to many players' (and teachers') irresistable tendency to mimic the sitting manner and technique of famous players they admire.

To avoid excess stress and strain you have to use a combination of chair seat and foot stool height, along with lateral positioning that works with your body proportions - not Bream's or Isbin's or the Old Man playing by the reflecting pool at the Alhambra - however idyllic the images.

By proportions I mean primarily the length of your torso versus your legs. Many people use a far too-high foot stool setting, which bends their back excessively. I use the lowest height the stool can be set to - but again, that's what works for me. I haven't had back or shoulder problems playing guitar since my very early years before I decided to ignore my teachers and images of players around me and go for comfort.

Of course sitting comfortably is only part of the game unless you position and play the instrument without excessive tension. Shearer's books (including the latest editions) remain for me the clearest explanation of the 'contact points' to be aware of.

I'm on my third foot stool in almost 40 years of playing classical guitar. The rubber floor tips tend to wear over time and will scratch a wood floor. I once accidentally applied full body weight, which broke off one of the underside notches, rendering the stool unstable. Other than that, these things are pretty much indestructible and cost just a couple dollars more than they did in the 70's. I used to be annoyed the stool wouldn't fit easily in the standard hardshell case I used at the time, but today's well-padded gig bags have large pockets that will hold it snug and safe from the instrument.

title: My "Worst of the Vibes" award - scroll to see other alternatives tested
comment:
I wanted to like this pedal. In the store, I tested it beside a couple other higher priced vibes and decided it sounded "good enough" at the price point of $129. Then I took it home and realized it was totally just boosting the bass and stripping out the mids and highs. That sound might be good in the bedroom, but on stage will get lost in the mix. I also noticed a real defect in the effect anytime that the intensity is set above 50%. So I went on a "vibe pedal mission" buying and testing several -- and in the end this was my least favorite vibe - at any price point, once I compared them side by side.

My gear used for testing: Fender American Deluxe Stratocaster. Mesa Mini-rectifier amplifier, Mesa Rectifier 2x12 cabinet with Celestion V30's. Pedal train pedal board, voodoo labs pedal power power supplies.

My evaluation is:

PROS
- Standard 9V center negative power supply, with jack correctly located on the back where it doesn't conflict with patch cables.
- Controls - simple, easy to see the settings
- Speed is "toe tweakable", at least kind of...
- Quality construction, metal case, decent switch, etc.
- Acceptable footprint - slightly larger than a boss, basically a single pedal size.
- Has an LED "on/off" indicator

CONS
- The sound - which is why it came in last place
- Massive bass boost, major tone muffler stripping away mids and highs
- Second issue with sound -- with intensity > 50%, an annoying womp womp womp of a bass note below the overall sound. That bass note doesn't seem to come from the sound being played, and is a defect in the design of the effect. You have to hear it, then you just say...Oh. That's bad. With the intensity below 50%, the sounds are ok (other than the EQ effect of boosting bass and
- LED does not pulse rate
- LED is VERY bright blue -- its obnoxious unless you are outdoors in bright sunlight.

SUMMARY
- They just did an update to this pedal to remove the goofy disco graphics on the old version, and updated the marketing messaging to shift from calling it a uni-vibe style pedal to calling it a rotary speaker simulator. Why they didn't put their energy into the TONE is a mystery. Sound wise, it boosts bass and muffles mid and highs. It also has an annoying womp womp womp of a rogue bass note below the tone your guitar is creating, anytime the intensity is set above 50%. That noise is not apparent when the intensity is below 50%. It's just a tone killer....really changes the EQ of the signal much too dramatically. Last place in my uni-vibe comparisons. Not recommended.

--------------------
Other pedals tested
--------------------

Shaky Jimi Vibe - A steal at $60 -and my number one pick based on sound and features, not price. A great pedal, doesn't take a lot of space up, standard power supply, good visibility on control knob settings. Does not add or subtract frequencies to the signal or add noise. This is the only pedal in the uni-vibe group I tested that I can say that about and it's why it's my #1 pick. Might have slightly less intensity capability on the univibe effect as some of the others - but seems like enough to me! Overall I LOVE this pedal at under 60 bucks. Only downsides I see are the LED does not flash the speed rate, and the speed control is top left which makes it hard to "toe tweak" with my right foot. Quality seems ok, but time will tell on durability. Some users reported issues - but at this price, you could buy two and have a backup, and you would still save $60-80 over the price of most uni-vibes.

- MXR Univibe M68 - This is a really great Univibe pedal -- great physical form factor, and sounds fabulous. It's a tie with the Shaky Jimi in my book, and has an ever so slightly brighter sound. But because it costs about 2X the price, it comes in 2nd place to the Jimi in my evaluation. This is a great vibe and worth checking with your own ears.

- Fulltone Mini Deja vibe -- highest quality construction (as expected at $200). The large knob in the lower right corner for speed is great and allows toe tweaking -- very useful on a vibe pedal. My complaint is that this vibe colors my tone - it boosts the mid range tones while maintaining the crisp highs and slightly losing the bass. This probably helps the sound stand out in a mix, however, I'd rather that my vibe not provide EQ because when I switch it on or off, I don't want to have to compensate at my guitar tone knobs to try to get back "my sound". I was able to match exactly an authentic original Uni-vibe sound with some adjusting. It seems to kind of muffle the pick attack or compress it also. I have a love-hate relationship with this pedal after about a week of testing it. Some days I hate it...but I like the build quality and the speed control. I originally liked that it was one of only two pedals that didn't strip the treble off my tone -- until I realized the mids were boosted, the bass was cut and the pick attack was somehow tweaked. Mixed bag, and at $200 shouldn't be.

- Dunlop Uni-vibe -- Cool stainless steel construction. Knobs were hard to see settings. placement of vintage switch on the back of the pedal was easily forgotten at worst and awkward at best. "Doublewide form factor takes a bit more space, and Non standard 18V power supply takes double slots from my power supply brick. Sound was very good but did also color my EQ sound by boosting the bass, and muffling the mids and highs slightly. On the one hand, this sound matched a lot of recorded uni-vibe sounds I've heard. On the other hand, once you strip the treble its hard to put it back in the mix. The seller had a 3 day return policy (3 days??!!) so I returned it and they lost the sale. Otherwise, I would still be thinking about this one. I was on the fence about whether it was my winner. I was able to match a true uni-vibe sample, but the treble was slightly lacking and bugged me. At $220 I'd have to love it to keep it.

- Voodoo Labs Micro Vibe - Liked the form factor, the simple controls, high visibility knobs, ability to toe tweak controls, standard 9V power supply, and placement of all jacks. Can get a very lush, even excessive univibe sound except the bass is very boosted, and mids/highs are cut. The EQ effect just kills the pedal for me. It went back. Not my pick at $150. In fact, this is 2nd from the bottom on my list.

- Dunlop ROTO-Vibe : A good choice (perhaps the only choice) if you MUST have expression pedal controlled speed adjustment. Didn't color the tone - and that was a problem on most other uni-vibes tested. Limited speed range and awkward controls placement were most annoying to me. Intensity range seemed less than other pedals too. Large footprint if mounted on board, but also offers the ability to take NO space on the board by not mounting it. I didn't like that when you switch the effect on, you are automatically at high speed due to the mechanical nature of the design. Some awkwardness in the placement of controls and power plug vs guitar cables when plugged in. Std power supply a plus. LED indicators good and flash the speed. Durable construction although the mechanical nature means in time something will need lubricating and you may need to replace internal gears or pots. Overpriced 1.5 to 2X in my opinion.

- Other general modulation effects. Univibes are effectively a phase effect - without the swooshy space-age edge on the sound. You can probably modify a modern phaser pedal to get the univibe sound which is like a vibrato plus analog chorus combined. A modern phaser is close, a modern flanger is much too space-age sounding. Vibrato pedals can capture the vibrato aspect of a uni-vibe and I'd recommend the behringer vibrato at $25 if you like vibrato sounds. uni-vibes are normally used for their "chorus" setting, not the vibrato setting. Didn't care for the Behringer time machine.

title: Squier hits it out of the park.
comment:
I understand the skepticism about Squiers, and there's a time when it would've been warranted. These days, the lower-end stuff is vastly improved, and I'd argue that the better Squier axes -- like this one -- rival MIM Fender without the accompanying price tag.

This guitar arrived with a flawless finish (the photos don't do it justice, by the way), no fret sprout, and reasonably close to in-tune. The Floyd Rose-licensed trem and locking tuners are fussy, but once they're dialed in, this guitar stays in tune better than one with a standard trem. The sound's lovely, and while I might eventually swap in a nicer set of humbuckers, it's not as though I feel I *have* to. The neck's comfortable and not too dive-y, too.

I lucked out getting this guitar as backstock from ProAudio Star (via Amazon) at a price that seemed too good to be true (but that, thankfully, arrived as advertised). Even if I'd paid full price, I'd still have been perfectly happy. If you want something that's a bit outside the usual Strat sound but that still has the Stratocaster feel, you really can't go wrong with this guitar.

title: Fantastic delay pedal!
comment:
I bought my first effects pedals forty years ago...an analog delay cost me $300! How times have changed.
The Aquarius sounds fantastic. I run my analog synths through it, and often the delay is almost as important as the synth itself. There's just something about using pedals that adds some character that is often missing when using delay plug-ins and VSTs. The Aquarius can be totally clean, which is great, and the analog and tape settings do what you'd hope they'd do. (I love listening to those long echoes slowly dissolve into mutated weirdness!) The other settings all have distinct personalities as well.
The looper mode works great with a little practice, and it can be a perfect copy of what you sent in or a chorused or "tape" sounding version. Nice touch! You can add overdubs, and even still use the delay while playing along with the loop. It can record loops up to 300 seconds. That's pretty long! Fun stuff. Everything else on the pedal works and reacts like it should.
I do have a couple of beefs wit though. First, even though it has stereo inputs and outputs, it sums your input signal to mono and keeps it there. So no stereo loops. And second, in ping ping mode, it doesnt keep your signal in the middle, but pans your dry signal to the side...even when inputing stereo audio. Odd and not most desirable.
The build quality seems rugged like it needs to be, and the handsome blue lights adorning the sides can be set to flash the delay time, stay lit, or be turned off completely. The knobs are solid and very smooth turning with a little resistance...important when very small increments can make a big change in the sound. You do need to supply the power supply yourself: 9 volts, negative/minus center. I found a nice one here on Amazon for ten dollars.
I give the Aquarius five BIG stars...it's a winner! Flexible, rugged, looks and sounds great....and it doesn't break the bank! Excellent buy for me. (Special props to the seller and Amazon for getting it to me a day earlier than estimated.)
I hope this review helps you decide if it's the right pedal delay for you...it certainly is for me! It's a perfect compliment to my analog keyboards. Have a great day!

title: Neat starter kit
comment:
There's a lot of neat stuff here. Sure the unit feels like cheap Chinese plastic, but it works!

Pros:

Plug and play. Note that the manual is very light. What you want to do is install the software first, and only plug the unit in once the included software is up and running. Then it should auto-detect, and in fact the software has a theme to match the board- very handy. On my Windows 10 laptop I had the unit plugged in first, and I had to change USB ports to get it to re-detect after the software was installed.

Drag and drop. In the included software, drag two tracks onto the virtual "left" and "right" mixers, and you're running. There's separate gain and tone controls for each side, a slider to fade, and the included MIDI-drumpad-style buttons can do multiple functions. You can insert sound effects, loop, and more.

Smart software. The DJ software will automatically detect BPM (or, speed) of imported tracks and sync drum loops or sound effects, so anything you load in should be "on beat" automagically. I added a couple of the include riffs and recorded a decent dance track right out of the gate, my first live performance would only need a few minutes of edits to be a releasable studio track. Love it.

Light show. Okay so it's not exactly complex, but by default it flashes in sync with the beat which is really fun if you're just tooling around in a dark room, playing with your synths and samples. Great way to have a personal party, and there are more configurations.

Cons:

No computer output. The unit does NOT pass the audio back over the USB cable, so you MUST take the audio output from either the 2.5mm headphone jack, or the 2xRCA jacks on the unit. Neither of these is a truly "professional" connection, but they both work. USB output would have been very useful.

Cheap Chinese plastic. I really with they'd have spent a few more bucks on materials. Maybe a good metal case. And a replaceable connector. But, it works.

Overall:

I've just gotten into this kit, but I recorded a decent track literally just dragging my tracks in and hitting record/play. Queue up a couple drum tracks and effects, and I've got something that's a little bit of editing away from a studio-quality dance track. So it works, and it's powerful enough to take someone doing some home recording up at least as far as releasing their first mix tape. That's pretty good for a cheap little unit. And if you want to turn the lights off and get deep into your own beats, the light show is pretty nifty. If you knew a younger person who was looking to break into the DJ biz, they'd love this unit. Or if you're just a hobbyist noise maker playing with some tracks and synths, you'd love it too. Recommended for beginners and starter musicians.

title: As everyone seems to agree - this is a great learning tool.
comment:
I played the drums when I was a teenager. I think I still have my set of sticks once used by Larry Mullen, Jr., but I gave drums up when I realized they were not going to be the focus of my life, and I was never going to be Cindy Blackmon or Sheila E. When we took the Ingbelle tabletop drum set out of the box, I sat down right away to try it out and realized I was way behind on my practicing. Still, I was able to make a judgment about this set, and that is: it's a great learning tool.

At the risk of stating the obvious, when your budding musician decides that drums are the instrument for them, it's not easy to get the full-sized equipment and find a place for it and set it up (then everybody buy ear muffs). Even if - maybe especially if - they haven't decided it's what they want to do, this set makes it easy for them to develop even the tiniest interest or ability, without major monetary outlay.

Of course the Ingbelle drum set has all kinds of bells and whistles, and is fully compatible with Musical Instrument Digital Interface devices. Without relisting all the features already mentioned and fully described on the product page, I will say I completely agree with all they claim this not-so-little tabletop set can deliver. I think my favorite feature is going to be the option for the musician to use headphones and play just for her own enjoyment. At least for now. Well worth the price!

title: Perfect Alto Recorder for a Small Hand
comment:
This is an excellent recorder especially for someone with a small (ish) hand. I've been playing a soprano C recorder for several years now. I'm getting pretty good at it but am not a professional in any way, shape or form! I heard that the sound on the alto F recorder is much nicer than the soprano so I decided to buy the Aulos 309A alto recorder. According to all the reviews I read the two best recorders are either the Aulos or the Yamaha. In the end I decided to get the Aulos because most said the high notes were easier to get and that the sound quality was great. It is! The Aulos has a really great professional sound to it. I will NEVER go back to the soprano after hearing the alto.

But none of the reviews I read said that the Aulos is actually longer than the Yamaha. And for me...this has made all the difference. Stretching as far as I can, from my index to my pinkie finger is exactly 5 inches wide (on my right hand).

I really struggled trying to get a clear low F note on the Aulos (all holes covered). I could reach the holes but just barely. It was a struggle. So I decided to buy the Yamaha YRA-28B III alto recorder. When I measured both I found that the Yamaha is a ½ of an inch shorter in length than the Aulos. They are pretty much the same around but the Aulos is a little longer. I also measured the distance from the bottom two holes to the 4th hole up (holes that pinkie to index fingers cover on right hand) and found that the Yamaha is exactly a ¼ of an inch shorter in length than the Aulos. The Aulos is 5 ¼ inches and the Yamaha is exactly 5 inches. What a difference that shorter length between the holes makes! I have absolutely no problem covering all the holes and the low F note is much easier to get. And because I'm not struggling so hard to reach the holes I'm playing much better on the Yamaha than I did on the Aulos. So for anyone with a small hand like mine I definitely recommend the Yamaha over the Aulos.

I also want to say that if your hand is larger you will also be able to play the Yamaha with ease. And I found that I actually like the sound of the higher tones better on the Yamaha than on the Aulos.

title: I could rate this guitar five stars easily or I can rate it one it's kind of confusing
comment:
The pros -Value for the money seems pretty good yes beginners can play this but I'm a 33-year veteran and I think it plays great Id gig with it. For stock pickups they sound pretty good really good cranked up with some heavy distortion they sing as expected for a high output pickup but you can also back them down with the volume in the tone and you can find a nice array of different tones the tremolo is pretty much useless if you planning on doing some heavy dive bombs but for just mild trem dives it's not too bad just don't be too hard on it cuz it will go out of tune majorly out of tune the 16 radius neck is very nice for playing lead I haven't played an Ibanez in a lot of years since 2010 when I had a real nice RG was in the 700 class. It reminded me of how much fun that guitar was to play although I'm a serious Jackson lover with the 12-16 compound radius necks I could see myself playing a Ibanez again.

The cons-quality control is a real issue now I should have returned this by all means it has a knot in the middle of the neck in the very back thus making the truss rod almost not existing. It's already tightened to take the massive back bow out of it to make it play as the company knew this neck was bad but sent it out anyway, further making the truss rod useless now seeing that it's already tightened about to the max. Although it still plays good this will become a serious issue after a while my son plays it so hopefully he's not too hard on it and it'll last a while but honestly because of the hassle of returning things with Amazon I chose not to but I really should have.I will put further pictures up to explain where this knot is and what it's doing to the neck when you loosen the truss rod to zero it has a massive back bow I'm a professional luthier so I really know what I'm talking about to anyone looking to purchase one of these give it a real good look over and make sure your neck's not like this it's probably a one out of 100 chance but it seems that I had to get that one out of the hundred. To a luthier that also builds guitars the biggest no no of making necks is having any type of wood protrusion,knot,blemish or any kind of real imperfections you just don't use that wood but these guys pulled the El cheapo and really stuck it to me with this one honestly I really believe I'm going to go ahead and write the company and explain the problem in detail and what the problems are and I expect them to give me a new one I will update on that one at a later time. I hate to have to give this awful review but I have to be truthful and honest I happen to get the one that was a lemon but honestly it's a damn fine sounding lemon.
To be continued

title: Basic - and NOT robust
comment:
I had purchased the black finish version of these Grover GP800 strap locks, but the chrome, gold, and nickel finish versions all have the same unacceptable shortcomings and design flaws.

PROBLEMS:

(1) I had no choice but to remove the Grover GP800B guitar strap locks; the button behind the bridge was wobbling - and I cannot afford to have that break during a performance.

(2) The washer and nut were *frustrating* to install and use with *thick* guitar strap ends. Darned near impossible, in fact. 😠

SOLUTION: Schaller S-Locks. Their big, beefy nut is not only simple to install, it also has a little set-screw. You can hand-tighten this thing, and it will *not* move.

BONUS: Instead of using "Phillips-head" (cross-head) screws, Schaller's S-Lock anchor screw heads have *hex* sockets. Not only does this eliminate stripping any screw slots, but this makes installation *fast* and trouble-free.

I should have purchased the Schaller S-Locks the *first* time, but now the problem is permanently solved.

MORAL OF THE STORY: As with pizza sauce, fine musical instruments, and cookware, "you get what you pay for." Don't cheap out, Amazon customers - skip Grover's flawed products and buy the Schaller S-Locks, instead. 😃

title: It's Fun
comment:
I've only had this pedal for about a month... so i write this without fully exploring all of it's potential.

First thing's first... its a BOSS stomp box, so durability and design aren't really necessary to talk about. The only thing worth noting on that topic is that this pedal does eat batteries, even from just moderate use and leaving it unplugged when you arent playing. There are 3 solutions:
1. Buy a power supply
2. Remove the battery entirely when you arent playing
3. Deal with it and keep buying batteries (right now, i've chosen this route)

Aside from that, all i have to talk about is pure functionality and quality. Both of which this pedal excels at. The "T. Arm" mode is easily one of my favorite modes with this pedal. I treat it like a poor man's whammy pedal. It's cabable of pitching a note up or down 2 octaves and you can adjust its speed. For example, i've found if i set the pedal to pitch up 2 octaves very slowly then play some power chord up near the upper frets and jump that chord down the neck while "throttling" the pedal, it creates a *race car shifting gears* kinda sound. On the other end, its fun to set this function to pitch up 1 or two octaves very fast and just mix it in with some soloing. I've even tried this with some basic rhythm stuff which gives it a little more attitude.

The Pitch Shifter mode is also very fun... i very quickly fell into the habit of keeping this mode set at one octave down with the orig/efx mix leaning toward the efx... this gives the guitar a very rich, bassy tone. Add a little wah to it and it gets even more interesting. The other three modes, i regret to say, i haven't toyed with very much. I'm the type of guitarist who likes effects that push the guitar sound to extremes. But the manual of course lists a few presets for these modes which i have tried and they sounded great; just as they were described... it's just personally they didnt inspire me very much.

Another thing worth mentioning is that some of the functions for this pedal are very clearly suggested to only be used with single notes and not chords. So naturally i had to see what would happen if i played some chords on those settings... and sure enought weird stuff starts to happen. In the case of the T.Arm mode, if you play a chord and then pitch it up it starts to jump around a lot which i found to actually be kinda cool. Once i get that to happen i like to start bending the chord and it gets even more crazy.... add wah, feedback, delay..... you get my drift.

Long story short... this is a pedal that you take out of the box and spend countless hours turning the dials every-which-way and trying anything you can imagine. It's LOADS of fun!

title: The Most Versatile Karaoke System There Is
comment:
I've looked for many karaoke systems and finally came upon Singtrix. It works by using a mobile device to stream karaoke songs through easy to find karaoke song sites. The bundle comes with a high quality speaker that has bass and treble control. The real fun is using the signal processor. It tunes your voice and applies voice effects to match whatever songs you're singing. You can go to the company website and download more pre-defined effects. They are typically named something close to the original song like "hooked on a feeling" is "got a feeling". It comes with a platform to hold your mobile device but it best for holding smartphones. I rank it a 4/5 only because it would have made more sense to include a tablet support platform...easier to read karaoke lyrics from a larger screen. If you do use the supplied platform a 7inch tablet works almost perfectly.

An extra tip, if you have a android based device using kit kat 4.4 you can mirror your screen using google chromecast to a big screen for everyone to follow along!

title: Versatile travel guitar with a beautiful voice.
comment:
After spending a few weeks now with my Ibanez piccolo guitar, I can recommend this punchy little songbird without any reservation. At first, I was disappointed by a few small cosmetic flaws I noticed (spotty body finish, glue residue on tuners and neck joint, scrapes on plastic body binding), but my disappointment quickly disappeared once I started to play it. What a voice! It's hard to describe what it sounds like; not exactly like a mandolin, yet not exactly like a guitar either. It's in a class of its own, and it sounds beautiful with a sustain that goes on forever. And it has plenty of volume, perhaps not enough for a concert hall, but enough to fill your living room when you give your next party recital.
I must admit, however, that it might not be the ideal instrument for everyone. The nut is very narrow, which places the strings very close together. To play it well, you must be able to adapt your technique. Having previously played ukuleles with four strings spaced widely apart, this was at first a challenge for me...but challenges such as this makes one a better player in the end. The payoff from this effort will be the creation of beautiful music that this unique little guitar is capable of making.
In terms of versatility, this piccolo guitar deserves 10 stars. Tuned to ADGCEA, I can still play my ukulele chords (low G) on the top four strings as I gradually pick up the chord shapes for the guitar. I've already arranged a few songs for both ukulele and guitar; while it's confusing at first, it's manageable (just don't play them back to back :) ). The main point here is that you can play both ukulele and guitar chords on this piccolo guitar, and this versatility opens the door to entire libraries of songs and music written for guitar, as well as arrangements which you may already have for the ukulele.
One final plus to mention about this piccolo guitar: steel strings. It comes from the manufacturer pre-strung with D'Addario Phosphor Bronze Extra Lights. While I've been playing fluorocarbons on all of my ukuleles, IMHO there is nothing in comparison to the sound of steel string acoustics in general and on this instrument in particular. Depending on the type of music and how its performed, this little piccolo guitar can generate sounds that are downright haunting-- e.g. try playing "The Wreck of the Edmund Fitzgerald" fingerstyle on this and you'll know what I mean. I've been playing folk tunes and some classical pieces so far, but I can imagine this guitar would be in its perfect element voicing Celtic music.

title: This is the best and cheapest on Amazon
comment:
Firstly, there is no other Blue Yeti shockmount for $9.99 on Amazon. That should be reason alone to try this out. Secondly, you should buy a second one!

Pros: My cheap $8 Neewer shockmount held this and my Blue Yeti Pro no problem. The shockmount is metal, so it’s very sturdy. The wingnut to adjust the angle of the shockmount is heart shaped, which makes it much easier to grip and tighten. This shockmount is black, so the heart shape is easily discreet if you’re worried about that, and honestly, the heart shape is more functional! The wingnut does not feel like it will break off either. It is metal and also feels sturdy. The 5/8 screw to attach to the Yeti in the shockmount is actually permanently fixed into the shockmount so you never lose it! And it fits perfectly, unlike some other reviews for other shockmounts and their adapters not fitting in correctly. Both the shockmount and the 5/8 screw turn and screw smoothly, with no threading problems. Lastly, this shockmount is black, so it looks really classy if you’re using a Blue Yeti Pro or Blue Yeti Blackout.

Cons: The joint where you adjust the angle of the shockmount seems a bit stepped, where if it’s not in the right groove it will sink s little until it’s in the right pocket. I’m used to tightening and that’s it, but here you have to make sure you’re in the groove. Also, the shockmount is as big as my face. All Blue Yeti shockmounts are that big—but just to let you know—it’s a big round saucer! So if you’re looking for a more minimalistic setup, this will give you more real estate on your desk, but it will look huge on your boom arm. Lastly there was no 5/8 to 3/8 adapter included (required to attach to some boom arms), but I have a ton so that’s not a really a con for me.

title: User Friendly, Great Price and Endless Possiblities
comment:
After being used to analog I wanted to learn and hear the sound created by digital without a huge learning curve.
This baby is exactly that! If you're used to digital, you'll be pleased, and if not with some patience you will be
off and running in no time! The possibilities are seemingly endless, a massive library of tone, amp simulation
and effects. The percussion section of the unit is the same, you can use presets as far as drum beats, steal
part of an installed drum beat and add it to a loop, or Tap the pads and play all or sections / fills needed to
fit your needs.
Though I am still in the learning curve phase, I am recording easily as I learn which is great!
I suggest you read reviews, watch videos and hear what this unit can do. You get a LOT of bang for your buck!
Lastly and not at all a negative, being an "Old School" musician, the "warmth" of analog still is king. That being
said I searched that very term "Warmth" using digital, and there are many tips and hints to come as close
as one can to achieving that warmth.
If this analog warmth is a goal to be reached, the Zoom R8 is totally capable in my opinion!

title: Unbelievably awesome. Love them.
comment:
I just moved to a very dry desert climate (Nevada), and wasn't thinking about problems that may be caused by lack of humidity when I bought a box of Rico reeds to join the local community band. I haven't played in about 3 years. Well, with this dry air, my clarinet sounded terrible with cane reeds. I thought I had to buy a new mouthpiece, or maybe I just reallllllly sucked after not playing for so long. It took me a while to think about the climate and wonder if that had something to do with it.

After reading online about advances in technology in synthetic reeds (I had one for my oboe 15 years and hated it), I decided to bite the bullet and reluctantly hand over $15 to my local music store for a Fibracell reed. Honestly, I was expecting to HATE it. Little did I know! This thing is ******AMAZING!****** It sounds tons better than the expensive cane reeds I used to buy a few years ago! And you don't need to keep it constantly wet, it just does its thing the moment you pop it on the mouthpiece. I'm in LOVE and will never buy a regular reed again. Fibracell has heightened my ambitions and now I'm looking into joining the university wind symphony because it just sounds THAT GOOD. If you live in a very dry climate, this reed is a must. I bought the 2.5 and it was too soft, so I upped it to 3 and that was the sweet spot. I have a plastic Yamaha clarinet by the way.

title: LOVE Diamond Painting!
comment: I have bought approximately 20 of the diamond painting kits.  Yes, the instructions are bad, but there are videos on you tube that are very helpful.  The pens work fine, you just have to stick it in the glue first.  The creases will come out.  They suggest you unroll the canvas and place a heavy book on it for a few hours.  I skip this step and find that one the diamonds are in place, the weight of the diamonds will remove the creases.  ALSO, some of the canvases come with heavy white paper on it instead of think plastic wrap - if you get the heavy white paper, be careful as you remove it as the glue can stick to the paper rather than the canvas.  If this happens, just press the paper back down and try to remove the white paper minus glue.  It takes a bit of time, but is doable.

title: Nice modern update of the humble string winder
comment:
Like many people, I imagine, I’ve had the same basic plastic string winder for years (decades in my case) and it does the job reasonably well. It fits most guitar tuning pegs and doesn’t really fit bass tuning pegs (kinda works at a weird angle).

At no point have I felt the need to buy a new one, and requested this expecting to be underwhelmed but..

Ernie Ball nailed it.

The larger handle is more comfortable to turn (and turns more smoothly).

The acoustic pin remover is a better shape and better at getting under the pins.

The head fits ALL my guitars and bass tuning pegs. See pics.

The only downside is that it’s slightly bulkier and doesn’t fit in my guitar toolkit pouch as nicely but.. I damn well squeezed it in because this thing is a keeper.

Can you get a cheaper string winder? Yes. But they’re crap.

Get this instead.

title: Great Low Pass Filter
comment:
Based upon the circuitry on the classic Moog module, this low pass filter delivers great sound for a reasonable price. Having three CV inputs is a great feature, potentially freeing up a mixer. As one would expect, the filter self resonates using regeneration (resonance).
The three position range switch is a great feature, as the ranges are roughly an octave apart. If you use the apply the filter to noise with regeneration maxed out you get a near sine wave. Changing the range switch will transpose the part up or down an octave.

title: Noise generator, dirty amps.
comment:
I found these due to the stellar reputation of the original version. I'm guessing the MkII (New Version) was a cost saving effort by JBL, and they are a mistake.

Cons:
These speakers emit substantial white noise when powered on. This is true for me with no signal source connected, even when plugged directly into a power conditioner will all nearby electronics switched off. It's not an interference issue, nor a dirty power/signal issue.
The bottom few clicks of the volume control mute both the noise and any sound output, and are useless. The white noise level is substantial at max volume. I use them at the middle volume setting and the noise is noticable but not usually annoying.

Pros:
Detailed sound. More than sufficient bass output when using them as studio monitors. I switched the bass down a notch for my setup.

I didn't return these because they will suffice as starter monitors for me. I decided I can tolerate the noise level. For those seeking good value, these might be an option (mine were $133 each new), but it seems like JBL screwed up big time with the MkII. If you're looking for high quality electronics, steer clear of these.

title: very refined guitar
comment:
Playing and working on/building guitars for 30 years. I own afew REAL vintage strats and telecasters, also many midrange epi's and other imports.
I can say that this jazzmaster was the BEST out of box guitar i have ever seen. The finish is thick,high gloss. The maple fretboard is much nicer the my 1975 strat. The tone is much nicer then my trad lespaul and has a wider tonal range.

One thing i have not seen mentioned in other reviews; this body is like it was carved out for a seated with footstooled,jazz player. It literally lays against you like a molded glove! I cant believe this style was rejected by early jazzplayers. This new design that is minus tremolo but adds stacked tone/vol for each pickup. That is much differant then the strat styled controls of other teem model jazzmasters.

Some complain about the design of the hardtail bridge.....this is nonsense, the bridge is every bit as sturdy as my 1998 US telecaster deluxe.....albiet a differing design. It stays in tune perfectly and there is no need to 'modify it' like you need to do with the tremolo models.... And frankly do you really need the tremolo??? Fender tremolos have ALWAYS sucked so unless a guitar has a floyd rose or a kahler, its gonna be a constant pain.

I recently bought a 50's tribute gibson les paul...900bucks! It could not hold a candle to this 200buck jazzmaster. I took it back!
Now is this guitar good "for the money", a phrase i hate. I would value this guitar in the 450-500 range if handed to me to be priced. 200 is the street price if you look around. Incredible!

Now remember as mentioned i build/repair guitars, i love to tinker and mod......i frankly cant find one thing to change/improve on this guitar.

Pros; if you are a seated,raised foot player, you will not find a more comfortable guitar.
The stacked pots reduce knob cludder,they are responsive non crackling and have wide range of tone.
Neck and body finish is flawless. Nut was cut to perfect height and finished nice. Pickups were semour duncun design and bares the SD name on pup covers. Strat style jackplate was solid and goodquality. Tuners are average and do the job fine. The neck is fast and finished in a very nice vintage yellowing gloss,much nicer then my 1975 strat. Frets were finished nice and seemed pretty level already. This is a great guitar for ANY level player and is priced for ANY player! i play solo jazz,funk and sometimes old metal.....it does them all great with its huge coil pups.

Pull the trigger but shop around!

title: ... a guitar for low key doodling and this is perfect. The price is actually unbelievable for the quality ...
comment: Wanted a guitar for low key doodling and this is perfect. The price is actually unbelievable for the quality of this product, it feels like it's worth over $100 easy. When I first opened it the strings were wound up but had no tension, and it took a few days and a lot of retuning before it reliably stays in tune. The biggest drawback for me was that the 13th fret was slightly too high, resulting in buzzing when I played the 12th fret on the EAD strings. I just took a sander and shredded it down until the buzzing stopped. I kind of ruined the fretboard for the 13th fret doing it but its not a huge deal.

title: Very disappointing
comment:
I've used Schaller strap locks for years, without any issues.
I noticed that they had replaced the old strap lock design with the new "S locks" and was super excited!
Got them installed on my guitar with no issue and thought they were great.... until I inspected the design.
The new "noiseless" design is only noiseless because it is a pain in the rear to actually move the strap locks once they are on the guitar. You are more likely to rotate the strap lock on the strap rather than actually rotate the strap, due to the insane grip between the strap lock on the strap and the button.
Also, the finish is chipping away! I've had these for about 3 months, and have not abused them in the slightest, yet the finish is chipping!
And, to top things off, on both my PRS and Kramer I put these on, the lock always unscrews on the body of the guitar. I make sure to tighten as much as it will go, however, after a few weeks, it's unscrewed again.

I wanted to like these, but with all the problems I've had, I will not be purchasing these ever again.

title: Excellent keyboard, put USB in front!
comment:
Great value, and a marked improvement over the old 500 and 505 models. The speakers produce a richer sound tone, and the display window is much clearer and easier to read. Additional features over the 505 include one-touch "auto-accompaniment", where you can try different background rhythms to your recorded notes (jazz, rock, salsa, etc) at a single touch of a button. There are a few more voices to choose from, and the "sweet" sounds are more realistic than on the old models. The unit looks better as a whole, being a sleek silver instead of a dull plastic beige. Also, the controls are a lot more "touch friendly", especially the selection "spin wheel" that lets you choose features much faster than the older models did.

Connectivity is much simpler than on the 500, requiring only a simple USB cord instead of a special MIDI cord.

The one downside of this keyboard is that a USB drive is the only portable media compatible with this unit, and unfortunately they place the plug-in port in the back, which make it somewhat difficult to reach over and plug the drive in while you're trying to play and select. Because of this, I take away a star, and hope that in their next model, Yamaha will put the USB port somewhere in FRONT where it's easy to reach and plug the drive.

I actually recommend this unit over the DGX 620 (Yamaha's highest available model at the time of this review). All features are exactly the same on the two models, except the keys in the 620 are weighted to as close to a real piano as you'll get. Yamaha calls them 'Graded Hammer Standard' keys.

The 520 does not have the GHS keys, but the trade-off (besides a considerably lower cost than the 620) is a lot of weight...the 620 unit as a whole weighs 19 lbs more than the 520 unit. So if you're a traveling musician like me and have to load and unload your keyboard all the time carrying your instrument to your gigs, you're actually much better off with the 520. Get the 620 only if you want a more realistic feel and don't want to move the keyboard a lot.

Overall, an excellent instrument...and if you have any of the older models, I highly recommend an upgrade!

title: everyone who buys this is already planning on buying it
comment:
I bought this to go over the corresponding "Fender Pat. Pend." ashtray bridge to make a knockoff Esquire out of a Squier Affinity. It performs the task admirably. When received, it was a little deformed and didn't fit correctly. Just a slight squeeze with the hand was all it took to reshape it to snap on to the bridge as intended.

I'm not actually sure how much this actually works to reduce feedback, hum, or microphonics, and it definitely doesn't suit modern playing styles where people pick or strum really close to the saddles, but the way I play I find I have a nice place to put my palm down while fingerpicking closer to the neck. Plus it looks super cool. I've always liked the early Fender basses with the pickup covers, and I definitely like the look of this pickup cover as well.

Since my retrofit of the guitar left behind some ugly holes, it performs the added function of covering up the obviously Squier-y elements of the guitar, like the old bridge holes left behind by the longer and wider Squier bridge. I'm convinced that Fender made everything on Squiers just a little bit bigger than their Fender equivalents so you couldn't ever retrofit their guitars with Fender parts and pass it off as a Fender. I'd recommend if someone else was making an "eSquire" that they shouldn't pass this up either.

title: Good, But It Could Be Better
comment:
I ordered this unit to use with my Positive Grid Spark Amplifier.

This is a nice hands-free wireless controller for your Spark amplifier. This pedal controller will enable you to change presets, turn on and off effects, and more. After testing the wireless footswitch, I found that the battery should last for at least 4 hours. The footswitch is constructed well with a hard plastic shell and the switches should last for years if used properly. I found the pedal connects easily and I never had any connection issues.

Suggestions for improvement:
I would like to see this unit upgraded to have an LED readout to indicate the selected settings.
The unit is not hard to set up and use but I would prefer it to not require the use of a cell phone to set up. I will admit that the Spark app is very useful and easy to use.

Overall, the unit is nice and does what it is made to do but with what is out there today I expected a little more. If you need a simple footswitch for your Spark and you don't mind using a cell phone or tablet when playing you may like this.

title: A very playable, very lightweight MIDI keyboard at a very attractive price
comment: I was looking for a lightweight stand-in for a very heavy (50lb!) 76-key weighted-key Ensoniq synth that I've been using as a MIDI keyboard and this was just the ticket. It arrived this morning, I unboxed it, plugged it into the USB port of my old Macbook Pro, set the MIDI input of the software synth that was already running on the Mac to the Nektar, and presto! it worked right away. It has a very reasonable unweighted-keyboard feel and is generally pleasant to play. I tried a voice in the software synth that uses velocity input and it worked fine -- very responsive and easy to control over a wide dynamic range. It's very compact -- barely larger than the keys themselves, and seems quite sturdy. Also nice is the fact that it uses no batteries or AC adapter, just USB power, so setup is really simple. I'm looking forward to being able to go to rehearsals without needing a wheeled dolly to get a keyboard there.

title: Not Just Useful for Guitar Read On!
comment: Boss EQ-200, based on reviews I wanted to give it a try. However, my first intention was to use it not for my guitar but as a Microphone input EQ. I’ve been looking for a dedicated microphone EQ and didn’t what to have to purchase an entire amp system or a unit of larger size that only adjusted Low – Mid – High range. Based on some Amazon buyer feedbacks, I figured I give it a try. I can report that it worked great and I was surprised on how much I was able to improve vocal output. It did such a great job with the microphone I decided to buy a second unit for my guitar.  Using it with guitar I found it was much more difficult to fine tune and shape the sound of the guitar output to my satisfaction. I think if the slider had twice the travel it likely would be a big improvement. Still, it’s was obvious that with more practice it likely will be a very useful tool. Also, the fact you could save 4 setting as presets was Great. BUT WAIT! Roland/Boss just updated the firmware so that you can now save 200 presets. For me that much more than I will ever use, but you can program the unit to have much less so that you don’t have to scroll through more than you need.  I do feel it’s a bit over priced for an EQ pedal but based on the reviews and it’s feature it’s a nice unit to have. It might be even be THE Unit to have.

title: Worth is just for the low-latency output alone, even if you don't record
comment:
I got a new Windows laptop recently, it's a 10th gen I7 which is fairly respectable so I was surprised to find that the audio-processing latency was horrendous out the box (with the builtin wsapi device). I improved it a bit by switching to ASIO4all but I was getting a lot of crackles and quality degradation when I switched to low-latency settings.

This little audio interface solved all those problems. I actually wasn't even expecting it to, since I only bought it for the purposes of recording, but it ended up killing two birds with one stone. Even if I don't use either of the recording inputs on this, just the fact that I route my computer output through it dramatically reduces my latency when doing effects processing in Ableton or modular synth in VCV Rack.

As for the recording itself, well I can't speak too critically about it because I am not a pro recording engineer or anything. But I will say that I haven't noticed any problems at all in terms of hissing or anything. On the other hand, the 8-input Tascam interface I bought before this crapped out super quickly and stopped registering any input at all. So, as long my M-Audio continues to just work, I am happy with it.

comment:
El micrófono Rode Lavalier Go es la solución a los problemas de sonido en tus videos, te permitirá tener un audio limpio con un buen manejo de graves y agudos en cada grabación, dispone de 1,5 metros de amplitud de cable lo cual es perfecto para llegar hasta el bolsillo del pantalón.

Este producto funciona muy bien con el Rode Wireless Go 2 y con cámara que tienen la entrada Jack 3.5mm, pero debes saber que esta versión no funciona para usar con teléfonos celulares o entradas de audio que dispongan de canal doble de recepción de audio y reproducción de sonido.

Recomiendo tener cuidado de no jalar el cable con mucha fuerza debido a que no es un cable grueso y podría dañarse con cierta facilidad si se enreda con algún objeto, es mejor usarlo siempre debajo de la ropa para evitar posibles percances.

Subí una revisión a mi canal en YouTube Lozanojk de estos Micrófonos para profundizar en su funcionamiento y accesorios que complementé su uso.

title: Polarity switch - makes this universal pedal a good choice

This pedal has a polarity switch, so it can be used for either style keyboard.

Just stunning. almost 3" wide with a soft padded leather backing so the comfort level is high. As with other buyers the only drawback here seem to be the chemicals used to condition or tan the leather material. I've bought 4 now and the room they were all in really REEKED. I'm already very chemical sensitive but It was just so strong I started getting nauseous in my music room. So I filled the sink with hot water with baking soda and threw em in to soak for about 5 minutes. The oil slick on top of the water was markedly noticeable but after taking them out, wiping them down and then rubbing them with coconut oil the smell is gone and the material is soft to the touch with no toxic chemical smell. These are incredible straps that are worth every penny. Just give em a good cleaning before you stink your house up tho.

title: What a great addition to our online ministry!
comment:
I'm the pastor for our church, and after using a wired lavalier system for about two years it finally gave up the ghost. It started out as little glitches with sound quality and connectivity issues here and there. it eventually came to a point we needed to buy a new lavalier model. I had been looking at other models both wired and wireless.

Since my wife and I sometimes deliver a message together I opted to go with a dual lavalier setup and the wireless model for simplicity and conveyance. We struck gold with this bad-boy!

The Tunerismer Universal Wireless System has a great range, the sound quality is perfect for doing live internet broadcasts, several people have personally commented to me about the dramatic boost in the sound as well as the quality of the sound in my weekly sermons/messages.

The hard case allows us to keep both lavaliers charged up and safely protected. the case even has a real time charge indicator so I can see the power level at a glance. Another convenient feature is that we can keep the iPad/iPhone connected to power even while performing a live broadcast with the charging passthrough port build into the receiver, SUPER COOL!

I had used an expensive lavalier set up in the past and figured i was going to have to do the same again, man was I wrong! This inexpensive but NOT cheap model has truly become a God send to our ministry. I know it can deliver solid results for you in many different applications as well you.

I hope this review helps you to make an informed and wise decision.

God bless.
Pastor/Personal Trainer,
Michael Rodriguez

title: Overall, I like the Power Pins. They're staying on my Taylor 414 and I may purchase another set for my Taylor T5.
comment:
I like the Power Pins. I installed them on an older Taylor 414 without any problems. From years of use and changing strings often, the bridge pin holes were showing some wear and enlarging. I like the fact that the Power Pins will stop that wear. There may be some tone change, but if so, I think it is minimal. I did not make recordings before and after installing; I think attempting to keep all variables the same, strumming and picking exactly the same from before samples to after would be nearly impossible.
Examining the Power Pins installation, I believe the angle of attack of the string to the bridge may be slightly less. The string did enter the holes in the saddle at a point closer to the bridge; but again, I mentioned wear of the bridge pin holes, so the angle was changing with time, this actually may take it back closer to the original angle of the string to the bridge, when the guitar was new. As the advertising claims, it does make string changing simple and fast. No searching for bridge pins in the carpet. Overall, I like the Power Pins. They're staying on my Taylor 414 and I may purchase another set for my Taylor T5.

title: The Best In Its Class
comment:
In 35 years of playing, I've tried most options for powering pedals, including high end multi-units with isolated transformers. For powering a single pedal, the Godlyke is the best--quieter and smaller than the other popular brands, plus you get great customer service.

This adapter can be used with Godlyke daisy chain products. I bought mine so that I could leave one plugged in at my home practice space, while I have another in my gig bag to powere my small board with four pedals. In my experience, no daisy chain solution is as quiet as a unit with isolated outputs, but the Godlyke system comes very close.

If you need to power one pedal with a Boss-type adapter, or you need a spare for your daisy chain, this is the best adapter you can buy.

title: Yeah, it's a freaking cool guitar.
comment:
Edit2: Just noticed this review 2 years later.. Guitar has held up like a beast!! Got a new 120W Randall amp, and pedal board, the sound is just magnificent. Definitely still my "desert island" guitar.

Edit: So now I've had it for a while, I thought I would follow up. It has held up well, used it for a show as well. A few things I didn't mention before: It did come with slightly high action out of the box. This is actually how I prefer it, but many probably would want to lower it. I actually have a '79 ML too, which came with far too low action out-of-the-box.
The case is huge, *barely* fits into my Civic. If you have an even smaller car, I would recommend a gig-bag or something smaller to just get it around. The exterior also scratches up super-easily, but the case has held up very well otherwise.
Price-wise, owning 2 MLs, I would direct people wanting to really own one to the 79 MLs. That one is similar in quality, and is altogether a good-looking and great-sounding guitar. I'd only recommend this specific one if you really are a huge Dimebag fan, and/or you absolutely love the paint-job, like I did. And are willing to save up for it. Again, don't know how many are left, saw a bunch on eBay too.

Now I don't know how many of these are left, considering there are 333 overall, but I'm bored, so I'll write a review. I have #35, for what it's worth.

Before I continue, I am just going to be up front about the things I do not like:

1. The graphic design does not cover the entirety of the guitar. It covers the front and back of the body, and the front of the headstock. The sides of the body, the neck, and back of headstock are a flat gray. Doesn't really matter since nobody can really see there, but I wish it was all done for the sake of completeness. As far as I can tell, the original Sledge had the back of the neck covered in the design.
2. There was a (very small) chip directly underneath the floyd rose, when I received it. Basically underneath the the knob that you turn to release the end of the high E string, there's a little metal piece sticking out the bottom. It looks kinda like the bridge was pushed down hard enough for that piece to chip the paint there. Again, almost unnoticeable without heavy examination. I just am a little insane about things like that :)

Now I am a huge fan of Dimebag, but I generally don't want anything to do with replicas, or musician series guitars. *Especially* the ones modeled to already look dated, haha, but that is my personal preference.

This guitar caught my eye, however. Not only does it have an awesome design with a personally-pleasing color choice, but it uses a 6-in-line headstock. I never was a huge fan of the ML's horned headstock.
According to Dean, this is the only ML so far (Besides customized ones, obviously) that uses such a headstock. Regardless, I am a fan.
The rest of the overall design, I have so far liked. It IS a big guitar. The length of the guitar is a bit more than I expected. Not an issue for me at all, just observation from someone never using an ML before.
I also love that the input jack is on the face of guitar, instead of the side, like it is on my other main guitar (92 Warlock NJ).
Let's see what else.. the back of the headstock says the number of guitar, mine being "35 OF 333". I don't see a serial # anywhere. Maybe underneath the bridge plate, although the # out of 333 is a pretty good serial indicator already.
I don't see a place of manufacture either, although I'd honestly assume Korea or Japan. The screenshots of the display model from Dean say "Korea" on the headstock. I don't really care, seeing/hearing the quality of the guitar, but it would be nice if it was made in the US. It's not in the catalog, so I can't see if it had the "Made in USA" badge next to it.

Now I am certainly not good at describing the tone. It is certainly superior than any of my other guitars, even my Warlock. It has a *much* superior sustain to what I'm used to. Has a more rounded tone, bass and treble. It is easier to procure pinch harmonics using this guitar (Not surprising, if technically a Dimebag guitar), but it doesn't like to produce normal harmonics as easily as my Warlock.
Floyd Rose seems to work fine. I need to work with it a bit more first. I have always been a little lax in using it, because the bridge is slightly damaged on my Warlock.

If you're going to play tracks from Cowboys From Hell, or Vulgar Display of Power, this guitar will certainly do the job.
I usually use a Boss Metal Zone, and a Peavey Vypyr 15, and it sounds great to me. Both distorted and clean. I know most of the tracks from most of the Pantera albums, and the guitar nails the tone on most of them. I also play a lot of Alice in Chains, Megadeth, Metallica, anything from alternative to death metal.
Generally, though, I create my own music, and that is exactly what I plan on using this guitar for.

Now accessory wise, this guitar comes with a great case. I don't know if I'd trust it on an airplane, but it is a great case overall. Pretty nicely sized compartment inside. I am used to only gig bags, to be honest.. The case also locks, and comes with two keys.
It came with hex keys for various maintenance on the guitar, unlocking the locking nut, etc.
Also, as mentioned, it does come with a certificate of authenticity, signed by Vinnie Paul. Not sure what I'll do with that... but I'll keep it safe.

Oh, and price. Well I certainly wouldn't mind it being cheaper, but it is quite a bit cheaper than a Les Paul Standard, and many other guitars, and is still a kick-ass guitar. The graphic design and fact I am a huge Pantera fan didn't hurt... A normal ML can probably get most of the same tone and overall quality, although I wouldn't know (yet). After using this, I would be comfortable recommending other Dean MLs.

Alright, well hopefully that was decently in-depth, and being helpful doesn't hurt either ;)

title: Great quality XLR to USB converter. Strange problems with Windows, but easily solvable. Recommended!
comment:
One thing to remember with this device when using it with Windows 7 is that you need to set it up properly for it work as beautifully as it can. The recommended settings are:

Set Windows recoding volume for the Shure X2U to 5 from 100. This will reduce gain within Windows and allow the Shure to manage its own gain and volume as it is the best at doing so.
Set volume on the Shure to the very top, and set gain to two from the very top (as if, the very top was 10, set it to 8). This will ensure that the Shure generates no internal noise and results in the Shure ignoring background noise from the mic.

With gain set at 8, the sound picked up in Windows is crystal clear with no distortion. The Shure only includes a USB 2.0 port, but I suppose that bandwidth demands from this device weren't enough to push it USB 3.0. Make sure you are using good quality XLR cable as well as the USB cable that comes with the device, this will ensure you have the best quality.

The device itself feels premium and is metal. It also has a headphone jack, though I never used it.

There are other reviewers who mentioned the Windows problem. If you want verification on how to set it up properly, read their reviews. I am highly satisfied with the Shure and wouldn't hesitate to recommend it.

title: Mute switch, Battery operated, large display, sturdy
comment:
I bought it used orignal non-plus version , i was mainly looking for a mute switch to be used outdoors. The model is 5 years old. its only one i found that uses battery. Dumb decision by company to save few dollars by not providing an easy access to battery inside. assuming every body has a pedal board loaded with pedals and onboard power supply. the same assumption led them not to design it to NOT consume battery power when in true bypass mode, which is easy thing to do and in that case battery would last years if not months.

I want to use to mute outdoors battery speaker with just a battery powered multieffect head fed through a wireless guitar dongle for a busker. Mute comes in handy when recording bass and chord loops on a looper.

i was interested in the info about how long battery would last and can i usea usb power bank with pedal power converter cable without introducing extra noise if the internal battery runs out, and it was no where to be found.

I used a lithium rechargeable version of 9V battery thats says 600ma and put the pedal in bypass mode to see how long it would take to drain the battery. I did put the pedal in tuner mode few times. After 24 hours the the pedal was still working but LEDs are little dim when tuning.

Using with the 5000ma power bank, with usb inserting power cable cuts off the power to battery and switches to external power and that also works fine with no noise. that should be able to power it for months i guess.

The mute switch works wether ther is any power or not , which nice so i only loose tuner function when power runs out.

The foot switch click for me is a feature as outdoos i get strong resistance and a click to know it switched without out looking at the display.

Did nt like the small shar/flat symbol but standerd tunning doesnt need one.

also very responsive, actually too responsive , good for real time note displays, so it looses the note too quickly or you keep strumming the string, people tend to like ones with small delay in letting go of previous notes. is it seems more stable while they tug at the tuner peg.

overall it works for my application, better than my other choice , orignal polytune noir
, was too small for individual non pedal board mount, with clickless switch and smaller slightly slower/stable display.

Being a non gigging guitar player I think I probably don't stress these babies out like a pro musician would.  I did throw these nines on my stratacopy and they bend amazing and feel good even on my fingers.  Overall,  if you don't like spending the money for these (I have found much cheaper strings out there) and are a hobby player you probably really don't absolutely have to have these, but the price doesn't bother me much.  If I was playing for my supper though, I would definitely think about using these as they have been on my guitar for about a month now and I normally noodle around for an hour or two a night and they are still holding tune great.

title: Cheap is as cheap does.
comment:
The price is the only appealing aspect to this purchase, which can scant be called a violin. Obviously it is incredibly cheaply-made, but the construction of this glue-and-composite monstrosity is an insult. The strings are solid (as in, not wound like a normal string - just a cheap gauged metal wire) and sound raspy and just dreadful. The bow came heavily fringed but, if the strings were better, it would be usable. The rosin cake is harder than rock candy, came shattered, and even after vigorous scoring with a serrated butter knife, it wouldn't coat the bow properly or at least uniformly. The bridge feels spongy like cork, and it isn't arched, fitted, notched, and possibly not even sanded. I've gotten free bridges of much higher quality. I won't even get into the physical defects or other issues.

My point is that, true, you get what you pay for. But if you're considering this then you or someone you'd be buying it for is obviously a beginner, and an "instrument" like this is not only terribly offensive to all sensibility, but would be very discouraging for a new student. If you're this cheap/poor, at least get a cecilio or medini. You'll spend less in corrective "upgrades" to make them usable.

Or if you're willing to drop a few bucks for an instrument you will want to actually play, and for a very long time, check out the bunnel g2 on amazon.

Update: after only a few days, the hairs of the bow quite literally unraveled in mid-stroke. I've never seen anything like it. I have decided to keep the violin because it produces a sound that is so comically ridiculous that it has gained cult affection from friends who say it's so terrible that it's great. First violin I've had that actually makes people laugh out loud.

Now that I have, I can't see being without one again even though I just received it today. I have several other Line 6 products in my studio such as the POD Hd Pro X and a Spider V240, with foot controllers for them that have the expression/Volume pedals built in so I didn't think that I would benefit too much from buying the pedal for my Helix. Well, I stand corrected. Having only spent a couple hours with this pedal, I can already see my future presets being much more versatile (crossfading from 1 amp to another or from clean to distortion etc...). The Pedal is very sturdy and matches my foot controller. I like the fact that the tension of the rocker can be adjusted and the toe switch can be adjusted making it harder to accidently press while standing or easier while sitting. If I decide to add another expression pedal in the future, it will absolutely be another one of these giving how well built they are.

title: Almost perfect - A Comprehensive Review
comment:
TL:DR if you're on a budget or just starting out as a vlogger and want something better than your phone's built-in mic, this is what you want. It's sturdy, surprisingly high quality, and crystal clear.

There are so many cheap lav mics on the market and in the Amazon Vine Voices list, I honestly struggled to pick just one for review. For reference, I'm a musician and ex-YouTube creator, looking to eventually get back into video content creation, but I have a lot of experience working with both video and audio.

Build Quality: Stunning. When I first opened the box, and dumped the draw string nylon bag into my hand, my very first thought was "Oooooh!" Yes, it's a nylon bag, but it feels thick, stiff, and protective. The same goes for the microphone's cable, soft woven nylon that, while not the thickest cable in the world, won't easily tear under normal use as far as I can tell. Time will tell whether the 3.5mm jack will be a point of failure (like so many of my cheap braided headphone cables have been).

Audio quality: Tested with my Galaxy S10+, audio quality is crystal clear. I didn't find myself clipping with the mic literally an inch from my mouth. The windscreen didn't do much to stop plosives, but it did soften them quite nicely. And they shouldn't be a problem if you attach the mic to your shirt. I was intentionally trying to clip the audio at the time.

My only complaint with this mic, and why I called it "almost" perfect is that it's very sensitive and may pick up things like movement.

Additionally, the spring clip is extremely strong. I actually had to use quite a lot of force initially to pry it open. This could be a pro or a con depending on your situation and your finger strength, but I don't see the spring wearing out before the mic itself does.

Bottom line: as I said at the beginning, if you're on a shoe string budget, but want to start vlogging, or if you need a spare interview mic that you can record audio to a smart phone or camera, this is a good bet.

If you're looking to use a professional audio mixer or USB audio interface with a 6.3mm adapter, you're not going to have any luck, as these kinds of microphones require 1 to 10 volts of phantom power (depending on the microphone). Which means if you plug this into a straight XLR to 3.5mm converter, you will literally blow out the microphone.

Rode makes an XLR to 3.5mm down-converter for $25 right here on Amazon, called the Rode XLR+. I've ordered one to test with this microphone as well as another, older 3.5mm microphone I have lying around. I'll update this review with my findings when it arrives, but since that is NOT the intended use case for this microphone, it will NOT affect my review of this product. This is pure, academic curiosity.

title: Great feel and tone for my Jazz sound.
comment: I'm primarily a finger-style bass player, but I got my start as a child with acoustic guitar played with a plectrum/pick.  When I play guitar, I usually play melody and riffs.  I like the tone of electric guitars in jazz recording, so I'll play with a lot of neck pickup and tone rolled down to achieve what I hear (I can get the same tones on a Strat, LP, and Sterling Slio 3).  I normally play with Jazz III picks and Big Stubby Purple (3mm) picks. My current favorite picks are Jazz III Ultex, but I'd like something a little thicker.  These 208s are my new favorites for playing gypsy style jazz licks.  They are easy to hold, and are grippy enough for me, as I often have sweaty hands.  The are larger than a Jazz III.  I like the attack these provide and the thickness pushes strings with ease.  I will have to get a larger quantity in the future, these have a place in the toolbox.  I don't stick to a single style pick, I like to experiment and see what different tones my picks will offer and choose accordingly.
