title: Not a perfect case, but for the price it's very good
comment:
I am a professional violist and needed something to replace my much heavier 1993 case with broken zippers. I do a lot of gigging and have some gigs requiring air travel in my future, so I wanted something easier to transport. Is this case perfect? No. Why did I give it 5 stars? Because for this price point (Editing to add: I paid $248 for it), it is a 5-star case. If they were charging the price of a BAM, I would probably give it 3 stars. But at half the cost of a BAM, it's certainly a very good choice.

PROS:
6 latches, including 2 that lock - although I've read elsewhere that the newer ones are phasing out the locks. I think that's a shame! I like having a case in which I can lock my phone and keys during rehearsals. Also, I think the lock would help guard against accidental latch failures as some of my colleagues have experienced with other cases. But having 6 latches is great - I don't think there would be any accidents.

My 16 3/4 viola with a very wide lower bout just fits in the case, width wise. Length-wise there is plenty of room to spare, and I adjusted the bottom bumper to fit snugly. I feel like the scroll is not as well protected as I'd like - I'd feel a little better with another bumper around it, but I may get some foam and fashion my own.

The straps attach very securely using a screw type clip (not a cheap plastic clip) along with a secondary wire to act as a backup in case the first clip fails for any reason. It seems like they really paid attention to detail when it comes to making sure a failure of the case doesn't send your instrument spilling. Having lived with a viola case for 23 years with no straps option at all, I am thrilled to be able to throw my instrument over my shoulder and backpack it.

I must have cello envy or something because I love the look of this case. It looks very cool. We borrowed their Bach, why not their case?

CONS:
It does have an odor as others have noted. I am very sensitive to smells (my teenaged daughter can spray perfume in her bedroom and I can be in the basement and know it right away by the headache that ensues.) This isn't a great smell, but it doesn't overly bother me.

The top is a bit wobbly when it's open. Doesn't bother me, and doesn't go anywhere, but it's not solid like my old case. It's a side effect of the cello-shape.

No place to store music - but this may be a pro for me. My case with all my crap in it weighed 14 lbs. Having huge storage meant I threw the kitchen sink in there. The storage is limited with this case. It's just enough room to put my rosin, some extra strings, humidifier, earplugs, pencil, and shoulder rest (more on that in a bit). I'm getting a separate tote bag for my music, although not the one that attaches on the back because I think that looks weird and defeats the purpose of lightening the load. My plan is to put the viola on the back, tote bag in hand or on one shoulder.

The clips that the straps attach to on the case are sharp. Before I put the straps on, I slid the case across my table to make room for some music, and they put a nice scratch in my table. It's kind of a cheap IKEA table, but still. With the straps on now, I'm not sure if that buffers the clips so it is less likely to scratch things, but I think I'll just lift it to new locations instead of sliding across wood surfaces from now on.

Probably the biggest con is the bow storage. I have one carbon fiber for outdoor gigs and another fairly expensive bow. The bow storage consists of a little fabric bag for the tip, and a velcro enclosure for the frog. I don't think it would have cost that much to put in something a bit more solid for the tip of the bow. That said, I don't think the bows are going anywhere, but it just pains me a bit to store my bow in such cheap accommodations. But I think they'll be safe enough.

Last, my Mach one shoulder rest does not fit in the case. I can either put it in the tote bag I'm getting, or I may switch back to my old Kun collapsible, which does fit. I'd been thinking about going back to Kun anyway and this may be the push I needed. But any large, inflexible shoulder rest will not fit in the storage available.

Again, not a perfect case, but for the price compared to a BAM, I felt it was a very good value for the money. I know I listed a lot of cons, but they are all pretty minor things to me at this point. I'm really enjoying the case and glad I got it.

title: This review is for Monster Instrument/Speaker Cables in general
comment:
I've been performing for years and the last thing you need when on stage is having an equipment failure. That's why it makes sense to pony up the extra money for a Monster cable as instrument cables are a common failure point. I've been using all Monster cables for both my guitar and my bass rigs for the last four years and I have no complaints. These cables are very sturdy, even though I have had a couple fail on me. One time the cable end fell apart, which I think was a fluke, like a "lemon" chord, the other time was due to abuse caused by me. ( Bass cabinet loaded into the truck with the speaker cable still in the back, got laid on it's back and the cable end got bent off)

The best thing about Monster cables is that they have a life time guarantee, really no matter WHAT happens to them. In both cases I simply took the broken cables up to a Guitar Center and they were replaced, with no questions asked or even receipts. This is my first time ordering from Amazon, but I understand that GC will replace these cables the same way, either way, the lifetime guarantee and the sturdy build of these cables as well as the great sound with no hissing, pops or cracks make them a sound investment.

Keep in mind when ordering that Monster makes their cables instrument specific. They have one cable designed for bass guitars, another designed for guitar and another designed for your head to speaker. For the best sound response, pay attention to this when ordering.

You might pay a little more but the peace of mind and the great quality of these cables is worth it. Also, for this 21 footer GC charges about $49.00 so I saved $15 or so going through Amazon.

title: Music teacher who says NOOOO!!!!!
comment: As a professional orchestra teacher who has done battle with these instruments for years, I'm actually quite shocked to see how many positive comments there are.  I wonder how many of these reviews are actually coming from qualified music teachers?  Please ask your music teacher's opinion before purchasing one of these instruments.  I actually take one of these violins to my parent meeting each year to show the parents what NOT to buy.  They are low quality wood, the strings break within months and sound like metal, the bow hairs fall out, the case has a zipper that usually breaks within a year or less, and the bridge will not stay put, is too high and too thick, and usually does not even have grooves carved to hold the strings.  The pegs are my biggest complaint.  They WILL NOT hold the strings in place.  I know it's hard to believe online reviewers are who they say they are, but if I can prevent one future budding student having to go through the hell of thinking they are failing learning their instrument when it's actually their instrument that is failing them, then it was worth the time I took to write this review.

title: 3/4 of a good product
comment:
I've bought two Pedaltrain units - a PT2 and a PT Jr - and in my experience, you should always simultaneously order some other mechanism to attach your pedals to the board. The Velcro supplied with the PT grips well to itself, as it should, but its adhesive backing is just not up to the job. The velcro just doesn't stick to the PT's cross-bars or to the backs of pedals well enough to prevent them coming loose. This is an especial problem with narrow but heavy pedals where the stress on the adhesive is concentrated. I ended up using cable ties to keep my pedals from falling off, which kinda defeats the purpose of the rig.

I've now resorted to using Godlyke Power-Grip Pedalboard Velcro Alternative tape. Not only is the tape-to-tape connection better, but the adhesive is too. For this reason you should take it as a given that the PT will end up costing you at least 15-30% more than the advertised price to work well, since you have to buy a better tape as well.

And because of that, you have to set up the bridge (instructions are included). So after that, it plays like a dream. Other reviewers have complained about stuff like high action, warped neck, and frets needing to be filed down, but I didn't have any problems there. Maybe I got lucky? I don't know. The tone itself is good for the price, and the resonator is a nice touch. A closed back resonator on a banjo in this price range is rare. If you're looking for the sound of a banjo with the familiarity of the guitar, this Dean is probably your best option unless you've got the $1600 to spend on a Deering 6-string.

title: Top notch and affordable product, paired with stellar customer service.
comment:
I'm extremely stoked about these guys at Donner. You need this pedal in your life. Here's why:

They sent me a significantly more expensive and all-around awesome pedal when the one I wanted was temporarily out of stock. At first they offered to refund my purchase, but I explained that I wanted to try their gear, so they offered me their looper pedal. I could not be happier. They were exceptionally quick with responses and really care about what people think about their products, so I get the feeling that they really put their best efforts into their work.

This pedal is ridiculously easy to use. Just press the button with your foot or finger and when the light turns red, your loop is being recorded. Hit it again and then you can play over it, then hit the button yet again and you're now recording a second layer or overdub.

The best part:

You can overdub and record over a loop as many times as you want. You are no longer a lonely, one-man band. You are a composer/producer with infinite sonic possibilities at your fingertips. Go melt some faces or break some hearts.

Donner asked me to write this review as a token of good faith, but I can assure you that my gratitude and happiness with this company is genuine. They aim to please and do not miss.

P.S. That other pedal was out of stock because it's in very high demand. They sell these things like crazy.

If you lift it by the seat, the seat folds up and you have nothing to grab except for the backrest.  In my opinion, this chair is meant to stay in one place most of the time. title: THE BEST PICKUPS I EVER USED!!!!
comment: I am so happy that I ended up with this set. I'll be honest, I was a little hesitant. I did not want to loose the tone of my baby, first love, and the first guitar I ever personally bought with my lawn cutting, and snow shoveling gigs in high school in 1998, my Fender Stratocaster. I grew frustrated with the worsening hum of the old pickups especially while recording, but I did not want to loose my classic Fender sound either.  I did not want to get just another humbucker set, I already have that in my Epiphone Les Paul. But when i found out i could toggle switch and split the neck and bridge pick ups, I jump on them.  I am so glad I did.  I got rid of that annoying hum all together even when I toggle the pick ups to be single coils.  Next I rarely use my Epiphone now.  These pickups have as much, if not more bite than the Les Paul does (I know, shocked!!!)  The tonal possibilities i get now are endless.  Also, I used to almost never use the center pick up, now I use it a lot.  It has such a warm and inviting tone that works amazingly with an acoustic simulator.  I play just about everything rock including but not limited to punk, grunge, metal, and classic rock, and it can play all of them with ease, and sounds great.

title: Harsh and uninspiring
comment:
This pedal has an unmusical harshness to it. An overdrive should be responsive to you as you play, it shouldn't be stuck in one sound. The tone knob doesn't give you any control over the midrange, so you're stuck with a mid-hump. And the "hot" mode is muddy and unusable no matter how far you turn the tone knob. I put this into a Vox AC15 and yeah, I could get a usable sound in one particular setting (under half on the gain, tone almost all the way to the left), but it was completely uninspiring. Granted, I'm used to the tubes on my amp, so somebody who hasn't had/doesn't like that experience might like this pedal. But I get better overdrive-like tones from my TC Electronic Dark Matter (which is a distortion pedal, not an overdrive.) With this pedal, I couldn't get there and had to send it back.
Yes, the pedal is cheap. But I can't agree with people who say, "for $30 it's worth it." I need gear I can actually use, and if that means saving up for a better pedal that inspires me to play better music, so be it. I think for $50, or maybe even $30, you can get something much more usable.

title: Epic tone, great versility, and top shelf playbility.
comment:
I recently purchased this guitar after an exhaustive search for a new acoustic to replace an old friend who just plain got wore out.

After playing a ton of instruments, I narrowed my criteria. Haunted by that "Clapton Unplugged" tone, I decided that I wanted a guitar with a spruce top and rosewood back and sides, but I couldn't afford the high-end Martins. So after weeks of trying a ton of instruments, I found myself sitting in a guitar store torn between a very nice Taylor 214 and a wonderful Parkwood. While trying to select between them, I grabbed on of these off the wall just to give my hand something to do while I made up my mind.

The next thing I knew, I forgot all bout spruce and rosewood. I forgot about the comfort of the Taylor's auditorium body or the volume of the Parkwood dread.

I'd played other Seagulls before, and somehow the combination of a cedar top and cherry backs and sides didn't speak to me- but all those Seagulls had been dreadnoughts.

Yet those same woods in this shape give a unique tone which has an earthy, mid-rangy soul voice not too different than the spruce/rosewood combo while still providing a clear and compelling bottom end. When strummed hard, it delivers crisp, clear chords that speak with all the authority you'd expect from a well equipped dread. And yet the combination of the generous string spacing the warmth of the cedar top make finger-style playing a comfortable and beautiful experience. This thing is so versatile and would be at home at a bluegrass jam, a folk fest, or any unplugged blue/rock affair. (And you church-going guys would love this for some praise and worship too.)

The neck on the Seagull was every bit as playable as the Taylor, and as a big fella, I found the mini-jumbo to be a perfect fit.

As an added bonus, the rustic sunburst is truly stunning and stands apart from the sea of natural spruce finishes with their yellow hue.

I'm giving it a 4 because the light, satin finish makes it very easy to put dings on the soft cedar top and because they didn't install a strap pin on the neck heel, meaning that I've got to tie my strap on the headstock- which isn't that big of a deal. Tone-wise, this thing is simply flawless. You can find guitars with more bells and whistles to be sure, but you wont find a better sound at any price. And I got it for under $340.

Oh, and one more thing about that price. This guitar is made in Canada- where they have high labor costs and many of the same issues facing American manufacturers. If Godin (the parent company of Seagull) can build excellent guitars in Canada at this price point, why can't American companies do it too?

Bottom line- this guitar is a steal. It easily blows away every other guitar in its price point as well as most instruments costing three to four times as much. I know because I played them all. I would have gladly spent much more to get killer tone and superb playability like this, but thanks to our friends in the Great White North, I didn't have to.

title: One of my most used pieces of gear
comment:
Love this little thing. Use it just about every single day.
Works great in many different situations. Comes in handy a lot more than you might imagine. When i first bought it i was worried i was wasting 100+ $$ on a glorified voice recorder.

Now it stays plugged into my computer for work or it comes with me in my bag nearly everywhere i go. Use it to record music jams, deep philosophical talks, as an amp for a gaming mic, take it backpacking... etc.

The features are limited but very plain and useful. Turn that gain knob (gosh i love that gain knob), turn on the lo-cut and the limiter with a simple button; it doesn't get easier. Pop on a fury windscreen or plug in a 3.5mm mic and Bob's your uncle.

The only cons i can think of: Battery life could be better, be sure to use high quality batteries and don't leave it on if you don't have to. And the pattern of the Microphone pickup is a little wide for some situations. It can tend to pick up ambient and background noise because it's so sensitive. Make sure your recording space is quiet or you might just hear your dog panting in the background of your recordings :)

title: I can't stop playing this thing!!
comment:
//11/18/16 UPDATED BELOW//
Original Review:
This is a fantastic product with immense potential for any musician who loves live synth performance. The Microbrute has always been a tiny beast, and the new transparent red look doesn't change any of that.

First, for folks who really don't know what they're looking at, the Microbrute is an analog monophonic synthesizer - there's no little computer inside telling it how to sound, and it plays only one note at a time. This is all good. To compare it to other types of products in a similar price and versatility range, the MicroKorg and Novation's Mininova are *digital modeling* synths - they're very sophisticated computers that mimic the types of sounds analog synths do by their very nature. The differences are many: The digital synths can play whole chords, and have robust effects processing right on-board, and have extensive software packages available if you connect them to your computer. But the modeling synths have significant shortcomings in comparison to an analog synth like the Microbrute. In order to change settings on the Microbrute, you just turn a knob or move a slider - they always do the same things because they're connected to a specific function. The modeling synths use fewer knobs to do a LOT more, so you have to make sure you know what each knob does in each context. Additionally, the Microbrute can more easily produce authentic analog sounds because it *is* an analog machine. There's tactile, sonic, and emotional value to using one of these.

For those who know a bit more about analog but maybe haven't followed Arturia, the Microbrute and its bigger brother the Minibrute were big surprises when they came out - proof that an outstanding analog synth could be had for a small amount of money, and proof that everyone wanted an analog synth. The Microbrute is in most ways superior to the Minibrute - the Micro has a small patch bay and more options for CV, plus an excellent 64-step sequencer (that's *sixty-four* steps. 8 bars of 8th notes.). On the con side, the Microbrute has had to forgo the filter-specific envelope, noise channel on the oscillator, and a few other options for the LFO. The keys are smaller, too, and the lack aftertouch. They're awfully spongy, too. For my money, though, you won't see me wishing for a Mini. This seems to be the consensus among players.

Some general observations: The build quality on this is great. The 'brute is hefty and solid, the knobs are pleasing to the eye and to the touch. The zero notch on relevant knobs is easy to feel when you hit it. The pitch wheel is quick enough. The patch jacks feel tight and secure. The sliders are great, very precise, and the envelope is nice and snappy. The transparent red shell is really, really cool. If you missed out on the Microbrute SEs, this is one to grab. The sequencer tempo has a very wide range, and is quite precise. It's easy to dial in to the tempo of any other sequencers you might have going. I haven't tried to sync it with anything else though. The tempo tap button works great, too.

On the sound: You have one oscillator that can pump out multiple waveforms all at once, creating a pretty amazing versaitility of sound. By comparison, the Volca Keys (at $150) has 3 oscillators, but they only output sawtooth waveforms. If you watch reviews of these two synths, you'll see what you get and what you lose with each. The rough account is this: More versatility with the Microbrute, but the Keys can play 3 separate notes at once. Both create fantastic lead sounds, but the 'brute can do things the Volca Keys only dreams about. The reverse is also true.

This thing is amazing for the price. You need to know, though, that if you're going for cleaner, more classic synth sounds, that you have to keep the waveform selectors at or below halfway or they start to drive the filter and you start to get distortion. This is a pretty awesome feature, but apparently some folks miss out because they think the 'brute just sounds dirty. Turn the knobs down and turn the volume up!

For people who've been following Arturia, you need to know that this is a Microbrute in a new shell, not a Microbrute SE. Unlike the Microbrute SE models, it doesn't have fancy stacking patch cords, just the standard ones. The SE came with a gig bag as well, which this lacks.

If you poke around the hardware synth scene for long, you'll see people talking about the new, inexpensive analog synths as though they were "toys." A few of them (the Korg monotron line) certainly are. But many more - like the Microbrute and Korgs Volca lines, just to name a couple - are versatile enough to feature in any performer's lineup. Don't get me wrong - if I had the money for a massive modular setup or even a Moog Sub 37, they would already have that money. But do not discount this synthesizer for its size, price point, or cute red shell.

If you want proof of how well these new, smaller synths can be used, take a look at Burg's youtube channel (where he goes by the name ollilaboratories) or some of the stuff by Sequentonal. This is a great piece of hardware.

If you're in the market for an analog synth, this should get a first look. If you like the look of the Microbrute Red, I'd grab one while they're hot.

UPDATE:
After using this for a few months, I'm ready to add some additional comments. I've been borrowing a Moog Sub Phatty lately, and playing with the Moog, the Volca Keys, and the microbrute all together has given me some very interesting results that are worth sharing.

First, playing the microbrute alongside the Moog reinforces the challenge of playing the minikeys. A quality keybed and full-size keys makes the microbrute even more fun to play, and I've often enjoyed playing the 'brute using the Moog as a keyboard controller. I have narrow fingers, so the width of the keys isn't the greatest issue. It's the length of the keys. Minikeys force the player to play arpeggios with the fingers in a more constrained position, whereas the larger keys let you play much more naturally.

Second, and on a very positive note, I tested my claims about the ability to get warm, classic synth sounds on the 'brute by running the Moog through the left channel and the microbrute through the right channel on my mixer, then attempted to create similar sounds. The basic waveforms sound very similar when the filter is wide open and the waveform mixers are kept below 50% on the microbrute. The 'brute's oscillator is very, very good. However, when you start introducing multiple waveforms into the oscillator, you end up with a very complex sound that can't be reproduced on the Moog. On the other hand, most of the sound combinations on the Moog CAN be created on the microbrute. They're more difficult to accomplish, mainly because the Moog's got 2 VCOs and a sub-oscillator, whereas the microbrute has a single oscillator with complexity out the wazoo. But it's surprising how much you can do with it. One other thing to note, and this should be a surprise to nobody - the microbrute's "ultrasaw" wavefolder thingy that sounds sort of like detuned sawtooth waves is less expressive than either the detuning capabilities of the Moog (with two true oscillators) or the Volca Keys (with 3 true oscillators). Playing with the envelopes on both synths helped me realize that there's a tiny bit of popping with the microbrute with long attacks that you don't get with the Moog, but what do you expect for $300?

Third, the LFO on the microbrute has much more interesting characteristics when you push it into the audio range than the Moog does. This is a subjective claim. However, the limitation of assigning the LFO to a single parameter is a huge disappointment after so easily assigning waveform *AND* filter amount to the LFO at the same time with the Moog. I'm told that the stackable patch cables that came standard with the microbrute SE enabled one to do this, but the microbrute Red comes with the standard cables, as noted in the original review.

As noted in the original review, the microbrute forgoes the filter envelope and a few other options that are present on the minibrute. Two of those options are a random LFO waveform and white noise. The white noise is still not something I miss, but after playing the microbrute and the Moog together, the random LFO waveform and filter envelope really are a big loss. With the microbrute, I can set VCA to gate and use the single envelope to control the filter, but it's obviously more difficult that way. And there's just something astonishingly beautiful about a randomized filter on your lead with swelling supersaw chords in the background.

Finally, I found that there was a bit of truth to the idea that the microbrute is a little bit of a "dirty" synth. It seems to want to make complex, crunchy sounds. The filter isn't smooth as glass like the Moog's, but it also shouldn't be. This isn't a mark of the cheapness of the product, but an important positive characteristic for the 'brute. Pitch bends and long glides don't quite have the same polish on the 'brute. What they have, though, is buckets and buckets of sass. In my original review, I said that if I had the money for a Sub 37, Moog would already have my money. I was implying that I would have chosen the Moog over the microbrute Red. I'm now ready to amend that statement: I would not now want to part with my microbrute in exchange for a Moog. I would miss it more than I would enjoy the Moog.

title: Very Happy After My Saga
comment:
I'm not very happy with Kala ukulele. I started with the gloss mahogany and it arrived with a brace having fallen out in transit. I returned it and bought this one. The sound is poor with a very buzzy and annoying C string. I'm just getting started but I know this one isn't great because I bought 2 others and they play beautifully.

Kala service is slow as well. They were going to exchange the gloss model but it took weeks to arrange and Amazon handled it instantly.

I am pleased with the Cordoba ukulele I bought and the limited edition Kala from Austin Bazaar. Why so many? After the first failure, I decided I wanted to have a backup so I bought the other 2. This one was an impulse based on the looks.

After a month, I can play several chords and few songs. Having the different ukulele allows me to directly compare.

This one is pretty bad and I should have returned it on receipt but I thought it was me so I kept it for a couple weeks. Now it's been about a month and I hate this ukulele. Buzzzzzzzzzzz. KALA OFFERED to send me one string. That just wasn't acceptable. I decided to send it back for service. I will update if it comes back fixed. I still can't believe they would only send 1 string.

UPDATE:
I returned the buzzy ukulele and KALA returned it after "extensive service". It still buzzed. I'm not expert but this was awful and obvious. When I looked inside, I found a split brace. There's the source. A simple inspection would have revealed this. So back again...

UPDATE:
Received a replacement today. KALA sent the wrong model. This has been going on now for 2 months. It's easier to learn to play a ukulele than it is to get a working one from KALA. I am very disappointed with KALA.

UPDATE:
YAY! KALA came through. I now have a perfectly working Ukulele. It was a saga but KALA never stopped trying so I taking my review UP to 5 stars. They sent a replacement and it's everything I hoped the first one would have been. I am very pleased. Thank you Stephanie at KALA!

title: Good Bass, Not the Best Comfort for Me
comment:
These headphones have excellent bass reproduction but are just a bit muddy for my tastes on the high end for music. Midrange instruments and vocals are very good. In general, they seem weighted toward the lower end of the sound spectrum. They excel for games, especially first person shooters.
The biggest weakness for me is on the comfort end, since the earcups are too short for my average adult male ears, so I either need to fold my ears up to fit inside, or part of the pad sits on top of the outer ear, which isn't very comfortable for long sessions. The earcups appear to be round, not oval like most over-ear headphones. They also lack any volume control. The build quality is excellent, with a robust design that should be quite durable in the long run. They came with a black cloth drawstring bag for storage, but had no instructions or spec sheet included. The large phono plug unscrews to expose a standard PC earphone plug.

Not having any experience with setting up to record on my own, I was hesitant about all aspects of it, but let me tell you that within ten minutes I had recorded my first track with my e-kit.
The initial set-up and ease of use was pleasantly impressive, and the sounds that the 2i2 produces are unbelievable, whether you are monitoring through your headphones, or out through powered speakers or a pa system.
I would highly recommend the Focusrite Scarlett 2i2 for a novice, or someone that's been recording for years. You can also pack it up and take it with you for recording anywhere, anytime. I just love my new 2i2 !

title: Adds Music At An Affordable Price.  SEE UPDATE....Dead Battery
comment:
Since there were no other reviews at the time I thought I'd put my rather un-technical review in. I'll preface this by stating I'm no sound engineer or electrical genius. For an expert opinion please go somewhere else. But I know what I'm looking for and usually do enough research to make a sound purchase decision. Now for this item, I had to throw all that out the window because I could find absolutely no reviews or detailed information about this product anywhere. Even the Pyle site didn't help. So I took a deep breath and ordered it anyway because it seemed to have the things I was looking for at an affordable price. I planned to use it mainly for football tailgate parties about six or seven times a year. It probably won't get heavy duty use all year long but I might also use it in my garage while working on projects. It seemed smaller out of the box than in the photos but I was actually happy about that. I'd rather not have to move around a large speaker. At first glance it seems solidly built and could stand up to repeated trips to my football BBQs. I think it would even withstand the abuse from one of my drunk friends if it was knocked over. I don't have any need for the Guitar, Drum and Karaoke jacks and I doubt I'll use the wireless microphone since I sing like a bleating goat. The microphone itself is plastic and probably not of the best quality but I'm sure you could substitute a better one if needed. The unit has an attached trailer handle and wheels which seem useful even though I'll probably put it on a bigger hand cart to move all my stuff. I doubt the wheels would survive the terrain I would put it through so it goes on the cart with the beer and the burgers. I plan to use it with a USB flash drive with mp3 music and after testing it out it works just fine. The volume controls also have seperate bass and treble to modify your sound. There's also an FM radio that searches and pre-sets available stations that works very well. The sound quality is good but certainly not overwhelming. If you want something more powerful you will have to pay more. But for my simple needs it will certainly blast out my music and annoy the other tailgaters. Even at loud volumes I don't notice distortion which is the main reason why I am replacing my old Sony CFO-G700CP Boombox with this Pyle Disc Jam. I'm even able to attach my old Sony Walkman CD player to the AUX jack and it sounds great. Also included are the power cord and three different cables for attaching various players and devices. The disco lights on the front of the unit are fun....for about 5 seconds. After that you'll probably want to turn them off with the switch that's on the back. But kids would probably enjoy them. So far, so good and I'm happy! Now on to the disappointing part....the instruction manual. It's practically useless because it tells you nothing. It's almost generic in that it could be used for various Pyle products instead of just this one. I have no idea how many hours the internal battery will last. I'm not really sure about the charging proceedure although I guess I'll figure it out after experimentation. The charging and full charge lights on the top panel seem to only work with the main power switch and not with the DC 12v switch. It can be a little confusing without detailed instructions. I've e-mailed Pyle about this today and will try to update my review when....or if they write me back. But except for this, I can say I'm happy with my purchase. Hopefully, there will be further reviews by others to help buyers make a sound decision.

UPDATE 9/28/14 - After having this speaker for less than two years the battery has gone dead. Up until this point it worked very well. The unit no longer charges but still works by plugging it in the wall. I've reduced my rating because of this. I know battery operated electronics usually perform worse over time and it would make more sense to be able to change the battery instead of dumping the unit. Unfortunately, these things are designed in a way to make it impossible to do so for the average buyer. So now this Disc Jam becomes just a box with no usable function at a football tailgate BBQ. I can no longer vouch for the reliability of Pyle to make a quality product.

title: Bad Near Field Monitors - Be Ware
comment:
Buyer be ware. I am a professional film composer and bought these pair as an additional listening comparison in my studio. The low end and mids are completely exaggerated and give very muddy referencing feedback. My room is acoustically treated and I have mopads underneath these speakers. Even with the included room control features on the back, they are still very weak.

Your mix will not be accurate if you reference or mix with these. Rather, look at the Yamaha HSM 50 or 80's which is by far the best referencing monitors in their price class.

I am not kidding you. Folks who give this a good review obviously are not professional musicians. If you need a pair of speakers to listening to CDs or play Xbox on PC games on, then they might do.

Dont take my word for it though, go and try them out yourself. And don't let the salesmen influence you, just because they are a brand new JBL product that they need to promote.

Anyhow, the quality is about right for <$10. At 51 inches, the straps are too short for that Jimmy Page / Steve Clark look, but otherwise I'm sure they work fine. Aside from being the wrong prints, they look pretty nice. The feel, however is cheap. The stitching is the only thing holding the print fabric and leather together. This gives me the sense that the fabric will tear easily. The fabric is thin as well as the leather which has that cardboard-ish feel and is covered with that fuzz that rubs off in little bunches.

That said, I still feel that they are worth what I paid, and I would have kept them if the prints were correct.

title: One Defective Sample
comment:
Sample packs are excellent!!! Quality control is very important in a sample pack, especially in a sample pack where you might only get one of something. This variety pack contains 8 picks - 1 x 0.73mm (defective, see attached picture), 1 X 0.88mm, 1 x 1.0mm, 1 x 1.5mm, 1 x 2.0mm (standard), 1 x 2.0mm (jumbo), 1 x 2.5mm, and 1 x 3.0mm sample pick. Lucky for me the 0.73mm pick is the one I was least interest in trying, still, for some it may be the one they most wanted to try. To be fair, I have purchased 100's of Dunlop picks over the years and this is first time I have seen a defective pick, so I can only assume this is very, very uncommon. I have informed Dunlop about the defect, guessing this is just one of those freak things and this defective pick slipped by their quality check. Picks are very subjective to what is best, that is why sample packs are so great!

Update: I contacted Dunlop about the defective pick and they have been very fast to respond to the issue. Plus, I noticed something about the Flow picks. They have a "Standard" size and "Jumbo" size pick. This is why you get two 2.0mm picks, one is Standard and the other is Jumbo. Interesting, there is not much of a difference in size by looking at them, however, you do notice the difference when playing, this is how I noticed the difference, made me look closer after noticing the different feel while playing.

title: Battery Drain Issues Dealt With a Long Time Ago
comment:
The Zoom H1 for the money, is a spectacular performer. Whether using AUTO LEVEL or carefully modulated user levels, with prudent placement, you can get exceptional audio quality.

As for the battery drain issues many have cited here, I am surprised that no one spoke up on behalf of the people at SAMSON and how they responded. I had the same problems with an earlier iteration of the Zoom H1 I purchased. Their tech support people responded to an inquiry with a pre-paid mailer, and sent me back a new unit.

Apparently a small batch of Zoom H1 units had a faulty capacitor, which was initiating the battery drain. I never had this problem again, and I have purchased two additional H1 units, which again, operated flawlessly.

title: Almost a requirement
comment:
This really should be the first additional purchase for your NS7 if you don't already have the NS7fx, it works with itch easily, and allows for per deck FX assignment, both decks and FX can be mixed and matched (Two FX on one deck). The FX are tempo synced so delays, tremolos and such are to the BPM, something an external FX unit is unlikely to be able to do without preprogramming.

Cycling FX can be a little time consuming, and there isn't really (to my knowledge) an FX preview for cue. The bar mounts to the laptop bracket, however if you're unlucky like myself and your NS7 did not come with the laptop mounts, it'll be left to rest awkwardly.

The stripe takes an additional USB port, however, if you're using a USB Hub (unpowered), it'll still function with another device or two. I bought a cheapo Belkin 4 port USB Hub dongle, and was able to plug in my NS7 + NSFX + APC40 into the hub, both the APC40 and NS7 being self powered.

With any luck, the NSFX bar will improve as Serato abandons Itch for its new Serato DJ software package, (NS7 users get the upgrade free).

title: Updated - fatal flaw! Dont buy!
comment:
Update - on my Fender Villager 12-String this capo is not consistent up and down the neck. The neck design is such that this capo will not work on frets 1 through 5. I found the Shubb C3 for 12-string to be a better option for my guitar.
I'll leave the star rating at 4 because other than the price this is a seriously sweet capo.

Further update - I'm changing my star rating because there is a fatal flaw with this capo!
The design of this capo relys on a thumb screw which screws down into a slot on the clamp arm that squeezes against the back of the neck of your guitar. This slot has a plastic or nylon insert that deforms when the clamp is tightened. This results in the eventual loss of the ability of this capo to correctly fret the strings of your guitar rendering it unusable in a very short time. Save your money and buy the Shubb C3.

title: Mediocre and Overpriced as a "Digital PIANO"
comment: This is a revised review. I was originally impressed with this digital piano. It was the first non-toy keyboard I owned. Having had it for six months, by enthusiasm has waned. Here's what's wrong with it from my perspective. There is too much synth and workstation stuff included in the design of this piano. That's fine if you are a keyboardist but this is a fully weighted and graded hammer action piano. I'd prefer if there had been more resources devoted to realistic piano feel and tone. One of my chief complaints is that the Sustain pedals (and you can only use a maximum of 2) are just simple on/off switches. You CANNOT execute half damping, or better yet, continuous control of damping. If you buy Casio's cheap particle board stand, you can get a proprietary pedal assembly that plus into the bottom of the piano with a cheap fragile interface. It still doesn't allow continuous control and I don't want a cheap unadjustable particle board stand to begin with. Another issue with this keyboard is that the piano samples are mediocre at best. I have been forced to resort to the use of external piano sample libraries because the internal pianos are bland and lifeless.  Third, the PX-560 boasts tri-sensor key position sensing. It supposedly does have it but it was poorly implemented. The middle sensor is too high to allow the execution of rapid notes on the same key without allowing the key to travel most of the way back up. That technique works flawlessly on a Kawai VPC1 or Kawai MP11SE. On the Privia PX-560, the notes will sound intermittently unless you let the key come about 3/4 of the way back up. The Kawais work the way an acoustic grand piano does. The Casio does not. So. unless you are a synth enthusiast (who for some bizarre reason wants a fully weighted and graded piano action) this digital piano is not worth the $1200 it costs. If piano is your focus, you'd be far better off putting in a little more money and getting a Kawai VPC1 master controller, a Kawai MP11SE, a Dexibell Vivo S7, a Roland RD2000 etc...

title: Easy to Use, Decent Sound Quality and Range
comment: There are a number of these wireless microphone systems on the market, and this one is of good quality, especially considering the price point.  It comes with 2 transmitters and one receiver that plugs into the USB port on the cellphone.  Pairing the microphone and the receiver is incredibly simple, you just turn on the transmitter, and the LED display flashes red and blue until the pairing is complete, and then it displays a steady blue light.  The set includes one USB-C cable, although both transmitters need to be charged, but one cable is adequate since I already own several USB-C cables.  Unlike some competing sets, this set does not include a case.  Also, on some of these sets, the clip which attaches the microphone to your lapel swivels on the microphone, making it easier to attach to the clothing.  In this set, the clip is fixed at a 90 degree angle to the length of the mic, which slightly limits your options in attaching the mic to the lapel.  I tried the range, up to about 50 feet, with no loss of signal or degradation of sound quality.  The sound quality is OK, certainly not of broadcast quality or something you would use to record music, but for travel vlogging it is very adequate.  The primary virtue of this system is its ease of use, it really could not be any easier.  It has limitations, such as the fact that it does not allow for audio monitoring of the signal, but for a system this simple that is not a huge inconvenience. Please note that the receiver connects to a USB-C port, therefore, this system will not work with an iPhone having a Lightning port instead.

title: Too good to be true?
comment:
So far, these seem like the perfect strings.... I just ordered a bunch of 10's because the 9's feel so sweet like 8's or something thinner... Extremely beandeble... So it suits my style... I hesitantly switched my strings on a guitar that had a few day old GHS Boomers... My strings of choice after the NYXL's D'addario , but I can't use the NYXL's on my strat or it gives me some terrible G string buzz or wobble because of their heavy magnetic pull... So GHS are my favorite go to strings for my strat... I really love the sound and the ability to bend without fear of breaking them... Now, this set of Electro Harmonix is ridiculous!!! I literally can bend 4 semitones without problem... That might even be counterproductive to my hand strenght, but feels super comfortable non the less...
One of my concerns with these strings though... Are that 1) they do not come in an air tight package... They come with a little package of scillica for freahness but no factory fresh strings here...,
I'm writing this only a few hours after installation, so I don't know how well they'll hold up in a few days... I read a review that claims the strings rusted after only one day... But that seems like an isolated inscident as most reviews are very positive on any site you wish to look fotmr the EHX strings...
Right now I am totally in love so I will update if anything changes... My ither concern is that , they really do feel like nylon, very stretchy , therefore they fall out of tube quickly.., I've been bending and retuning and the problem seems to be going away little by little as wuth most other strings...
I tried to do research and find out who really makes these strings, ehat factory... Is it even here in the states? I think so, it is electro harmonix after all...
But if you are in search of a different string brand to try.., I really recommend these...
GHS are the best, no doubt, but these feel better at least.
If you are an Earnie Ball guy, I don't know what to say since I can never find a good sound out of those... Lifeless, brittle sound... Please don't buy Earnie Balls... Get D'addarios or GHS's ... Or these EHX.
I'm ordering their 3 different gauges as the 9's feel ridiculously comfortable to play... People say their 10's feel like 9's .... It all sounds too good to be true... So, we'll see...

title: perfect for online piano class, a Corvid reality
comment:
Corvid has forced us to switch my daughter's piano and violin class online. We tried zoom, facetime and skype using our laptop, phone or ipad. Each time our teacher wasn't quite happy with the sound quality, until we found this USB phone device.

Our teacher at the end can now hear some very minor differences in the way my daughter is playing, too strong or too soft, or whether the piano requires a little bit tuning. She says what she hears is close to professional recording device --- of course we didn't pay anywhere close to professional device prices.

Installation is easy. We plugged it to one of the USB port on our windows 10 laptop and in about a minute windows indicated the driver installation is complete. We switched the sound input to this microphone and then went event-less from there.

The tripod is sturdy but small. In our case, we just have it set on top our U5 piano (perfect in that). Violin setup is a little tricky as the microphone seems needing to stay very close to the sound source (to avoid picking up environmental sound), so we used another tripod to compensate.

title: Good option for the home studio
comment:
The M-Audio M-Track 2X2 C-Series is an audio interface intended for home studio use. On Macintosh systems, the interface is fully-class complient, which means that it "just works" when you plug it in. Windows systems require drivers and since I don't use Windows for music production, I didn't test using that OS.

One thing many people may like is that the unit will sit flat on your desktop and all of the knobs and indicators are visible and easily accessed. I've used interfaces that are controlled/viewed from only the front and its necessary to place the unit at an angle, or elevate it so its eye-level.

There is an instrument/line input, a phantom power switch and a headphone jack on the on the front edge of the M-Track. There's a USB-C interface, left and right outputs and a Line/XLR jack on the rear panel. The top panel has gain knobs and level indicators for each channel, a large monitor knob in the center, a headphone level and a USB/Direct blend control which adjusts the balance between sounds coming from the USB interface and the inputs when they're sent to the headphone jack and left and right outputs on the rear panel.

The M-Track comes with a "regular" USB to USB-C cable as well as a USB-C to USB-C cable, and licenses for several downloadable sets of software. The software suite includes a license for CuBase LE8, C-Series VI Suite and Creative FX Collection. I've used CuBase in the past and it's a fine option for many people. SInce I'm locked into Logic Pro X and Ableton Live, I'm reluctant to install packages that may interfere with my current setup, especially if I switch audio interfaces. I've had negative experiences with SoftTube, which was packaged with one of my FocusRite interfaces and it was painful to deal with.

Using the interface is extremely intuitive. Once you make your connections and set your input levels, you're good to go. My Mac automatically selected the M-Track as the default input and output. The only other thing to deal with is setting the sample rate inside your DAW. I noticed that the M-Track was a bit sensitive to overly-hot signals, so I took care to reduce the output levels of my bass with active pickups before plugging into the interface. If you have chronic level issues, you can get XLR and 1/4" attenuators to knock the signal levels down a bit.

Overall, I think the M-Audio is a good option for the home studio and a great way to produce your own podcast or create music at home.

title: I wouldn't recommend it.
comment:
I want to give my objective opinion. I am a professional guitarist and have many pedals. I have Donner Boost Killer pedal (bought on Amazon), which is a very good clone of Xotic RC, which so far works great. The Alchemy pedal is basically Donner's version of NUX modulation pedal. I've read reviews on it and many people said it worked fine for a few months until it broke.

So I waited for Donner Alchemy to come out (hoping it'll be more reliable) and ordered it from a different website (where you bid). When it arrived, straight out of the box it was defective: on the Vibrato effect, there was no effect on the Deluxe setting, and also there was no way to turn the pedal off once you switched to that setting. Also, I think a couple of their effects are mislabeled, but that's not a big deal as long as you remember which one you'd like to use. So I contacted Donner and at first they wanted to give me some money back if I kept the pedal. I told them it was ridiculous, specifically because I wanted both Vibrato settings (Normal and Deluxe). Then they said I could ship it back and get a refund, but not on the return shipping. First they wanted me to ship it back to China and only when I mentioned that I know from looking at their site that they have a warehouse in the US, they said it'll be okay to ship it there.

Still, I felt like I should not pay for the return shipping, the pedal arrived defective, and I have not done anything wrong. Only after I contacted the customer service of the website where I ordered the pedal, they basically made Donner accept the responsibility for the return shipping. So this was an ordeal for me. Then they wanted me to leave a positive feedback for them. I told them I would leave an honest feedback, indicating what they did right (fast shipping, returning my e-mails on time) and what was wrong.

I will let you decide for yourself. My other Donner pedal is still working fine and it's been a couple months since I bought it (keep in mind their warranty is only 90 days), but Alchemy did not work right out of the box... They probably need a few more years to tweak the quality of their products, but for me right now, Donner is not a very reliable brand.

title: Monster Gain
comment:
I almost don't want to leave a review for this because its so good and I don't have one yet (i.e. want a cheap used one!). The clean sounds are typically forbidden as orange likes to do; despite having some of the nicest cleans I've heard they are very committed to making sure you can't really use them because "rock" or something. Headphone output is pretty bad with the pedal on - with it off and feeding it other preamp pedals its a very passable V30 sim and could definitely do in a pinch, just leave this thing off (the sim is always on).

The high gain sounds this thing can generate are right on the doorstep of the Empress Heavy - but with way more character and a bit less gain overall. It absolutely roars with bottom end girth pedals typically can't get! Pretty high achievement if you ask me. Its one of the most amp-like high gain sounds I've ever heard out of a pedal. It was very reminiscent of playing an OR15 amp at about the 3rd gain stage where it sounded pretty modern, but before the 4th where it got into more vintage metal saturation.

I absolutely want one of these but the asking price was a bit high given its lack of flexibility and the so-so headphone out. If this thing allowed for a low-gain mode to access some clean sounds and for custom IR's to be loaded it would be a total monster!

title: great little drum set
comment:
Got this for my 6 year old rock n roll obsessed son. I have to say, the set is pretty well put together and definitely a HUGE jump up from the typical "toy" drum sets that are sold at other retailers for nearly the same price(if you are looking at a First Act or similar set, skip it and buy this one). You get a bass drum with working foot pedal, two tom-toms mounted, a real snare drum, a floor tom, a working high-hat, and a cymbal mounted to the bass drum, plus a sturdy drummers throne (seat) and 2 drum sticks, and even a drum key to tune the drums.
The set comes mostly disassembled so if you are an elf prepping this for Christmas, give yourself some time. I highly recommend adding a pillow inside the bass drum to give a more professional studio sound.
The highs:
drum sound
build quality
sturdiness
size for a small child (probably up to 5' tall)
looks professional

The lows:
cymbal and high hat sound "tinny" but not near as bad as most "toy" sets
"unknown" if you can buy replacement drum heads
"unknown" if you can buy replacement drum sticks (ie will "real" sticks tear up the drum heads too fast)
bass pedal needs periodic oiling

Once I find out the question about replacement heads and sticks I'll update this review.

Overall if you have an aspiring drummer in your family, this set would be hard to beat!

title: A Truely Lovely Guitar....
comment:
The Takemine G30CE-NAT is a Truely Lovely Guitar. It was a little dirty coming out of the box, it was a waxy like build-up on the fretboard caked on at the edges of the frets. It also had a very small patch on the back side of the neck that was just slightly rough, a good cleaning and polishing pad took care of it in less than a minute. It also had a small section of binding on the backside of the cutaway about an inch long that was worn through the clear coat. Propaply happened at the manufacturer during the final polishing with the indusrtrial buffer wheel. really hard to notice anyway, but two minutes with my polishing pad and you can't even tell. However the guitars overall quality and condition was and is excellent.

I feel it is important to note that UPS delivered my guitar and I was not happy about their service. As it arrived at my home, I had seen them pull up to the house and so opened the door. As the driver brought the package up to the door he failed to pay attention and literally slammed the box into the concrete steps as he began climbing them(there are only two steps). He then pushed the box at me (which was mashed at one end, turned and went back to his truck without so much as a word, let alone a signature. Like I said 'not happy'! Who knows how they treated it throughout the rest of the shipping process! Though it was packed well and nothing was broken.

OK back to the important part of this...the guitar.

I'm not a big fan of the D'Adarrio EXP bronze coated strings that come on the guitar. They are ok strings, though a little rough on my fingers and I find them a little duller in tone than I prefer. Since she needed a good cleaning anyway, I pulled the strings and replaced them with a set of Light Gauge Elixir Polywebs and she sounds awesome! Great Resonance, Tone, Harmonics, Warmth really a very lovely sound, nice bright, clean and crisp.

The Pre-Amp / Tuner is great. Tuner is spot on and she sounds awesome plugged in (though my preference is still acoustic. Easy to use 3-band EQ, nice sized gain knob makes adjustments easy and precise, easy and intuative tunner display, that turns itself off after just a few minutes to save your battery and the quick battery change access cover is a nice addition as well (battery was included). The Pickups are the Under-Saddle variety. In fact the Saddles actually clip into the pickup right inside the saddle slot of the bridge. Direct Tone Feedback...Awesome!

The most impressive part to me was the way she came set up out of the box. 'Perfect' couldn't have been better... Neck was spot on, Saddle adjustment was spot on, and the nut was notched spot on... This gives the guitar a wonderfully low action, nice and fast, easy on the fingers with great inntonation. Really great work. I can do all of that work myself, (and regularlly do so of others as well), I am very focused on the details and strive for the best results possible. I didn't need to do any adjustments to this guitar, she played liked a dream right out of the box.

The Neck is not gloss coated. It is a flat finish with no clear coat and has been treated with tung oil, I love it! It keeps the neck smooth when playing even if your hands get a little moist.

The Solid Spruce Top is beautiful and elegant and still provides great tonal qualities. The craftsmanship is excellent. you can barely see the seam between the two pieces.

The Mahogany back and sides are absoluetly beautiful. This really is a lovely guitar. Do yourself a favor and give Takemine a try, you wont regret it.

The new Thread Through Bridge style they are using is nice and easy to use, though if you have fat fingers youll want a pair of needle nose pliars during string changes to help get the strings up over the saddle(s) as you thread through. its a simple design that looks and helps make the guitar sound great.

The Duel Saddle is an awesome addition that really helps with the innotation and turns the undersaddle pickup into Two seperate pickups so your B and High E string have their own pickup. it really makes those high notes ring when shes plugged in.

I have been fortunate enough to spend some time with a few other models of Takemine recently the GN30-CE-NAT which is Takemine's NEX body style of this same guitar, still beautiful with great tone, just a little smaller body style. Great for someone that find the Dreanought a little bit big. The other was an EF340SC in their higher end guitars (discontinued now) and to this day is probably the nicest acoustic guitar I have ever played. Seriously though they have something in about every price range. Give them a look and give one a try, you won't regret it!

So to sum up...
Issues:
Dirty Guitar, Small Rough Patch on the Neck and a tiny clear coat issue on the back binding. (5 minutes to resolve all of them)
Not a fan of the intial set of strings (personal preference, Elixir Polywebs - Light )
UPS Delivery... (crappy package handling)

Wows:
Beautiful Guitar, Wood, Hardware, Neck...
Amazing Set up right out of the box. SPOT ON!!!
Great Sound! Reasonance, Tone, Warmth, Brightness...Really Excellent!

I am very happy with my purchase! I got a great guitar for the money without having to sacrifice my wallet to pay for a big brand name. Takemine is well know and respected in the industry, you're just getting more guitar for your money.

A TRUELY LOVELY GUITAR....

title: This thing is awesome!!!!!
comment:
So yes, I have been a Boss user since '89 when I got a Digital Metalizer. Now, with that out of the way I will say that this is one of the BEST distortions that Boss has put out in some time. It is VERY versatile. It does a really good job of emulating that wonderful Marshall tone ranging from the old Plexi Superlead/Superbass, to the JCM800, all the way to the modern stuff like the JCM 2000. I heard someone say that live it was terrible, had feedback issues, was noisy, etc., etc., etc. I am sure it is if you stand in front of your amp with the gain maxed out at stage volumes. Here's the trick with any distortion: First find your bedroom tone. Once you've found that, throw that setting out the window because if you're playing loud enough to be heard over a drummer, and bass player then your bedroom settings no longer apply. nor do any claims about noise at stage volumes. ANY distortion pedal will put out white noise at loud volumes because by nature, a distortion pedal and to a lesser degree an overdrive all add COMPRESSION which in turn adds noise.

Happens with a real Marshall, my Peavey 5150, my Boss digital metalizer, my Fulldrive 2 Mosfet if the gain/boos are cranked, and will with pretty much any other distortion that doesn't have a gate/noise reduction circuit built in.

So are there really any negatives with this pedal that i have found yet? Not really. In fact I have yet to find a tone with this thing that I couldn't use either in my studio or live. This pedal even has what I like to call that "cabinet whack" that a cranked 412 gives off in the lower registers.

Everyone mileage may differ, but you owe it to yourself to at least give it a try if you like your distortion with a "British" flavor. This thing sounds scary good. When you factor in the price, it should be a no brainer.

title: Waste of Time and Money
comment:
I have had this guitar for a year (maybe longer give or take) and this guitar is the bane of my existence. It is NOT a good starter guitar and here is why.

A good 5 frets will constantly buzz when pushing down on it and also it may buzz on a fret below it.

This guitar is extremely tight no matter how low the tuning is. It is extremely hard to get used to and you hurt your fingers when trying to make chords and even tabbing.

It will always sound off-key and I have not one idea as to why that is.

title: String changing is a lot less annoying now
comment:
I am a fairly new guitar player, and am not a big fan of changing my strings. A big part of the reason used to be that I hated pulling the bridge pins out. I was using one of those muli-tools (string winder, snipper, bridge pin puller) but sometimes the bridge pins would really be stuck in there and I'd end up using a set of pliers. There's a danger of marking up my rosewood bridge or damaging the pins themselves.

Well, that's no longer an issue. I bought this device and all I need to do is slip it over the bridge pin, use a rocking motion on the rubber pivot, and the pins come out easily and are gripped in the device. Nothing gets stressed, worn, scratched, or damaged in any way, and it's quick and, strangely enough, immensely satisfying to do.

Now I have one less reason to dread changing my strings. This is a device I will use on all my acoustics for the rest of my life, and it is well worth the price. I would recommend it to anyone with an acoustic guitar that uses bridge pins.

title: Too much glue, and pre drilled holes for tuning pegs are off
comment:
I was excited to put together my own guitar and give it a custom paint. I saw "quilted Maple top" and expected at least a half inch to work with. Instead I find that it's paper thin, taking away any ability to do a lot of sanding back or getting colors to truly soak in. Worst part was there was so much glue residue left behind from the binding you had to sand through the the top just to remove it. Then when I went to install the machine heads, two of the pre-drilled holes were off. Making it impossible to install the machine heads for the "D" and "G" strings. This flaw would be a major quality defect that shouldn't have been missed if everything was "fitted before shipping" as advertised. I had to modify the hole for the mounting screws to get them to line up straight. Which is a tedious hassle.

Moving forward you should cut back on the amount of glue used. Or make better effort to remove the excess. An actual "quilted top" with substance would be cool. But more importantly, measure twice, cut once. No one likes crooked tuning pegs.

title: Dream hardware (after a couple of system setting tweaks)
comment:
I've been craving this device since the first release reviews but I already had an excellent Roland (Sonar-branded) unit. Aesthetically this is a wonderful unit: LED lights respond in intensity to levels and change color to show overloads, solid as a brick and hefty enough to stay put but as small as a thick paperback book, attractive black/aluminum contrasting exterior, satisfying tightness to the front panel knobs (feels high quality), locking combo jacks for front panel inputs 1/2, easy to understand routing and monitoring (enhanced by those LEDs), and just all around classy. The inclusion of 4 ins and outs plus MIDI in and out (for those of us with legacy synths and drum machines) combined with the visual appeal and solid construction make this a top choice in this price range.

Installation tips: for Windows systems, reboot the computer after running the driver executable file (that you download) or your system may not see the device as a sound option. At the NI website, you may need to log out and back in to see your KA6 added to your account as an activated device. YOU WILL NEED TO OPTIMIZE YOUR SETTINGS to avoid dropouts (which you may see mentioned in other reviews); for me, that involved changing the fast/save buffer options from within my DAW music software (even though I wasn't using the software to playback my sound files, it was only there that I could get to the buffer setting. NI states that their own control software is downloaded along with the drivers and installer, but I could not locate it.)

CAVEAT: I am not noticing any appreciable sonic difference on WAV files through a pair of Sony MDR-V600 sealed ear headphones between this unit and the Realtek hardware/software built into my Acer laptop, but I can't do a true A/B comparison because I need to change the Sound settings in Windows each time. Youtube music uploads sound crisper and better balanced with the KA6, again with the same juggling act of changing the Sound settings before I can A/B each source.

title: Dissapointed
comment:
After a few weeks of some issues, I finally got the Apollo x16 and x8p working well together. A few times I sat on hold with UAD tech support for over an hour waiting for someone to answer the phone (once it was 2 hours on hold after calling just 5 minutes after they opened their support). They must be busy fixing a lot of issues or very short handed. Their turn around on public forums is near nothing where their customers have posted questions and listed issues with no responses. Their staff si nice and friendly, when you get them, but be prepared to wait on hold a long LONG time.

As for the gear, I have the Apollo x16 and x8p. I also bought EVERY plugin they have. All of them - which according to my account is over 162 plug-ins.

The signal passes well and the sound is good for sure. I had upgraded from a Focusrtie Clarett expecting this to be outstanding in comparison, but it's really only a slight negligible improvement in sound. However, the Focusrite software is far FAR superior for routing IMHO, which is a huge surprise given all the hype around UAD Console. But after some research, I found many others offering similar reviews in comparison. Yes you get lots of outputs in the x16 and x8p combined, but the software ties your hands on how well you can configure it with only 4 cues to route multi signal paths too. Also, the ALT monitoring, only sends through one at a time. For some this might be fine, for, I found it useless. I wish I could us the Focusrtite software over Console. Focusrtie isn't fancy, but the routing is a breeze and very flexible. I spent a few days learning the Console, and finally accepted that it is just limited in comparison. Check the reviews, look deep and you'll find the same comments from many others.

The UAD plugins are okay. But to be honest, I don't think they are really that spectacular. I have about 3,000+ plugins from many vendors. The UAD holds their own fine, but they are NOT much better then many others I have. Many I doubt I would ever use. And they are very expensive. Be prepared to spend a lot of money if you want them all.

I spent more than $15,000 on this system and wish I never looked at it. I wish I never even knew about it. Huge regrets. Save youresefl the money and hasle and check out the Focusrite Clarett, save a LOT of money, get better routing and just as good latency. (yes the Clarett shows thee same latency with USB and I see with TB3). And seriously, the pre-amps on the Clarett are not as good, but they are very close in the ballpark, You'd be surprised.

My comments here are very similar to many others I have found, but I think UAD and other suppress (Sweetwater included) these types of reviews. As an experiment, I am going to pose this on other sites as well, including UAD. You check and see if it posts.

Oh man, I so wish I never bought this. Don't get me wrong, it is decent for sure, and the preamps are really good. No doubt. But it is definitely not worth the extra aggravation and loss of flexible routing ease at a huge price tag. Not even close to worth it.

Summary:
Pros:
- great pre-amps. Unsion is great. Even the basic line ins on the x16 are really good. This is the best feature of the system.
- Console app can handle some plugins and offer near zero latency (it is NOT zero). So, tracking is nice and that part is flexible
- the hardware seems solid.
- the staff is friendly

Cons:
- Expensive plugins - but only marginally average performance over others that cost much less
- Horrible routing options. I mean horrible when you consider the hardware's capability and what Console does not allow you to do! Makes me wonder why even have the expensive outputs when they are so limited. This is my biggest gripe at this point. I needed to put my x32 back in action to do what it can't. grr!
- their support is friendly, but you may need to take vacation day to a hold of them!

title: Zippers on my case fell apart with very little use
comment:
I bought this less than 5 years ago. Don't take it out of the house a lot, I switched to a larger board, so the case never got a ton of work. The small one stayed at home to hold the tuner for my bass and an occasional pedal I was testing.

Today I pulled out the case because I thought to make a small board, just for something like an open mic. Pulled one of the zippers and the toggle came off in my hand. Ugh! Hate it when that happens, but the casting for the zipper just came apart.

That's OK, There's a second zipper so I'll just use that. Closed the case up using it and thought I was good. But then I went to pull it open and it fell apart too. The casting for the metal part broke at one half of the part that the teeth go through. Obviously it cannot be repaired or rigged in any way.

So, both zipper toggles are trash now. With no mistreatment, just a few years of age. I can't say as I got to use that case to transport the board more than 5 or 6 times, but now that I want to it is trash.

The board isn't anything special. Well built, but the slots are too small to fit a lot of pancake plugs through. It isn't tiered or anything, so if you stack pedals nose to tail your foot messes with the knobs on one pedal when you step on the switch for another. Same problem with pedal trains, except those you can actually route any cable you want under the board.

title: Really wanted to like this amp, BUT
comment: Why would this product have such glowing reviews when its performance is so mediocre?  I can't believe I'm the only one disappointed by the poor sound quality, noise and inadequate volume.  I wanted a small head to travel with; I compared this amp side-by side with my Jet City 5 watt head, running both through an excellent quality 12" enclosure.  5W vs 5W.  The Jet City is louder, has better signal-to-noise, and is just all around far more "musical" sounding.  One serious problem with the Bugera is that there seems to be an aggressive noise gate circuit built in, which admittedly does cancel noise very effectively when there is no guitar signal at all.  However, when playing quiet passages, a VERY audible hiss comes up, ie when the noise gate is open, making the amp essentially unusable for recording anything with dynamic range.  Even gigging (micing through the PA of course) would be difficult with this level of noise.  Clean volume at the two lower wattage settings is almost nonexistent, but even at the highest (5W) setting, clean volume is inadequate for most purposes.  The only way to get the volume to a usable level is to dial in significant gain, making the amp pretty useless for anything but very high-gain styles.  Yes, it's pretty cheap for an all-tube head, but what is the point if it's not usable for its intended purposes?

title: Good sound isolation for the price
comment:
I'm using this with a Sennheiser e835 and it fits perfectly. The sound isolation is remarkably good for such an inexpensive mount and the build quality seems pretty good. It came with a spare set of cords which is sensible.

Now that I have lived with it for a while I wanted to post an update. I have it mounted to a small rig ballhead and that is on a King Joy mini tripod base sitting on my desk. To be fair this is a far from ideal situation; however it is what it is.

I get excellent isolation from knocks and bangs on the desk so my typing doesn't transmit as thunder - only the clicking of the keys. However I get absolutely no isolation from the motor vibration from my backup drive. I don't hear the head noise - only a constant droning from the platter motor for as long as that is spinning. As a test I laid the microphone directly on the desk and get exactly the same level of sound.

If this were an expensive Neumann mount I would be upset but, at this price point, I can't really complain. Just be aware that you may want to re-locate your backup drive to another surface if you use this mount!

I have been primary buying them from Amazon. I practice 2 1/2 hours a day still in my mid sixties., I play in two bands,. I found that they seem to last for about four practice session before they start chipping.Its very frustrating because this is an awesome stick, Just a little smaller than a 5B, A 5B is .595 in diameter, and this stick is .565. It is super balanced stick, with a long neck and the oval tip that creates a super bounce and rebound. I am going through 1.5 pairs a week. I have switched to the Vic Firth 5BW, is has a shorter neck and a Acorn tip, it is more durable, but it does not bounce like this stick plus the 5BW is hard to find.
HAPPY DRUMMING

title: Five stars, but I returned it!?!
comment:
Excellent quality. Excellent value for the price. However, I returned mine. Why? I am so used to the "nut" width of a ukulele (most of the standard widths are around 1.38-1.40 inches. I feel like I'm starting all over. At first I thought "what was another 0.3 inches?" Well I found out it is significant. I bought this because I have large hands and it is difficult to make certain chords (D, for example) and picking is difficult. Do you have large hands? Are you a beginner? Then this is the BEST ukulele you can get. Even the "high end" ukuleles have a nut width at 1.5 inches and those are over $500. This is a unique uke. I just wouldn't buy it if you've put many hours of work in at a standard uke width. I have played my friends uke, which has a width of 1.5 inches and I love it, however his is a $800 one. I thought this would unlock things for me, but did not want to "start over" all the muscle memory I've gained. I rarely need to look at the fret board when changing chords. Playing this Wide fret guy I felt clumsy again. Happy uke trails!
As a side note- before I purchased this uke I search high and low within 50 miles of my house to see if there was a shop that had these to try out. I hate to return things, but this was the only way I could experiment with a wide neck.
