title: M4 review
comment:
Nice Interface!

I had previously given this product fewer stars due to some driver interactions with webex software on windows that caused audio glitching.

I'm happy to report that Working with Motu's US based support was a great experience, within a week they were able to replicate the issue and about a day after that they had a beta driver that resolved the issue for me on my system.

With that out of the way, I see no reason this wouldn't be a 5 star product at it's price point.

Here are some Good points about the M4:
* Assembled in USA
* USA based support team that works for Motu (not outsourced) is great.
* Construction quality is great.
* Screen is great
* Knobs are great
* Connectors are great
* Powerswitch is great

If you do any kind of recording, the VERY granular meters on the screen in the front are SUPER important - recording a microphone or instrument too loud will distort the audio - this is true of all interfaces at all price points! - having visual feedback right on the device is EXTREMELY helpful and puts this in a different class from ALL the other interfaces in it's price range.

Sound Quality:

The M4 sounds 'fine' to me - I don't have 'golden ears' and after switching back and forth between this and a focusrite scarlet 4i4, nothing immediately jumps out as drastically different.

Also, I don't do recording, so I have no comments on the mic preamps.

Overall the Motu M4 is a great product - but it's in a weird spot. Knowing what I know now, I think the M2 model (the one with 2 inputs and 2 outputs) is an easy recommendation. The M2 is easily "best in class" on all counts I can think of. The M2 is a MUCH better choice than any of the 2 input 2 output competition I've looked at. Quite frankly the M2 is a steal.

The M4 adds two more inputs and outputs and this is where the M4 starts to fall down vs the competition. Adding those extra inputs and outputs adds the need for a driver that supports more than just basic routing. It adds the need for the driver to be able to control the gain of the different inputs and route audio (so you can hear inputs 3/4 on monitors connected to 1/2, etc) Right now the M4's competition is better at this, but I think it's something Motu can catch up to with software over time (this was written 11/2019)

So the bottom line is, if you need an audio device with just 2 inputs and outputs get the M2, it's HANDS DOWN the best in that class and offers more than ANY of the competition - honestly they will probably sell TONS of the M2's and It wouldn't surprise me if they run out of stock, it's that much of a value leader.

The M4 has that same potential - the hardware is great, I hope we'll see driver updates that bring it feature parity with other 4 input/output devices.

title: Pros and Cons
comment:
i've been playing guitar for over 20 years. not a pro but far from a beginner. my main guitars have all been wood. sizes from parlor to deadnought.

i was attracted to this guitar for two reasons.

in my experience wood guitars, even the good ones, eventually have issues. my taylor played amazing for 12 years, then action went high, and even after a set up does not play as well. this after years of monitoring the room humidity. what a pain.

second. travel size. with a removable neck you get to have a full-ish sized guitar and carry it on. awesome. full size = full sound.

there are some terrible travel guitars. i started with the martin backpacker an awful transition if you are used to playing a good sounding full sized guitar. then i went to the baby taylor which was a big step up but still far from a full sized guitar. i decided to scrap the travel guitar and just bought one for the east coast.

then i started thinking about carbon fiber guitars. no issues with humidy and temperature. the OF660 checked more boxes then anything else i could find. i knew i should play it before i bought it. luckily there is a dealer where i am. down i went with my taylor 414.

my impression: it's a lot quieter then the taylor. mind you the taylor is a grand auditorium style and not as loud as a dreadnought. also sounds sterile in comparison. the style of the guitar was a little clunky, bulky. the inside of the soundhole, which is easily visible, was kind of rough and unfinished looking. the neck is heavier then a wood guitar so the headstock wants to dive down. not a feeling i like. the neck worked great as in all the videos i've seen. felt very solid. i didn't get a chance to plug it in so no comment on the passive pickup. i played it back and forth with my taylor for about 20 minutes and ultimately decided not to buy it. but i still haven't ruled it out. i just haven't played many carbon fiber guitars yet. i have watched a lot of videos comparing carbon to wood and the wood is always louder and boomier.

last thing. i don't like the way amazon reviews are skewed. there is nothing wrong with the middle of the bell curve or a 3 star. i reserve 5 stars for things that are exceptional which by definition is not much.

title: The footswitch WILL break and TC Electronic does not do service
comment:
The Ditto is a very nice looper pedal. But the momentary-contact switch will break eventually, be that a month or a year or several years. And TC Electronic doesn't believe in supporting their products, so when it breaks it is a throw-away. What looks like a footswitch isn't an actual switch - it just activates a small switch that's soldered to one of the circuit boards, making it more-trouble-than-its-worth to repair.

I recommend buying from a different manufacturer.

title: Nice for the price
comment: I have a love-hate relationship with compressors. On the one hand, I think to myself "if my technique were better, I wouldn't need this darn thing" - on the other "it sure evens out my sound". I like it for performance, but I practice without it because I want to improve my technique and dynamics. Don't get me started on other types of compressors, especially mastering compressors. I'm not blessed with the ears to hear the subtleties other guys claim to hear - I often tend to over-compress things and only after listening to the finished mix for awhile, hear the pumping or over-squashed result. I tell ya, a good mastering guru / house is worth the expense. Thankfully, that's not an issue with a guitar pedal compressor for me - least ways, not that I've heard. This one is good, cheap, and transparent as far as I can tell. I like performing and recording with it better than without it.

title: Good starter mic, not a professional choice
comment:
PROS:
Good quality of recording. Love the different modes.
This mic picks up everything, it's very sensitive, the way it should be, so be sure to turn off your AC and be away from your fridge. Or use EQ in your favorite software to remove the hum.
Doesn't require a separate audio interface
Overall good starter mic
I use it to record vocals and podcasts

CONS: No ASIO driver, though the 3rd party universal ASIO4ALL driver does a decent job for me in my Ableton Live DAW. Still, I'd love to see a native ASIO driver by the manufacturer. Regular DX/Wave drivers come with latency so be sure to switch to ASIO4ALL
No analog XLR output, so in the future when I do get a professional audio interface, I'll have to buy another mic because you can't use both at the same time via ASIO drivers.

title: Works as advertised
comment: Works fine, no obvious shortcomings. Not the sturdiest, precision-made, knock-em-dead product, but a moc stand doesn’t need to be. I tried three similar scissor-type clamp-held stands available on Amazon at this time in this price range, though some also come with a pop filter, and kept this one because of a small design difference: note the extra hole that allows you to anchor the spring at a higher point, thus increasing its tension; note also that the other ens of the sprung is attached to a sort of bump-out. Both of these details give the spring a little more oomph. Other than that, the three stands were literally the same in every way. I rated the sturdiness at 4 stars out of five because when you move the mic around, you can feel a bit of play there, but when you tighten the screw at the back of the clamp, it all becomes solid.

It seems to me that the instrument requires more frequent retuning than one with internally geared tuners. Also, the plastic fretboard is actually fine, except that the frets are black on black and hard to see. For someone advanced who plays just by feel, that might be fine; as a relative beginner, I marked the frets with indelible silver marker to make them more visible. For both those reasons, four stars instead of five.

title: Wow! Just Wow!
comment:
I don’t often write reviews on things I buy, but I had to with this guitar. This thing is blowing me away! Now I’ve only had it for 2 days, but so far it’s crushing my expectations. So I’ve been a semi-professional guitar player for over 25 years, and I’ve owned a lot of guitars, from American Strats and Teles, EVH Wolfgang, Gibson Les Paul, PRS Mira. You name it, at one time or another I’ve probably owned one. The neck on this guitar honestly is perfect! Now neck radius, profile, and fret size are all subjective, but IMHO this neck, and specifically the frets, are perfect! The rounded ends are SUPER comfortable, and the fact that they are stainless steel at this price is even more amazing. My guitar needed just a bit of truss rod tightening, and I was able to lower the action to 1.25 millimeter, with absolutely zero fret buzz or choking out. The bridge is super easy to adjust and the included tuning key is super cool. The only weak spot on the guitar is the neck pickup…it’s not bad, just a bit muddy. But the bridge pickup is punchy, and clear. Notes are defined, even at high gain.
So look, I’m not saying that this is of equal quality to my PRS, or my Fender custom shop, but I have to be honest that it feels, to me, way better than my Fender Player Series. At less than $375 this guitar is an absolute steal! I’m going to take it out on the road, and will update on how well it holds up! If you’re on the fence, go ahead and pick one up before the price goes up! Lol!

title: Fantastic, Eternal Pick
comment: I Love This Pick!!! I have tried many plastic picks and have always been frustrated by how quickly they wear out. Plus you get a collection of pick dust on your guitar, under the strings, which is difficult to clean. The ICE PIX is awesome. It holds up well, makes a beautiful (subtle) chime tone, improves control and accuracy. The pick feels more secure in my hand. I haven't dropped one yet (6 months). The color starts off shiny, but fades with use, creating a weathered and personal look, reflecting your finger placement on the pick. The Brass PIX are similar in thickness to a medium plastic pick - I find works well with .09 guitar strings. The Stainless Steel PIX are like a heavy plastic pick which I like better with .10 guitar strings or Bass guitar. The Steel PIX do shred your strings. I have not had excessive string wear with the Brass. The Copper PIX is the light version - a little too light for my taste - feels like it is bending as you play. Don't let the $5 price for 3 picks scare you off. It is well worth it as these picks will last longer than 1000 plastic picks.

title: Great for Deadening Sound!
comment:
I got a great tip from a sound engineer a few years ago, when I started "sound proofing" my walls: which was to nail them in instead of using the adhesive. I see that on some of the reviews, the adhesive isn't as good, but in my experience, when I've used adhesive, it can damage the paint behind the sound proof, so I've resulted to using nails, which help it to never lose it's stick and doesn't actually damage anything when I've removed it.

These octagons are amazing. I love that the offer them in white, which helps it to blend in with my paint in the room I'm using. I definitely know that there is a large difference from sound deadening and actual sound proofing. If you're looking for sound proofing, these do not do that, but they do keep out a fair amount of noise from the outside. I have a neighboring dog who loves to bark at anything, which is really annoying when I have to work. These haven't completely shut him out, but it's helped to make the sound in the room nice and quiet for meetings or recording something.

This pack of 12 almost perfectly lined up 1/2 of one walls in my office which is about a small 7x7 or so, where I need them the most. But I would need another pack of 12 to make sure it actually covers the whole wall, which I would just take into account when looking at these. Since they fit with other companies which are similar, these were a great addition to really help improve my sound quality.

For recording, this is a big upgrade for that "airy" sound or room tone. Definitely recommend to get these for all of your quiet and recording needs!

title: Very high quality cable - these are what you buy after you bought the wrong cables.
comment:
These are great cables and I love the Neutrik XX connectors. I have a bag full of these cables and replaced some cheap cables I bought at a "stupid" deal of the day a while back. Kopul cables blow the other cables out of the water in terms of quality craftsmanship and value. All the cables came with a velcro loop to keep the cables wound - it can be easily removed if not wanted.

Neutrik XX XLR cables have "rings" that you can replace cheaply from a Compass named retailer with a Full line of product and allows you to add a color ring to each mic if you need to distinguish which mic is which.

I suggest to anyone buying good cables - learn how to wrap them over/under (youtube search for "How to Coil Cables | The Over Under Technique" Elon TLT is the easiest way I've understood it) because over/under will help your cables last as long as they were designed to.

The first set of junk "stupid deal" cables I bought had buzz, hum, and were just .. cheap. Kopul is what you buy after you realize your mistake. Take my advice and buy these first.

Along those lines, K&M Mic stands - the second - and last mic stand you'll buy.

title: Brings that SM7b / Ribbon to life.
comment:
This little box is pretty amazing. Functionally, it's meant to be placed between your preamp and a quiet dynamic or ribbon microphone. It takes phantom power from your preamp but does not pass it on to the mic, which makes it safe for use with ribbon mics. The boost in gain is immediately obvious, and it does, to my ears, improve the clarity of the microphone as well.

I've used this box with my SM7b and my Cascade ribbon mics, all with great results. If you own an SM7b, you know how hard it is to drive it quietly with a preamp (even an expensive one) and the Cloudlifter solves that problem instantly. For best results, use a short patch cable to connect the Cloudlifter to the mic, and then run your longer cable back to your preamp. This allows for maximum signal boost right at the mic. The CL-1 has a velcro strap underneath it so you can securely mount it to your micstand.

For those saying they would never use the CL-1 in front of a high-end preamp, my answer would be "what sounds the best?" Personally, I often use the CL-1 in front of a 500 series Vintech 573 pre coupled with a Trident 80b EQ and it does nothing but improve the already great tone I get. Recording is all about what YOU think sounds the best in your room with your gear making the art that YOU want to make. For my money, the CL-1 is a great tool in the studio.

title: Will sound great if used appropriately.
comment:
This is a solid collection of models and effects. It's really handy as a supplement to more dedicated boxes, or for when you just want to grab something and go. I've found it to be particularly useful for the niche effects that I wouldn't want to spend the money/pedalboard real estate on. It will not replace a nice pedalboard of dedicated boxes (that would be five stars), but it's an awesome swiss-army knife.

I see people complaining about the sound quality sometimes, but I think this is the result of a mistaken application. The amp models are not really designed to work like stompboxes. They will not sound very good if you plug them into the front of an amp, particularly a decent tube amp. (I suspect they would sound good in front of something like a Roland JC-120.) Some specific high-gain models will sound freaking horrible: thin and extreme and noisy. If you have a decent tube amp, ignore the amp models and use your amp. Amp models are not intended to transform your amp into some other amp. If however you are using the effects loop, or plugging directly into powered speakers/PA, the models should sound and feel quite natural. This is what amp models are designed to do: *be* the amp when you aren't using one.

comment: I rarely write reviews, but spend lots of money on electronics on Amazon... so they are pretty much limited to truly terrible products, which is where I would rate this one. I did test it prior to use and I was disappointed that it would not work with my higher quality lavaliers (which I've used wired to my Zoom H4n over many years with no problems).  As far as I could tell, you can ONLY use the included cables and mics.  Anyway, I set it to record to my Zoom H4n during the event (only 15 feet away) and the audio dropped intermittently throughout, making it nearly useless except for some excerpts.  I guess I should have paid 6X more for the Sony set.

title: Not What Our Company Expected
comment:
The item advertises it can split the two audio channels, A & B (stereo), which it can on the receiver, BUT NOT when actually plugging into a camera to record, which makes it pointless. We needed something that if we were using both transmitter/mics, each was on a separate channel when recorded in camera. The receiver clearly shows both channels separately reading levels, but it only has ONE output to plug into a camera! It also indicates a "safety" mode which records a lower decibel track, but it does not record a separate track at all. The Rhode mic system (for which this is a lower priced clone) does offer that feature, this just has a misleading description, but does not include the feature. Amazon support was no help other than to return the item.
The audio quality (in bench testing only) is pretty good. Have to have gain setting very low on unit, but does isolate decently if its at -6db or lower. No way to adjust the "background noise filter" which is a little annoying. For instance, if you do use both transmitters at the same time, it actually kind of mutes the levels a bit, which is not acceptable in a professional application when having a two person interview. If the channels were truly separate, that shouldn't be an issue. The screens are easy to read, but levels on the receiver and transmitter aren't that necessary, as if you're using a professional camera, you'll be monitoring at that level with headphones.
Not sure how long the batteries will last, but it plugged in and charged fine. Did not test distance, but works from 10-15 feet just fine.
They are smaller than I thought, which is nice if trying to conceal. Pretty much all plastic, and I feel the audio output jack on the receiver would likely go out prematurely, as some of our other mics similar to these have.

title: Zoom B1Xon
comment:
Original Review - 1 Star

Defective out of the box. Powering on, I get a faint, fuzzy static for all features. Disappointed. Troubleshooting and a reset produced no results.

June 19 Update - 4 Stars

An update. Amazon return/replacement is fast and convenient. As soon as I contacted Amazon Returns, they provided a link that allowed the printing of a return slip. This was during a weekend, but on Monday UPS picked up the defective item. The very next day UPS delivered the new, replacement Zoom.

Amazon has been great, to say the least.

About the Zoom ... I don't typically have need for an effects pedal, but I bought the Zoom because I specifically wanted two effects: polyphonic octaving and delay.

The various amps and distortions are great, as well as the overdrive and chorus, and I will use them, but I really wanted polyphonic octaving to give the bass an 8 string effect (octave up, not down) for some songs. Happily, Zoom can do this (up or down). While not a perfect emulation, it certainly sounds good, which is good enough for my purposes not wanting to spend $1000 or more on an 8 string guitar. The delays aren't exactly what I'd hoped for (even the lowest settings are too long for what I wanted), but happily the Zoom has a fretless effect which turned out to be almost exactly the sound that I was looking for. So, well done to Zoom, especially for less than $75.

The musicians who developed the patches obviously put a lot of thought into them. Many of the clean and distortion patches (along with the amp effects by themselves) are so similar that one has to have a discriminating ear to favor one over another, especially when heard through a combo amp or cabinet (like my Peavey and Behringers). Listening through headphones is a good idea here because naturally the effect becomes more attenuated when heard through a sound system. That's not Zoom's problem; it's just a fact of life when dealing with sound equipment.

Several of the patches do sound goofy and unnecessary, like some of the cheesey 70's disco effects, or the Space Martian-ey sounds, but I assume they were created to demonstrate the versatility of the pedal itself.

The ability to use 5 effects at once, and to put them in whatever order you want, adds another order of magnitude of sounds that might be produced. It will be some time before I've had a chance to explore just some of the thousands of permutations this little pedal offers. In fact, between bass, Zoom pedal, and sound system, there is no reason why anybody couldn't create their own sound thumbprint that nobody could ever duplicate.

Another plus is that your changes to a particular patch can be saved automatically, or the unit can prompt you whether you want to save it or not. I don't auto save. I'm glad for the option to discard a patch, because my initial impulse is to experiment with every patch I play, and not every experiment goes as planned or needs to be saved.

Now, one negative I will mention is that there is no On/Off switch with the Zoom. Why I don't know, but it adds an unnecessary set up step (plugging in somewhere, depending on how you decide to power it) each time it is used. It's not a huge deal but it would be nice just to flip a switch with a foot and play.

Another negative is the lack of blank memory space for patches. If you want to save a patch you've created, then you have to overwrite a patch that somebody at Zoom put thought into creating. Losing any patch is a pity. Even if I don't plan to use a patch, like the goofy disco sounding patches, someday I might want to reference them for some reason just to see how the experts created it. A reset would get those patches back, true, so I'll have to save my created patches to a notebook for that possibility.

Now, if this had cost $150 or 200, I would be inclined to give Zoom a lower rating because of the negatives mentioned above. The original I received was defective, too.

But I didn't pay $150 or 200 for this box, not even close. There are lots of effects, and they sound good. The only limitation to this pedal in the near future is my own imagination. And Amazon got a replacement into my hands within two business days of my reporting a problem. Taking everything into consideration, I can give the Zoom 4 Stars. It's well worth the money.

title: Not perfect, but an important tool for college students
comment:
This mute does its job of making you quiet enough that you won't annoy people. I'm a college student who doesn't always want to haul my euphonium to a practice room, and a mute was a must for playing in the dorm. That being said, intonation with this mute in is very inconsistent across the range of the instrument. No matter what you do with your tuning slides, certain notes, especially on the lower end, will always sound sour. This is somewhat true with every mute, unfortunately. The back pressure from this mute is minimal, but still enough to cause discomfort if you play for a long time. If you've ever used headphones to play along with external audio, I don't recommend doing it with the mute in, it's a very uncomfortable experience.
In terms of feel and appearance, the mute looks fantastic, but you'll never be able to justify its cost to someone who doesn't play euphonium. It is indeed extremely lightweight and fits completely in the bell. I frequently forget it's in the instrument until I start playing. The portability is what, for me, makes this mute a worthy purchase, especially for college euphonium players.

title: Excellent Speaker!
comment:
I've owned several Monitor speakers over the years including Mackie 824 and 624 speakers as well as Rokit 5 speakers. I've used them all in professional studio environments. The Pioneer BULIT6 compares right up there with the other speakers. It has much better quality and richness than the Rockit 5 and sounds just as good as the Mackie monitors.

Right now I've connected a BULIT6 to my 24" Samsung HDTV monitor in my exercise room. I realize that this is overkill and not what they're ordinarily used for, but I received just one of these as part of the Amazon Vine program, and would have had to have two to have replaced my editing system speakers. Plus I think that these would have been overkill for my editing suite since the space in that room is small.

The normal sound from the Samsung TV in my exercise room is very low, however once connecting the BULIT6 to the Samsung, the sound easily fills the room, with lots of level left. The sound is clear and crisp, and it outdoes the sound quality of the Bose speakers I'm using on my large TV in my den.

These are very sturdy speakers.

The speakers are larger and heavier than I expected. I believe they weigh in at a little over 18 pounds!

I expected to receive 6" speakers but these are much larger that that.

Their size is:

Height 13.4"
Width 11.6"
Depth 8.9"

I also like the fact that these speakers have XLR, TRS and RCA inputs. This means that you are not limited by what equipment you wish to connect these speakers to.

If you're looking for a great set of monitors at a reasonable price I suggest you check out the BULIT6 speakers. You won't be disappointed. It also makes an outstanding TV speaker though it's large.

title: Great physics!!
comment: These actually did a very good job of absorbing sound from all different sources (monitors, singing, talking). They're very soft and plush, which makes sense in terms of physics. Sound waves are physical vibrations that travel very fast with relatively high velocity. So logically, the softer and deeper the surface, the more sound waves that are prevented from bouncing off the hard surfaces back out into the room. Very satisfied, would definitely recommend!

title: Good sound on a budget
comment:
I looked at many options for covering a 40Hz to 20KHz range in a home studio. That's the frequency response of the PA equipment I'm producing MIDIs for. All things considered, the Studiophile BX8a met those needs at the best price. There's no subwoofer to buy. This one box is it.

The frequency response is very smooth but not reaching as low as I'd like. Bass clarity is moderately good, perhaps hampered by the ports facing my wall. The rest of the frequency response has excellent clarity. I can now hear mixing and EQ problems that I was missing before. It helps get the production right the first time.

I have only a few gripes. The power and gain controls are in back where they're difficult to reach on such a deep speaker. There's no protective cover for these very fragile, close-range speakers. Both speakers have some background buzz regardless of gain and input.

title: Beautiful Rich smooth sound

I am a 54 year old self taught violinist probably playing all wrong who learned by watching YouTube videos.  I have an old German Stainer violin copy. I love the violin but I was always in search of a deeper richer sound as I love playing romantic pieces.  I read about these strings and took a chance and bought them.  Yes, I had to tune them every 15 min the first day.  Every 20 min the next etc.  Its been about 2 weeks now and I can play for about 2 hours without tuning it but let me tell you - SO WORTH IT.  I will buy these again.  The D string is SO SOFT.  I am in love with these mellow warm strings and the beautiful rich sound.  I never thought I could sound so good.  Gone is the tingy bright sound that I never liked coming from my old German violin.  It's like a fine smooth wine.  Its quieter and smoother and warmer.  I love these strings.

title: Like investing in a 4K TV! Freakishly Amazing!
comment:
Well what can I say about this speaker that has not been said...
I know this does not sound sane but I installed the Gold in my Fender Mustang 2 v2 practice amp. I also have the Fender Mustang 1, 3 and 5 v2 and just for kicks I previously installed a Gold 10" in the Mustang 1. I was totally blown away so I "invested" in the 12" version for my Fender Mustang 2. I was again completely blown away at the huge difference in the complexity of the tone. I had changed the stock speaker many times previously and had settled on the Tonker and loved it but it was a tad too aggressive/loud for practice if that describes it. The Gold is just perfect. I think it is the best modeling amp available period. I have auditioned a lot of well known speakers and they all seemed to be really good at something but not all things. The Gold is great at everything I can throw at it from super clean jazz to power metal. I was astonished when I could hear the spring reverb ringing clear on the Mustang as the notes carried and trailed. I would have sworn there was an actual spring tank set in the Mustang. It is very much like investing in a 4K TV. The detail is just freakishly amazing. I HIGHLY recommend the Gold if you want to hear the subtleties of your playing and equipment. The Gold is fairly linear (small upper mid bump in a good way) and has no harshness at all. Bass is all there and not flabby. It does sound great a bedroom volumes as well. My speaker/tone quest is finally over - Hallelujah!! It would have been so much cheaper if I would have started with the Gold. There is a reason this speaker cost so much... it's worth it! I have spent a ton on guitars, pickups amps etc to find the perfect tone and convenience at the best price and it turns out to be the Fender Mustang v2 series with Celestion Gold speakers. DONE! I have the Mustang 3 and 5 for gigs as well. I will be putting the Gold in them as well pronto.

title: Amazing Microphone for Voice
comment:
A lot of people have been talking about how this microphone is an "Amazing Microphone for the price", I want to go a step further than that and drop off the "for the price" part of that sentence. This is just a great microphone, obviously it is not professional quality equipment, but for a home studio... why would you waste your money on anything else. Audiophiles might scoff, but it isn't worth buying anything else when such a great microphone is available for under 50 dollars.

Thousand dollar Microphones, gold plated cables, and all of that extra equipment is the greatest waste of your money when setting up a recording space. You should be spending money on a mixer with the setting you will need, and equipment for sound dampening your room.

I do a podcast, and some voic eover recording, voice acting, you name it, and this microphone is about 10 steps above the old cardioid stage microphones I was using. Sound comes through clear, the high range of the mic allows me to basically remove clipping (in loud laughing parts)without having to go in with software and edit by hand. Buy this and expect a huge leap in sound quality over cheap microphones, because this is a cheap microphone that doesn't sound like one.

I would like to say that these are the opinions of someone recording voice, no instruments or anything, I don't know how this microphone performs in instrument and band recording studio situations, I just know that it is great for voice, and after buying one, I went out and got 3 more.

title: Fun, fun
comment:
This is a marvelous instrument. Fun, fun, fun. It is very light. I tinkered with my Ampeg & the bass's treble until I got a very nice thump & sustain. I play in a bluegrass band & everyone played it including the dobro player. It does everything it should. I need a a little baby powder, but not the other guys. I noticed when I spilled some powder on my khakis that I could get enough off my pants leg. It is loud enough to practice w/o plugging in. Tuning is no problem; just pinch a sting & take the weight off. One major problem (listen up Gold Tone). The fret lines wrap around the finger board & have little dots at the ends. The dots on the finger board are in the right place, between the fret lines.. This is really confusing when I look at my fingers - which I do a lot. I would like to try one with frets. The tuner is not top end, but works well enough. The strings are magical. One can see wave forms in them. This thing is fun, fun fun.

Update - the bass pictured on Amazon has the dots placed correctly. The dots' placement on the bass sent to me are different & confusing. It's still fun, but I will contact the vendor or Gold Tone. Must drop it a star. Here's a pic.

I can't say it's worth paying twice as much for similar digital pianos but this one does stand out!

title: Not happy
comment:
[[VIDEOID:978e6251b7a397e85e91c9b1b8945e5a]] This system is honestly really difficult and I’m not particularly happy with it.
Disclaimer: I have several different wireless mic systems so I am well experienced and know how to operate them.
First off, the included lapel mic did not even work. Not with this system or any of my other wireless mic systems.
I tried another set of lapel mics that I had and they didn’t work either. However, I used my mic headset and it worked good.
After further investigation, I found that the system would only work with 3.5 mic jacks that had 2 rings around the jack (I demonstrated this in the pic above with 2 jacks) and will not work with plugs with 3 rings.
That being said, the included lapel mic had 2 rings and still didn’t work, so that mic just doesn’t work at all.
I had to do too much to get this system to work especially when they system is supposed to be ready to go out of the box.
I don’t recommend this until some improvements are made.

Also - just general advice here, but if your tremolo arm is all floppy until you screw it all the way in, you’re missing the spring that is supposed to hold tension on the trem arm.

I’ve talked to guys who have played 30 years who don’t know that.

title: Ping! Boing! Bing! Good Idea, Inferior Materials
comment:
[[VIDEOID:c359ac68fc86293e5e1969cc6268df0e]] 2nd revision 12/29/21: Everything in my previous comments still hold, but the Stainless Steel zero fret does help somewhat. You won’t find this anywhere online and it took quite a bit of observation, but here’s what is happening – the ping comes from behind the zero fret. There is extremely high tension between the string and the zero fret (due to the downward force of the tight string). No matter how smooth the fret is, no matter how much Teflon lube you put in there, that tension holds the string in place pretty hard. When you bend the string, the tension breaks and the string whips laterally from where it was resting and into the retainer (nut) slot wall, thus causing the ping. The ping is actually the note you get if you pluck the string behind the nut.
In theory, if the retainer slot is perfectly identical to the string width, the ping won’t happen because the string won’t move when the tension is broken. But it appears there’s no way to get the string and slot exactly matched. Even if you use the exact unwound string gauges as the units come with, there’s still a little play in them. If you use higher gauge, they’ll bind in the slot and need filing. Either way, you’ll never get them exactly where ping doesn’t happen. Or if you can it’ll be a heckuva lot of work. This problem is really only fixable by having a damper behind the retainer (nut). A piece of felt, a slice of a rubber band, whatever. You just need something that will dampen the strings and not let them vibrate behind the nut. Add this to everything I said earlier and you have a nut that is superior to a standard one, but way more work and pain than its worth.

Edit: ZeroGlide was kind enough to send me some Stainless Steel replacement frets to address the issue described below. The SS does fix the problem around the nickel frets being too soft (and thus they indent immediately). But, ping and boing can still happen if the nut slot is not perfectly the same size as the string. Since most people buy the slotted nuts or use standard sized slot files, it's pretty likely that the string and the slot will be slightly different. Thus, ping and boing are still going to happen unless you get the slot sized just right. Upgraded to 2 stars and that's where I stay until they ditch the inferior nickel frets and replace with Stainless Steel as standard in the kit...

The “zero fret” concept works such that the downward pressure of the strings is born by the zero fret. Instead of a bone, metal, Corian, Tusq, etc. nut where the strings sit in slots, the strings just sit directly on a piece of fret wire. In this product’s design, a slotted retainer sits adjacent to the zero fret and acts as a string spacer. It performs the string spacing function of a normal nut, but it does not bear any of the downward pressure of the strings. That is, the slot is lower than the height of the zero fret and thus all the pressure is on the zero fret. The fret wire that comes with this product is polished nickel, which is considerably softer than stainless steel.
I’ll start by saying, I’ve done many high-end builds and have done a lot of nut cutting work. This product is difficult on that front. They don’t come anywhere near “drop in” shape. It takes a ton of milling to get them in shape and they are fragile. This is not for the amateur or faint of heart. Be prepared for failure. If you make it to where you can install it, you’ll find that the included fret wire will start to develop ruts from the downward pressure of the strings (see photo). These ruts develop in a surprisingly short time period. I’ve done three builds this year with this system and all three have the rut problem already (see video). Once the ruts are deep enough, the strings nestle into them, almost like a little string slot in the zero fret wire. But since the rut isn’t very deep, when you bend a string it pops the string out of the rut. And when that happens you get the pings and boings (see video). If you think its something you can live with, trust me, you can’t. It’s really annoying and defeats the purpose of having this smooth and seamless system in the first place.
The problem, of course, lies in the soft nickel fret wire that comes with the product. Nickel is fine as fret wire, but it is not hard enough to act as a constant support for the string pressure. Stainless steel would certainly be better. Now here’s the sad part of the story…I’ve shown this all to the founder/owner of the company. He refuses to even admit that there’s a problem and instead suggests exhaustive workarounds to address what could easily be fixed by using better material. What’s more, he even sells stainless steel replacement frets on his site. Yet he continues to use the cheaper and inferior nickel in the product for sale here.
End of day, its hours of difficult and time consuming work to shape this thing to fit in your nut slot. That’s if you can get that far without breaking it. Then once its in you’ll run into this string rut problem within a few months. Then you’ll have to dismantle it and replace the wire with stainless. For an acoustic guitar or hardtail electric, this product is mostly pointless. They claim better tone. Don’t count on that. For a tremolo guitar, this is indeed a great idea. No more binding in a nut slot, just smooth sliding across fret wire. And while a great idea, its not a new idea. Zero fret guitars have been around for a long time. This seems a noble attempt to bring the zero fret to the masses. Unfortunately, it’s too difficult a process for most. And sadly the owner refuses to acknowledge the time bomb (soft fret wire) so even if you can get this thing installed you’re guaranteed the pinging issue down road.

title: Lexicon Ionix U22
comment:
After scouring the web and various catalogs for bang to buck ratio, I decided on the U22. Especially since the deal included a free microphone, and 85.00 off the MSRP by a very helpful sales associate.
The U22 interface routes into my Mac book with 4g of ram, and the fastest processor for that model. The instruments used for this review were: Hofner Jazzica, Neumann TLM120.

I found the software CDE install a bit of a nightmare, as I had to download Stuffit for the application to run the install, and this is with Leopard. It was not nearly as bug free as it should have been, as the Panthenon Reverb would not open and required more de-bugging. After the Men in Black install, I find the unit runs nearly perfect, and the Reverb Plugin makes the unit worth the effort. The unit is very transparent, and the noise floor is very low for the cost of this unit. The headphone output sounds warm, and monitoring shows very little latency on the playback and tracking. I found that even the most basic sounding track comes alive with the Panthenon!!!KUDOS there.

One very major problem: The Mic/Line/Inst input knob has a nasty overdrive spike near the end of the pot/knobs throw. I find it continuously difficult to get a proper signal level that is full sounding and does not consistently distort. The boasting about the DBX preamps is just that. If the Pots were upgraded the unit would probably be improved at least 50% imo.

I would buy again for the value, but if you can save up for the Apogee Duet or something that does not overdrive to create sufficient sound, then do it.

title: Chinese artistry and value has arrived...

Compared to the Cordoba C12 (lattice top), the C10 with its traditional build and lesser price tag is a better buy with lots of bang for the buck. What the Japanese have accomplished through their higher end Hirade and Asturias labels, this Chinese Cordoba’s a worthy contender.  The C10’s cedar top’s got a great palate of sound, good melody sustain on the top string, nice ponticello/tasto shadings, and plenty of rosewood richness. Very responsive guitar which will give advanced beginners/intermediate players lots of mileage and inspiration. Make sure to check the intonation, fret finishes, and neck before you keep it. Cordoba occasionally has quality control slip ups. It is hand made, but not similar to much more expensive concert guitars.

title: Mac studio users beware
comment:
I didn't buy this from Amazon, but there are some issues that potential buyers should be aware of. If you plan to use this unit exclusively for live performance you can probably skip this. If you plan to use it in a studio using Mac... read on before you buy this unit!

The whole reason I bought it was for the control software that let's you use the unit's effects like a VST or AU in a DAW. Sounds great, but I've had some nasty surprises right out of the box.

1. The control software doesn't ship in the box or on a chip inside the unit. Not that big a deal, as I'd go to their site and download the latest version anyway. The manual that shipped in the box makes no mention whatever of this feature... lame, considering how much their marketing emphasizes it. You have to go to their support site to find out how to use it.

2. When I got to the company's support site, it was pretty easy to find the control software download, but the instructions on the page were a rather a shock:

"First you need to update your M350 firmware to a newer version, as this is required in order for the control software work with your M350 unit."

OK, pain in the butt but I guess I can manage that. But then...

"Disclaimer: The M350 firmware upgrade can only be done using a PC running Windows."

So I can't use the feature I bought the unit for because my studio is Mac!! LAME! They should tell you that up front in their tech specs! (But no Mac users would ever buy the unit if they did.) I wish Amazon was available where I live because this would be on it's way back. Then to add insult to injury:

"We have only tested it under Windows XP, but it may also work under other versions of Windows."

LAME LAME LAME! I can't even believe how lame that is. XP is dead. Even if I jump thru the hoops and go borrow a Windows machine, it's not going to be running Windows XP! So I cannot count on the fact that I can upgrade the firmware, so I cannot count on being able to use the control software feature with my DAW.

If you want to use this as a VST/AU in your DAW running on Mac, think again. The effects are OK given the price of the unit, and the controls are pretty easy to figure out and use, but the price is still too high considering the hassle involved if you want the VST/AU feature with a Mac.

title: Lots to like, some items to dislike. Weak tuners and not standard Fender/Shaller 2-pin tuners.
comment:
I bought one of these used but in "almost like new" condition for about $350 with a transparent blue top. By "almost like new" I mean the body and finish had no marks or dings, and the frets had no visible wear. The electronics however, were starting to wear. There's a lot I like, but a few things I have to downgrade my rating for.

PROs
- attractive finish and top
- dual HB tele with coil splitting
- decent fit and finish

CONs
- cheap, non-standard tuners harder to upgrade cleanly (different 2-pins location than typical 2-pin fender/schaller modern tuners)
- cheap import electronics don't last, and sound somewhat muddy
- lacks a case at the $700 price point, on an Indochina(current)/Korea(older model)guitar

DETAILS AND COMMENTS ON UPGRADES
On the upside, the top of mine looks really nice in the flame maple trans blue stain. I like the set neck and the feel at the heel is ALMOST as good for reach as my american deluxe strat with a tapered heel. This isn't tapered, but the shape is less boxy because it is a set neck. The neck is narrow both in width and thickness, so players with smaller fingers can get a bit better reach. Some people don't like the Jumbo frets but I don't mind them at all. The weight of the guitar is really light - which is nice if you play or practice long sets. The body cutaway on the back is also a big plus for comfort.

Most notable, the tuners are not great, and if you try to upgrade them to a normal 2-pin drop in replacement tuner for strats and tele's you will find they do not fit. This guitar does use a 2-pin tuner, but the pins are about 1 pin thickness closer to the center post of the tuning peg so that a "normal" fender/shaller tuner will not fit. And worse, it's designed so that if you try to drill it, it will be very difficult to do even with a tuner pin jig, because you are basically trying to drill on the side of the holes. Minus 1.5 stars for this...I normally replace all my fenders with locking tuners but I only do drop in replacements for a clean look. I don't like to drill additional holes or leave exposed pin holes, because then it looks like a hacked job. There are plenty of tuners that I'm sure CAN go on, but I now have to hunt for one that is going to fit and cover properly. (Update: locking Sperzel tuners will cover the two pin holes, but require drilling a single, centered pin hole. I have a pin jig for this, but...the black Sperzels have a silver center peg?! so from the front, you lose the "blackout" look. I ordered planet waves locking tuners, and installed them. They just cover the 2 pin holes of the original tuners, but do require the drilling of a small hole for the anti-rotation screw these tuners use. I measured the bore of the tuner holes in the headstock and it is 25/64". This is the same bore Gretsch used for the Sperzels, and the Planet Waves auto-trim lockers fit this nicely as well. I'm overall very pleased with the Planet Waves, they provide a clean install, good function, and full blackout on the front. Also, they are not as expensive - $52 vs the Sperzels at $70.

On my model, it used Fender Pickups instead of the Seymour Duncans. They are ok but not great. Perhaps a bit muddy. I expect to replace them before long. A few years ago they switched this model to SD's, but I'm not sure they are the ones I would pick to put in here. No downgrade in rating, just an observation. After all, someone else might love these pups.

The electronics (switches & pots) on mine are "import grade", and I can feel it, hear it...and in fact, they are shot. Now I bought this used but it has pretty clearly seen limited use so for the electronics to be crapping out this early in life...is indicative of the cheap import parts. So I'll be replacing those, hopefully with American parts assuming they will fit properly. I am downgrading half a star on that because frankly, there is no reason to use the cheap crap on a $700 guitar. Fender has such huge purchasing power that they could easily standardize on the same high quality electronics for everything, and from a cost perspective (cost not price) it would save them money. A little searching on the Fender site produced the factory wiring diagram, but changing the pups will change all the wire colors anyway. So...more to come on that as well. I will most like put Pearly Gates in both neck and bridge. (Not the "Plus" found on current models, and not the '59 neck RP pup.) Again, I will update this review when that is done. I'm exploring if I need to get an RP (reverse polarity) neck pickup similar to what was done by Fender. I like the push pull pot being on the tone knob for the coil splitting.

Last, lack of a case. This model sells for $700 without a case...that's a lot for what it is. This is made in Indochina...(mine was made in Korea). So they really could include a case at this price and still make a good profit. At least throw in a decent gig bag, Fender!

All in all it's an interesting Telecaster for the dual humbuckers and flame top, but if you are used to player american fenders or even current MIM fenders, there are some features of this that fall short as shipped. I'm not saying don't buy it, I'm just saying be aware of the differences it has compared to a MIM or American product. I think buying used then scrapping the import parts and upgrading may produce a better guitar for the same, or less money. (That's my hope, anyway.)

FINAL UPDATE: I have had this guitar for some time now. the planet waves locking tuners are awesome and the perfect tuner to upgrade this specific guitar with. It does require drilling 1 small hole for a screw, for each tuner, on the back of the headstock. But it covers the fender 2 pin holes, and they work GREAT. I did upgrade the pickups, and all the electronics. I put in Seymour Duncan Pearly Gates at the neck and bridge, and I replaced all the pots and switches. I also added a second push-pull for phase switching. Those pups are the bomb on this guitar. It's awesome for ZZ top and pinch harmonics. A very comfortable guitar...and with those upgrades it is reliable and sounds great.

title: Adequate inexpensive stand for situations where the microphone won't be disturbed
comment:
This is an inexpensive desktop microphone stand. It comes in four pieces (see photo). Assembly is simple and easy (note: there are no instructions, but it's not hard to figure out).

The base is sufficiently weighted to support a standard microphone, although I would not trust it with oddly shaped microphones with a long base or a very heavy speaker ball.

The clamp that holds the microphone is made of plastic, and there are no adjustments. You slide the microphone into the plastic and the plastic bends back to accommodate the microphone. I found that this works OK if you do not plan to touch the microphone after it's in place. However, if you try to adjust the position/angle of the microphone after it's in the plastic, the microphone has a tendency to slip out. As a result, I would only recommend this for use in the scenario where you carefully adjust the position/angle of the microphone and then don't touch it again. In other words, this is for simple home use, but certainly not for professional use of any type.

Given the price, I guess this is good enough--just know what you're buying.

title: ...why did they have to complicate the design?
comment: I bought a Hercules in-bell trombone stand 6 years ago and have used it 3-4 times a week to hold my King 2b Silversonic (small bore tenor).  I recently picked up a large bore Bach concert horn with an F attachement and was happy to find that my Hercules could not only support the weight, but also held it high enough that the weight of the horn was on the stand, not the tip of the slide.  Since I use both horns frequently I decided to purchase another Hercules stand so I could keep one in each case.  The new stand is everything I would expect from Hercules (solid build, quality materials), but there is one big difference between my new and old stand.  The new one has an adjustable down post.  Basically you can hold down a button on top and make the stand taller before you raise the in-bell piece and lock it in place.  I'm sure this was done to accomodate larger horns, possibly base trombones, but this just complicates that design and makes everything feel less stable.  Still a great stand, and you can't beat the convenience of sticking in the case when you also have to carry a big music book, stand and mutes, but I just wish they hadn't messed with the design.

title: Gets the job done
comment:
Pretty good product overall but there aren’t many other options, especially for this price range. The pickup certainly doesn’t cancel all noise but it is good enough for me. The sound level is uneven across the string. The high strings are much louder. Adjusting how the pickup sits in the guitar (move it closer to the low strings and farther away from the high strings) helps a little bit but it doesn’t level it out completely. A good amp/EQ will help address this. My biggest issue is the volume level overall. It’s not as loud as I’d like. I use a portable amp while playing out with my acoustic. That amp has more than enough volume when used with my other acoustic guitars that have other types of internal pickups installed in them. It’s loud enough to play with a full band with drums. This product is not loud enough using the same amp. I have to plug into a pedal to boost the volume. It works well with that setup. I already had a compressor pedal and a volume boost pedal that boost the volume well when used individually, but I wouldn’t be thrilled if I had to purchase a pedal to achieve a playable volume with this pickup, using my portable amp.

Otherwise it’s good. The pickup chord is plenty lengthy. It does not require batteries.

title: Good Quality & Easy to Set Up
comment:
This review is for Wrugste 2i2 USB Audio Interface with MIDI. My experience with these types of devices is medium-low, but this a very nice item for the price. It has all the right features. I have also been a proponent of XLR connections for all types of audio equipment, which this has. XLR is the best analog connection available. Not only is it superior in every way sonically (I have A/B tested it on equipment with XLR & RCA connections), but I have also seen it eliminate background noise and ground loops. Speaking of connections, I also like the USB-C connection. At this point every new device should be USB-C. They even include a USB-A to USB-C cable and a USB-A to USB-C adapter. They also include a sturdy XLR cable.

This audio interface appears to be well made, and it has nice controls. This is a very easy device to install and setup. Actually, there isn't much to do for setup. The only real setup is just plugging everything in. The device works nicely, and it's a great value.

title: The Venova is fun learning how to play
comment:
First off, I've never played an instrument with a reed before, so this is a totally new experience for me... I have played flutes and fifes before. I am really enjoying the learning experience with the Venova. I am far from good, but I am learning and improving daily... and it's fun.
I would recommend buying a pack of 2.0 cane reeds if you're a beginner. It comes with a synthetic reed that is rated at 2.5... the first sound I was able to get out of the instrument was something like an insane Canadian goose honking... after a few tries I switched to the cane reed which I'd bought at the same time as the Venova and it made it a lot easier to get started.
Does it sound like a real sax? No, not when I play it... the musicians in the videos make it sound really good and maybe someday I'll get there, but I don't think it has the rich brass sound of a real sax. It has it's own sound. For what it costs, in comparison to even a student sax, it's worth the money to me since I'm playing it mainly for my own enjoyment, not to perform on stage. Who knows, maybe one day I'll post a video on FB...

title: Great Case - Recommended
comment:
I've gone through two previous rack bags over a period of 4 years of heavy gigging (100+ shows per year), and though those cases served their purpose for a while, I figured I'm just going with a hard case from now on.

This is a very nice case for storing and transporting a drum rack. I typically gig with a fairly large rack (4 T-leg assemblies and 5 curved horizontal bars of varying lengths), and this case handles it with room for a little more. It is rugged, as are the wheels, and I'd highly recommend this case to those looking. My T-legs don't fit in the section supposedly for that purpose, as the base bars are 24", but taking the bass bar off isn't a big deal, and it all fits.

I rated this with 4 stars because of two reasons.

First really isn't Gator's fault, as it would lead to an even larger case - but the "I" design shape will only serve it's purpose in allowing you to keep your base bars on the legs if you use a short base bar. Since most racks come with larger bars used as legs, this design is kind of pointless. But if you're so inclined, put shorter bars on, and it will probably work fine. I personally would have liked a slightly larger case to accommodate my rack's legs, but I can see where many gigging players would prefer the smaller case...in which case, they should've just kept the "I" shape out of the design.

Second is that there is no velcro strap inside the case to wrap around your bars and secure them from bouncing around. This would have been a nice addition - nicer than the "I" design concept. With a strap, the bars would still rub against each other, and move slightly, but without one, you can expect some wear marks on your bars eventually.

All things considered, though, this is a great case, and I'm ultimately more happy with it than either of my previous bags. No stitching or zippers to wear out!

title: Matched impedance stereo mics produce pretty amazing results on a budget
comment:
Paired this with Behringer UMC202HD preamp to record choral arrangements using an ORTF pattern (110 degrees, with 17 cm separation at mic tip) which mimics human hearing. Kinda like recording for dummies, because it creates a nice stereo image and you don't need to be a genius to set it up. Just need a few adapters to prevent XLR cables for interfering with mic positioning.
In any case, the mics perform as advertised. Perhaps there are better mics for professional work, but I'm recording family and friends and we're getting pretty amazing results. I'm using a MacBook Air, Garage Band, and that UMC202HD preamp I mentioned above. Great little, portable recording studio.

title: Nice Replacement For A Factory Zinc Block
comment: This brass block is a nice upgrade to the cheap zinc ones you often get with factory guitars.  While it’s not going to affect tone the way a brass bridge or nut would, the brass trem block does enhance sustain over zinc blocks.  The brass transfers vibration more purelythus allowing strings to ring longer and more consistently.  This one item won’t totally revolutionize your guitar’s performance.  But it will enhance it a bit.  And hot-rodding guitars is all about the sum of many little improvements more than about any one improvement alone.  If it fits, it’s sure worth $22 to give it a shot.

This is with a Tom Holmes 5600 in the bridge, which is a bit bright admittedly, and compared to the black dunlop 1.0 mm pick I have been using, with amp settings that are moderate distortion, mids are not scooped.  I'm sure I could find an application they were suited for, like a darker distorted tone, but that's not what I've been doing.  But I really do like what they did with chord definition, a lot.

I lowered my rating to 4 stars because when it arrived the case was not fully closed and the bow was sticking out from the case bottom. Because of this the cello has a few prominent scratches and the bow lost a good amount of horse hair.

I would recommend this cello for beginners if they need a 3/4 size. I would recommend an adult purchasing something else as there are more options for full size cellos in this price range. title: Really does change the sound, but wear-out fast
comment:

Switching from the Dunlop Jazz III to one of these gives a much brighter and more articulate tone; it's kind of like I switched from the neck pickup to the bridge pickup, but ... the guitar isn't even plugged in!

(Dropping the different colored picks on a hard surface -- even two picks of the same gauge, but in different materials -- makes noticeably different sounds.)

So now I have a new guitar thing to geek-out about; not just pick gauge/shape, but material/density as well. GraphTech has just unlocked an entire palate of tones for my instruments, and just with picks.

(Update: I'm not a career guitarist, and these picks are already pretty worn after not a lot of use, so I am removing a star.)

comment:
This is a fun karaoke machine. Out of the box, everything was in perfect working order. Charging did take a few hours for the player and the 2 microphones but expected. Tested it with both microphones and Bluetooth connection to my phone. Bluetooth connection was made in seconds.
This has some cool features, the best is the vocal removal from the music being played, this is a mixed blessing in my case because I can't sing- I'll leave the vocals on-LOL. The effects help but they are a little strong for my taste but they definitely help.
To connect the microphones, just turn them on, that's it and they have volume control. Now sing along and be a star!!
Also, this is surprisingly loud for its size, and a nice benefit is it can be used as a standalone portable Bluetooth speaker which is nice for anywhere you need music and can fill a room. Lots of input options for music sources.
Lastly the LED light fun and bright in a dark room and can be turned on and off. If you are looking for some family and friend fun this is a great karaoke machine and a bonus Bluetooth speaker.

title: Great Parlor Guitar, Great Value!
comment:
I recently decided I wanted a smaller guitar, and since I own a Seagull Original S6 and am very happy with it, one of the brands I looked into was Seagull. The Coastline Grand has a solid cedar top and laminated cherry back and sides, like the S6, but unlike the S6 it has a narrower neck - 1.72. It's also got 14 frets above the body, which I think is a big plus, so I decided to go for it.

My Coastline Grand is as well made as my S6 - no surprise since Seagull offers consistently high build quality. The semi gloss finish feels good and looks nice, and everything was good. True, it needs a setup, but I like particularly low action, so every guitar I get needs a setup. The only thing I'm not thrilled with is the nut width, and the guitar is still very playable.

This guitar sounds especially good to me when strummed or fingerpicked softly. That's when you get the mellow cedar overtones. Played harder, it's got a brighter, midrangy sound that is surprisingly loud. If you don't care for that type of sound, just throw a set of Silk and Steel strings on it. While some of the more expensive parlors do have more bottom end, this one isn't a very bass-rich guitar, but it does sound good.

The bottom line - if you're looking for a smaller guitar, particularly a parlor, this is a guitar you should definitely check out. Good sound, build quality, finish and tuners - plus customer service is good about answering emails. I'm very happy with my Seagull Coastline Grand, and expect to be playing it for many years.

title: Practical and portable, but not the best
comment:
I really like how small this drum set is, you can fold the arms in and fit it into a small car which is awesome. I keep it in my dorm room for practicing. The snare drum on this is really nice- good response and velocity. The same goes for the toms. However, I am NOT impressed by the cymbals and the foot pedals. The cymbals trigger very poorly and are too quiet, so that will be very annoying unless you can edit them using a midi connection to a DAW. The foot pedals are THE WORST!!! My Rock Band drums had a better bass drum pedal than these did and that's saying something because it sucks. They're both unsensitive at best and totally unresponsive at worst. The bass drum pedal broke about a year after I bought the HD-1. The kits that come with the drum set are GARBAGE. They're fun to mess around with but not quality at all.

Pros:

-Very compact
-Nice snare and toms
-Good for practicing

Cons:

-Expensive
-Terrible cymbals
-Cheap pedals

title: G-Classy
comment: Short review- the BEST double pedal I've ever used. I've had experience with single-chain, duel-chain & direct drive DPs: this particular double pedal is easily the best in my opinion. The music I play tends to call for loud-quiet-loud moments a ton, so I needed a double pedal that could accommodate borderline stomping & lighter taps in the same song. Everything, from the beater down to the footboard, screams of durability & customization. The majority of DPs I've used has, to some degree, compromised durability for customization & visa versa. Gibraltar has successfully broken that depressing trend with the G-Class DP. One note though- the bag is something to get used to, if you've had other pedal bags before. However, it's not enough to knock a star off the review. Summary- this is the double pedal for power & precision in your drumming. A friend of the light or heavy footed among us, the G-Class double pedal will make a believer out of you. Classy indeed.

title: A home studio's friend, indeed
comment:
This is a surprisingly great little compressor. It doesn't have much character to speak of, just a good solid clean compressor.

It accepts either a single mono signal, or a single stereo signal, and does great on both.

All of the controls are exactly what you'd expect if you've used other hardware or software compressors. The Super Nice mode cascades 3 compression circuits so you can get a substantial amount of gain reduction without it sounding too conspicuous.

My main gripes are few, but here's what I'd like to see if they ever do a "revision 2":
- A dual mono option. That'd require a 2nd set of knobs to control each side, but it'd make this a slam-dunk winner over the ART PRO VLA II. The ART's dual mono and stereo link make it pull ahead in the bang-for-buck category for me.
- less (or more pleasant) distortion at higher gain reduction. It gets pretty nasty sounding when you really slam it, and not in a good way. It'd be nice if it maintained its clean sound as I really dig into the GR.

I can't really say that this adds much over what you could get with a good software compressor, but its small form factor and mono-or-stereo operation make it a good choice for a portable live rig, and of course it is right at home in a home recording studio like mine.

At this price, it's a really attractive option.

title: Great Value, Great Playability and Sound!
comment: Outstanding value. The things you really want right, out of the box, are.  The neck is a real joy... they've got a nice hybrid of fender and Ibanez going on... Very comfortable and fast.  The pickups are amazing for an inexpensive guitar.  This model will strike some as dark sounding, not as bright and twangy as other Gretsch hollow bodies.  There are aftermarket pickups for that.  Personally I really like the range, from super full & round to twang. Righteous with some slap echo. Works well with high gain, if you must, and the feedback is easily manageable.  Fit and finish were excellent.  Not much you'll need to do but get a roller bridge because of the bigsby - standard tune-o-matic dimensions.  Also have some nut sauce handy.  These tweaks will let the Streamliner hold tune really well.  Factory intonation and action were good but needed minor adjustment.  Some day you'll appreciate the drilled bridge post holes.  Solid build and feel, medium weight, less than a Les, more than a Strat.  Very nice finish work.  Some folks have criticized the painted f-holes and cheap black pickguard... I think those are smart ways to save on cost...  bound f-holes are nice but more for wall-hangers than players.

In there, putting one on the stand is fine but if you try that on a crowded stage, it's not all that effective. When I saw the vib. mode...for the price, I thought I would give it a go in a loud environment. It passed the test. Do not expect the transposition feature (to Bb) to work and I had read that in the reviews prior to purchasing it. Since I think in concert pitch, it is not a concern. I also read it was flimsy being made of plastic. I've used it all month...14 gigs and practice sessions on top of that...it's fine. I would recommend this little tuner.

I love it— beautiful tone, beautiful resonator, beautiful hardware. The ONLY problem I had was a strap swivel that kept coming unscrewed. I put some permanent thread lock on the screw and put the screw, washers and nut back together, and that annoyance is gone forever.

I used my Amazon card I got for Christmas and bought a wonderful fiberglass case that makes me smile every time I open or close it, or pick the whole case and banjo up with the heavy leather stitched handle.

But I fit banjo playing in with other important projects, so I’m always eager to fit an hour in two to six times a day. My wife complained that I bought such an expensive [not] banjo, until I showed her $2,000 and $3,000 offerings. I have a particular appreciation of one YouTube video producer who plays anything with strings! I’ve paid close attention to his fairly new banjo, and even though his is a leading brand name, they are both made in China with brands stenciled on the headstock. I’m on an ever decreasing income with rapidly increasing prices for fuel, Rx’s and groceries. So I pass on $2,000 when I can get the same banjo with interchangeable parts for 1/8 the price, both made in the same little factory.

I purchased this string action gauge with a discount code.

title: Decent for headphone practice, not so great through an amp
comment:
I've had this guitar for some time now, it was a gift from someone who worked on the Volkswagen commercial (for the car that this guitar came with). It's a relatively cheap import, but the built-in preamp gives it some extra value.

The preamp sounds kind of nice on headphones, but not so great when played through an amp. I've played this guitar with and without the preamp switched on, on all different tonal settings, and through 2 or 3 amplifiers. A lot of feedback just ruins the live experience for me, really. The pickups are so microphonic it's unbearable through an amp. If you plan on cranking the amp up even a little, be ready for a lot of feedback - worse if you switch on the preamp.

On a clean channel, it actually has a rather nice clean sound to it, especially the bridge pickups.

When the preamp is on for a clean channel, I'd describe the distortion as a vintage, gritty kind of boost. Not amazing, but not terrible either. Reminds me of one of those cheap Danelectro or Behringer distortion pedals.

Neck is wide enough for my tastes, standard tuners, easy to restring, minimal noise when plugged in, holds well for light or heavy gauge strings, nice sparkly finish is a plus. The nut on the cord jack comes loose a lot, but can be tightened longer with the right tool.

Altogether, this is OK as a first guitar for a beginner, or as a practice guitar.

I'd have given it 2 stars if not for the direct-to-headphone capability and the decent boost from the preamp through the headphones. The microphonic pickups just ruin the experience otherwise. Perhaps a nice set of pickups would do this guitar some good!

title: Excellent wireless set-up, at a great price!
comment:
I'm a LONG time hobby guitar player, and I've never used wireless, so I ordered this XIAOKOA package because I've occasionally thought about getting a wireless device, to avoid having to use cables at home. Right off the bat, let me say I am really impressed!

I love the design and how it's very adaptable to different guitars and situations, and pairing was very easy. Just turn on the transmitter and hold the plus button down until the lights start blinking, then turn on the receiver, and within a few moments it will pair (blue light on the receiver stops blinking). From then on you don't have to keep pairing, it will do it automatically when you turn them back on.

The only slight negative is that when I plug in the receiver to my pedal chain, it does add a very tiny bit of hiss, which the cable does not. But it is at such a low-level it is not bothersome in any way.

The sound quality is just like using a cable, and there's absolutely no lag at all. It feels just like I'm playing with a cable.

While the instruction manual is very clear and well written, it doesn't say much about the volume controls. So I experimented a little bit and found that the volume at 5, which is its default, is basically the same level as I get when I use the cable. I even used to decibel meter app, and actually found it was a little bit louder then the cable. So there is no need to increase the volume on the transmitter to get to "normal".

But what I found after playing a few nights, with my 5 and 15 watt tube amps, is that if you increase the volume of the transmitter it's actually acting like a boost. Almost like having active pickups. The amplifiers are driven into an overdrive which is very pleasing.

If I lower the volume below 5 it's acting like a cut. Kind of like plugging into the second input of your amp, which is usually lower DB. Both of these features are very useful, and they really should explain it in the manual.

So the bottom line on the volume control is: don't worry about turning it up or down, you'll have normal volume at the default volume 5. Just use the volume controls if you want to boost or cut your guitar signal. I also suspect if you run it at a higher volume it's going to run your batteries down much quicker.

I've had the units on about four hours on the first charge, and it was still reading 1 bar, which means about half power. When it starts blinking, that means you're down to 25% power. It also charges up very fast, with the dual USB charging cable.

Also included is a high quality eighth inch adapter, if you need to plug into a phone or other device.

I have my amps in a music room upstairs, and I walked downstairs with the guitar, and at the bottom of the stairs it started breaking up a little bit, but considering it was going through walls and everything else, that's not bad.

Being UHF it has 15 channels to choose from, which is great if you're going to have multiple people playing.

So five stars from me. I'm super happy and think the asking price is absolutely a bargain!

title: Worthless
comment:
Before I begin, I have to say that the product was warped when I took it out of the box. I mean, when I put it on the floor, I had to put a sugar packet under one corner, else it only sat on three feet. I wasn't going to return it for that reason because I could have just shimmed a corner with a larger rubber foot. This was a preview of things to come, though...

My goal was to use this pedal on stage, loaded with backing tracks. It seemed like a simple prospect; load up some backing tracks over usb and then press play. Never did I think I would waste four hours of my life trying to get the loading software to work properly.

Digitech Jammanager Librarian software is required to import loops from your computer. First off, you can only import .wavs. Who uses .wavs anymore? How hard would it be to include a decoder for more commonly used files (i.e. .mp3). That means I have to decode all of my loops every time I want to upload something. Whatever, I'm nitpicking at this point, but don't worry, it gets worse. Second, you have to install Microsoft Silverlight in Mac OS to even begin the installation. I knew this wasn't going be pretty (Microsoft and Mac), but I did it anyway. Sure enough, the software was something from a looney bin. It began by locking up and not offering a way to cancel or even close it. After a force quit, I tried again. It locked up...again. Restart, reconnect, freeze. Repeat. Repeat. Then, after feeling completely insane for trying the same thing four times, it transferred one loop successfuly. Finally, I got it! Nope, it froze when I tried it again. I began to suspect this was a known issue, so I booted to Windows 7 and installed the program (I even changed USB ports and wires). Same problem. Lockup. I repeated this painful process for two hours and finally was able to transfer four short loops. I decided to do some internet research on this and found that all forums are dead-ends filled with frustrated customers. There are no answers to the freezing program. Most users bite the bullet and just buy another SD card. I'm not going out like that...

Third, this pedal creates a considerable noise when plugged into my amp. I have multiple true-bypass stompboxes on my rig and don't have even a hint of a buzz, but when I plugged this in, it buzzed like a cheap alarm clock. I reduced the noise by taking it out of my input chain and plugged it into my effects return, but what good does that do?

Bottom line: This is a cheaply made pedal, with noisy architecture and worthless software. Why would you buy this? If you want a simple looper, go get a TC Electronics Ditto. If you want a complicated looper/sampler, try a Boss. Do not waste your time or money on this trash. Digitech has fallen off the map as far as I am concerned. Boo!
