title: Great value, get what you pay for.
comment:
This instrument is a great value, if you are reasonable with your expectations.
It’s a short scale, 30”, and has a narrow neck so it’s easy to play. The tuners work well, and the painless bridge makes string changes easy. I haven’t tested the electronics because if I want to play electric I’ll get a real electric.
The construction is where the cost savings are. The frets have rough ends, even sticking out over the cheap neck binding. The finish is cheap. The control panel on my model wasn’t tightly screwed in, and I don’t think it’s a perfect fit. The cheap plastic nut is too high making the action too high. The side markers are not even.
But it’s not all bad. The overall playability is ok, and can even be improved fairly easily. The sound is ok, just as any acoustic bass will only sound ok. The included tuner works well and is simple but easy to use.
The real value of this instrument is being able to keep it in my living room, handy when I want to strum some notes without being attached to a cord and amp. If it gets banged up it’s a cheap replacement. You will not find a better value for what it is.

title: Mounts in less than a minute and it works...
comment: This review is for the Vibramate and B7 Bigsby both. I will mention several modifications that one may want to try that still don't change your guitar from stock. I put this and the Bigsby on a '73 Goldtop Les Paul Deluxe. This era Les Paul has 6-32 threaded bridge posts that screw directly into the maple top of the guitar. When I had the bridge off I noticed a slight wobble in one of these posts so I screwed it out. I was only 1" long so I started screwing it back in and it kept going in further and further into the top. I went to the hardware store and bought a 1 foot section of 6-32 threaded stock for $2.00. I needed a 1/2" to mount the bridge on to and I wound up screwing a 2" piece of stock bottoming in the hole with a 1/2" sticking out. Did the same with the other bridge piece so instead of a 1/2" in the top I had 1 1/2" inches sunk into the top. Way more solid connection. I also changed the original ABR style bridge to a $29.00 roller bridge from Stewart MacDonald guitar supply. The roller bridge has two allen screws that tighten onto the bridge pieces/posts to make a very solid connection. The Bigsby went on in seconds and the only other changes you may have to make is you may need a larger strap button on the rear where the Bigsby tail piece screws on. The additional hardware makes it hard to get the strap on the stock button so a larger button, like a Fender style  and some washers to shim it out further will make it easier. It took longer to string up the guitar than to install once I got the other things sorted out. With the bridge posts changes and the roller bridge replacement my guitar is more solid sounding than it ever had before. There is no vibration loss from sloppy hardware. Now about the Bigsby itself. I love electric Neil Young and this is a huge part of his sound. The Bigsby works smooth, and does exactly what I expect it to do. 1/2 step bends and not much more, but that's all I need to play "Cortez the Killer". I put a little 3 in 1 oil followed by powdered graphite in the nut, work the Bigsby to get it in the slots well, and I'm in tune all night. I got both the Vibramate and the American made Bigsby from amplified parts for $175.00 total w/o any shipping charges. I'm happy. The Vibramate only works with the American B7. The imported B70 won't work with it. The only other thing is your guitar may not fit in the case as well as the Bigsby adds some height to your guitar, so you may be using a gigbag unless it does fit OK.

title: Super Nice, Well Made, BUT maybe too thick for its own good
comment:
This would easily be a 5 star review if design, material quality, execution of manufacture, packaging, appearance and feel in-hand were the only things to consider. Aside from the attractive bronze anodization, the rugged and substantial gauge of aluminum is the distinguishing feature, but it is also the rocker's Achilles heel. The piece is superbly engineered, but the engineer forgot "Form follows Function".

The rocker is cut from such a thick slab that the side-to-side "level/not nevel" condition becomes an extra factor in use. The edges are perfectly straight (verified on a .0005 surface plate), and when held exactly perpendicular to the fretboard it may be the best fret rocker you ever used. You may dent it but you'll never bend it. And it feels great in-hand.

BUT unless you have robotically perfect hands, when you are checking up and down and across the fretboard and feel that telltale slight bit of rocking, you have to first verify that it's longitudinal (across more than one fret) rather than lateral (the edge of the rocker landing and departing the frets as you move it back and forth) because it's SO thick that the edge becomes a significant plane that must also be perfectly aligned or else will feel very similar to uneven frets.

I felt bad returning it because it's such a nice piece of metal. If it were EDC it would score highly on every aspect other than functionality. Maybe if I was a professional luthier, my technique would be so dialed in that I'd like it. In my use, the number of false positives across several fretboards that disappeared after rechecking and taking the extra time to ensure I was holding it perfectly perpendicular to the fretboard made it too much work. A cheaper construction from a thinner sheet (if as perfectly staight as this one) is better for me, where the edge is not thick enough to become its own factor in use. I only want to worry about one plane of movement, not two.

The switch to this rosin fixes that right up and stops the scratchiness. There is a light build up of dust of the body, but then again I really work my rosin into the bow and wipe the excess off. Comes with that Melos rosin smell that can take some getting used to!

Melos uses freshly tapped sap from living, no by-products! A big plus in my book. Some people may complain about the price, but then again think about how much you spend on strings and your violin. Strings alone can cost $50 or more and last 3-6 months usually. This rosin for $20 can last a year+ Should you really complain about the price after thinking about that?

title: Really nice instrument

But now that tenor is one of my primary instruments, I figured I'd step up. And what a step-up it is! I'm not big on the bling, so I can take or leave all the shiny bits. But it's a great sounding instrument, makes the Ibanez sound like a toy in comparison. I'm also a big fan of the ukulele-style open minor 7th tuning, but rather than tune like a ukulele, I simply raised the D string up to E, thereby creating an instrument with the same notes as the ukulele, but in a different order (CGEA). I am very pleased with this arrangement - it lets you have an open minor 7th tuning but has a much fuller, richer sound. You have to turn your brain around a little - the lowest two strings are swapped vs a ukulele, so the chord shapes are a bit different, but just as playable.

title: So much potential, but lacks a level adjustment.
comment: Been working on getting this to work with my rig for several months. It sounds wonderful.  It has a nice analog sound, breathy and warm, and is very versatile, going from an EVH-style, to an Alex Lifeson swish, to lasers, dive bomb effects, shimmery chorus-like doubling. However, there's just a major flaw with the volume.  I've tried it through the effects loop, in the front of the amp, before and after everything in my chain and no matter what, it's too loud. The takeoff effect is just ear-splittingly loud, and even the taxi side has a noticeable volume boost that's just a little too much.  Maybe a hint is in the booklet illustrating Paul Gilbert's rig, where he puts it right before a compressor going into the front of the amp, but my compressor adds gain that I don't want, so that's not in the cards.  Oh well.  I'll keep trying, but I think the next step is to take it apart and see if there's some adjustment on the board that can be made.  If not, I may have to sell it and get the MXR.  So far, I can't justify giving it an upper echelon rating, despite having potentially one of the best flange sounds out there.  I'll update if and when I figure out a solution.

So I received the Maschin Mikro MK2 as an early xmas gift. It was brand new and came with the installation disc and Komplete Elements disc (contains sounds). The install disc wouldnt read in my Mac, it was defective, and I was using version OSX 10.5.8. I called Native and the first time they were jerks! He actually hung up on me! He told me I needed to upgrade my OSX to OSX 10.7. I told him clearly on the website the driver supported 10.5.8, and he told me I needed to learn how to read! I was pissed! Anyways, I upgraded to OSX 10.6 (snow leopard) and began my installation. I finally had it working and it was an uphill battle from there. I have never used this prior so the learning curve was high. There are lots of videos on YouTube so that helped. If you buy this MAKE SURE YOUR OS WILL SUPPORT IT!!!!
If you are looking for an MPC this is not for you! You have to use a computer to get any sounds to come from it, so DO YOUR RESEARCH. I am integrating it will Logic Pro 8, and the work flow is great. comment:

Also ordered a center channel cable, but that won’t be as critical from an install point. Glad I didn’t order the cables for the front too, as I had planned. Something said to wait on those. No way I could like looking at these, as would be the case with the fronts. I had also planned on ordering an AC mains cable with matching sleeving. I will remove those from my save for later list. No point in visually matching a cable I wouldn’t use for free. Make no mistake, these have been photographed from flattering points of view, and you will not enjoy looking at them snaking to your fronts all twisted and unnatural looking. These would have been in the wall running to the very back left and right speakers, but the last foot or so, would show. I can see now they will not lie in a way that looks pleasing to the eye. Just trying to straighten them to fish them up all the wall and across the ceiling, was a major pain. After two tries, I stopped to write this review. Not sure what else Amazon has to offer, as the results that fit this length and other parameters, are not showing up in search. It’s 11:25PM at the moment and I’ve put two red bulls on ice for the fishing expedition that lies ahead. I have professional gear for that, including poles that are 16’ when screwed together, so I should be good to go for that part, but I need someone to keep this garbage from coiling as it’s pulled. If these were round, it wouldn’t even cross my mind.
*Update*
Installed - Definitely needed the help I received. The flat nature of these cables is indeed a hindrance when fishing them through a structure. All that aside, upon further inspection, one screw-on banana plug cover was missing. That prompted me to notice how crudely these cables were assembled. Crooked cuts on the rubber sleeves, wires not inserted straight and fully into the plug. Also, no hot knife was used on the external sleeping. Very, very sloppy construction. I took photos. Had to.

title: You don't sacrifice sound quality for Price
comment:
I don't always write reviews, but when I do it's because the product is so great I get excited to tell people about it. I bought the DR-40 primarily for use in video production, but I'm also a musician and I can say this: the DR-40 is one of the best handheld recorders I've ever used. The Zoom H4N has nothing on this product. The most frustrating thing about the Zoom is that all options are buried in digital menus and you have to go digging to change any settings. The DR-40 has a lot of quick adjust buttons, like a mixer button so you can adjust levels of individual channels, panning of each channel, and sends for each channel quickly and easily. REC mode button lets you reroute inputs and change between 4ch, stereo/mono in seconds. The stop button acts as a home button, so if you do get buried in menus it's one button and you're back to monitoring levels and ready to record. This saves a lot of time, and the video crew doesn't always have to be waiting on sound anymore.

As far as sound performance, I was blown away the second I hooked up my headphones, the onboard mics are cardioid mics that have comparable quality to higher end recording microphones I've used. It's great at picking up low frequencies and does very well in the high end.

If there are any downsides, it's the SD slot is impossible to get to without a knife, but the housing is rubber and completely unnecessary; I'm considering just cutting it off. Also, when trying to do 4 channel recording with inputs set for EXT w/phantom power and 2nd inputs coming from Internal mics, I noticed a strange tone, but I haven't had it happen since. That's a very particular application though, and I think avoidable if you play with the settings.

The biggest bonus: ONLY $140. the Zoom H4n, which I like far less, costs $300. This is the best recorder, and doesn't require you to sacrifice sound quality to save some money. It's a no brainer.

title: Sounds great
comment:
[[VIDEOID:eb9c0caae417543d8cb58f3be42f5ee]] This is a great little ukulele for the price.

You get a lot of accessories (some are useful, others not so much) including a carrying case, extra strings, cleaning cloth, tuner, strap, and guitar picks. The case is actually pretty nice and has plenty of storage room for the rest of the accessories. The strap works, but since there is a bolt for a strap on the bottom, I'm not sure why the included strap is for hooking in the sound hole. The strings are a bit cheap, but they still sound decent. For a budget, beginner set, they aren't bad. Cleaning cloth is nice, but the guitar picks are kind of useless since the Ukulele is traditionally played with finger-picking. The tuner is actually really nice and works very well for this instrument. The only downside is that, while the battery is included, there is no indication of which way to insert the battery (positive side up, negative side facing in). The only thing that is missing is any information on the "free online lessons" mentioned in the item's name--there is no paperwork or mention of these lessons anywhere in the packaging or the items.

Th Ukulele itself is rather nice, especially for the price. It's smooth polished and looks great, but I have strong doubts about it being solid wood. The instrument is easy to tune (G-C-E-A is the standard tuning if you weren't aware) and the fretboard feels nice, although, if you have large fingers, it's a bit tough to hit chords accurately.

Overall, this is a great beginner's kit for Ukulele at a great price.

title: Your child's band teacher says "no thanks"
comment: I've been a band teacher for 10 years, and a percussionist for much longer than that. This snare drum kit is not acceptable for students of any age. The sticks are too light for students to gain any muscle strength in their arms. The practice pad is too thin and made of the wrong kind of rubber to be effective. The case falls apart within a month or 2. The stand isn't tall enough for most students to stand and play. As for the drum itself... you will need to purchase better quality drum heads if you're going to use it, and the hardware isn't built to last. When my students show up for the first day of band with one of these drums, I call their parents immediately and tell them to buy either the Pearl or the Yamaha kit instead. So it might seem like this is a good choice to save money, but by the time you buy enough spare parts to compensate for how terrible this drum is, you have actually spent just as much as you would have if you bought a quality drum in the first place.

title: Great Value! My only complaint is that it gets ...
comment:
I bought this to replace my Yamaha MG16CXU Mixer. This mixer is loaded with features. First and foremost are the balanced XLR-outputs. Each mic has a gain control, Mic 1,2 have compression and if you have high impedance equipment or just need to give a singer a little bit of a boost, each mic has a 26 dB pad. There two stereo line in connections with both 1/4" and RCA and the third line in that has a direct USB feed from a PC using the Yamaha Cubase driver at 24 bit/192kHz. The line in from the PC using the USB has very little latency and has zero ground loop interference. To top it all off, there is phantom power to the mics and 24 SPX effects that can be injected into each channel and you can use the Cubase AI code included to mix it all up yourself on the PC. Great Value!

My only complaint is that it gets hot. Sometimes a little too hot that makes me feel uncomfortable leaving it on when I walk away from my PC.

title: Great value - but watch out for glitches during power-up.
comment: I've been using this M-Track Plus for a few months now as part of our church sound equipment. Basically it's used as a USB I/O interface between a Win-7 PC & our 32 channel mixer board for video playback & recording on the PC.  Plug & Play set-up was very easy & straight forward. The PC recognized & loaded drivers without any trouble and the sound quality in & out is top-notch. Having a headset jack with adjustable volume definitely helps our sound techs monitor the audio quality to/from this device, and getting all it's power from the USB cable helps keep the sound room clean & noise free.  The only CON is that I've noticed "occasionally" that the unit doesn't always power up correctly during the PC power up, causing loss of functionality, but this issue is only minor because it has always corrected itself in a few seconds by un-plugging & re-connecting the USB cable.  Overall, I'd recommend this unit for small budget sound systems needing a simple stereo I/O interface.

title: Good basic protection for the price.
comment:
This is not a bag you would want to use for gigging every weekend, but for occasional gigs where you have total control over your instrument during loading and unloading, it is more than adequate and well constructed for light duty.

My Roland V-Combo VR760 fits perfectly, even though the product specifications indicate that the bag is 1/2 inch too short in length. In fact, my keyboard fit t like a glove, so the trepidation I had in ordering it turned into both relief and delight.

The pockets are large - I have a Moog EP2 expression pedal and an On-Stage KSP-100 sustain pedal in the bottom pouch (both in their original packaging), and a 21' instrument cable plus the power cord in the middle pouch, with both room to spare and no need to use the top pouch. Had I dispensed with the original packaging for the expression and sustain pedals I could have used the top pouch for the cables, the middle pouch for the pedals, and the bottom pouch for music (it will easily hold the Real Book plus more charts.)

The only weakness is the shoulder strap - it is not padded and cuts into my shoulder with the relatively heavy VR 760 in the bag. However, for the price this does not detract from the value ... it is what it is.

Bottom line: a decent bag for occasional transporting, and a great bag for storing your keyboard when it's not in use if space is limited. I am very pleased with my purchase.

title: This Gorgeous  C12 Lookalike Sounds Wonderful Too!
comment:
MXL V67G. This was the very first MXL condenser microphone that I ever bought. Since then I've added a bunch of affordable models from MXL's huge lineup.

If you've got about $100 to play with you can't go wrong with this microphone. If you aren't spending the $100 on another SM57 or an SM58, get the V67G instead. The sound quality is excellent, plus it looks alot like the famous AKG C12. Your friends will think you hit the lottery when they see this sexy looking microphone sparkling in your studio! Go ahead, do your research and check out the many positive reviews on the V67G microphone...it's all really good news.

MXL is a great company that provides a lot of bang for the buck with their products. They have a huge selection of models. I have pairs of the MXL 2003A, 603s, 993, R40 ribbon, and 960 Tube mic (similar to V69me), I also own the MXL 4000 and yes, the V67G. I can recommend them all. MXL also back most of their products with an excellent 3 year warranty.

I worked in radio broadcasting for over 20 years and I've used the very best microphones available, costing thousands of dollars, Neumann U87's. (see my review for the U87 on amazon) The V67 uses a circuit design, similar to Neumann's, to reduce the brittleness and reduce the harshness of the microphones' K67 capsule. I think that the V67 is quite impressive for the money!

Famous mic designers and mic modders really like the MXL products too... I think that most of their current models sound just fine as is. MXL is doing the neccesary QC. They have their own design team and build the microphones in their own factory, so many of their new "stock" versions are actually pre-modded. The MXL microphones are gaining in popularity with music industry insiders & recording artists. Grammy nominated (winning) engineers and producers are using them everyday on their own professional recordings......that my friends, speaks volumes.

Based on the many positive reviews I've read, I'm looking forward to auditioning and perhaps owning the CR-89 & Genesis tube microphones when I can afford them.

title: These Strings are PHAT, (But need modification on some basses)
comment:
It's a simple known fact that if you're looking for a fat, warm, and mellow tone, these are the strings to install. If you've never used flatwounds, you'll love the feel! However, some people can experience more fret noise while others experience less--it is completely determined by your specific techniques and finger mechanics.

MAKING THEM FIT: On many basses, the B string in this set presents a problem of being threaded through the bridge hole. This can easily be overcome quickly and easily by trimming the blue thread down at the base. True, why should we have to alter or modify a new set of strings? Well, if you want the smoothest, fattest sound without going to black Nylon, you'll take 30 seconds and a pair of scissors to make the mod!

INTONATION: If you're going from a round-wound to this set, you'll need to do an intonation set-up. These strings change the intonation considerably on every bass I put them on. You'll also probably notice, on most Fender basses, that the G string set-up will actually be longer than the D (on most non-flatwound strings the smaller strings are a progressively shorter set-up). Also, there's a good chance that you will be at, or almost at the MAXIMUM length possible on the B string for accurate intonation on Fenders.

*IMPORTANT NOTE!!!

LENGTH: PAY CLOSE ATTENTION to the amount of string needed if you're going 'THROUGH-THE-BODY'! If the body set-up requires more than 1/4" of additional string in the body, it might be better advised to get the SUPER LONG strings. *I tried to put these on a through-the-body 34"-scale Epiphone and came up with the thread being 1/2" into my first fret. On that guitar, I have to use the Super Long. On the Fender AM Jazz V, the thread, once the strings are tightened, was right at the nut. So a Super Long set would have been fine here as well if you didn't mind some fat on your B tuner peg.

Summary: GET THE LONGER LENGTH for through-the-body if needed, TRIM THE THREAD ON THE B, and DO THE INTONATION SET-UP. Don't be afraid to trim the thread and make these strings work for you. You'll be GLAD YOU DID!!!

title: BT1 - The only Roland product I do not recommend.
comment:
I bought two of these BT1 triggers, one from Amazon and the other from a reputable local music store where I live in South Africa. FWIW both indicate made in China on the box.
After a lot of back and forth with the local Roland representative it was determined that both are faulty.
In short, the symptom is that when I switch on the TM6-Pro trigger module, unpredictably, the BT1 does not respond to normal (fairly strong) hits for up to 12 strikes then suddenly works. Sometimes during playing they “go dead” and don’t respond to strikes which is very disconcerting. This unpredictability is highly frustrating and in fact totally unacceptable for performing.
I see there are other reports of similar behaviour by reviewers so I too must confirm beware of this product.
If you really think it’s for you, get one but don’t buy online. Go into a reputable music store and let them open one and demonstrate that it works right with your trigger module and for what you want to do with it. I will not buy one again.
It was not immediately apparent that mine were faulty, for a long time I thought that I may have just not got the setup right, but really the default settings should work, that should have been a clue right? That in itself wasted valuable time. The back and forth with my local Roland agent was also lengthy and ultimately it turns out the BT1 is the problem.
I have many other Roland electronic drum components and not one has ever missed a beat. They are Rock solid in my opinion.
Unfortunately the return window has closed for the Amazon one but Roland South Africa is making a plan to exchange the other locally bought one for something else. I must say Roland has been very helpful and I’m still a fan of their good gear, just not this particular product, the BT1.
Go ahead and read lots of reviews about the BT1 to get the full picture before buying. You will see that the BT1 is a unique device, being designed to mount directly on the side of a snare drum or Tom-Tom and not be affected by hard snare hits and cross talk. For this to work the BT1 actually “consumes” both sides of a dual trigger input (Head & Rim) so this is inherently inefficient. Whereas you easily get two discrete channels (H&R) and therefore sounds with a PD or PDX pad for example. It can be done with a BT1 but takes some settings magic to setup two BT1’s on a dual input successfully. Read about BT1 vs BT1-sens settings in the trigger setup in your module. Too many if’s with a BT1 … just get two PD8’s plug in and go.
I hope this is helpful and provides you with good info to consider. I am not an E-drum expert by any stretch so take this review from whence it comes and make your own choice. I hope this review helps inform your choice.

title: Good product; hellish customer service
comment:
This product easily hooks up to your iPad or iPhone via GarageBand. It has the perfect number of keys for composing, and the keys are comfortable to play.

However, heaven forbid you need to purchase any kind of accessories or replacement cable for this device. IK multimedia barely answers their phone, and people give you conflicting information If you do happen to finally reach someone. Over the span of two weeks, I tried to contact the company through Facebook, Twitter, and phone to figure out which replacement cable to buy (It turns out that it is proprietary and cannot be bought anywhere else except through IK multimedia).

By the way, the company has a no-returns policy, So when I inevitably purchased the wrong cable and was not able to use it, I had to jump through ever widening hoops to return it. There was a series of communications within the company about how and whether I would be able to return this cable that I only purchased because of incorrect information given to me by someone that works at this company.

Of course, I ended up having to pay for the shipping myself and no return slip was provided. However, I did have to fill out an involved form about why I needed to return this product.

I can’t believe I went through all of this for a refund of less than $30. If I have to do it over again, I am not going to deal with this company and will instead purchase a competing product.

title: Does the job, but could be better built
comment:
I own two of these stands, to add to a set of several similar stands for use by music ensembles. I chose these because they can support heavier loads such as a binder of music, while remaining reasonably portable. For these jobs it works very well.

This stand is very similar to the slightly more expensive Pinnacle stands, of which I already own some. I had hoped for similar quality construction, but find the Stagg disappointing. The desk seems to be made of a lighter gauge metal that flexes easily, and the fittings are not as robust. I don't think these stands will be nearly as durable, and they will not go on the road with me unless absolutely needed. If I could find the Pinnacle stands for sale, I would swap these out in a heartbeat.

The shipping carton and packing are inadequate. One stand arrived damaged and had to be returned. All three stands, including the replacement, arrived with bent desks. But the metal is so light that the desks can be restored to reasonably flat.

In summary, this stand is sturdy enough to hold a substantial load, but the quality of construction might not stand up to hard use.

The reeds are fairly consistent, and the lower price means that breaking a reed is not too much of an expense for whomever is footing the bill for reeds (teacher, student, school, parent...). The provided plastic cases do a good job protecting the reed, as long as they are not pushed in too far. I usually move students to Rico Royal or Vandoran after their first full year or two. Amazon does have excellent prices on reeds - definitely recommend buying here!

You will damage your Volca!!!
They’re giving out terrible advice regarding how they hook theirs up to monitors. Obviously they don’t know what they are doing and have no business giving out advice. You can NEVER use an audio splitter cable in REVERSE!! There’s a good reason those cables are called ‘splitters’ and NOT ‘joiners’. Because they are meant for one purpose and one purpose only. To SPLIT an audio signal. NOT TO COMBINE IT!! Worst advice ever. Just Google using a splitter in that way and you’ll see what I mean. I don’t want to get into the whole explanation of why. But short answer is if you feed a monitor (or any piece of audio gear) two inputs like that, their impedance is not equal. Causing imbalanced impedance to be fed into those monitors, and what happens is as each inputs impedance rises and falls, the imbalance causes the audio signal with the highest impedance to flow back into the Volcas output jack! And the Volcas were not meant to handle signals from other Volcas coming into the output jack. You will eventually damage your Volca. So please, use a headphone splitter for splitting headphone signals into two. But don’t try and invent something like ‘a backwards splitter joining two signals and yay! We have a backwoods $5 mixer!’
No…you don’t.
You’ll actually need to get a real mixer with resistors and circuits designed to safely join multiple audio signals into one signal path.
If cost is an issue they have tiny little usb powered 4 channel mixers here on Amazon for $24 that will do the job. Wish there was a way to remove that review telling people to ruin their Volca using a Belkin headphone splitter the complete wrong way.
https://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/B08393DLGV?psc=1&ref=ppx_pop_mob_b_asin_image

title: The best purchase I could have ever made!!!
comment:
A few months ago, I bought a controller from another brand. It was really ok, but after a little while, it turned out to be quite basic, so I quickly outlearned it and started thinking about buying some professional cdj's and a djm from Pioneer. However, there were some problems with that idea. Besides the obvious budget obstacle, there was the fact that my entire music collection is in MP3's in my laptop, not in cd's nor in flash drives or SD cards.

With this in mind, it was really futile for me to spend that much money buying a CDJ bundle like the 350's, the 900's and specially the 2000's, if I was only mixing from my laptop.

Fortunately, I accidentally stumbled upon the DDJ-S1, a dj controller that performs almost all the features presented by any of the professional CDJ's, like saving hot cue points, editing loops (not just making and doubling/dividing them), enabling master tempo mode, which allows you to change the pitch without altering the track's tonality, and even counting among its prowesses the slip mode, which lets you resume the track from the point where it would be if no loop had been made when you exit the loop mode, because the song would have actually been playing in the background. And many others!!!

And if that's not enough, then there's the look. Other than the weight, which is significantly lighter than the CDJ's, and the fact that it is a complete unit and not split into three components (two players and a mixer), it has the appearance and the feel of the professional relatives from Pioneer.

Oh, and it costs half as much as the CDJ-350 and the correspondant mixer. Imagine how much less compared to the higher models, like the 850's and the 2000's.

In all, there simply isn't any better option, except perhaps the DDJ-T1 (not the DDJ-SX, by the way), if you want a controller that lets you perform like a professional, either in your own home or in larger gigs, but don't really wanna spend a fortune in your gear.

The thicker body takes up more space in the cradles and it stays upright, like how you'd expect. The cradles are bendy enough that you can bend them a little bit to fit your need, but I still wouldn't use them for an electric instrument.

I planned to pay the $79 to the dealer, play the Makala for a week, and then give it away when I flew home.  It would be cheaper than renting.  I didn't even bother getting a box or a case.  Imagine my surprise when I started playing it and fell in love with it.  It comes with Aquila strings and it has a bright, punchy sound.  The action seems a little high but that's easy to get used to and it lets the strings ring out more resonantly.  If you are just learning to play, you'll be making all your chords down near the nut, so the action won't matter that much anyhow.  This is a great buy for a beginner, or even for a fussy, semi-expert like me.  Make sure you buy a Snark tuner to go with it.  After a week of playing it, I went back to the shop and got a Kala padded case and took it home with me.

title: Too flimsy for an SM57 and lots of resonance from other drums
comment:
For a smaller mic these might work better but I would not recommend on an SM57. To get the angle I wanted I'd need to pull the extension out a bit, and once you do that the mic starts wobbling all over the place. Each time you hit the drum, the mic will move around considerably.
On the floor tom it worked OK because it wasn't connected to anything else. But on my rack tom, the mic picked up so much resonance from the kick because every time the drummer hit the kick, the vibrations transmitted from the kit to the attached rack tom and right to the mic. There was definitely some of this resonance on the floor tom mic too, but I was able to remove most of it with a gate. I could not get the rack tom properly gated without sacrificing tom sound. I am back to lugging around more mic stands.

With a smaller, lighter mic you might have better results?

title: This is not an XM8500 with a Switch
comment:
If you are expecting a Behringer XM8500 with a switch. you won't find it here. Their responses are completely different.

The SL85S has an overly bright sound with very little midrange. It has a lot of midbass handling noise and will require a high pass (100Hz or higher) filter. It has noticeably lower output than the XM8500.

It has far less presence in the mid-range than the XM8500. The highs are crisp and intelligible in a good way, but are not in balance with the missing mids and the bump in the mid bass. The overall sound is a "scooped-out" mid with emphasized highs and lows. This may work for some applications like mic'ing an instrument, but for me, the "scoop" removes too much "presence" in the vocal range for singing.

title: Good enough for the money
comment:
It holds up guitars. I can't give it an enthusiastic endorsement, but it does hold up guitars. So you get a base 3 stars. Here are some of my minor peeves.

I remember the assembly instructions being rather poor. Just sort of a photo copy without enough detail to tell you which way a part was oriented without really scrutinizing the tiniest details. I managed to assemble it just fine, but it took some deciphering and trial & error.

The angle that the guitars lean means I need to keep the stand at least 6 inches from the wall or else the guitar heads will bang into the wall. I suppose that could be seen as a positive since it makes it less likely the guitars will tip out.

The pegs are spaced about 4" on center, so anything but solid body electrics will occupy a couple slots. My Gretsch hollowbody isn't that big but it would be bumping into its neighbor if I didn't leave an empty slot between.

Balancing the guitars in the stand isn't as seamless as it could be. There are three points of contact: the two bottom rails and the back rail. You often wind up with the guitar resting against the back rail and front bottom rail and rotating a bit. You have to take a moment to wiggle the guitar a bit so it's resting on all three rails. Then you also need to adjust it a bit so the neck leans on the right peg to avoid bumping into its neighbor. I'm not griping, just giving full disclosure. I imagine there are better designs that would let the guitar settle into a stable position more easily.

The foam is alright for now, but it feels like if I accidentally snagged it with anything remotely sharp it would get a big gouge in it or tear.

You get what you pay for. It does the job, but if you want to fill it with your collection of 4- and 5-figure guitars you could surely do better.

title: Good quality audio, not great noise reduction
comment:
This is a wired lavalier mic with a long cable, designed for use on a camera or phone. There is a selectable switch to indicate if you have a phone or camera, which I think is there because phones have those combo mic/headphone ports, and another switch for the mode -- on, noise reduction, and off.

I have included a video that compares the sound quality of the built-in 2017 iPad Pro mic to this Nikoeo mic. The dynamic range is good; it picks up the higher tones far more clearly than the ipad. Finger picking sounds clearer and rings truer, tho it's not as "warm" and mellow sounding as the ipad. The pickup of voice is where it really shines. Voices are very crisp and clear, it also reduces plosive crackles and pops, I imagine this would be the perfect mic for quick run and gun style interviews. Although this claims to have noise reduction, don't mistake this for some high end noise reduction tech -- I think it's just a filter on certain frequencies. The noise reduction reduces some noise but not fully. There is less ambient static but it's still noticeable at the end.

This mic is compact and super quick to set up and use, with a sturdy clip and seemingly sturdy wind guard. Also comes with a really convenient and low profile carrying bag. Overall fairly good quality audio but don't expect great noise reduction.

title: Strongly recommended for bottleneck slide playing - any musical style!
comment:
One of the best slides out there, imo.
While certain different qualities still cause me to want to use other material slides (ceramic, glass, steel, etc.) at certain times, this thick-walled brass slide is definitely my main go-to!
There's an impressive breadth and fullness of tone with this Dunlop 224, and it's a blast for both acoustic and electric guitars.
It's hefty weight can fatigue my pinky after a while, but I can't get this tone with any of my other slides!
Due to the heft, it can be challenging to avoid hitting the frets, but with some practice (and perhaps higher action), it's worth the effort!
I have a dozen or so slides of various materials, but I've yet to come across a better slide for robust, amazingly toned, thick and yet perfectly clear slide sound quality!

title: These are excellent picks!
comment:
I've experimented with all sorts of different picks over the decades. I've now got quite the collection which I keep in dish in my practice space. I often just grab a random pick from the dish without looking and play with whatever ends up in my hand. It's amazing how much of a difference a pick can make in the tone of the guitar and effect the style of your playing, I like to challenge my self with this variability.

The Jazztone 205s are one of my all time favorites and it's what I tend to carry with me. I rarely leave the house without a pick in my 'change pocket'. If I had to limit myself to only ever using 1 pick, this would be the one.

The 205 is smaller than a 'normal' pick, about same size as a 'jazz' pick but slightly narrower. It's 2.5 or 3mm thick, so quite thick. It produces a very nice warm sound and provides a lot of variation on attack depending on how you hold it and how much you dig into the strings. The tone is comparable to the flow or 'tone masters' which cost significantly more.

I usually take an emory stick or fine sandpaper to contour the edges slightly which further improves their playability in my opinion.

You really can't go wrong with these!

title: Good for smaller monitors
comment:
Good isolation pads for smaller monitors and near-field speakers. I am using these with 3 way Moukey near field speakers, and they fit almost perfectly.

Isolation pads are really a good investment for multiple reasons. First, they keep the speaker from reacting to it's location...meaning, it won't create nearly the resonance by the surface it's sitting on. I could tell that the bass isn't carrying thru the walls into other rooms after using these. It allows you to hear the speakers based on their merits, and not the reactions to other materials.

It actually makes the speakers sound cleaner. I also use this type of isolation for other speakers, and you can tell the sound difference.

These are also good for living in shared wall environments (apartments/townhomes). It can cut down on the sound traveling thru floors, etc...and bugging your neighbors.

They are worth the cost.

title: Fantastic Guitar, Fantastic Price
comment:
I bought this guitar from Amazon for an awesomely priced Lightning Deal around 4 weeks ago. The first one that arrived from Amazon had a deep divot on the 11th fret near the B string, making string bending impossible (as the string got stuck in the tiny metal valley). This was unacceptable. Also, the intonation was unable to be set on the G string, as the furthest back you put the saddle would not quite get the intonation right.

This was a huge bummer, as the guitar looked amazing. I had never ordered, much less returned a guitar anywhere via shipping, but it was a painless process. I put in a RMA, Amazon paid for the UPS shipping label back, and I packed it back up like I received it, and sent it back. They promptly sent a new one.

The new one's intonation was not set in any way, shape or form. In particular, the (low) E and A strings were WAY sharp once you got up to like the 5th fret. Luckily though, the bridge and saddle were such that intonation could be set perfectly with some time and effort.

Also, as the guitar comes, I'm not a huge fan of the stock pickups. I play metal, and there just wasn't at all enough attack crunch for metal. As a comparison, I think it's very similar to the pickups on my Epiphone Explorer, which is used mostly for rock, country and southern rock. These pickups are just not made for metal.

So with all of that said, I put some EMGs (EMG 81 in the bridge, EMG 60 in the neck) in, and it sounds amazing.

So, as it comes, I give it 4 stars. If the intonation is set like I received both of my guitars, it will absolutely require being set by yourself or someone who knows what they're doing. But honestly, this isn't a big deal, as long as it CAN be set. And also, the pickups were not for metal IMO. So they need to be replaced I honestly think.

With the intonation set and the pickups replaced, however, this becomes an absolutely perfect metal beast. The look is downright amazing. It really is a beautiful guitar. And the sustain and sound is fantastic. Playing Flying-Vs sitting down, you need to put the crook of the V on one of your legs, which isn't uncomfortable IMO. But playing in a standing position, the outside of the V is PERFECTLY laid out to rest at the bottom of your forearm, and this becomes the absolute best/easiest guitar I've ever played (standing up). Not that other guitars are 'hard' to play, but this just seems perfect.

Overall, with the stock pickups, I'd give it four stars. But as I said, replace the pickups (IF you want, different people have different tastes) and you've got yourself an amazing guitar. I'd give it six stars if I could.

title: Generally ok ... but be very cautious if using for bass guitars or other heavy instruments
comment:
I've been using it for my two five-string electric bass guitars for a while. Generally, it's been very dependable. The auto-grip system that grips the instruments where the neck meets the head seems to work great. However, unfortunately, I found out recently that it has the potential to be very unstable.

Since the two instruments are hanging on opposite sides of each other, and the support base is a tripod, it's not possible for both instruments to have a support leg directly underneath them, which I assume would make it more stable overall. Generally, it hasn't been a problem, but the other night, I unintentionally bumped the stand from the empty side, and the bass on the other side went crashing to the ground.

I have been using this both at practice and at gigs because I switch between the two basses for different songs relatively frequently. It's something I've been depending on for quite a while. The tipping and falling over only happened once for me, but that is still once too often for me to be comfortable using it moving forward.

I suspect that if you just use relatively lightweight instruments, you're probably fine. My 5-string Fender jazz bass (which fell) weighs 10 lb. Fortunately, I only ended up with a broken string, I think. But it's not an experience I want to repeat.

title: Robust design -- but look out for tipping
comment:
While I own small gig quality pro/ semi-pro gear (Electrovoice / Crown), I already have stands for those. But I also have small bookshelf size speakers that I wanted to put on stands because there are no shelves nearby.

These stands work well and are easy to adjust height and are sturdy enough for 90 pound speakers.

But there's one problem. Your speakers are liable to slide off the "device resting plate." In fact, if you, a child or a large pet merely bumps the stand, the speaker is liable to fall off.

Some bracing will be needed if you want to avoid this and leverage the other good features (mainly robustness) of the stands.

It should be noted that these are not "designer" stands for domestic, non-industrial style interiors; they're patterned after what I would be carrying to a gig and using to place monitors on -- though with the pro/semi-pro gear, the stands better secure the speaker.

comment: I have a Yamaha Revstar (820cr) and while it's a nice guitar and I like it, it's not one of the two big name brands in the U.S.  I was looking to upgrade the stock tuners to some locking ones and found myself in a world of measurements and visual comparisons to try to find a set that would align with the existing drill holes on the back of the headstock and with pegs that were the right diameter width and tall enough.  These fit the bill quite nicely.  I tried a set of hipshot GOLs, but the backside that you screw into the back of the headstock was slightly shorter, which would have required me to drill new holes.  I gave these a shot and they fit exactly.  It should be known that the pegs on these aren't quite as tall as the stock ones, so it creates a slightly more acute angle (thank you geometry classes!) from the nut to the tuner pegs.  This may slightly increase the tension of the strings, but it's nothing major.  If you have a Yamaha Revstar, these are the locking tuners for you!

title: Big sound in a small package!
comment:
I'll start by saying, I've only had about 4 days with this amp, so this is just my first impressions. I may update this review after I have ran this beast through it's paces. I have tested most of it's features so I felt I could give a fairly through review.

First off, the look and feel of this amp is really nice. It seems very well put together. All knobs and buttons seemed to be installed tight and snug, so no wiggle when a button was pushed or turned. The exterior was flawless. No blemishes or unglued corners. The screen covering the 10" speaker was centered and stretched very tight for a nice finish. All in all, this amp seem very well put together on the outside.

Now for the components on the inside. After a through inspection everything looked perfect. I did notice when I picked up the amp to move it there was a rattling sound, so after a closer look I noticed the the spring reverb housing was just a little loose. Nothing to warrant any concern, but it had a little wiggle when I put pressure on it. A quick turn of the 4 screws and it was a non-issue.

Now the fun part. I plugged this thing in, and man was I surprised at what came out of such a small amp. Keep in mind this thing is only about 18" by 18" by about 10" deep, but this thing had some crunch! The clean channel was crisp and the EQ, which is just a simple low, mid, high, seemed to work great. With the push of a button you switch over to the dirty channels that have four options from a simple distortion all the way to what VHT calls "redline", which is what is close to what I would call a fuzz. All dirty channels are adjustable with a gain and level knob that can really dial in each distortion channel along with some more tweaking from the EQ to really make the sound your own.

Now for the cons: I was a little disappointed with the reverb on this amp. It's claim to fame is that it has a real spring reverb and not a digital one. When on the clean channel it was more noticeable when turned from 0 all the way to max, but nothing like the reverb I can get out of my Fender acoustasonic, but the clean channel is not where this amp shines. When on the dirty channel reverb was almost unnoticeable, but not to worry, with the gain and level adjustments, plus the EQ, you can really jam.

There was one more complaint I had, but this is very minor, and I'm not going to ding VHT for this but when you switch over to the dirty channels it illuminates an LED light that burns with the brightness of a thousand suns, so when you look down on the amp to select the different distortion options you can't see which one you are on. This is really not too big of a deal since there are only 4 options and once you memorize them, there is no need to stare into the abyss anymore to see what channel you're on.

Final thoughts: All in all this amp is amazing. This amp has great sound at low and high volume. No crackling from the speaker when cranked, and still great crunch from the distortion and low volume. This is a great combo amp for fun at the house or with friends. It definitely could hold its own beside bigger amps, but this is not something you would use as a stand along amp at a gig. I have a fairly large living room and it will blow your socks off in that kind of setting. I have not had enough time to really explore the far reaches of what this amp can do, but maybe I will update this review later. I highly recommend this to anyone for a great practice amp, and for the price it could be perfect for a beginner amp. With it's light weight(only about 16 pounds) it can easily travel with you anywhere. Hope this review helps, and keep rockin'!!

title: Got one year of good service out of it...
comment:
The SP4-7 is just 24lbs, in a truly compact design. Nevertheless, it offers 76 semi-weighted, full sized keys that feel very responsive. 128 available sounds, with another 128 user banks, are carefully chosen and programmed to power through a bar, club date or wedding gig. It's simple and easy to use, with split or layer on the fly. Instant access to the pre-programmed, customized effects for each sound sample.

What's not so great? Compared to competing keyboards like the Korg Krome or Yamaha MOX, there just aren't nearly as many sound choices. Though the sounds are "borrowed" from Kurzweil's flagship PC3 series, they're compressed, dithered versions so the smaller processor and system software of the SP4 can handle them. This stripping down shows mostly in acoustic sounds like piano and guitar; they seem dated and less nuanced compared to what's offered by major manufacturers these days. Yes, there are instant controls for effects and timbre, but for many sounds they are about as basic as they come. Two or three sliders that might control individual volume for split and layer would also have been welcome.

The worst two problems with this keyboard are: Obsolescence and Reliability. Kurzweil has already begun selling its PC3LE series, a lighter, less involved version of the PC3 line, effectively overshadowing the audio appeal of this model.
A little more than a year after I bought the Kurzweil, out of nowhere, my SP4-7 began having serious electrical problems. Lights and display were on, but the sound would stop and start, right on stage, accompanied by sizzling and buzzing noises. Took it twice to a repair shop, they're still not sure what's wrong with it--might be a microscopic short in the circuit board or a crack in the keyboard bed somewhere. I traded the SP4-7 in two weeks ago for a Roland Jupiter 50.

The SP-4 gets an A for ease of use, overall sound, great feeling keyboard bed and portability, a C- for lack of more modern design and obvious shortcuts, and D for reliability.

The D’Addarios, too, will tend to break near the ball, but I discovered that using a very tiny amount of modeling plastic, heated until it’s pliable, then fixed around the winding and allowed to harden, solved this issue completely. title: Affordable way to see if a kid enjoys piano
comment:
I’m giving this 4/5 stars for what it is, a beginner keyboard that’s afordable.

These remind me of the cheap keyboards you used to find in electronics stores and malls. The sound is passable, the sample quality and selection is adequate, and the tinny cheap speakers make it sound thin and treblely. Which is what you’re paying for at this price. The value comes from having a seat and headphones included.

I’m a former professional musician and I realize it’s unfair to compare this with professional quality gear, but some people might like to know. Don’t think you’re getting anything that will be sufficient for a live gig.

But hey, it’s for a kid who’s starting out. Parents know that most of the time a kid gives up on a hobby so don’t waste a lot of money up front. You have to test the waters first.

Heck, my parents made me use sticks and a $20 drum pad for a year before I got a full drum set. Which gave me time to focus on rudaments, timing and sight reading sheet music. By the time I reached high school I was playing professionally at large county fairs, doing studio work for local musicians and performing around the state.. Point is, you do NOT need top of the line gear to start out, no matter how much a kid might insist.

If they enjoy it and have talent, then eventually you can upgrade. This is a good place to start.

One annoying thing for me is the power light. For some reason it’s backwards, they have RED for the one position and BLUE for the off position. No other electronic device does this, very strange and we often leave the keyboard on because it looks like it’s off.

I don’t like the touch controls, but that’s a personal preference. Would prefer real buttons for kids.

The finish is just fine, the fretboard has no sharp fret ends, neck pocket is tight.  I really have no complaints whatsoever about this guitar, especially at the price they're selling em for... no, it's not a 'custom shop' equivalent, but it suits me just fine. I give it a solid 4 stars.

It is a lot better than the cheap ones at the guitar store that sound like a toy. There are ones out there that resonate better but then you are paying at least double without getting the extras.  I would recommend getting this if you are a beginner or just looking for something to mess around with. Normally I don't buy a guitar, or ukulele,unless I can try it out first but I am happy I purchased this one.

It’s lightweight, yet sturdy.  On the left rack goes a Peavey PY6BT mixer with an Apple MacBook Pro on the right. It balances out perfectly and the shelves are adjustable so you can customize it to fit your individual needs. The lightning fast delivery and follow up from Deal Chaser was a nice bonus. I would highly recommend this product!

I was disappointed when I discovered that the outlets are on the Pyle power supply are not switched (outlets are On even if the device is off.) I will probably open the unit up and install a relay to add this functionality.

On the other note I noticed that the db display is a little behind the actual audio, Not a big deal i guess, but it doesn't look as good. Other than these two things I am happy with my purchase.

title: Not all slots are curved

The nut included is the fender curved style with the tab for flat bottom nut slots. While this will most likely work for my purposes, I would have preferred a flat bottom as pictured since I want the nut to make as much contact with the instrument as possible. But if this doesn't work for my intended use, it will eventually find a home in another guitar.

title: Beautiful, and colorful sound

Setting the tone to the out of the box blackface setting and pushing the volume to 5+ and driving an Ibanez TS-9 this speaker is the final piece for that sought after colorful sound I was looking for. It breaks up at the right time, and gives that warm fuzzy feeling. Perfectly timed sustain can raise the hair on the back of your neck. I play both a Les Paul studio and a Strat loaded with Texas Specials. I focus mainly on classic rock and blues. I have finally found the tone I was looking for.

title: Good quality, *GREAT* value XLR patch cable !
comment:
I've been using GLS connectors for a little while now, when I'm making my own cables for home studio, pedal board,etc type projects. Not being in the mood to solder a bunch of XLR ends, i thought I'd cast my eye about for some 6 ft XLR patch cables. The GLS seemed to be right in the sweet spot for me price-wise,and FREE ONE DAY SHIPPING. (Say what?)

I had to check it out!

These are much better quality than you'd expect for a under $15. cable. The ends are nice and solid, the solder connections were good and the wire in the conductors is nice and meaty. The black jacket on the cable seems supple and sturdy as well.

I'm using these for connecting home studio/audio equipment, so it's not going to be in a 'rough working environment', but I would gig with these in a heartbeat!

I've certainly spent more money on crappier cables too, and lucky me-even the one day shipping was on time.
-I would be very satisfied with the cable even with longer shipping. WAY better quality that the price would dictate.

GLS are awesome, and one of my first 'Go to's" when I'm looking for cable ends, nice to know the quality extends to their cable line as well!

title: The best boom arm I've had (out of three!)
comment:
I've tried three different boom arms on my desk and this was the best overall product I've used.

Don't get those puny boom arms that have the springs on the outside, they are generally too short of reach for a desk with some depth. I'm talking about the ones that are the cookie cutter ones that I've seen from many different companies which use the same design and spring system.

This one is the REAL DEAL. It handles a proper studio microphone mount instead of coming with and having to use an adapter. I was surprised that I didn't see that it HIDES THE CORD within the arm too. I didn't understand it when someone said it could do this, but the arm has a thin but sturdy plastic piece that covers the top of the whole arm on both parts. The cord just lines up perfectly within the groove and you cover it up.

The arm is plenty of length AND it can come down at an angle that won't bump my arms when I'm using the computer at the same time. This design was stellar, haven't been this excited about a product in a while and I'm so happy I found this one!

title: If you use the 1/4" source inputs, XLR outputs don't work...
comment:
Just got this device tonight and really like how it makes my speakers sound. There's definitely a difference in using the Sonic maximizer versus not and I definitely prefer it.

One thing that kind of annoyed me though is that while it offers inputs and outputs for every connection with 1/4" and XLR options, if you connect an 1/4" source, the XLR outputs don't work. I think this has to do with balanced / unbalanced, but the point is I'm a newb and didn't realize it and it's a bit irritating. My amplifier will take XLR, RCA, or 1/4" and outputs to Speakon of line level, so I getting a 3.5mm breakout to 1/4" would work fine with XLR outs to the sub and speaker, but I was wrong.

Anyway, if any other newbs are thinking of buying cables to go with thiis and need a breakout cable, just make sure whatever you choose, it matches how you want to output from the thing.

title: Wonderfully overbuilt product
comment: Expecting a music stand type item I was enormously surprised to find it was nothing of the kind.  The stand portion is equivalent to the heavy duty speaker stands I use for large 12" powered speakers.  It included stopper holes and a steel pin to take the weight off the tightening knobs just like the more expensive speaker stands.  The plate to hold the computer or in my case my smaller multi channel sound mixer is very heavy duty,  probably the heaviest part of the assembly.  Its made of solid steel and yes heavy but I will have no fear of mounting expensive equipment on it.  I suspect the entire assembly is strong enough to actually hold two concrete blocks (not that I have tested it in this way you understand).  Since I plan on replacing the old table I use to lug around (of which I only needed 1/3 of the end with this stand for my mixer It will be easier to locate near my cameras.  Since it will allow me to place my sound mixer right next to my several cameras it will enable me to perform better when recording multi camera recording like our recent community mayoral debate.  I like to also control the front-of-house audio in these productions thereby enabling perfect audio for my recordings later to be broadcast on television and this stand will be perfect for this use. Only negative which in usage is actually a positive is the weight of carrying this stand from the truck to the place of use. But then again the strength of this stand in its intended use will more than makes up for the additional weight.  BTW this is probably overkill if you are only putting some light sheet music on it. But if you have heavy things to mount on it, this is your product.

title: Rather Odd
comment:
(I originally wrote this review about the 14", but I've played both sizes and the same comments apply.)

One doesn't see a lot of flat cymbals, and the ones that do appear tend to be rides. Until I bought this piece on a whim I had never even heard of a flat hi-hat top. And after working with it for a while, I am beginning to have a sense of why they're so rare.

First the general stuff: Like all of Soultone's products, this cymbal is well-manufactured of quality alloy, with a nice "vintage" luster to it. I paired it with a variety of bottoms (K Dark Fusion, Bosphorus Gold, Stagg Black Metal), and found that the sound was largely similar with all of them, except for variations in volume. The chick on this cymbal is VERY soft--jazz players, except in the most intimate acoustic settings, are likely to be dissatisfied with its projection. Stick hits are woody, fuzzy, and diffuse--not really the "old As" sound I had expected, but rather a low, swampy "chug". This is a hat I could see using for particular effects in the studio, but not as a "daily driver."

The unique problem I see with this top is that the lack of a bell makes it VERY flexible under foot pressure, and this can result in a weird twanging/boinging/buzzing sound when it's hit in a closed position, or on an upstroke. I had to learn how to close it without bending the center, and using a very fluffy felt on the clutch to boot. In short, while this is an interesting cymbal, it's very niche-y, and probably more trouble than it's worth.

title: A $60 drum machine with surprising depth and audio quality in a tiny (and very fragile) package
comment:
I remember the first time I saw a video the Pocket Operators from Teenage Engineering - I want to say it was from some outdoor music festival in Scandanavia in late 2014 (in front of the huge release at NAMM 2015). I was baffled and intrigued by this little device that looked like a fragile computer chip, but was kicking out some pretty serious electronic music sounds through a 3.5mm audio jack. There are now 6 models of the Pocket Operators, and I remained skeptical for about a full year-and-a-half before trying any of them. Having had the chance to take home the PO-12 Rhythm and actually learn how to program the darn thing, I can say that this is truly a 5-star product IF you enter with reasonable expectations about its extremely affordable price point ($59.99) and durability.

BUILD QUALITY/EASE OF USE - The first thing anyone notices about the Pocket Operator is the rudimentary, "bare bones" design aesthetic. This thing is literally built on a computer chip (complete with exposed PCB solder joints on the back side), a small black-and-white LCD screen reminiscent of a 90's era handheld video game, and a series of 23 buttons and 2 rotating knobs affixed directly to the device and anchored with a small metal cover. The device runs on 2 AAA batteries (not included) and they are held in place by a wire cage and can be somewhat difficult to install and remove. This thing is undeniable FRAGILE, but at least in my impressions of it, everything seems to function pretty well. The buttons have some snap to them, so each press is reliably acknowledged. There is a kick stand on the back so you can use it on about a 33-degree angle on a table top, or hold it like a calculator.

Programming the device has a bit of an early learning curve, but was actually surprisingly intuitive. Even better, most of the core functions (such as how to adjust the volume or delete entire patterns) are common across several/all of the Pocket Operator models. The PO-12 is primarily a 4x4, 16-step sequencer with limited polyphony. I think you can play maybe two instruments on the same step simultaneously, but not much more than that, and it's probably better limited to one instrument per step. The two green knobs usually modify the pitch and waveform of the individual instruments, which include a bass drum, snares, hats toms, and even a bass tone. Despite the limited interface, the PO-12 allows all of the following:

- Live play of instruments (press-and-play)
- 16-step sequencing
- "Song mode" with pattern chaining
- Step multiplier (inserting a re-trigger on a given step and switching between 2x, 4x, 8x, and 16x multiplying)
- Universal live effects (ranging from bitcrush to filters to stutter/repeat)
- Programmable automation with the pitch and waveform knobs (this also allows you, for example, to change the pitch of a bass note through a sequence)

SOUND QUALITY - I've read various criticisms of the Pocket Operator series - usually targeting their fragile nature or practicality as a musical "instrument" - but few have criticized the sound quality of this $60 device, and for good reason. The bass drum is powerful and articulate, and there's a great variety of percussive sounds. The PO-12 have some core similarities to the sounds of an 808, but with more of a lo-fi or "glitch" emphasis. Also, I was pleasantly surprised how tweakable the sounds were. I don't think this would have been a very useful device if it were just a static sample player with 16 sounds, but you can actually vary the pitch, aggressiveness, and other qualities of each instrument quite a bit (and automate those changes through a sequence). Everything sounds great into a decent pair of headphones or set of powered monitors, and you can chain multiple Pocket Operators together using a standard 3.5mm audio cable. This not only merges the audio from each device, but you can tempo-sync each device and even marry them to similar technologies, such as Korg's excellent Volca series or a Korg SyncKontrol output.

CONCERNS - I think the primary concern about this device has to be its longevity. Check the bottom of my review to see if there's a little "UPDATE" added with a date next to it. If one of my PO's breaks, I'll be sure to update my review about it and see how it changes my overall impressions of the device. But the reality is that this is a $60 device, offering functionality that you really cannot find until you get up to the level of a Korg Volca Beats ($160), and arguably even higher. I think it's fair to assume a reasonable risk of failure at this price point if I get some enjoyment out of it.

There are some other limitations to the device. Obviously, the PO-12 does not have MIDI, so you have to think of it more as a sketch device than a full-featured drum machine you would incorporate into a home studio. Also, the naming of the series is deceptive because I absolutely would not recommend throwing this in your pocket (it would break, or constantly be powered on from button presses). That's frustrating because the size of this device really would make it fun for bus rides, waiting rooms, etc., except that I'm afraid to take it out of the house. Finally, the device doesn't really "shut off" - it goes into a sleep mode after a few minutes but still runs the (mostly useless) LCD screen with the clock. Teenage Engineering claims (I think) that it has about a month of standby battery life, but I have a suspicion I'll be doing a lot of battery changing to keep it running (and my use a good set of rechargeables). They make a silicone case for each model, but they retail at $40, which significantly increases the cost of the device without really providing a great deal of additional protection.

FINAL THOUGHTS - Even though I barely know how to use thing (and am no ace at programming drum beats to begin with), I cannot over-emphasize just how FUN the PO-12 Rhythm as a creative experience. I own several of the Korg Volca devices (though not the Volca Beats), and the PO-12 is just a very different experience from that. Whereas the Volca aims to provide a true analog instrument in miniature form, the PO-12 is more like an entertaining gadget that happens to have surprising depth and audio quality. Still, I reject the idea that it's "only a toy", because while you can mess around with it and create things at random, it absolutely has the depth and feature set to translate specific musical ideas in your head and create them accurately and efficiently. It may not still be working 5 years from now, and there are some frustrating limitations with some of the other models in the series (particularly the PO-14 and PO-16), but the PO-12 Rhythm, in particular, is an amazing value and a fun, unique experience to play.

title: Bridge P/U worked for one day
comment:
I returned this and asked for a replacement. I'll get the replacement in a couple days. Of course this does not pay for the $15 set of strings I wasted. I have locking tuners and you can't take the strings off and reinstall them. I did check each P/U with my meter prior to installation. They sounded good for the one day that the Bridge P/U worked.
Problems: 1. One button would not go all the way down on the tone pot. (I could have lived with this)
2. The Bridge P/U worked for one day then went dead. Checked all the connections with my meter and
found no reading coming out of the P/U.
These look like good items. They come in a fancy, expensive box. The vender should pay more attention to the quality of the parts rather than the fancy packaging. I hope the replacements work better and last longer than one day. If not I will return them again and buy name brand parts. To bad the vendor can't reimburse me for my strings. That alone stops me from recommending these to anyone.

UPDATE: I received the new parts and everything is working as it should. The seller has contacted me and offered to replace the strings I lost. I accepted the offer. This shows the seller cares about their customers satisfaction. Not many sellers would do this. I realize any part can go bad and I was unlucky enough to get that one in a thousand. However, the seller was willing to go out their way to make me happy. I would buy from them again. The replacement pickups sound great.

title: They really do grip nicely!
comment:
I bought the .73mm and the .60mm. I thought they gripped better than the Snarling Dogs Brain Picks I have used for so long and that's saying something. These Max-Grip picks have a gripping surface that reminds me of those diamond plate aluminum tool boxes you see on truck beds. Anyway, they grip great and I m no longer distracted with having to think about holding on to my pick. It feels natural. I have very dry hands and this is the best pick I've found yet. Now I have nothing else to blame my poor playing on! lol! But ,you know, as they say, "Practice makes perfect".

After using the picks for awhile I can honestly say that they have improved my playing. I say that because I'm not doing anything differently as far as my playing except that I'm not having to think about holding onto my pick. I find these picks to feel natural, almost like an extension of my picking hand. Wow! Find another pick you can say that about!

title: Smooth volume and tone control
comment: Replaced pots (along with switch) on a '73-'74 Telecaster. The old ones were long gone. My soldering skills are pitiful but I managed to get everything (pots+switch) replaced going connection by connection (plus a homemade wiring diagram and plenty of photos). You can find wiring diagrams on-line no problem. The pot shaft lengths were identical to the original. They come with lock washer (goes underneath plate) and chrome washer (above plate) and nut. If you have an old beater like mine, you may want to swap out the capacitors too while you are in there. Volume and tone are good to go now.

title: Almost perfect…one major flaw.
comment:
Plan on using this for a church podium. The biggest fault I could find was that it doesn’t exactly remember the last color temperature used. I like mine somewhere in the middle, not too warm and not too cold. I like to dial in the color just right. However, turning the device off and on can be a hit or miss. The color will be a tad too warm (yellowish), or a tad too cold (blue-ish) from the last setting used. So you will have to once again dial in the temperature. This is only an issue if you like the color somewhere in the middle. If I have the lamp color extremely warm or cold, it will have no problem restarting in the same color temperature. It’s just an inconvenience to have to repeat the process over again. This can be a major issue for musicians where you might have to conserve battery. You may not have time to fidget around and dial in the temperature again.

So if you like your color temperature just right, don’t turn off the lamp. I really wish it had a physical switch to lock in the led temperature. It would be perfect.

For reference, I try to dial in the leds to a 50/50 mix around 4000k. It’s just a pain that it can’t remember that middle setting :’(

title: Easy to use. Super long battery life! Good dynamic response.
comment:
This is an honest review after about 7 days of daily use.

I must say that I was skeptical about this product at first, but that quickly changed.

The product arrived promptly and very well packaged (see images).

Once I opened it, and fully charged (charging cable included) the wireless transmitter/receiver, the set up was very easy. The product states that one receiver can handle up to 4 transmitters. This is great! I can only review it using one though. ;)

To test the range, I played all over my house. After about 3-4 walls in between, the signal started to break down. In a club gig, I will not have any walls in between, so I am not worried.

I played through it during practice for a few days trying to get the receiver/transmitter to signal low battery but did not manage to kill them before I just decided to plug them in and charge them again (they charges super fast). I love this super long battery life!

Now on to the good stuff: the musicality! I felt the system had a good dynamic response. It relayed me rolling off the volume very well and when in direct line of sight, I did not have any issues with signal degradation.

Final verdict:
GET IT!!! It's a bargain and works great! To be honest, I am considering buying a second one to use from my board to the amp.
