title: Every Reason, Cubase, or Bitwig user should have one of these
comment:
I have several DAW controllers and of the few I don't own, I've at least had a chance to test out pretty thoroughly. Whether or not I would recommend this controller is pretty simple. If you use Propellerheads Reason, Cubase, or Bitwig this device is an absolute no-brainer.

The integration is the best and easiest out there. Nektar is one of my favorite companies in the industry right now and having owned several LX-series Midi Keyboards and a GX series as well, I've come to the conclusion that there products are unbeatable at the price range they fall within. This DAW controller is no different. While the unit itself is made of a hard plastic, the knobs, faders, and buttons are all made of a solid rubber that feels stable without lots of wiggle or bounce. The display makes it easy to control instruments or mixer options without having to look at your computer screen.

Since I own the 3 pre-mentioned DAW's I've had a chance to really put it through its paces and I can tell you the integration is deep. Controlling Reason in all its rack-based complexity is an absolute joy and both Bitwig and Cubase have similar integration. Nektar released an update not too long ago that provides support for many more VST plugins for both the P1 and the P4 controller. I would agree with the review earlier that pointed out the lack of a flat base allowing it to be mounted more easily as it looks great angled up at your face but this is easily remedied with some creativity. Overall, I'd say that if you own one of these DAW's, the Panorama P1 is a steal at its current asking price.

title: What It Does, It Does Well
comment:
I bought one of these (the red one) about 10 or 12 years ago. It's seen extensive use, including having multiple sticks dropped into it at once from the top, which is bound to stress the seams at the bottom--and it did; recently the holder developed a couple of holes at the seam and started leaking sticks. I bought a new one (this black one) for my show kit, and sewed up the old one for one of my practice kits. Another practice kit has an On-Stage brand stick holder, which is permanently tilted. Angle is a matter of personal preference, and I prefer Gibraltar's vertical approach, but admittedly it should be a small matter to make the angle of these adjustable, manufacturers.

Another review mentioned difficulty getting sticks out due to their movement while playing. This sounds to me like a problem with hi-hat adjustment. None of my hi-hats wobble enough to move the sticks. Adjust the feet so they're all the way down. Heck, even my open drink sits in a holder attached to the hi-hat stand. No spills.

If the unthinkable (and inevitable) occurs and you lose a stick in mid-song, well, that's why you have your spares in one of these. I keep three in the holder. I angle two of them away from me and one toward me, and the one is immediately available should I lose one from my hand. I don't even think about trying to catch it on its way down, I just quick-draw that spare sucker from the holster--uh, holder--and keep on going without missing a stroke.

What would I do to improve it? Well, the adjustable tilt I already mentioned, and maybe slightly stronger material. Then again, I can't complain about 10-12 years of dependable service.

title: These are great pegs - attractive and real ebony. Worth much more than they cost!
comment: We purchased a low cost 4/4 Cello.  My wife and I are watching YouTube, and teaching ourselves. There is no point in paying for expensive lessons initially, knowing that many hours will be "wasted" on elementary concepts. When the basics are mastered, we will take lessons locally.  Although we keep our Cello on a Cello stand, "somehow" it fell and the D string tuning peg was broken at the pegbox.  Although I have owned many instruments and know how to treat them, I was surprised that the broken peg wood was so soft. The broken peg was soft wood painted black, but I knew what I was buying for $400.  The four replacement pegs arrived the next day after ordering, and they are beautiful.  The new peg fit almost exactly, and since I don't have a shaver or reamer, I put some abrasive toothpaste on the peg, and rotated the peg in the pegbox until it fit perfectly. When cutting the peg to the correct length, I had never worked with ebony, and the hardness of the wood was a surprise.  Finally, I drilled a 0.051" hole, in the middle of the exposed peg in the pegboard. Finished!

It uses 3 AAs, which is non-standard for a gig light (AAAs are the standard) so keep extras with you, and you should be fine. I've also used the provided cord, which, if you have a near enough plug, go ahead and do.

Pros: BRIGHT, neck puts the light where you need it. 2 brightness settings (all you'll need) ability to plug it in, wide surface area coverage

Cons: a bit larger than the "Mighty Bright" clones out there, AAs instead of AAAs (but the power draw probably requires it)

Great light!

title: Weird "Clipping" for High End
comment:
I bought 4 different JOYO pedals (EQ-Used it for 3 weeks, Tauren-used for a Day, D-Sed II used for 3 days, and Vision-used for 3 days). When I got them, I liked them for the most part (with maybe the exception of the Vision). However, after using them to lead into my Behringer Audio Interface, I kept getting a weird high-end clipping noise that was noticeable when playing any string higher than the A. At first, I thought it was my TRS cables, so I bought new ones. That didn't solve it. I thought maybe it was my pick up height, nope. Guitar? Tried 4 different ones, all reproduced the issue. I plugged my guitar directly into my AI and it went away. So I tested each of my pedals one at a time. After some trial and error, I learned that no matter which JOYO pedal I introduced into the chain (even the EQ), the high-end "clipping" sound was reproduced (yes, if I lowered the high end to -12db it would go away, but that defeated the point seeing I was looking to use the EQ to boost the high end).

I was using a 9V supply with 8 independent rails, and none of my other 4 pedals reproduced the issue and it went away when I removed all of the JOYO pedals. Thus, I had to conclude that JOYO uses something that is either incompatible with my AI, or there is a common circuit (power?) that can't handle treble well. So I sent them all back. I cannot say how they sound or perform on an amp. I do have a nice '72 Twin Reverb and never tried since I wanted to use them for recording too. JOYO might be great for certain applications, but for my home studio, they did not perform well.

title: Great system, indeed!
comment:
After owning it for a week, I'm convinced that I made a right choice with this wireless system. I'm not a gigging musician and I didn't not want to spend a lot of money to be able to play/practice at home without dealing with a guitar cable. So here are my thoughts and observations:

*The price: the price of $40 is very reasonable which translates into the great value for the money.
*The sound quality: I didn't notice any signal degradation at all, 100% transparent and true.
* The latency: when you're in the same roof with your equipment, there is no latency that I could hear. However when I stepped away the latency seemed to increase proportionately to the distance, although there was not a direct line of sight.
* The battery life: I played for about 1 hour every day during the week of the ownership and didn't have to charge it. The charging is easy, plug the y-cable into both units, they light yellow and go off once charged.
* The operation: it's nearly plug-n-play, just have to pair the transmitter and the receiver.
*The build quality: that's where I'm not so sure. It seems to be constructed well enough for a bedroom player to use or maybe for an occasional rehearsal, but it feels too fragile to take any beating or abuse that most of the music gear must withstand if you play out. Obviously the manufacturer didn't target pro-players hence the price.
* The compatibility: I own several guitars with passive, active EMG and piezo pickups and the system works in with all of them flawlessly.
* The longevity: we shall see. I have not owned long enough to judge.

Like I said above, it's only been a week since I got it and I'm very happy with the system. For what it is, the system does the job, it's very light, very compact, doesn't degrade or color the sound. I wish it was more robust, but you get what you pay for, or so they say.

Update:
I have added a wireless transmitter holder that attaches to a strap to keep the transmitter from spinning in the guitar jack, which was a bit annoying. title: I love this guitar!
comment:
I don't know why, or if it will stay this way, but if you search Amazon for "Yamamama", this same guitar is sold by the same company, for the same price, but comes with a very nice hard case. The only downside is the shipping takes longer. That's what I did. I'm putting my review here, because if you search for this guitar model, the other one doesn't come up for me that often.

I have an entry level Pacifica, so I know that I like them. I decided to upgrade to this one. This guitar looks amazing in person. It also plays easy - the action is set up pretty good, but not perfect. I am having it set up by my local guitar shop anyway, because the A string buzzed a little. It's my understanding that most guitars should be set up professionally.

What sold me on it was looking up videos to see how this guitar sounds. The first video was incredible! I thought that he must have a super awesome amp, and he did. But, I saw lots of videos by different people and different amps and they all sounded really good. The pickups sound to me like they have a high output and sound great clean or distorted. The volume can be turned down some and the sound is more like other guitars. There is a wide range of sounds that this guitar can make.

The other hardware on the guitar are nice as well and quite an improvement over the entry level Pacificas. The locking tuners feel expensive when tuning the guitar - very solid and easy to adjust. Yamaha outdid themselves and I am very happy with it.

The only other picky thing I noticed is that the knobs don't have a mark to easily see what the volume or tone is set at. It would probably ruin the aesthetics of it if they marked the knob though.

title: Wonderful Machine!!
comment:
If you have never played with a synth before, and want to get a quick, affordable introduction to the world of electronic musical instruments: you will thank yourself for buying one of these!
You will be pleasantly surprised by the available features:
-Pressure-sensitive VCF and LFO buttons for extra control on top of the dedicated Filter and Osc.
-Option of Saw or Square LFO wave
-Battery Powered only (4 AA)
-Cutoff and Resonance
-and more.

I suggest the Mark 2 over the prior versions if you want the most features.

This is a GREAT gift for someone who knows something about synthesizers but doesn't have one yet.

I would suggest the purchase of a mono patch cable and a mono to stereo adapter plug if you want to listen with stereo headphones or hook up to an amp/speaker (Radio Shack has any combination you might need). The built-in speaker is ok but you can really get quality sound if you hook up to external speakers.

Thanks!
~Walt

title: Great EQ, perfect for tone sculpting or boosting volume
comment:
[[VIDEOID:ffb80c8f856224aef97068c826be1080]] Every serious guitarist needs an EQ pedal; not only can you cut or boost certain frequencies, you can also raise or lower the volume overall with them. Yes, your amp has an EQ section, but it's only at the end of your signal chain. With an EQ, you can really open up many tonal possibilities.

I thought I'd give this one a try, as I like the look of the "Seeker" series, and this one is a 10-band as opposed to your standard 5-7 band. A 10-band allows you to use it for bass and keyboard in addition to guitar since it has a wider frequency range, improving usefulness and versatility.

Upon unboxing, I noticed how this series comes in really nice, professional packaging--always a good sign for product quality. I get nervous when pedals come in paper-thin unlabeled boxes, but that wasn't the case here. The EQ feels good in hand, with sliders that are easy to move but have a good amount of resistance so that they aren't accidentally bumped. The sliders all feel quality and not sloppy.

The enclosure feels very solid and made of good components that seem durable. I am happy to report that the switch does not make any noise whatsoever. This pedal is quiet, and does not introduce any noise. If anybody thinks it does, it is just because they don't understand how an EQ pedal works. An EQ pedal will boost whatever frequency you slide up on the sliders, so if you have a noisy signal it will make your already noisy signal noisier.

One trick I like to use an EQ pedal for is simply to use as a boost pedal. With this model you can cut or add 15 decibels of volume. You can put this at the end of your signal chain and stomp on it for a nice volume boost for solos.

Another cool EQ trick is to put it before a drive pedal to drive your overdrive or distortion pedal harder. You can also use an EQ pedal to drive an amp harder to get break up earlier.

Another thing I use them for is to make humbuckers more like single coils by boosting the highs, and also to make single coils sound a little beefier by bumping the bass and mid-range. A good EQ is also a cure for a muddy sounding amp. Put it at the end of your signal chain and boost the high frequencies and cut a little bit of low.

An EQ pedal is an absolute must for any serious guitarist, and this one is a stellar choice. It is high quality, very affordable, and extremely versatile. Plus it looks great too! I give this one a thumbs up.

First issue was the dead G string, I think the winding at the ball end was poorly finished and so it was completely dead, no sustain or brightness at all. The rest of the wound strings, while nice, buzzed terribly just about halfway up the neck. I thought it could be the gauge, too thin maybe, but I've used 11s before without this issue. Then I realized these have a nylon core, and I think that may be the culprit? They're too expensive for me to want to roll the dice on a heavier gauge, especially since the 12s seem to be all roundwounds and not partially flat, which was the reason I wanted these to begin with. Oh well! if they work for you, I'm jealous!

title: A Technological Triumph
comment: So far, I've actually been able to make sound, mix, and add effects in a band-practice situation.  In other words, I've gotten it to work in a controlled environment.  This mixer has a staggering array of capabilities.  I'm not sure when I'll use them all.  If you are intimidated by the complexity of the mixer....don't be.  I didn't have any problem getting it to do the stuff that a standard analog mixer does.  And, don't let the fat channel scare you.  Presonus has figured out an appropriate number of knobs and displays that makes the setup intuitive (more or less).  No criticism so far....sounds great.  I just received an email from Presonus that new software is available so multiple mixers can be cascaded!  Presonus has really staked out the high ground in terms of flexibility versus ease of use.  Presonus has figured out things that this mixer can do that you didn't even know you wanted.  For example, two channels can be linked such that the dynamics on one control the gate of another.

title: The Best Value in Electric Guitar Design
comment: I'm a long time player (since 1970).  I've played expensive giutars that fell flat on their face and inexpensive guitars that blew me away (1972 Electra Workingman!!!).  I am not a buyer of "names" rather I buy on what feels right, plays well and sounds great!  First off, out of the box, it was very playable and the neck and frets were dressed very close to American standard.  I thought I would scotch bright the neck but seriously think with about 2 hours of playing I won't need to.  I also noticed a good deal of residual wax on the bridge pick up.  These were pre potted so again, something I won't have to do.  It has a graphtech nut which would be an upgrade I would do.  The finish...I wanted to do a relic job but wow... I'm not going to mess with butterscotch blonde this pretty.  So...the pick guard.. standard black appropriate for color but I have to change SOMETHING on this guitar, thus I went tortoise shell.  I find nothing wrong with the Chinese saddles but in order to match the nut I'm swapping them for graphtech string savers.  A new set of 10s a tweet of the neck wait a week another tweek of the neck and I'm done.  So on to the tone.  I've had a couple different standard teles, g&l asat deluxe and a few FINE EXAMPLES of old G&L SC2 (hard tail versions) to compare.  Blind taste test tells me that quite literally for pennies on the dollar, I've got 95% of the best American standard.  I don't have money to waste but this experiment paid off.  If I tinted the neck with gun oil, soaked the neck decals off, replaced with a stock fender decal ( or leave it blank), change the neckplate on back and hope they don't notice it's top loaded, only a rare few guitar guys would be able to determine it's a squire Affinity.  The tone is amazing, it's bright and womanly vocal on the neck and middle position.  The bridge is deep, rich and can cut through the mix.  And remember I didn't change anything critical...just cosmetic ( for my taste).  The tuners will take time to tell...they are fine now so I have no worries about the future.  The fact that While Gibson is bankrupt, putting out substandard and inconsistent products FENDER can put this out for this price is amazing.  I'm sure many many guitar guys my age are going to snob this, but those of us who are impressed by quality over a name will love this guitar.  Any of my students wanting to upgrade to electric already know that I will set them up with one just like mine and their parents will be happy with the cost savings.  Highly recommend this for vintage players like me and young players alike!

title: Received "Generic" Version from Amazon - Didn't Work
comment:
I use an iMac OS X. I received a "generic" version of the USB MIDI Cable. It came in the clear plastic packaging with red white and black labeling inside. It didn't have the funny G-clef symbol on the interface as pictured on Amazon's product page. Beware of MODEL NO:GCB1, ASIN NO: B0017H4EBG. It didn't work, it didn't work, it didn't work!

Instead, get the "USB MIDI Music Cable Converter PC to Music Keyboard Supports Window Vista XP Mac OS", sold by HDE (which also sells via Amazon.com). It costs about the same, and it's worth it to purchase a product that performs as expected.

title: At last... this is the OverDrive pedal I've been searching for!
comment:
First off, I am rarely motivated to write such a lengthy review. My apologies if it is too wordy, but this pedal warrants it!

I like the pedals I currently use, but I have been on a mission to find "the right Overdrive pedal" for several months now. The Boss OD-1X was one of many pedals I tested. Upon receiving the pedal, I set my Marshall half-stack on a clean channel and dialed in just a touch of gain. I then plugged in my Gibson Les Paul and almost immediately knew it was the tone I was searching for. It produces very smooth/rich/articulate sounds and power chords are... well... powerful! Lower (bassy) power chords simply sound amazing. Each of the pedal's controls are very responsive and they change the overall sound nicely. The OD-1X is very responsive to the guitars volume setting and makes it easy to dial back the level of overdrive. The pedal produces very little noise and transitions well when engaging/disengaging. Apparently Boss's MDP technology really works!

As we all know, guitar tone is subjective and dependent on many factors- personal preference, the guitar/amp used, the interaction with other pedals, playing style and technique, genre of music, etc. I guess that explains the few neutral or negative reviews I have read on this pedal but I'm not sure why this pedal has so few reviews (compared to many other OD pedals). I do appreciate that Boss is keeping with this pedal's OD-1 heritage but aside from the pedal color and very similar name, these are two very different pedals. I wonder if it gets overlooked by players who think its 'just another OD-1" (it is not!). I also know there are so many pedals on the market to choose from (many are cheaper too) so maybe it's just getting overlooked? Regardless- this is a great pedal and I highly recommend giving the OD-1X a try. It may just give you the great OD tone you have been looking for.

Based on my experiences with this pedal, I just purchased the Boss DS-1X and will post a review for it upon testing. Based on this pedal, my expectations are very high!

Update (1/24/18): The Boss DS-1X was a huge disappointment and is getting returned. How disappointing it does not provide the same awesome sound quality as the OD-1X.

title: Customer watt matching-maybe wrong.
comment: The only point I want to make and hope this helps people before buying any speaker. I was taught that when matching an amplifier wattage to a speaker, I should go by the RMS, and not peak or program power rating. So a speaker with a 1000 watt peak rating really only should be around 500 watts RMS. that is the number I always use and have not had trouble burning speakers. One more thing. a person should go easy while braking in a new speaker. You still want to heat it up, but checking up on it. If you buy them for a party, you should find its limitations beforehand.  The last thing is I bought a pair of 12's from Rockville, and I did not remember the amp rating correctly. I overdrove them by about 100 watts and I could smell the voice coils heating up. and I was displeased until I found out that this was my fault because I made a mistake on wattage. They still work fine though but plan to buy a pair of 18 inch Rockville's so I can open up the power.

title: Unexpectedly nice.. and LOUD.
comment: I had vouched to never buy anything Behringer ever again, because I have had nothing but problems with their gear in the past. I was in the market for a set of powered floor monitors for my rehearsal studio. I blew up my old wedges and my amp was acting up also. So I decided to switch to powered speakers. I use my PA both for vocals and keyboard at band rehearsal (loud rock band) and for my drum students to play along with music. I had a budget of $200 a piece for these powered speakers. The B210D was really the only option out there in my budget. The reviews were all raving, so I took the plunge. I decided to just get one first, in case it was a disappointment. Well, it wasn't. At first, when I took the speaker out of the box, I was thinking "I'm screwed. This thing is way too small and is not going to be powerful enough." Then I plugged it in. Mackie board, XLR cable, iPhone plugged into the board. Holy crap. This thing packs a punch. With your eyes closed, you'd swear it was a speak at least twice the size. It's LOUD. But it doesn't distort. I was able to crank the volume up to pain threshold, where I couldn't possibly go any louder before my head would explode, and the sound was still clear and not distorted. And the volume knob was halfway. This thing sounds really nice, focused and much bigger than it looks. We'll see how it holds up, which has always been the issue with Behringer, but for now, the second one is going to be ordered asap.

title: No Baby like a Cry Baby

Which has more flavor and more pedal to work with, also two control knobs to shape your sound. Probably the best pedal I own.  But when it comes down to getting that distinctive Wah, there's no baby like a Cry Baby. I love the Red Sparkle edition too. For the additional 10 bucks, for me it's worth it. Not a nickels worth of difference than the standard Cry baby, but it sure is pretty. And it really sounds great. I also spent the extra 10 bucks for the AC power adapter. I hate running out of batteries while playing, and have a habit of keeping things daisy chained together and not unplugging the cords when finished. And if you don't unplug on battery power, it will kill the battery. Anyway, great unit. My last one, before I sold it was a good 10 years old. I expect nothing less from this one. So many iconic songs that just can't hardly be replicated without this Dunlop Cry baby. So if your seriously into guitar, whether you buy the Red Sparkle or the Black, this is one pedal every guitarist needs in there gear. But, you already know that or you wouldn't even be on this page.

comment:
Ok. I don't write reviews often but I feel like this product needs an honest one. This is really a desktop item that is attempting to give you more for your money by giving the user the option to mount it.

Because of this, you need a much tougher and sturdy mic stand which most people are not prepared for. In other words, if you really want this item for your desktop, it's a steal! It's made well and feels pricier than it is.

title: great sound
comment: This is an exceptionally nice sounding guitar.  Sweet treble and bass balance, warm mids, great volume and sustain.  Solid Cedar thin double layer top which increases volume. I played many guitars in this price range.  I will give you a hint.  If you buy a Ramirez which costs the same or even a bit more you will mostly be paying for the name.  It does not compare.  I could never find volume, sound quality, bass and treble balance, playability (nice neck), and warm mids in one package.  I always had to give something, until I found the Guo.  I don't work for Calido Guitars, or for Mr. Guo.  I simply want to help anyone find the guitar they want.  I know how happy I was when I found everything I wanted in my classical guitar and I wish every guitarist could experience that feeling.  My advice... Buy this guitar.

title: Unbeatable for the Price
comment:
First, let me say that I really didn't know what to expect when I first ordered this power supply. I run several of my boards with the "higher end" supplies - MXR, VoodoLabs, etc. They work great for studio stuff of course, but for general day-to-day gigs I just didn't want to carry expensive gear like that anymore. I got this to pair with my workhorse pedal board because hey, if this breaks or gets damaged its not like I'm out an arm or a leg, and so far this thing has served its job perfectly!

This thing is quiet - low noise even on those terrible power outlets you find in bars. It adds no hum or unwanted static to my signal chain.
So far I've never had a problem with it powering my rig properly...no shorts or any similar issues.
Lastly, if you're one of those cats that values a bit of aesthetic flare on your board, the LEDs are very bright and poppy - but if that's not your speed it does have a separate switch for the lights.

Overall, very happy with this purchase.

title: Dampening goes too far
comment:
I have a nice pearl decade maple kit, which has a wonderful tone. I wanted to get a set of heads which would allow me to forego the moon-gels ive been putting on my single ply tom heads.

These heads remove all tone and sustain from your toms. I can't even tell which tom I'm hitting from the sound of it. I have these heads on 8, 10, 12, and 16 inch toms, and the first three all sound almost exactly the same- like whacking a cardboard box. They also take a lot of projection our of your kit.

I know these are intended to reduce overtones and sustain, but I can't imagine a use for the onyx heads. I will be trying g2 clear next.

Two stars instead of one because they are very sturdy heads that will have a long life, and the level 360 technology makes them easy to seat.

title: Fantastic so far
comment:
Hey there, I'm a beginner to recording music and Pro Tools 11 was my first DAW. I did not expect Pro Tools to be so easy to use. After watching a few hours of tutorials about recording, mixing and mastering (and a few on Pro Tools in general) editing in PT11 feels extremely intuitive and natural. There are a few drawbacks though. DON'T BUY AN iLOK, it comes with one. I didn't buy an extra but the description isn't very clear on this. Also, you can install PT11 on as many systems as you want, the ilok is required (as well as an audio interface plugged in) to run PT11 on any system though,

For one, the price. It almost seemed like a big gamble spending $500+ on my first real DAW. And the need for big money doesn't seem to stop there. If you're going to be doing lots of direct inputs with guitars and such, instead of miking an amp, then you're going to want to find some plugins. There doesn't seem to be a lot of plugin options out there in AAX format (which I believe is what Pro Tools accepts.) But the ones that I did find are in the hundreds of dollars. I haven't bought any plugins, since I'm miking amps.

Also, prepare to invest plenty of time watching tutorials and videos if this is a beginner experience for you. I used Lynda.com for tutorials since they were all in one place and made well. But to watch videos there it costs $25 for the membership.

Another mild drawback is the need to plug in an audio interface any time you run Pro Tools. If this is going to be installed on a stationary studio, then this isn't such an annoyance.

Some pros: my laptablet can run this. I'm currently running Pro Tools on an Acer Iconia W700 slate. It has a gen 3 i5, 4 gigs of ram and running Windows 8. I haven't run into any trouble with my computer running the program.

Installation for Windows was a breeze. Just follow the instructions that come in the booklet. It's a lengthy install but nothing confusing or difficult.

Overall, I would recommend this to anyone with the money to support it and the willpower to learn to use it.

title: Warm, sensitive, great for brushes.
comment:
I’ve been exploring brushes more lately, and I didn’t like the texture of a regular coated head. I found it too noisy and difficult to get a soft sound, and in the case of the Ludwig Weathermaster head that came on my snare, the coating was so rough that the brush wires were constantly snagging and making painful scraping sounds.

This Calftone head is perfect for getting soft, subdued brush sounds. The coating is only slightly textured, and not bumpy at all. It is a very sensitive head, with great response at very low volumes. Perfect for jazz. It also has a warm, fat tone when you lay into it on a rock or funk groove. The overtones from my brass snare are subdued but not absent, so the natural tone of the drum still breathes.

I’ve read that this head isn’t very durable. I’m not a heavy hitter, so I can’t comment on that. I’ve never broken or even dented a drumhead. If you have, consider working on your technique to play off, rather than into, the drumhead.

If you’re looking for a warm, vintage sound and look, this is a great head. I’ve always played Evans heads because the quality is consistent, and now that they’re making face shields for healthcare professionals, I’m even prouder to play their heads.

The delay sounds pretty digital to me, but it's also a usable effect. Mine is stacked after two other delay pedals, so I use it to get into psychedelic territory. If you're new to effect pedals Donner seems to be a good brand to get you started without breaking the bank. The thing I like best is, I got this pedal for a small board, and the size is perfect. title: Don't buy this tuner, there are better ones for less!
comment: Looks as though I'm the only buyer who didn't like this tuner! It comes locked in "Chromatic" mode so that they can claim it works for any instrument. The photo on Amazon and even on the product box it comes in shows it in "Guitar" mode. The only thing you get from this tuner which makes it different from some others its that you get an extra battery with it and a lifetime warranty. About a year ago I bought another tuner here on Amazon (Tuner on Guitar) which was half the price of this one. It allowed you to select the instrument desired. You could also select "Chromatic" mode if you desired that! Why lock the thing into only Chromatic? In addition it wasn't very good at detecting my guitar strings either. It was all over the place at times. Don't waste your money on this tuner if you simply want a flexible, easy to  use tuner for your guitar, violin etc. You can get one for about half the price which is actually better. You won't get an extra battery, but so what? Sorry folks, I just feel misled. Amazon is allowing me to return the tuner for a full refund. Thank you Amazon, you're the best!!

title: Its pretty cool as a training Uke
comment: The jury is stainless out. My first thought was to return it. My beefy digits made any chords almost impossible and Tiny (yes I named it) wouldn't stay tuned at all. I so I knew that new strings take awhile to stretch but then I thought "hey, what the hell" and put my favorite new strings on. So now I keep tuning it and tuning it and hope that in a few days or a week, it will hold a tune. Also I wish I could lower the action a bit at the saddle but it seems that the saddle is glued in place and so no bueno. There are a couple of big benefits fo me. I can put it in a backpack for flights out of town. Even if I find it too difficult to chord, I can always practice scales. And here's the kicker. it is so difficult to make chords on it that my fingers have to be place absolutely precisely to get the true sound of the chord. because of that when I play my other Ukes, I find that I play better since I don't have sloppy finger work. I carry over that accurate fingering to other ukes. So its a good training tool. Hope that makes sense. I am still giving it only 3 stars because of the difficulty to hold tuning. The tuners also seem sloppy. If this changes I will update this review. I also ordered the "guitar stick"" to just keep in the car. I''ll only use the bottom 4 strings like on a Uke

title: Don't over look Pure Blues if you play acoustic or acoustic/electric models
comment:
These strings are one of those little gifts you stumble across from time to time. I had been trying different strings on my Takamine Legend Series OM, a smaller bodied acoustic with killer electronics, including a vacuum tube to warm up the sound and in-sound hole mag and body resonance pickup. Problem was, nothing really grabbed me as far as the acoustic string sets, either for unplugged or electric playing.

Tried a set of these for the hell of it, and they sound FANTASTIC on this guitar. They have a unique character that I can only describe as richer, more three-dimensional. I tried a couple of other expensive acoustic sets and wound up cutting them off after playing a few minutes; they clearly were not in the same league as these Pure Blues.

If you play acoustic and especially if you play amplified acoustic don't overlook these strings. I was amazed how much I like them over what are considered to be for-acoustic set...these guys are killers.

title: Difficut to get working but it works like its suppose to
comment:
This product is not as bad as most people claim. It is definitely not a professional tool but it still works great when you want to record yourself or you have no amp. The biggest, and only, issue I had with this product were the driver and programs that came with it. The product's driver were crap (same for the programs) so I just downloaded the ASIO4All driver, and it solved my latency issues (I have ASIO4ALL set to 512 sample rates, which is produces descent quality for me). I run this device through Guitar Rig 4, and I have no delay issues when playing.

The computer that I run it on is this an AMD Phenom II N660 w/4 Gig ram. I have yet to figure out how to output the guitar's sound to the speakers without a third party dual-end 3.5mm male auxiliary cable and a 1/4 to 3.5mm convertor. If you are able to plug your head-phones into the head-set jack, you will only hear a "clean guitar" because the effects (such as distortion) occur on the pc and not the device itself. You'll need a third party dual-end 3.5mm male auxiliary cable and a 1/4-3.5mm convertor to hear the effects; just connect the auxiliary cable into mic-input on your pc.

Pros:
1. Works as advertised (Once you tinker with it and get different drivers/programs)
2. small and easy to carry/use (just plug it into the USB port after you install ASIO4ALL)
3.(with guitar rig 4) good replacement for amp if you do not have one

Cons:
1. A few additional third party supplies are needed for desired results (mainly cables and convertors)
2. The given drivers and programs are not the best
3. VERY THICK AND USELESS MANUAL THAT COMES WITH 3 SEPARATE SMALLER MANUALS TALKING ABOUT THE SAME THING (i just thought the manual was unnecessarily thick redundant; you'll know what i mean once you buy it...)
4.the led light never turns on like its suppose to
5.Did I forget to mention the crap-ass software and programs that come with it? Seriously don't use em...

Conclusion:
I will give it a 4 still because it works just like it said it should and that is what matters. Only the useless and crappy software/drivers annoyed me...and the excessively large and many manuals...

UPDATE: Go to the manufacturers website and download the driver for this product (it's not crap anymore). If you do not wish to use a 3.5mm converter cable then you're going to have to mess around with your audio input/output device on your computer to get the audio and effects to work; I'd still recommend a converter cable even though that defeats the purpose of the device. Still recommend this product if you want a cost-effective solution to playing your guitar on a digital device.

title: Great Microphone...at an Amazing price!
comment: I'm a sax player...and perform regularly.  For years I've wanted a clip on microphone, as I've always struggled with standing very still so my miked sound doesn't change while performing, but never felt I could justify the high cost of buying a good clip-on instrument mike.  When I saw the Pyle-Pro Sax mike...I simply couldn't resist trying it, at a price of about $48.  What I can tell you, after about 3 hours of practice/playing tunes over my Bose PA system, as this is one GREAT microphone!  It sounds absolutely great...good lows, good highs...and a very "natural" sound reproduction.  I can't wait to use it in my next performance...as I'm now going to be free to just play my sax and not worry about positioning myself in front of the mike!  If you're like me and have been balking at purchasing one of these mikes...well, I couldn't give a higher recommendation that the Pyle-Pro Sax Microphone...GREAT Prices and GREAT sound...and has NO issue with feedback! title: Perfect acoustic for me; just needed a little TLC
comment:
I should start off by saying I got this for a really great deal through Amazon Warehouse (over 60% off), so my review might be a bit biased compared to if I spent the full price. Of course, I'd also have bought a new guitar instead of used and had a warranty, so there's that to consider, too. I should also say that I also tried the Newporter Player and Newporter Special (I reviewed these also) both discounted through Amazon Warehouse, and was very underwhelmed by both of those guitars (maybe I just don't like the Newporter shape? I'll get to the Malibu shape later).

The reasons why it was discounted:

1) two finish cracks on either side of the input jack (they didn't go through to the mahogany -- I checked inside -- and haven't spread since I got the guitar 7 months ago, and I play standing with a strap putting pressure on that area daily, so purely cosmetic). Thank you other reviewer who mentioned you had this problem and sent it back! (though the preamp and tuner work perfectly, so maybe not your return?) :D

2) buzzing on the B and E strings (fixed the neck relief and also removed some black gunk in the nut string slots for these two strings; the buzzing immediately stopped)

3) the ebony bridge pins were splitting (maybe Amazon wasn't aware of this, or maybe it's just a Fender QC issue; and I'm still trying to find good replacements; any suggestions for new ebony pins that'll fit? I'm currently using D'Andrea brass for durability and because I need that extra arch/notch in the pin head area)

4) and what Amazon referred to as "major cosmetic issues to the body of the item" -- not chips or cracks or anything, but the braces were bulging because the guitar needed humidification badly (I went inside and took pictures of the braces, posted above, which look perfect and have never rattled in the 7 months I've played it daily, so I don't think they're loose).

At the advice of acoustic guitar forums, after daily 2 hour play I returned the guitar immediately to its included case (a great bonus and wonderful gig bag), and then when winter hit humidified it with the included basic humidifier (another great bonus). So, since November, the top has all but straightened itself out (you can sort of see the difference in the before pic with the original ebony pins, the after pic with the brass pins). If anything, I think the problem allowed the top to open prematurely, because for such a little guitar, it has the biggest acoustic sound out of any acoustic I've ever tried (I haven't used it much amplified). Even in my digital camera recordings, which have poor audio, it sounds like a much bigger, richer guitar, easily beating out dreadnoughts I've recorded similarly. Does it compare to $1000+ guitars? Shrug. I'll find out when Amazon Warehouse discounts some $1000+ guitars so I can afford one. :P

Now to the Malibu shape: It's smaller size and scale is a joy to hold and play; I'm 5'6", 140 lbs (most acoustic guitars look bigger than I do) with medium hands, and I can get to the 16th fret on all strings even though this isn't a cutaway, 17th on all but the low E, can stretch for the 20th with a little finger finagling, can make all the open chord shapes. It took some getting used to, coming from a Stratocaster background, but I think I actually prefer this. The neck is notably thicker than the Newporter's neck was (much more electric) but the shorter scale makes it easier to play. Other acoustics have given me issues just to hold and strum. So the Malibu neck is sort of halfway between acoustic and electric. And all of those upper register notes sing! Cutaway guitars I've tried have sounded dull and lifeless that high up the neck (and have been more difficult to do bends on, to boot). This one sounds great all over the neck and responds when I really dig in or back off with my strumming. Sustains for days.

And I love the vintage tuners. Stringing on "modern" tuners always used to take seemingly forever and inevitably resulted in some emotional explosions. Not these. Super quick and easy to string up, acts like a locking tuner, and I love that I can remove the string for whatever reason and pop it back in afterward. The one reviewer who had problems with these tuners had way too much string; mine look like they have one wind around the post until you get to the thinner strings. (maybe I'll post a picture? Edit: included one -- that's cutting the string a little bit more than two posts further on from the post being strung)

As others have mentioned, the guitar is beautiful. Much more so in person. I'm not a huge fan of blue (I would've preferred the red, but beggars can't be choosers) but I think the Cosmic Turquoise (which goes from teal to Sleeping Beauty turquoise depending on the lighting) is perfect with the rich mahogany and golden koa accents. I'm interested to see if the top changes color as the guitar ages, as spruce tops usually turn amber. Green? More brown? Hopefully not gray lol. I also love the silky smooth roasted pau ferro (feels just like ebony!) fingerboard and bridge -- I'm assuming roasted because it's so chocolatey in color; along with the mahogany neck, it almost looks like a solid rosewood neck. The maple position markers are also a nice touch and make me want to go back to wood bridge pins to complete the (nearly) all-wood look.

Overall, it's the best acoustic I've ever owned. Maybe even the best guitar period. I would buy another if something happened to it, even at full price. (I'd buy the red then, though ;) Try one out, you might be surprised.

title: fun bass
comment: This has been my main gigging bass for about 5 years. Its a very very solid instrument. I've owned several high quality basses including several American made fenders. this one hold its own with all of them. Sound and playability wise its right up there with the best of them. Where it lacks a little is the quality of the hardware. After a few years of heavy gigging and beach shows most of the screws and bridge saddles got rusty. I replaced them all with stainless screws. The bridge has some rust under the adjusters as well. I've had to clean the pots out a few times and replace the main tone pot that just fell apart. The tuners and chrome all are in good shape with just normal  wear.  The number of permutations with the switching is a little excessive but you can get a lot of different sounds out of it. I tend to keep the active/passive switch in the passive mode and use the bottom set of switches more often. I switch it into active mode when I need more of a boost for those boomy bass lines.  My only criticism is the weight distribution of the instrument. Its awkward to play sitting down, which I do very little of. With a strap it tend to sit a little awkwardly but not enough that it bothers me. It is on the heavy side too. The thing holds tuning incredibly well. I wouldn't normally even pay attention to that but its almost eerie how seldom I have to tune it. It still plays and sounds great and I have no plans to replace it as my main axe.

This is a fabulous violin for an intermediate student! My now 10 year old daughter's first 3 years of Suzuki classes at school were with inexpensive 1/8 and 1/4 size Mendini violins, which although they were certainly not high quality instruments, they were fine for a non-prodigy (but still musically inclined) elementary school student just learning the basic mechanics of playing the violin. When she started taking it more seriously, with private lessons and practicing almost every night, I began to get the sense that the level of instrument she was playing was holding her back, and decided it was time to spend a little more money on something better.

This outfit was perfect for her. It sounds noticeably better, and she seems to enjoy playing it more as well. It's also a gorgeous instrument, really stunning. I personally don't really like the super-shiny violins that look like they were molded out of plastic, and this one has a softer, more satin finish, and is more of a brownish orange color as opposed to bright orange.

One other note, regarding the case. I didn't like the large red "Kennedy Violins" lettering embroidered on the outside of the case, as I had seen in some of the photos on the company's website. But I emailed them to ask about this, and they said that they had transitioned away from having this lettering on their cases, which I was pleased to hear.

title: Great long term ownership
comment:
I did not buy from Amazon, but FYI on long term ownership: I bought the original S6 CW in a guitar shop under recommendation around 1992. I didn't like the look of the headstock (and still don't love it) but the salesman convinced me of the functionality in design (and he was right) so I bought it. It sounded good then and -thoroughly worn- it sounds great now. The guitar has seen me through several cities and stages of life. This purchase has turned out to be one of the best I ever made. The sound quality and playability of my s6 cw is excellent for the price. From other reviews, it sounds like this company has kept on track. A few years ago, I bought a beautiful Gibson AJ. It is a superb guitar in a much different price bracket with a lot more projection, bass, and clarity. But the Seagull sits right next to the Gibson and gets picked up all the time. Super fun to figure out finger work on it, wail on it without worries, take it out on the deck/ teach a beginner. AND it still sounds great. It can hang with the Gibson -NO PROBLEM.

After 20+ years Only 2 things: 1)The bottom edge of the body was a sharp angle and not super comfortable for long sessions: fixed with a DIY stick on hard (thin) foam piece (maybe new s6 models better?). 2)A crack has developed between the glued pieces at the base of my S6 neck ...BUT, I DID move this guitar between a humid & dry climate several times, I DID play it very hard at times, and I did not keep it cased with a humidifier; it still sounds good -if you take better care of yours, the cracking might not be an issue.

title: For a budget audio interface, this does the job really well!
comment:
I think it's clear that one has to get their expectations to be realistic. If you're expecting this $140 unit to be exactly as good as one of the industry leaders that costs five to ten times as much, you'll probably be let down. If you need a very specific monitoring setup that this device does not support, it makes little sense to try and save money here, and you should just purchase another device that does do what you need.

That being said, for stereo recording from two microphones, this device does the job very well, with a relatively clean signal and fairly simple software. I use it in a fully mobile recording setup with a MacBook Air M1 running Big Sur and Logic Pro in a concert hall with orchestral musicians to produce symphonic backing tracks for a variety of applications, and it's exactly what I was looking for. Small, simple, and bus-powered. I have a variety of nice microphones available to me these days, but am happy with the results that I get from even inexpensive mic pairings like the Audio-Technica AT2020 cardioid condenser and the MXL R144 ribbon, and for my music students who are getting into recording for the first time, they are able to put together a functional setup for their machines for under $400 including the interface, mics, cables, and stands.

title: P-90 punch in a humbucker-sized casing!
comment:
Tonerider is a nice, up-and-coming aftermarket pickup company that I just recently learned about. There are a lot of great aftermarket pickup options out there, but Tonerider is nicely positioned between the "budget" and "premium" options. Actually, for the price, their build quality is outstanding, in my opinion.

The Rebel 90 is a P-90 pickup that fits into slots that would hold a humbucker pickup. This is a big deal, since very few guitars will accept a soapbar P-90 without significant modding or routing. Competitor products for the Rebel 90 would be the Dream/Mean 90 from GuitarFetish, or the Phat Cat from Seymour Duncan. But I thought the tone quality of the Rebel 90 was excellent. Output hovers around 8k for both neck and bridge. P-90's have a distinctive mid-range "punch" to them that traditional single coils do not. Some people describe the tone as "between" a single coil and a humbucker, and I think that is a pretty fair assessment. Highly recommend!

title: Nice thick distortion without compression or shrillness
comment:
[[VIDEOID:ddccf63ace115bbce4e8f79404dbc6a7]] One can never have too many drive pedals. I have a nice tube screamer clone and a Metal Zone, but I needed something for that in between spot. I have warm overdriven trebly covered, but wanted a sound between there and compressed, super high-gain. Enter the $20 Riot clone.

Seeing as I didn't want to drop 2 bills on a boutique pedal, I thought I'd try this out. I wasn't expecting too much, but I've got to say I'm pleasantly surprised.

The first thing I noticed is that the build quality seems really good for the price. It's in a sturdy metal enclosure with a nice firm footswitch. It looks pretty attractive in person, moreso than the pictures.

I like that it has only three knobs: volume, gain, and tone. Playing my Les Paul with p90s, I had the gain and tone nearly dimed. But that just could be me, I like a lot of treble. I will probably keep the tone between 8 and 10, but the gain is a different story:

If you the put gain at about noon, you get a pleasant distortion that is bordering on boosted overdrive. It's not super overpowering, but just heavy enough for some classic rock type stuff. If you dime out the gain knob, you get a really beefy and thick heavy distortion tone, similar to Smashing Pumpkins or Bush ala 16 Stone. I find the tone also suitable for metal.

This is a unique drive that occupies a special space that few others do. I think it is closest to the Suhr Riot obviously, but it also reminds me at certain settings of a Big Muff, RAT, or Mesa Boogie TriRec.

This is a versatile little drive that stands out from among the average drives that everyone has. I like it, and I'm glad I bought it.

lockdown and I settled on the ukulele. I hoped that this would be pleasant-sounding enough that I wouldn’t completely terrorize the rest of the tenets in my apartment building. I’m sure everyone sharing a wall with me is relieved that I ended up with a ukulele instead of a drum set. I definitely wanted a start kit that comes with the tools you need to start learning right away. This really fit the bill and got me practicing in no time.

My biggest piece of advice actually comes from my musician friend who encouraged me to get to new strings if I was going to continue playing on it. New strings did make a huge difference, and make the sound much richer. The strings that come on the ukulele work just fine for learning, but just don’t hold their tuning very well. Unfortunately I have not continued to progress as work has started back up again in earnest, but for those few months of learning it was a lovely companion.

title: Fun little synthesizer
comment:
I've been a fan of the original Stylophone for a number of years, and I was excited to see a more serious instrument when this synth came out. I say "synth" because the added features like the filter, envelope, and LFO make this much more like a synthesizer than the original, which I considered much more akin to a solid state organ.

The controls should be familiar to synth users, all functioning as you would expect and without too many surprises. The sound set this synth creates is really impressive. The filter is pretty thick and the added analog delay and resonance make for a pretty full sound. For serious applications, you'll want to use the auxiliary port because, as noted by others, the little on-board speaker is pretty lame.

I didn't notice any latency between contact and the note sounding, as noted by another reviewer.

If you like the quirky and zippy sounds of the original, you can still get a lot of that, but note that you'll have to modulate vibrato through the LFO instead of using a dedicated switch. Not really a problem, the LFO is a lot more useful.

The cost of this unit seems appropriate to me. All in all, you get a really good sounding, fun to play instrument. I have no regrets or qualms with this synth.

title: Great kit for the cost
comment:
This is a beautiful and great kit for the cost. Some assembly required and there are no instructions included. Be sure to check out YouTube for assembly and tuning tips from DW if you need help. Excellent sound from this kit and the mag-lev throw-off is amazing.

The included heads are single-ply basic Remo heads, you'll want to swap them out in a few weeks. These are not the fancy custom DW/Remo heads included on more expensive DW kits. For the 8" tom, you'll need to hook it to a cymbal stand, the other toms and floor toms will stand on their own. Also, you'll need to get a snare stand if you don't already have one.

Overall, for the price, the quality on these are amazing and the drums feature some DW signature features that are much more affordable than their premium counterparts.

title: Beware of strength chart
comment:
I finally decided to try a synthetic reed. With a Vandoren M13 Lyre mouthpiece, a 3.5 V12, Rue Lepic, or D'Addario Reserve Classic is about the right strength for me, and I also sometimes play a 3.0 Vandoren Traditional (though some in a box turn out to be too light). Based on the chart on Legere's website, this 3.25 Euro Signature should have been a tad harder than the V12 3.5, but I definitely found that NOT to be the case... it was MUCH lighter. I'd say it was in the ballpark of a V12 3.0. But, on a positive note, though it was quite soft, it didn't have as much of that "buzzy" sound that light reeds typically give, and I actually ended up playing a couple of concerts with it. In the end, I decided it was too light, and I will be exchanging it for a 3.75 (I also purchased a 3.5, which, while also much lighter than expected, feels in the range of what is comfortable for me, so having a 3.5 and a 3.75 in my case should be perfect).

I'm glad that the manufacturer offers an exchange, but of course I would have preferred to have ordered the right reed from the beginning (again, according to the strength chart, a 3.5 or 3.75 would have been way too hard for me, but this ended up being about right).

One other minor complaint. Legere gives somewhat vague instructions as to the correct way to store these reeds, saying that they should not be stored in a case that "puts pressure on the tip" or something like that. I'm not exactly sure what that means, so I'm storing it in one of those little individual plastic cases that cane reeds come in, which hopefully is ok. At $30 per reed, Legere should provide a holder (it comes packaged just in a cardboard sleeve), so as to eliminate any confusion over how to store these.

title: Comfy drum stool with very sturdy hardware
comment:
I often prefer the “motor cycle” style seat but the cushion that came with this drum stool is very comfy. I’ve also had screw-in style post for the seat and it didn’t stay sturdy at all. It made me skeptical that this seat would do the same given that it has a screw-in style post. To my great surprise, the post is not wobbly at all as long as you tighten the hardware connection to the tripod legs. Another welcome hardware is the locking mechanism that allows you to lock in the height of the stool despite rotating the seat back and forth as you would when playing the drums. The locking hardware is essentially a memory lock that you’d find on some drum kits with an additional tab on that side that locks into the tripod legs attachment post.

Overall, this is a very sturdy drum stool that is also comfortable to sit on for a very long time. A few minor complaints is that when I unwrap the hardware, there were tons of fingerprints all over the shiny chrome. Not a big deal but it just makes it look dirty given that it’s something brand new. Another minor complaint is the rubber feet is not completely secured to the tripod legs. Nothing that some healthy portion of glue can’t fix but I want to mention it because the feet could likely get lost if you have to travel with this stool.

title: Don't let the Video Fool You
comment: Though I don't own this (I own the 18i20 version)... be weary of the video. Many of the videos for these show full band recordings done with this. You can certainly do full band recordings, but understand that this only has 4 microphone inputs. You'll see someone adjusting another piece of equipment during the video, showing the connectivity possibilities. However... that extra piece of equipment is called an Octi-pre, and it expands your inputs by another 8. So... if you actually want to record a full band simultaneously... and don't want to purchase both this and an octi-pre (which would cost ~$850 USD in total).. get an interface with more inputs to begin with, and save yourself some disappointment and cash.

title: One of the best MIDI controllers I've used to date!
comment:
I've been producing music for 15+ years now, and this is by FAR one of the best MIDI controllers I've used. The keys are SOLID. They're slightly heavier than most synth-weighted keyboards, and I much prefer it to the looser, more flimsy-feeling M-Audio Radium/Oxygen keys. The build quality is SOLID (especially on the faders), and Ableton Live recognized it right away. No need to install drivers!!

Plus you also get Bass Station and V-Station for free, included when you register your product.

The drum pads feel great, I've been using them more frequently than my M-Audio Trigger Finger. I love the feel of the knobs as well.

This things is simple, solid, and gets the job done. I've only used it for a day and I can already tell it's reliable -- not to mention super lightweight!

Awesome!!

comment:
I love that I finally found a digital piano that sounds and pretty much replicates the pianos from my lessons.

I am definitely a beginner who has been taking lessons for a little while and have hesitated on purchasing a full 88 and have mainly stuck with subpar keyboards to practice at home with. I found that I was able to get this keyboard to digitally sound more like my full piano from practice than any other I have used. An impressive feature is that it does mimic the key pressure to a real piano.

For the money, I feel like this digital piano is a great value! I have priced and tested out various others for awhile and this really does seem like the most bang for your buck! title: Fastest pedal I've ever played
comment:
This pedal is fast! Be aware that it takes some getting used to because you almost do not feel it under your foot. For heel down players this is the perfect pedal, and heel up players will quickly come to appreciate the response and light touch this pedal has.

Since I play jazz and acoustic, I replaced the hard rubber beater that comes with this pedal with the Slug Percussion Power Head Jazz Pro Titanium Beater (ASIN: B0002F5G2A). While pricey, this beater makes the Axis even faster. There is another less expensive beater for jazz players, Speed Ball's felt beater (ASIN: B0002F4M02) that is as good (in my opinion) as the Slug Percussion model. For louder music, or for players who bury the beater into the head I recommend getting the Axis Sonic Hammer (ASIN: B0002F7IBM), which will give you all the attack you could want. This beater was designed for the Axis A model, but will work with the X as well. Also consider the Slug Percussion Power Head beater (ASIN: B0002F5G16).

This pedal is easy to adjust, and a dream to play. It's also easy nto upgrade this to a double pedal by purchasing two pedals and the Axis double pedal conversion kit. Interestingly, you can mix and match X and A models, long and short boards with the conversion kit.

Be aware, though, that there is a definite love/hate relationship between drummers and this pedal. I've met rock, jazz and blues drummers who swear by this pedal, and I've met drummers from those same genres who hate it. I personally love it, but recommend that you actually try it at a local store before you buy one.

title: Definitely worth the money, but could be improved by...
comment: My package of two stands arrived fairly quickly, which was great. And I'm so impressed at how small each stand folds up.  Very portable and lightweight so it will be a great stand to take on away-practices or gigs.  It's an attractive item, clearly cleverly designed in many ways.  It's not as sturdy as my three other guitar stands but they would be more cumbersome and weighty to transport.  The Hola stand could be improved in the following ways:  The portion that holds the neck of the guitar all too easily can fold downwards.  If you'd not locked the neck into place by drawing the rubber guard across the neck, your guitar could fall forward.  So, do draw that rubber guard thingie across and latch it to the other side, as a pre-caution.  Also, wrap some tape around the place where the top section joins the main stem of the stand.  The manufacturer/designer should come up with a latching device.  // Sort of similarly, the cradle, at the base, which will hold the bottom of your guitar, can too easily spin around and then fall forward and out.  The design is that, when you insert it into the main stem of the stand, you spin it once and it supposedly is locked into place but it could all too easily spin around when the guitar isn't in the cradle and be dangerously at a forward precipice when you next do place your guitar onto the cradle.  I wrapped some tape around the joint.  The cradle can still spin or move side to side to best fit the bottom of the guitar (clearly the intended design) but the forked cradle can't easily come forward and out any longer.  //  The stand has a tri-pod design, like many if not most guitar stands.  But the rear 'pod' (foot/leg) is much shorter than the forward two legs.  This will allow you to set the stand closer to a wall and have a smaller footprint.  However, to me, it doesn't feel terribly stable.  //  Now, do I regret having gotten these two stands for the price.  I don't think so.  But I wouldn't put my most expensive guitars on these stands.  These are probably most ideal for Ukes, Guitarleles, and Mandolins, all of which I also have.  But, in the safety of my carpeted music studio, I probably will also use them for lighter guitars.  So, an overall good rating.  I'd love to hear the manufacturer/designer respond to these concerns or hear whether others have had problems for the issues that I mentioned.

I have a cheaper brand which I did not like. this one is a keeper. the piano sounds are just wonderful, there are about six grand piano sounds plus hundreds more.Pianos,trumpets,violin and many others are excellent.I am an electric guitarist{40 years} moving over to piano.The key feel on this is what you would expect from a real piano not like the little cheapo I have been using.I am thrilled with this one and plan on keeping it a good long time. I would have to spend quite a bit to replace it with one of the top four brands. This is well made. I have had the pleasure of owning many fine guitars so I know quality when I find it.I tried the top seller and like it but I wasn't thrilled with a few things like when you switch voices and it stutters.this one does not stutter. its seamless, one voice to another. People complain about the manual but I find it useful and helpful.this piano is intuitive to use, for the most part you won't even have to use the manual if you don't go too deep.You could do far worse and pay a lot more.

Edit. after seven months I regret this purchase deeply.already looking for something else. you get what you pay for.1200.00 is about what you have to spend to get a really good piano.

title: Overhyped, Piercing and thin top end
comment:
Outstanding design and hefty/solid build, looks and feels really good. Mine came with the Aston logo somehow lodged in the mic's grill--it had fallen off the body, it was originally affixed to the body with glue.

This mic has received great reviews on YouTube and elsewhere on the web--makes me wonder about the glowing and also respectfully reserved reviews by some of the well known and respected mic reviewers.

My take: this is a good sounding microphone, with a clear and full and yet not quite neutral and warm low-end (sounds like EQ'd and processed to try to sound full and warm--like the King Bee II--and doesn't sound naturally rich and warm and open/airy), mids are clear and crisp and prominent, and highs are a bit piercing and it's hard/painful to listen to the sound from this mic for an extended period of time.

Finally, as for the price, at $350 it's now selling at a significantly lower price than when it was introduced but given the sound quality it could be priced even lower, as for a microphone the sound quality is of paramount importance. At this price point there are mics that sound better. However, as I mentioned above this mic is well designed and looks like made very well too--when you forget the logo that fell off.

I would prefer to use my Neat Worker Bee (original version) and EQ it to my liking over this microphone or King Bee II, or use my LCT 440/AT440/NT2-A or even my P220. But this mic does look good.

Imo Aston Spirit is an okay/decent sounding microphone.

I did all my soldering work correctly, and the noise was impossible. I literally pulled the pickups out and put them in a pre-wired harness to make sure. They were still unbelievably noisy. I swapped out pickups in the harness to check that that wasn't defective: nope, it was these awful 57/62 pickups. I have never, ever seen such a bad product. I wasted so much time on these noisely pickups that I finally pulled them out and slammed them in the trash. I didn't want to resell them, as no human being should have to endure this crap.

title: Amazing value! Know what you are getting before leaving a crappy review...
comment:
TLDR: Buy it and some D'addorio strings and its a great value. Seriously, get better strings.

Is it a Gibson or a Fender? No. But compared to the many Asian $100 acoustics out there, this thing blows it away at 60% of the price, and is American. Strings are great to learn on. Once you get the hang of it, get some high quality strings, and you are still money ahead. Wasn't tuned, and should be that way (they were intentionally loose and wrapped in wax paper). But the truss setting was on the money.. The thing was wrapped in Styrofoam (the wrapping kind;not the stiff kind) and was kept.in place by cardboard inserts (similar to how a laptop ships).

Bottom line: it's a sub $70 guitar that plays like something almost twice the price. If you have twice the money and know your guitar, buy a fender. If you are starting out, are strapped for cash, need a covid hobby or want to introduce your teen to guitar, this is the best value around.

Only con: As some have said here, they don't do the best job cleaning up the glue at the base of the neck in the guitar. You might have to carefully pick some off to be able to adjust your truss bar. Long flat blade and a steady hand fixes this in 30 seconds.

title: Give Them a Try
comment: I have a Yamaha LL 16 and had a bone nut and saddle exchanged from the original material. I think both of those changes improved the clarity, sustain and top end. However, I still had ebony bridge pins that fit loosely in the bridge. Therefore, when I saw these pins, I decided to give them a try. I did not compare the wood pins with the new bone pins, but the new bone pins fit tighter and I think that improved the sound alone by transferring the vibrations of string more securely to the saddle and bridge. If you presently have plastic or even wood pins, I recommend giving these a try. It's a very simple and inexpensive improvement. If the the instrument sounds better you're going to be more motivated to play it. And the more you play it the better you're going to be and your ability will match or exceed the quality of the instrument. There is no substitute for daily practice.

title: Very cool flanger
comment:
I don't use a ton of modulation in my chain, but this little flanger pedal is very cool. It doesn't have a massive sweep, but the color and tone and nice and transparent, and it really does add a lot of depth to the modulation tone. If you turn the rate all the way up and the depth down it can easily double as a tremolo too!

The coolest thing here is the filter switch, which lets you use the pedal as a tone filter (like a cocked wah), so you can find that sweet spot for a particular point in the sweep that you really like and just sit there enjoying it.

It LOVES clean tones. It gets a little overwhelmed with a lot of gain, but I think like the chorus pedal from this series its small size and less aggressive circuit make it really ideal for a more intimate type of gig. I wouldn't put this on my modern metal rig, but accompanying singer in a neosoul or jazz context? PERFECT.

PS: I think the pastel color palette in this series is really hip too.

title: Money simply can't buy anything better.....
comment:
My son plays Viola at a very a high level. The thing he's struggled with the most has been finding a stylish case that fits his 16.5" Viola. On top of being a big Viola, he has an expensive one as well. Finding high quality cases are difficult to find as well. Before, I start my review I'd like to congradulate "The String Shop". My case literally came in 2 days. They're located in Marietta, GA, and I live close to New York, New York in Newark, New Jersey. In two days that case moved 900+ miles. That's amazing. As for the packaging, it was immaculate. It was wrapped so well, the case arrived in perfect condition. As for the case, it's beautiful. The case is solid hard fiber glass, meaning: If you scrape it, the paint won't fall off. It's solid orange. The latches, open super smooth. They're also super safe. There are four on the front and two in the back. The leather handles are also super high quality leather. They're amazing. The inside is also well protected, (it's black) and has a very very generous accessory space (good enough for a shoulder rest and rosin). The case has which are very smooth rolling. Although i'd recommend not using them, as they'll get dirty. The case also come with two backpack straps which are very sturdy. The latches also have two pairs of keys which you can use to lock the case. The only down side to the case, is putting the bow in is pretty complicated. After about an hour, I noticed that you have to put the bow on an angle so it'll fit. Overall, great case totally worth the money.

PS. The myth about the case smelling like petroleum is a lie. It smelled perfectly normal to me. In fact, it almost had a new-car smell.

If you have any questions email me at: amunevarcc@gmail.com

It does a great job on all of my combination of applications. One things for sure, you'll need a high quality AC adaptor, otherwise you'll have a high noise factor (hiss). I'm using a authentic Roland PSB-1U and it's operating very quietly. And I mean quietly. The volume knob on the unit allows to match bypass, and all other features work very well. I found a little goes a long way. It's favored for bass and there's lots of possibilities and I'm happy, especially for the price.

title: Surprising little stomp box!
comment: I hesitated a long time before pulling the trigger on getting this pedal. After all, it doesn't really "do" anything except "enhance" the signal.  Well yes, that's exactly what it does.  To my ear, the chain of devices on my pedal board was weakening the original signal from the guitar, and I didn't want to have to add distortion to boost that. When I finally got this and put it at the beginning of the signal chain, I could immediately hear the difference as the highs rang through clearly, regardless of what effects I was using.  The sparkle it added brought new life to my sound.  So far I just leave it always on, though down the road, when the lockdown ends and live performances once again take place, I can see how using it to bring more presence to solos could also be a valued feature.

I was impressed that the construction wasn't "as cheap as they could make it".  Nice to have a key lock (not that you couldn't break it in 5 seconds, but it does provide an extra step to prevent the drawer from popping open in transit)  The drawer rolls smoothly and fits in among the 11 other drawers (2-4 space) In have on stage, some purchased at more than double the price for 2 spaces back when companies like MCM weren't around and you could only get Mid Atlantic (or similar products) thru a dealer at exorbitant prices for the same product.  I am all for companies making a profit, but, wow, my mid-atlantic drawers had as many, if not more problems, than the generics.  Very pleased with this one and would buy another.

title: Great package for the Beginner - includes all the essentials...
comment:
For $11, this package is not a lot to pay to start a beginner off on the journey of proper flute maintenance.
When I bought my first flute in August last year, like a new pet owner/parent etc, I wanted to know how to best care for the instrument and found this kit most helpful.

Included in the kit
1) cleaning gauze - this is an absorbent, child hanky size cloth that is great of drying the inner tube of your flute - can't go better
2) polishing cloth - a fine soft cloth (blue microfibre like) for gentle wiping down of the outer side (external) of your flute - ensure it is kept clean & dust free and it will not scratch.
3) silver polish cloth (yellow) - use very very sparingly as it wears down silver layer with frequent use, and make sure chemical powder does not get into the pads
4) pad cleaning paper - for mopping moisture off your pads to prevent stickiness - do not rub against pads as do not want to tear delicate fibres - use as required

5) tone hole cleaner - haven't used this myself yet - I would imagine, use with care near pads - come to think of it I don't think I've seen it (!? - let me check)
Update: No tone hole cleaner! (well to be fair I haven't needed them but this makes the photo a little misleading! MINUS 1star but retain 4stars for utility.

6) basic flute maintenance instruction card - nothing better than a manufacturer's daily after use care instruction.

As one gains experience, one may decide thereafter to purchase more specialized items separately. For now, these are fantastic.

Time will tell if they hold. I did clean both surfaces with alcohol as that always improves adhesion. If they don’t hold, I’ll mount them using wall anchors.

For effectively $16.50 per panel, these are a tremendous improvement over those worthless foam equivalents all over Amazon. They look a million times better as well. I placed them around my room, especially at the reflection points from my speakers.

Will I probably replace eventually with proper sound panels? Yes. But for the price, look, and function, I’ll just move them to another part of my home.

title: My new #1 OD.  Amazing.
comment:
This pedal is great. The build quality is spot on and everything was just right out of box. Also includes simple manual and optional foam/Velcro stickers. I made a custom one from dual lock, but I appreciate the options.

The sound is amazing. Slightly warm, but not muddy and dark on my trebly squiers like so many drive pedals are. I was worried about not having a tone adjustment, but the touch knob is better than a tone knob when the tone is already as good as this pedal's. Touch knob adjusts dynamics, compression, and the feel of the drive. It's really neat how versatile it is. You can get raw grit or compressed, saturated sounds from the same pedal.

The gain goes from almost nothing to a mediumish gain level, nothing insane. It sounds amazing as a basic mild overdrive and even better when stacked with others. There is a bit of headroom in the pedal which makes my guitar sound like I'm hearing it in a wide open space, not confined in a small bedroom.

It cleans up well, responds very well to picking dynamics, and also handles double-stops and triads without getting too harsh and dissonant while also keeping single-notes nice and dirty.

It is very sweet. It is very creamy. It is not a sharp, prickly clipping like the Kaffir Lime (which is not nearly as good), but instead is a softer, milder type of clipping which sounds smoother, yet still badass.
