title: 50 pounds of Fun that goes Boom.
comment:
Bought this at the local shop here in Maui. Got Amazon prices but paid tax's. Bought floor model as is (the Cd. in not working). Got the PF-115HE at the same time (drywall chock rubs, nothing a whisk brush and a towel could not take care of). This is now my main amp, been using a Behringer BXL450 for a little over a year. Playing with a Yamaha RBX EW, and a RBX A2 AIR also have a Fender Modern Jazz and a factory modified Jaguar bass. All sound great and easy tweaks to the head to get the best sound out of the bass.
Once you get the sound that you like, it is very easy to get back to, controls are easy to understand and use. With this Cab the lows roll nicely and you can add or remove compression to soften slap, finger, or to pick. There an ultra Hi and ultra Low switches (I use the low always) , -15db switch for actives (have 4 if you include 2 Acoustics) ground lift switch, also effects in and out, Tuner out, and stomp in. If you are looking for that classic mellow bass sound that Ampeg is known for, you will not like this head or any PF bass head. The solid state can not duplicate a true tube head. Not close enough to win the cigar, sorry.

At 300 watts 8 ohms it is loud enough to cut though, A 60 watt 2/10 Fender, a 85 watt Marshall 1/2 stack 4/10, and a full Roland elec. drum kit at 70 watts 2/12. That's with gain at 5 and volume at 6. Still room to add more of anything you what to through at it. If that not enough get the PF 210 cab and stack the PF 115 the head now pumps out 500 watts. We play mostly rock and metal a little pop to (we all try to sing, We Suck!OK.) but it's just friends getting together 2x a week.

title: It's the tops
comment:
I have been using my Sabine STX1100 tuner for over 10 years, and I wouldn't be caught dead without it. This gadget runs on a 9-volt battery and it can help you get your tuning exactly right, not approximately right. Just add the music.
Bear in mind that it listens to your instrument with a microphone that should be positioned near the instrument in a room without much ambient noise. You can plug a phone plug directly into the tuner if you don't want to rely on the microphone. For a stringed instrument, you don't have to hammer the string, just pluck lightly. If the light flashes, try dampening the other strings with your other fingers.
This device also helps transpose between keys. It will fit on a music stand and it is small enough to put in a pocket. I change the battery every 2-3 years, just to be on the safe side. I have nothing bad to say about this product.
I hope you enjoy yours as well as I have enjoyed mine!

title: No Low-End
comment:
I will start by saying I've had some fantastic experiences with Joyo pedals in the past. Their Classic Phase is actually my favorite phase, and I've had many of their overdrives that definitely exceeded expectations, especially considering their price.

This pedal not so much. Honestly I feel a bit mislead by some of the YouTube reviews I saw (not Joyo's fault to be fair), where the pedal sounded big and meaty. In reality, this thing does have quite a bit of gain, and probably wouldn't be the worst tone for solos, but there is just no bottom end. I cranked my amp's low up to 8, and it still felt flat. If the pedal included a basic tone knob alongside the Mid control this might be different, but as is there's no effective way to add bass without resorting to outside help from other EQ pedals. Try their Extreme Metal pedal, that one at least has a 3-band EQ.

I have no idea why it does this and have found no solutions other than to keep it charging while it's being used, which sucks because when it's plugged in to charge it adds to the fuzzy static in the background.

For the price, I say it's worth it. Just don't expect any professional-sounding recordings from it.

The nylon tips are nice on the ride, to ping out every note with its ball type shape. The sticks are a little shorter than the 5A/B's, which are very popular but never really liked the tips on them. title: I don't know why people have such different experiences
comment: I agree with some about the software.  It is a bit hokey to install and I'm not crazy about how it functions, as much as it sounds cool to be able to use different strings or different fret groupings for distinct instruments and whatnot.  I really got away from their software and simply use the device to convert a guitar into a MIDI device.  There I'm not sure where people found so much trouble.  Some report spending absurd amounts of money on a PC and not being able to get it to work.  I build my own PC's, trying to make them as fast as I can get with a reasonably priced processor, and the synth pickup and connection have worked marvelously.  I do not have trouble with terrible latency, although it is a bit slower than my MIDI keyboard.  At the same time, I have owned 2 Roland guitar synths and they are much trickier to hook up, of course they are much bulkier and more of a nuisance to deal with, they are much more expensive, and the latency in my experience with those has been awful.  The Fishman unit is small and just connects to the PC with a USB dongle of a sort.  Using it the way I do, I can use any software synths and sound samplers I have on my PC, so I can produce tens of thousands of different sounds, and it really stimulates creative practicing.  Given the variety of experiences people have reported, it's hard to insist that you would do fine, but I really enjoy working with it a lot.

There is no screen, so you will have to learn the buttons and their alternate shift function. Thankfully, Arturia put has an excellent user manual that will aid you in this. title: Great for home recording!
comment:
My first multitrack recorder was a Fostex cassette based recorder back in the early 90's. I loved it - used it nearly daily to work out arrangements or learn new songs. When it finally quit working, I was a little heartbroken.

At that time, someone had turned me on to PC multitracking software - which was a suitable solution, until I started upgrading. I found that certain programs wouldn't work with certain operating systems, conflicts with various attached devices would cause recording errors. I felt like I spent more time troubleshooting my system than actually recording.

After much research, I settled on this unit. As a solo artist this is an excellent machine - I mostly record acoustic arrangements, with some electric guitar and drums. Although I've used this for a week, I couldn't be happier with the results...far superior to my old cassette unit, and much easier to use than software. The sound is much better than I hoped for...and the menus are easy to navigate.

The guitar effects are OK, but not great. I don't expect I'd use them at all...the sound I get when I mic my amp is far better. The vocal and drum effects, on the other hand are more useful. Overall, I expect I should be able to make very high quality demos.

You can only record two tracks at a time, but as a solo artist, that's not such a big deal. For bigger arrangements, such as with my band, I have a 14 channel Mackie mixer that I run into the unit that allows me greater flexibility.

The unit is easy to learn how to use, and the user's guide is written so that my 11 year old can figure it out. I had no trouble recording, mixing and mastering...following the instructions in the guide, I was able to successfully transfer the final mixdown of my music to the computer without any hangups.

Overall, I'm very satisfied with this unit.

title: Fender quality & 'vintage' part specifications seem to have drifted over time
comment:
Noted visible differences and quality issues in comparing these saddles with the Fender USA vintage saddles they are designed to match.

Noted differences:
1) Length of the saddle is shorter in comparision with 'vintage' USA saddles (ref 2000 Fender USA saddles from Classic Series 60's Strat)
2) Quality of the nickel finish is blemished (pock marked) - evidence that the surface of metal beneath is not smooth.
3) Stamping seems a bit substandard as if stamping tool is worn or registration of part in the tool is off. ("Fender" is non-uniform / not crisp as older parts)
4) Sides of saddles are uneven and slightly rounded in comparison with the older parts which fit together tight / flush. Could also be due to stamping process/tool.

All of this perhaps is an indication of changes in craftsmanship / quality over the past 10-15 years. No experience with Fender vintage saddle alternatives in terms of quality / value-for-money. Purchased these parts to replace a couple of saddles and find they are a mismatch to original Fender parts and substandard besides where I am reluctant to replace all 6 on the guitar.

title: Wonderful, Inexpensive Tenor
comment: I am not Jennifer, I am her 15 year old son. Alright, I've been playing ukulele since age 12, and 2 months ago I decided that I wanted a Tenor. Well, I was shopping around for a good price, and I stumbled upon this Makala. Makala is basically the budget line of Kala Ukulele. For anyone who knows guitars, it would be like Epiphone as opposed to Gibson, or Squier as opposed to Fender. This is the least expensive, true Tenor ukulele you can buy. And the best part? It's wonderful. The ONLY con I could think of is that the sound board is very thin, so if you are used to throwing your instruments around on the floor, then this is NOT the ukulele for you. However, it has so far survived 2 months of vigorous playing, without a single scratch. The finish is BEAUTIFUL in real life, it's a little darker than the picture makes you think. It's made of mahogany, which is very rare for an instrument of this price. It's relatively loud, but don't expect it to be as loud as a guitar or bass. Bottom-line, if you're looking for a Tenor Ukulele that won't break the bank, go ahead and get it. As an avid Ukulele player and collector, you have my word that you won't be let down. The only advice I can give you is that it has a high action, which can be good or bad depending on personal preference, but you can file down the nut to fix that.

title: A highly valued tuner
comment:
This is the best tuner I've used in over a decade of playing various instruments. The design is well conceived for guitar players and the results are spot on. Overall ease of tuning and ability to do so in a noisy environment are well worth the $14 I paid.

I like the ability to change the overall tuning to different standards [e.g. Baroque or NY Philharmonic] and the included metronome is also nice to have. Perhaps the most useful feature is that this unit will tune any note; guitar players can spot check a fretted tone and other musicians can tune to C or another preferred note that some guitar tuners do not have.

Mine broke where the arm connects to the back of the main unit. There is a ball and socket joint and two of the socket flanges broke. Glue and a rubber band now holds it together. I mention this so that you won't put yours in a tight exterior pocket on a gig bag or otherwise smash it into a broken state.

title: A lasting value
comment:
I have been playing clarinet for some 30 years and also teach private lessons. I've owned a Vito 7214 clarinet for 20 years.

The Vito is an excellent choice for any clarinetist looking for a plastic clarinet. Unfailingly rugged and dependable, the key work is perhaps the most durable ever made for a plastic instrument--it is not likely to ever bend or break even if not given the best of care. Any repair technician will gladly work on a Vito if it ever needs service. And, once the clarinetist has advanced beyond the beginning stages, he or she can have the spring tension and height of the keys adjusted by a technician--since the instrument's keys are so strong, the instrument will stay fine-tuned to perfection.

The Vito is no longer the very best plastic clarinet one can get--the tuning of the Vito 7214 is not as accurate as that of a Yamaha YCL-250 or Buffet B12, and the Vito also comes with a mouthpiece that is not of good quality. But, the Vito costs less than the Yamaha, is more durable than the Buffet, and the mouthpiece can be inexpensively replaced with something much better, such as the Fobes Debut.

You can buy a Vito with the confidence of knowing that unless it gets stolen or run over by a school bus, you'll never need to buy another plastic clarinet.

title: Great company - Trust this product !
comment:
I'm actually here to review the COMPANY.
I wanted to tweak the tuning on my high E and B strings i.e. sweetened tunings.
I wasn't sure if the UberTuner could do that, so I e-mailed the company, explaining that I wanted to buy in the next few hours if the item would work for my purposes. I got an awesome answer in that time frame from KLIQ. These folks are great, and this goes a long way towards establishing credibility. I have not doubt this thing really is within 1 cent +-, and highly recommend the product if it works for you.

Here's their reply to my e-,mail:

Hi Phil,

Thanks for writing. Unfortunately, I don't believe our UberTuner would work for "sweetened tunings." The Hz on the display refers to the A4 reference pitch calibration which is adjustable from 430-450Hz in 1 Hz increments. This does not display the Hz value of any note detected. The bars below and above the center 'in-tune' bar are about 4.12Hz each.

I hope this helps you make a more informed purchasing decision. Let me know if you have any other questions.

Best,

Laura, KLIQ Music Gear

title: Just buy it!
comment:
[[VIDEOID:5da6b85a070c1745eee9712fa07a4630]] I haven’t picked up a flute since I was in marching band over ten years ago and this was the perfect way to get back into playing. The quality is amazing for being a plastic instrument. It feels very sturdy but it’s lightweight enough that I can hold it comfortably for hours. Which is good because you won’t want to put it down! The case is very nice and the little accessories work very well. The extra lip plate works very well not just for when you’re too lazy to have good posture but it helps when you want to quickly practice some fingerings without trying to make too much sound.
The only con is they don’t explain how to remove the key cap extensions so be very careful. The A & G caps simply slide to the left and detach, DONT try to snap them off. And the G# extension has a little notch that locks it in place, simply lift the little arm on it and it’ll slide off. I tried to include a picture to show what I mean. Also it would be nice if they had included some extra black silicone caps because once you take off the extensions there’s nothing to cover the keys with.
Overall I’m in love with this flute and would recommend it to everyone.
P.S I also bought the upgrade kit which is why I showed the second lip plate on the straight head joint. Buying the kit saved me money vs buying the student flute upfront, so it’s like I got two instruments for less 👍🏼

title: VocoPro you've let me down
comment:
I have the older 3300 unit. It's been a work horse; for a cheaper set of mics they sound pretty good, for karaoke they work just fine. The MHz range those fall under are now not supposed to be used so I thought I'd go for the upgraded 3308 unit. I will say I love the option of having a place to put them and to charge them up at the same time. Very cool.

I bought the VocoPro 3308 Dual Channel VHF Wireless Microphone Set Regular Channel 4 unit. I charged up the mics using the supplied AA batteries. I set them up in my system and man, what a huge disappointed. I set up the Mackie mixer with the exact same settings as I use for the 3300 unit and boy oh boy, what a muddy and distorted sound came out. I had to roll off a lot of bass and ramp up the mid and treble to get anywhere close to the "clarity" of the older 3300 unit.

Granted, these aren't pro mics but again, for karaoke and as my drunk mics (people who can sing and care for my equipment get to use my corded SM58's); this "upgrade" sucks big time.

I'm bring them back and I'll look for a UHF unit (bleeding from the local water company radios came through this unit, even though it's not on the old MHz range?) that I hope will have either a recharge station of the AA battery option which last a very long time..

If you're looking for a set of rechargeable and cost effective mics, look elsewhere. Vocopro, you've let me down.

title: Good Entry-Level, Name-Brand Shotgun Microphone
comment:
Rode is an industry-standard with filmmakers, especially social media influencers. This mic is the entry-level shotgun mic, with the VideoMic Pro being the "pro-sumer" version. This one is unpowered, drawing needed power from the 3.5mm plug in your device. There are no frills on this, it's just a directional, front-facing microphone. Because of the design and shape, it doesn't pick up sounds from the sides, only what's directly in front of it. These microphones are great interview mics, as their range is fairly limited. The included foam pop filter does a decent enough job at blocking wind noise, but if you are in a very windy location, you're going to want a 'dead cat' filter, which literally looks like fur.

Overall, Rode mics are a bit pricey, but the quality is top-notch. You can definitely find cheaper mics out there, but the quality is hard to match. You won't be disappointed in the results from this microphone.

title: Easy to install and very secure
comment:
I didnt even have to drill out the existing holes in my Fender or Gibson guitars. These screwed in easily, although I did check that first before cranking on them..

The one thing to know is you are attaching these semi permanently to your strap. So...You can mount these on 4 or 5 guitars like I did and only attach the strap halves to your SINGLE favorite strap if you are like that. OR, attach these to your 4 or 5 different straps, as I did. and you can quickly with the press of your thumb, pop a strap off and pop a new one on or popit on a different guitar, and not worry about the guitar dropping.

This happened to me with my Alvarez Acoustic at a gig once. I caught the neck before it hid the ground hard but it did smack the ground. The guitar was built tough and there was no damage that I could ever tell and finished playing that night. Guitar is still fine years later..But It made me think to get something like this. You wear out the slots / holes your favorite strap putting it off and on and dont realize it.

title: Good for XP, NOT useless for Vista
comment:
In XP, this thing was great. I upgraded to Vista, and now the outputs have an unacceptable pop and crackle. The Line6 forums are all abuzz about this problem, and there is no solution yet.
<edit> I heartily apologize. There was no solution at Line6, but I eventually ran across a fix. Unsurprisingly, Vista is to blame, specifically the power profiles on Vista. The fault lay in the processor power-state transitions. This works ok on Vista on a laptop if you select the "Always On" power state. (Actually, there's an occasional pop even then; if you disable power state transitions in your BIOS, I think you can get rid of them.)

My apologies, and I revise my rating to four stars.

title: SO NICE
comment:
I am so much more impressed than I expected to be with this amp. I've been through so, so many amps trying to get what this amp dishes out. I have one of the earlier models that is actually made in England, and let me tell you, this amp is worth every dime.

The thing I love about it is that it sounds good at EVERY volume level. There's a 'sweet spot' on many, many amps. Some only sound good up to a certain volume, or only below it. This amp sounds good everywhere; I can't find a bad sound on it. Turn the bass up and you get a great jazz tone. Dial it back, crank up the mids and treble a bit (my fave setting) and you get some really, REALLY awesome rock sounds.

I love the clean tone. I put all Tung Sol tubes in it, and it's got this really smooth but chimey clean tone that hits the spot. The distortion, though, is killer. It's kicked every high gain drive pedal off my board when I'm using this amp, because NONE of them sound as good. That drive channel is in another world, folks.

Also, please don't make the mistake of replacing this speaker. This is the high end, made in England Celestion Greenback. Those are hard to find, and they're not cheap. Once it starts breaking in, you'll have a hard time finding anything that fits this amp better.

Just a great, great sounding amp. I should also add that Laney's customer service is the best I have ever encountered, with any company. They've sent me replacement parts for this amp without even charging me shipping, all the way from the UK (I live in Oklahoma!), and the best part? I bought it used. I even told the Laney service folks that. I specified that the amp wasn't under warranty, and that I bought it used. When the handle started falling apart, they just mailed me a new one. When I broke the bracket holding in the EL84, they just sent me a new one. Incredible. Check them out, folks. You won't be disappointed!

title: No Vibration causes no sound
comment:
I am always game to try out a new reed product. These were bad though. The first reed I tried I could not get any sound out of my clarinet. I put one of my tried and true reeds on my horn, because without any sound coming out, no vibration, no air flow, I at first thought there was something wrong with my clarinet.
With the brand name reed I was able to play my clarinet, so I tried another of these reeds with the same result. No air flow, no vibration, and no sound.

I have not tried all 10 reeds in this pack, so if any of them work, I do not know, but it is not worth the money if some work, and most don't. If you are seriously looking for a quality reed, for beginners look into the Vandoreen reeds, or a Rico for a cheaper option. In Reed quality Cheaper is not better. Buy a tried and true brand especially for a beginner. It is important to get that sound, and muscle memory of what a good reed will feel, and sound like.

title: Great and easy to use - w/one downside...
comment:
I can't say that the mic itself is great, but it's not bad either. It's fine quality for basic speaking voice use, though you may find you want to eq the resulting recording...as one would with almost any lavalier mic.

Where it shines, though, is the ease of set-up. I was frankly kind of amazed at how simple it was. I just plugged the receiver into my phone, turned on the mic, and it was instantly recognized and ready for use. And the receiver even has a female port so you can still keep your device plugged in.

One small downside in the design is that when it's plugged in, you now have this solid thing protruding out of your charging port, and that makes me nervous that I may knock it and damage the port. Or the receiver itself. But if you're very careful, you'll probably be fine. This model is for usb-c, but there is an adapter included to be able to use it with a lightening port device as well -- but that does make the protrusion from the device even more bulky and possible to bump and damage something.
Just a word of caution.

Pretty solid item. If you have a need for it, this is a good option.

title: Works as well as other foam panels
comment:
If you've never used foam panels before they do work but don't expect miracles. Nothing wrong with these, they work as well as all the rest of them do.

Now when you get the panels they are vacuum packed so all 12 together are about 5/8ths of an inch thick total. I tossed them into the dryer at medium-high heat for 13 minutes and they all fully expanded. I was worried that medium-high might have been too much but it worked just fine for me. The other method is to use a hair dryer and manually go over each one to expand them out.

The easiest way to mount them is with small tabs of blue putty like they use in the classroom or the command sticks sticky tape.

title: High Quality and Great Value--Buy These Headphones!
comment: I purchased these headphones solely on the basis of product information available on the Audio-Technica website, the reviews I read all over the web, and my experience with Audio-Technica ATH-CKS55BK Solid Bass earbuds, which are also a great value.  Did not sample the ATH-PRO700MK2 in person and in advance, which is unusual for me.  I have been very pleasantly surprised.  These are solidly made, with an impressive frequency range coming from the 53mm drivers.  Some have said that these headphones are designed for professional DJs, they are skewed in favor of more powerful bass, as opposed to the ATH-MH50, but I don't find that to be true. What you get is a powerfully clear, full-throated smooth sound with crisp, clear punches, great for any type of music.  They have way more finesse than the Beats, and can handle any power load you give them without distorting.  Some strongly recommended purchasing the replacement ear cushions that are made for the ATH-MH50s, and I did.  There's an improvement to comfort, but not enough to make a tremendous difference.  Buy the replacement cushions to have on hand when the originals get too worn, but don't necessarily rush to do that.  All over-the-ear headphones can give you some level of ear fatigue with extended use, without exception.  Because they are solidly built, come with coiled and straight screw-in cords, have a handy pouch (that could use a bit less of the in-your-face pleather experience), but most of all, because they sound and feel like cans costing so much more, they are a great value.  If you like to listen to every nuance in your music of all kinds, I highly recommend them.

title: Documentation is, so far, utterly inadequate.
comment:
The best parts are the sounds and loops.

The worst part is that the manual skips important details about how to actually use all of the buttons. There is nothing quick or intuitive about the quick start, and the main manual is on a disk and most modern lap tops don't have a drive for that. Here's a key excerpt I found online that should have been included in the quick start guide:

Playing back a loop phrase
1. Hold down the BANK/MODE button and press button 4 to enter Global mode.  
2. Press the BANK/MODE button (several times). The display will indicate Co (Common).  
3. Press button 4. The loop phrase will sound. Each time you press the button, the status will alternate between start (PLy) and stop (oFF).  
4. To adjust the volume, turn the VALUE knob when button 4 selected.  
5. To switch the loop phrase, press button 3 to make the display indicate L o P, and turn the VALUE knob (See Voice Name List).  
6. If you want to use a different program, hold down the BANK/MODE button and press button 1 to enter Live mode, and then switch the program. To stop playback, hold down the BANK/MODE button and press but- ton 4 to enter Global mode, then press button 4 in the C o (Common) page.  
note: You can’t change the playback speed of the loop phrase.

title: Nice for what it really is...
comment:
I know snob folks are not gonna agree with me. Of course there are way better quality parts p bass bodies out there. But for what it is, and for what I wanted it to be – a lightweight body for my Frankenstein P bass- I am so proud, happy and satisfied with it. My tech is pretty good at it, so he took care of all the little blemishes on the body, treated, sealed, coated and painted it, resulting in the pictures you see here. Relative lightweight, nice contours, it had the ground wire drilling already, so my tech was happy he didn’t have to go through that himself. He did a pretty good job. Bare in mind that it’s basswood, so yeah…if you hate to see dings and scratches on your bass (and they will show up eventually, make no mistake), this is not for you.

However, for my purposes, I now have the p bass I always wanted: lightweight, maple J bass neck and fretboard, high mass Fender bridge, Dimarzio Model P pickups, CTS electronics and Switchcraft jack. It doesn't hurt if it takes a little beating, it already has some on, but that’s to be expected.

Plays nice, feels nice, weights nice. Really happy with it.

title: Works too well.
comment:
I had a hunch this would happen but for the price, figured I'd give it a try. If you have an amp that is bottom-heavy or just heavy, this thing is great. I have a Peavey Classic 30, the 40-pound amp they use as an illustration, and for that, this little wedge does the trick. But my Roland Blues Cube Hot, despite being similar in size, is much lighter than the Peavey and a little top-heavy.

You can see where this is going.... Unfortunately, the Blues Cube, which needs the tilt more than the Peavey, just falls over backwards. It is possible to do a balancing act, and I'll try this at home, but no way could I trust this on a stage. You can see why Leo put tilt-back legs on his amps.

Ironically, the Roland stand, which isn't that expensive, easiy handles the Peavey but is big and awkward for the Blues Cube. Now, if someone would make a pair of collapsible wedges--a pitcher and a catcher.

title: Karaoke Anyone?

The result of 99% of the tracks you'll try will be tinny and sound dynamically empty. About the only thing that works well are light guitar singer-songwriter tracks that aren't too complex.

But hey, if you've got a hip party happening this weekend, and you got some killer tracks you wish you could sing karaoke to with all your buddies, try this out. Maybe with enough drinks, you won't notice the loss in quality.

I had always used a tuning fork, and after being away from music for awhile, technology marched on, and when I woke up, this gadget had arrived.  It's simple to use, and you just clip it to the headstock, and rotate the little screen so you can see it (to be fair, sometimes you have to re-clip it, so you can point the screen your way - which can be annoying) and then just pluck until the name of the note (ex. title: I'd pretty much given up on wireless systems
comment: I've played two gigs with the U2 so far and all is well. It works exactly as described. The price is more than reasonable. I'd pretty much given up on wireless systems. Too much hassle, pain to set up the receiver, battery changing in the middle of the gig. This system is actually easier than using a cord. There doesn't seem to be any "wireless compression". Its very convenient for me as I switch guitars often and can simply switch the button to turn off the transmitter before making a change. No more loud popping sounds or having to go back to the amp to hit the standby switch. My only concerns are the durability of the switches. Lets see how long they last. I would also prefer a more robust carrying case, the cardboard box the U2 ships with isn't going to last very long.  I suppose that would make them more expensive. To sum up: I've very pleased so far.

title: Very nice guitar, and Gig bag
comment:
The guitar is a nicely made guitar. Good quality, I had issues with the bridge position at first, but I tapped on the pickup with a metal item (just lightly) and it came to life. I also had issues with the tone, but it turned out to be the amp I was using. I tried a different amplifier and it was just fine. I consider this guitar to be about equal in quality to a Squier. title: Very Versatile
comment: Am using this for many different instruments with happy results. Not much of a guitar player; I have one and yes, this pedal does many things with it, love the lap steel, wah wah and rockabilly modules, among many others. But I also have a piezo pick-up and with that I can run my ukulele, ocarina and kalimba through it (Eddie Van Halen on the kalimba is pretty wicked). Also use my Yamaha P-85 with it. This is a pretty nice digital piano with a fatal flaw: only 10 voices, now it can make about a thousand of them (and you can tweak the sounds, unlike the native effects on the keyboard). Saw somewhere on a forum about this device a person saying that you can't effectively run a microphone through it. Incorrect. It'll do crazy things with your voice or anything else you send through via the mic. Can make my voice sound like Barry White, Dr. Zihn, Darth Vadar, Dino the dinosaur and many others. Got good results with all instruments that I used with the mic, but was really blown away by how the harmonicas sound. Never have been satisfied with my harmonica, mic and amp set-up (never was nearly loud or "dirty" enough). Sure is loud and dirty now, plus just the right amount of reverb. Or I can make it sound like space aliens. Or whatever. Not quite as effective on percussion, not all of the patches work on them (bongos, congas, bongo / cajon and darbuka don't seem to do as well on it, although some patches make 'em sound pretty wild (like wah wah); cowbell, guiro, xylophone, glockenspiel, triangles, woodblocks, washboard, claves, maracas sound pretty interesting depending on the module used). Only instruments I don't use on it are the didgeridoos (what's the point). I like to take the pedal (with batteries) with me along with a mini corded mike and my Bose bluetooth speaker, now I can play amplified and distorted harmonicas (or whatever) whenever I want. It can be used with 1/4" cable as a headphone output, and you can either get an adapter for 3.5mm or rca plugs for that, meaning you don't necessarily have to use an amp, which is good because I have the bluetooth and also a home stereo that can break all the windows in the neighborhood but only a $40 Gorilla amp. Also like the drum machine, most of the patterns are fairly basic (we let the drum machine dude take the lead sometimes when several of us are playing drums). It was a bit confusing at first trying to save patches but I figured that and some other minor mysteries out before very long. This is what I know so far about this pedal and I've only had it a short time.

title: Overall Poor Quality
comment:
I'm a guitarist who doesn't play percussion or drums, but I have owned bongos in the past to work on reading and counting rhythm patterns without the temptation to play songs I already know on my guitar. After I viewed a few bongos on Amazon, they sent me a link to these on a flash sale and I bought them on impulse. Unfortunately, these were too rough even for a non-percussionist. Along with the overall poor build quality and workmanship, the set I received was missing a couple washers on the tension lugs, which made tuning them impossible. On top of that, my dogs were unusually interested in the bongos--enough that I had to put them in a closet while not playing them, because even if I put them on a high shelf, the dogs would try to get at them. It wasn't until I was sitting down playing them and one of the dogs grabbed the edge of the head that sits outside the rim and tried to pull the bongos away from me that I realized instead of being made from goat or kangaroo skin like my previous bongos, the heads of these ones appear to be made from pet-store grade rawhide. This makes me wonder if I would have been able to get a decent tone out of them even if the tuning lugs did work. Since I didn't plan on performing with them, none of that was a big deal, but after a week of playing, I started to get bruises on the side of my thumb and palm from where the metal rim sticks up higher than on any of my older bongos. This may not be a problem for someone who gets one with functional tuning lugs. I'll most likely return these and get a nicer pair.

I won't go so far as to rate it one star because they were so cheap, I can't really complain. Also, other people complained about their bongos arrived broken due to poor packing, but mine arrived well packed and in original condition.

title: Full time Karaoke/DG Service
comment:
I purchased this sub for my companies personnel that couldn't lift our larger 18'' subs.
I ran this sub for 8 shows, 4hrs non stop....all consecutive days ----Sun thru Sunday.
I bought this sub basically to see what it could do, and for smaller venues its great!
The price point was worth the risk to see if a 12'' sub would work before buying a
name brand..... It does and IT does.... Theres no reason to purchase another sub.
Most reviews on here complained about low volume.....and thats because they don't
know how to work this equipment.....You need a preamp, bottom line....and thats for ANY
powered speaker without a mic input....This is just a LINE input which requires a preamp.
If you don't know the difference then you don't need this, you need to read more about why
you THINK you need this.
Now, its not light weight and is closer to 60lbs that the marked 45lbs...... But my personnel didnt have a weight issue, it was a handle vs arm length issue.... So the weight isnt really an issue. The knobs feel a little iffy, but 200 bucks that is expected....Sound wise, its a 12'' speaker, so it will not reproduce super low lows, but better yet it can mix with the lowest frequency of a 12'' top for a fluid sound so you dont push your tops hard to get lowend. Volume, this is loud, I cant get it to clip ... so I havent even pushed it as hard as it can go....overall for a cheap sub you cant go wrong..... just know you cant use it as a main speaker, its a sub and only has low frequencies, and know you need a preamp ie mixing board....once again, if you dont know what that is or how to use it, you dont need this!

title: good guitar, perfect for beginners
comment:
I'm an amateur musician and play guitar and bass in a few bands, and I value performance over price and name on the headstock so I decided to check these guitars since they had so many good reviews and seem to always be out of stock. Also, I know this guitar is popular with enthusiasts as a base for modifications. While it's a hobby I partake in (I even build my own guitars from scratch), I will focus on what you get out of the box, e.g. for a first-time player, not someone that already knows a ton about guitars.
I had checked out a few Internet reviews from about a year ago, so I'll post about the differences I noticed between my guitar and some of those reviews. Also I own many guitars, including an Epiphone Dot, which is at least twice the cost but is a closer (license/authorized) version of the original Gibson ES335, so I will also compare the Dot with the Firefly since they are both entry-level semi-hollows

The guitar came loosely packed in a double the shipping box, the neck only supported by a small piece of styrofoam, and a thin wrap around the body. that's pretty barebone packaging so check yours and make sure it was no damage in transit.
My guitar came with a really cheap cable, not the really nice braided cable it seems they used to come from back in the day. Also, no strap is provided (as mentioned in the Amazon listing, but I know they used to provide them), the black and white one you see in some of the photos was one I had lying around.

The first impression out of the box was great, it's got the classic "cherry" red color (it comes in many colors) and you get a nice wood pattern below the color. Whether that's a veneer, is faked, or is the actual wood the top is made from, it looks very nice. The binding looks great and the fretboard is bound (has the white stuff running along the edges) which is a cosmetic only feature but is usually found on more expensive guitars. The pickguard is pretty plain. The pickups and pickguard come covered with sticky plastic, take your time removing it completely.

Craftmanship is quite good especially at this price range. If you look very carefully at the photo showing a closeup where the neck attaches to the body you see how the binding (the white and black lines) appear slightly "smudged" and how there is a little red paint in the very corner by the neck. In the photo showing a closeup of the pickup, you can see how the white binding at the front of the neck has some scratch marks between the two lowest strings. The close-up of the neck shows how the inlays (the white shiny inset trapezoids for decorations) were routed wider than the inlay then filled with a wood filler that matches the fretboard color. That's about all there is that's bad about the craftmanship! You really have to look to find those and can't see them from a few feet away. I have guitars that I paid 4x the price that have worse craftmanship than that. Just be aware that you are not getting true perfection.

Now for the good parts: the fret ends are well dressed, meaning that when you run your hand along the neck you don't feel anything sharp that could cut or hurt your hand. The fret are also decently polished and don't feel "gritty" when you bend the strings against them (I've seen old reviews complaining about that, so it seems they have improved it). The action (how high the strings re off the fretboard) is pretty good and there is no string buzzing while playing it. In a word, the guitar came well adjusted ("set up") and is ready to play after you tune it of course. No need to take it to a shop for a professional setup as is sometimes the case for cheap guitars.
The tuning machines (the pegs at the head) feel surprisingly smooth for a guitar that price. they don't seem to slip and while I know many tinkerers will decide to replace them, those are actually just fine. I didn't care for the strings the guitar came with, they look like size 10 (aka 10-46) and they're fine, but I like my usual brand better, mostly personal preference. For people new to guitars, please note new strings need to get stretched before they stay in tune for more than a few minutes, so retune often for the first day, or look up videos about stretching guitar strings.

The guitar sounds great, I am not well set up for recording and plenty of people have provided recordings, but you get the classic semi-hollow "woody" tone. This guitar is not as hot (pickups as loud) as my Dot, just adjust your amp, and sounds brighter (more treble) although it could be due to the strings. Overall the guitar sounds great and there is no need to replace the pickups, unless you feel the need for tinkering. Some reviews say they are microphonic, which means the guitar will howl with feedback when played to close tho the amp. I did not experience that, so again maybe something they improved over time, unless I got lucky :)

One last thing: when reading "full-size hollow body" mind when to "Gibson 335/Epiphone dot shape", but it's not the exact same shape. See the side-by-side picture with my Epiphone Dot Royale, the Firefly is a smidge smaller, and the horns (the top part of the body) have a slightly different shape. Again more a matter of personal preference, however, it means that the firefly doesn't quite fit well in my dot case: you can see there is too much space around the body because it's smaller. So you'd want to stuff rags in the case if you used a 335 sized case. If you want to purchase a case, you should grab one that is designed for the Ibanez AM73 which is the same size as the firefly. Also the firefly is quite lighter than my Dot. The size itself is not enough to make the difference so it must be related to the materials used. Speaking of materials, I keep reading that the fretboard is made of rosewood but the description on amazon only says "wood". It looks a lot like rosewood I think it's "fake" rosewood, just designed to look like it, and they did a good job, but it doesn't quite look like real rosewood to me, see what you think from the closeup of the neck. At that price range, not surprising and makes no difference in the guitar playability. The fretboard felt quite dry when I received it (when I took the pictures), so I'd recommend oiling it, either with lemon oil or fancy fretbaord oil when you change the strings for the first time.

Hope you found this review useful, I enjoyed making it. It was too hard to show the details in a video, so I decided to only post pics.

title: An Amazing Tele
comment:
I have a handful of guitars in my arsenal and this is one of my absolute favorites . I bought mine with a rosewood fretboard and it has the best feel and playability of any electric guitar I've played. The body is solid alder, finished with a beautiful "cherryburst" finish. I can sometimes look at my acoustic guitars and they will go out of tune, but this guitar will stay tuned for days, even with me bending notes and finger plucking the strings. It has a pretty pearloid pickguard which complements the sweet color of the alder body. The samarian cobalt pickups are as quiet as a church mouse when the guitar is idle but cry and sing when the Tele is being played. One of the really cool things about this guitar is the "S-1 switch", which is a button on the volume knob. When the pickup selector is in the middle (activating the bridge and the neck pickups) and the "S-1 switch" button is pushed down, it changes the pickup circuit from parallel to series, resulting in a fat, Les Paul-like sound. The back of the body is "scalloped" so it is perfect to play either sitting down or standing up, which makes it a pleasure to use in both the studio and when playing live. One of the nicest aspects of this guitar is the neck. I have an American Standard Tele and it has a "clubby" neck. This guitar has a slimmer, tapered neck which makes fretting and moving up and down the neck a complete joy. It's hard to put this guitar down!

Made in the U.S.A. at the Fender factory in Corona, CA. It is a lifetime instrument, something you'll be able to pass on to future generations.

title: Boom arm fell apart one first use, mic is ok but not great
comment:
I ordered this more to get the boom arm than anything else. But I also wanted another mic to leave at the office so I wouldn’t have to carry mine back and forth every week. One less piece of stuff to pack into the backpack which is already rather heavy. I like the tripod as well. It makes the kit much more versatile.

The arm was easy to assemble, and I had no trouble getting it together and mounted to my desk using the arm, shock mount, mic, and pop filter. It was easy to adjust into the correct position for recording. The mic has a mute button and gain knob.

It attached to my laptop easily using the included cable and Win10 had no difficulty recognizing it. I did have to adjust the settings manually to choose to use this mic.

Everything is lightweight. That’s a plus for lugging it around. However, it is rather cheap. That’s the bad part for lugging it around. I’m afraid I might break something. In fact, the arm fell apart while I was putting it away after using it. As I folded it up to place it back in the box, one of the bolts fell out. It was easy enough to put it back in, but I couldn’t find the nut to screw on to hold it in place. I spent several minutes looking around the floor for it and now wonder if it was ever there to begin with. That makes the arm unusable until I can get a nut for it.

The sound quality of the mic is ok. Given the price of this kit, I wouldn’t expect better. For some of my purposes it is sufficient, but it is no match for my Blue Yeti. When I really need good sound, that’s what I will use.

For what you get, it is a decent value. But you must understand that you are not getting top quality equipment. If it hadn’t fallen apart, I would give it three stars, but it did so it only gets two.

title: What you need to know before you buy this
comment:
This is a pretty good product, but you should know what you are getting before you buy it. This is an audio product. Once upon a time you would go to the store and listen to it, along with many others, then choose. That's not possible with online purchases. So let me give you my opinion on the VS-6633. First the good news:
Lightweight, portable, great remote functionality (however factory EQ settings Rock/Pop/ etc are only on the remote, so don't lose it!). It's LOUD. It really rocks. The 2 wireless mics work really well. It sounds like it has a noise gate, so when you sing/talk you hear a slight noise (I mean it's very slight), but when you stop talking you can hear it mute and the amp is then silent. That's a nice touch.
The Karaoke function is fantastic! It has 2 modes, both of which cuts the music vocals nicely. Mode 1 stays in vocal cut mode; Mode 2 is voice activated, so when you sing it mutes the music vocals but when you stop the music vocals come back on. Again, nice touch.
It runs a long time on batter charge. I didn't time it, but it'll go all evening and then some.
The Bluetooth connects pretty easily. it's not 5.0 so not HD, but sounds pretty good. The Bluetooth level is strong, so it drives the amp nicely.
The SD and USB drives also drive the amp nicely. Just pop one in, it'll take over as source and start playing. However, I could not find any way to play random from the unit, you have to do that from the remote.
The Aux input jack does not drive the amp as hard as Bluetooth/SD/USB, so that's a bummer. You have to turn up the volume to compensate (or use a source audio with strong line output).
It advertises that you can record audio from Aux input to the SD or USB cards. Well it does, but it's weird. When you start recording the unit goes to a specific volume and stays there during recording (volume does not respond during recording). And... the recording when played back is a little choppy at times, almost like it skips. I wouldn't rely on this unit as a field recorder.
OK here's my issue with the unit... it's the sound, the tone. Yes it's loud as hell, especially for it's size it really cranks. But to me the audio sounds a bit harsh. Not distorted, harsh. I spent hours listening to it, with known music sources. It's uncomfortable to listen to it for very long. I don't consider myself an audiophile, although I do appreciate amps that sound creamy and smooth. Well this one doesn't. But it's subtle.
So if you are not overly critical of sound quality then this thing is for you. For it's features and price point it's great. But I took away 2 stars from 5 and give it 3 due solely to it's less then great sound. Also the bass could be better; even all the way up the bass is not as hard hitting as I would have expected.

title: Does the job nicely
comment:
I bought this because I found that while doing mixing (of recordings) that sometimes, in the quietest passages, the sound card in my laptop would create some spurious noise - so my intent here was to use it as a sound card (external) to improve the signal to noise ratio.

For the dough, it's tough to go wrong with this. I needed something with balanced outputs (which this has via its TRS outputs). I do not use this as a front end (for recording) because all of the recording that I do is usually 4 - 8 channels, and done on-location (live), so I can't say word one about how this functions as a front end.

However, once the drivers were loaded properly (easily done), it worked great both in CuBase as well the "Windows" sound card. Now, the down side is that it only supports 44.1 kHz and 48.0 kHz sample rates, but if you can live with that limitation, I think you'll find this to be a great value. A decided bonus here is the inclusion of CuBase LE5 on a separate CDROM; I know that Pro Tools is in much wider use, but if you are new to music production, I think you'll find CuBase quite capable ('been using CuBase for years now, and I have no gripes about it (for what I do)) and very, very intuitive and user-friendly. Back to the Lexicon Alpha...very good signal to noise ratio - if I hear any noise from it, it's in the source material, not a consequence of the unit proper.

Now back to Windows apps...whether I am using the Windows Media Player, or VLC, or other apps that are used to play audio / video, the audio functions perfectly. It's USB-powered, so you needn't worry about having to use a wall-wart, or deal with 60 Hz hum that might otherwise be present (as all USB devices are DC-powered). My only real caution here is that the headphone amplifier does not seem to have all that much power available (can distort at high levels), which is probably a consequence of the USB-power and (electrical) current limitations of the USB protocol. That is, it would be easier to deliver the required power to a headphone if the mains in were of a higher voltage and not necessarily starved for current. Mind you, in my case, this does not matter in my application because the device is feeding a downstream component that is designed to have adequate power to drive headphones. So, that's a potential caveat - if you use inefficient headphones, you may experience some distortion; your mileage may vary.

Again, I can't say a word about the fidelity of the card when used as an input device, because I don't use it in that fashion.

No regrets whatsoever with this one.

- Bright. The Snark looks nicer with its fancy multi-coloredness but this is easier to read and the contrast really helps you see what's going on at a glance.
- Seems quite accurate
- Top rotates and makes it easy to see wherever. It's simpler and smoother to move about compared to the Snark. This could be especially valuable if you use it on non-guitars (but I have not tried).

As others have pointed out, I am a bit wary of its durability over the long haul but it also doesn't live on the guitar. There hasn't been any signs of weakness or cracking, so only time will tell. Until then, I couldn't be happier with this little guy!

title: almost 4 years
comment:
I want to start off positive here because I am giving it 2.5 stars...
I have had this unit for just shy of 4 years now and it still works, even after bouncing around the inside of every case, gigbag, backpack, and rucksack or assault pack (yes, I am in the Army, and yes, I took this on deployment haha) I've owned! It is very solid and durable if you're not catching on!

When I first bought it, I thought it was so amazing to hear my bass in my headphones without bugging anyone else, and I could go for walks around the neighborhood having a silent jam as I went -- pretty freakin sweet!
But the tone was always an issue for me. It always sounded SO dark and muddy that it was hard to enjoy the sound of my own bass sometimes. I found one or two settings that gave a DECENT tone but you roll one control wheel the wrong way in the slightest and you lose all clarity in sound.

The "Bass" AmPlug special feature is a "fx - compression" circuit. I wasn't thrilled with it initially because it seemed to have no use whatsoever on my basses aside from blasting me with WAY too much sound no matter HOW low I turned the controls. I figured at the time that I had cheaper gear, and nothing active, so maybe that was the function issue. Nope. I own a beautiful, 1976 Gibson Ripper and a pristine, 2008 Musicman Stingray5 HH....these are my two best basses. One passive, one active (respectively). Both sound like complete garbage through this unit with the compression switch active.

In the end, I bought mine when they cost no more than $20, and it has lasted my nearly 4 years now, 3 of which have been in the Army (so mucho abuse). If you thin-out your tone and crank the highs on your bass, you can get a decent sound out of this sucker, but you really have to work for it (so do yourself a favor and right down the "sweet-spot" settings you find!). I felt it was very-well priced and an irreplacable tool for bassists everywhere. It's hard enough to find a decent-sounding stage rig without blowing your pockets up, and we all know there is next to nothing on the market for us in headphone- or mini-amps.

My advice? pick one up today and test it out. If you can get two, maybe three good tones out of it that you're happy to hear, buy it without question. The new price is...rough, but bassists always had to shell out more dough than our 6-string bretherin!

title: Nice looking, long XLR cable
comment:
We needed some longer XLR cables in the band room to go around behind the drummer for the drum mics. The shorter ones were not long enough to lie flat on the floor, and it was tripping people. 25 feet was more than I needed, but that's better than less than needed! The nylon weave makes it stand out from all the other plain black XLR cables, so it's easy to see where it leads, both ways. Functionality is normal, so far. Haven't noticed any noise or crackle. Hopefully it holds up over time!

One thing to note is that it has a larger, easier to use release button for the locking female end. Most of the time this will probably be a good thing, but it might be worth considering if it's going to be connected to a hand-held mic, because it might be easy to accidentally press the button and disconnect the cable.

title: Surprisingly good!
comment:
I haven't had time to put this thing through it's paces yet, but I have to say that right out of the box this microphone surprised me. Build quality is decent and they provide a 3.5mm to 1/4" adapter as well as a U-shaped adapter that is meant to allow you to monitor the microphone output via headphones.

The microphone did a great job of isolating a voice from the background noise of a stool rolling across wooden floors. An unscientific range test had a solid signal for 25-30yards from my laptop inside through several walls to my back deck. As I walked through the yard the signal was fairly broken up, but improved when I turned around and faced the house. Ideally this is not how you will be using this microphone so I imagine that if you maintain line of sight from the transmitter to the receiver you can probably get decent range.

The only thing that would make this complete would be if they included a windscreen. I haven't used it in the wind yet, but it did pick up some plosives while talking. I'm not sure if you can find a windscreen for a microphone this size, but if you can it would probably be worth picking one up.

title: Comparison between BX5a Deluxe & Fostex PM0.5 MKII
comment:
I have both the Fostex PM0.5 MKII and the BX5a Deluxe for a while now. While the BX5a tends to get higher review rating and is more popular, I personally prefer the PM0.5 due to its having more bass & volume. Both models cost about the same, with the PM0.5 roughly $20 cheaper.

The PM0.5 is bigger, heavier, has a heat sink in the back, with a combo XLR/TRS jack. While the BX5a has a bigger tweeter, separate XLR & TRS jacks. Construction-wise, both are well-made. If you plan to share the same monitor between 2 difference sources, then the separate input of the BX5a is more convenient (you can always get a Y-adapter to support 2 inputs on the PM0.5).

The BX5a has more clarity & boosted details, but is more on a tinny side. The PM0.5 has more boom but less treble. I guess the difference is due to the size of the tweeter and the length of the bass port (the BX5a has larger tweeter & 1 rear bass port, while the PM0.5 has 2 front bass ports). Sounds coming out of the PM0.5 resemble more of the ambience of my car & home stereo than the BX5a.

For my home-studio purpose, I like the warmer sound of the PM0.5 more. I do use them to watch movie once in a while, and the PM0.5 is more suitable as the main speakers, while the BX5a is better placed for surround speakers.

For further references, I have placed audio samples of a song I wrote & arranged (so there won't be any copyright violation issue), re-recorded with an AKG C414B condenser mic, placed at same height as the tweeter, normalized to -15dB. Hope they'll help making your choice easier (instead of having to buy both like I did :)

title: very annoying fixture, although:
comment:
1. i was able to write the fixture profiles for it on my laptop to use with an old jands hog 1000.
the people at Jands pointed me towards a link which i downloaded and used to edit the fixture profile parameters with.

2. the problem is consistency, you need to be sure to buy and actually receive the same model (wattage, parameter count, etc.) from them, otherwise your profile won't match all the units you've got. I've found that the 14/16s work better than the 16/18s for the Jands1000 once i got the perameters laid out when programmed.

that's generally the problem with using generic fixtures most of the time.
i haven't tried using them with the newer boards yet, let alone on the software laptop bundles so there's that.

tough to use but manageable if you have the time.
that's what you get for the cheap price.

3. also, the latency between fixtures is out of whack. you'll notice that their timings are off once you let them run for an amount of time (the movement speeds begin to differ slightly)

4. finally they break easy.

it's 3 stars tops but 2 is fine. 4-5 def not!

i would get them again though because they're the devil that i'm familiar with.

title: Cheap Mounting Hardware-- took an hour to make my own fasteners work.
comment:
Screw anchors were too weak to go through sheetrock and twisted off, leaving a big hole. The pegs that hold the guitar have to be bent outwards for a classic guitar-- a Martin in this case. I would not recommend this product to a friend.

Finally, the hole for the replacement screw is not a standard diameter, since the screws provided have a very narrow head and broad shank. They are actually used for decking and plywood, not wall mounting.

I wound up using hollow door anchors, and they work, but a pain to install. This took over an hour to install with many trips to the garage. I though I would wind up using a welding torch in the end (a joke, but barely). I'm good with tools, and this was a bad product.

title: Feels like a real piano, sounds great!
comment:
I did not check the dimensions on this baby, so when it arrived, I was amazed by its size. It is trulyl a massive full-size keyboard! Not only that, it FEELS like a real piano - I can almost feel the string being struck, including a little bounce! Amazing! You will probably NOT like this keyboard if you are into the standard electric keyboards that have very little tactile feedback, like a real piano. But if you are looking for the "real" deal without the gigantic associated box of strings and mechanisms, than this is an instrument you should consider.

Frankly, I don't think of Casio as a musical insttrument maker ... until now. I have owned several keyboards from Casio in the past, and they were fun to play, had great features, but I would not classify them ias "musical instruments." I think of Casio for watches, cameras, and other small electronic items, not gigantic piano keyboards!!! Bravo, Casio, you did one FANTASTIC JOB making this a very classy instrument.

Let's talk about the sound. They have included a really good sounding speaker system "in the box" that has enough volume to fill a small room. Output jacks of course are included. The pedal is small, but works very well (only one pedal, but then I seldom used the other two on my piano).

At first, I thought I was "only" getting a piano - admittedly multiple pianos, such as grand, modern, etc. But YEA! They also include strings, pipe organ, and several other organs, as well as harpsicord (and a few more I can't remember right now).

I have been too busy just playing it and enjoying the feel and sound to actually read the manual... :) I will do that, because I am not sure how to use some feathres yet, such as reverb and chorus. Most of the functions are simply pressing the function button and tapping a key on the keyboard (each function is shown above the keys for easy reference). The basic paino selections are also available via their own button.

So if you are a keyboarder (and I mean like a piano, not like a computer), you will certainly enjoy this instrument. While it is as widde as a full concert piano, it is also quite small in comparison, so it will fit in many plaes where a real piano is not possible. This gives lots of interesting possibilities for its location.

I hope I have given you a good overview of this product - you will not be disappointed! A great sound, a great feel, and a great price!

title: There appears to be some confusion.
comment:
If you are using a source microphone that is MONO, you will have some grounding issues with this adapter. It is specifically designed to work with microphones that are listed as stereo since it is wired to take stereo signals and split them to the inner and outer ring on the 1/8" end of the adapter.

If you go to the Hosatech website, you will see this particular description:

"This cable is designed to a connect a stereo microphone with a single XLR output to a mini stereo phone input. It is ideal for connecting a shotgun mic to a DV camera."

Just because you have a shotgun mic does not mean that it is a stereo microphone. And just to repeat the description in Amazon, "certain Stereo Microphones that use a single XLR as output for both Left and Right signals."

If you are using a MONO microphone, you are looking for the XVM102F. To try to help clear things up even more, notice the difference between XVM (M for MONO) and XVS (S for STEREO).

An example of some microphones that would work quite well with this particular adapter are the Nady CM-2S Stereo Condenser Microphone and the TASCAM TM-STPRO Stereo Condenser Microphone.

I have the Nady myself, and this adapter works quite well with hooking it up to a portable digital recorder as well as my wife's digital camera. It handles the stereo separation quite nicely.

Good luck!

title: it's difficult to attain a sharp attack like you get when striking a tambourine with your hand
comment:
Decent jingle quality and volume. Appears well-made. One possible improvement would be to supply it with adjustable replaceable strap with quick release buckle or velcro rather than the elastic one, which is a little tight even on my narrow size 9 foot. It's definitely a two-handed procedure to apply it, but once on it stays in place well. Also, that would address the possible issue of strap breakage and replacement after repeated heavy use.

One other observation about the sound, and this is not a fault; it's just due to the very nature of it's design: it's difficult to attain a sharp attack like you get when striking a tambourine with your hand, a stick, or a foot tap (when resting your foot on top of one on the floor). The LP188 (and probably any foot tambourine) jingle is initiated with foot motion before impact with the floor, so it can sound a bit blurred and delayed. Tapping your foot harder will mask this effect somewhat, but might lead to fatigue if you're not used to doing that. Another trick I've found helpful for this is to strap the tambourine up higher onto the midfoot rather than the forefoot. There is a little less motion there so the jingles will be less sensitive until impact. Also they will respond almost as well to heel tap as well as forefoot tap, giving you the option of heel up and heel down playing, thereby lessening fatigue.
Overall, a very handy inexpensive instrument, especially for cajon players.

title: Better design for smaller kids...
comment:
I had a four-barrel wood whistle as a youngster, and now that our 2½ year old is into trains, I've been eyeballing a wooden whistle for him. The problem was the standard "square" style whistles I was accustom to appeared to be too big for his mouth, not to mention excessively large and potentially breakable where the mouthpiece is carved away from the "block" of the whistle.

So finding this was a happy occasion.

It's certainly smaller than the wooden train whistles I'm familiar with. Its wedge-like rectangular mouth piece is still a bit large for our son's mouth, but he struggles to make it fit. The blowing part he doesn't entirely grasp -- sometimes, he tries to whistle into it, though he's gradually getting better with it.

From an annoyance standpoint, it's not so loud. Quieter, I'm pretty sure, than the traditional square-type whistles, though it's hard to gauge with a 2-year old's limited lung capacity. But this whistle also lacks some of the deep tonal qualities I'm accustom to: it's a bit shrill and therefore lacking that real train sound. It's a compromise, I'm sure, between small size and sound quality. And you know what? My son doesn't seem to notice nor care.

For a younger child, this is an appropriate whistle. For an older child, I suggest looking to a traditional square style, unless pocketability is of primary importance.

title: Works Great...Except When It Doesn't
comment:
I'm a professional musician and play a multitude of instruments. I have used my XVive U2 units for both electric guitar and keyboards ("keytar" style) in a variety of venues. Here are my insights.

They're easy to set up. Just plug one into your guitar and the other into your amp or pedal board. They sound great. They're easy to charge and last through a gig (if you turn them off during break). When they work, they're terrific. I love using them at home in my studio. It makes it really easy to pick up any of my guitars or other instruments and jam.

Having said all that...I have completely given up trying to use them for live performance. Why? First and foremost, they're way too unreliable. If you stay really close to your amp, they'll probably work okay. But then what's the point of going wireless? I've done gigs with them where they worked fine and I had plenty of range. But in some venues, they tend to crackle and break up. Changing channels doesn't help. I simply can't take the chance of plugging them in and having them not work, then having to switch to cable while the audience waits. Their performance is unacceptable for a serious working musician.

The other reason I can't use them live is because the instant I turn them on, my iPhone loses connection to our router. That means I can no longer remotely control my monitor mix as long as they're on. It's crazy. I've tried to work around it, but it's a hassle. Pair that with their inherent unreliability and it's simply not worth it. So now they stay at home.

I genuinely wanted to love these puppies. When they work, they really work great. But when they don't, they don't. I can't take the chance on stage in front of an audience. So unfortunately, I can't give them more than three stars. Whether they're useful to you or not depends upon your particular situation. They're fine for home use, or casual jamming. But there's nothing worse than getting on stage and having your axe sound like crud because your wireless units keep crackling and breaking up.

title: extremely surprised
comment:
I ordered this as a stop gap for my small dedicated home studio for some work I needed to get done before a better console showed up. I was very surprised by the performance of this USB interface. I have been using firewire for the Iast 5 years.
I had just built a new computer that could not even be bothered to deal with my old interface. this one lets me record with 6ms of lag (I was amazed!). I don't even need to turn on the direct monitoring option (which was all i ever did before). While the pre-amps may not be the bees knees compared to pro strips, they are not that bad. If you are a solo home recorder; I can whole heatedly recommend this cheap ass interface. Yes, it is cheap, and it does more than what it should do.
I do however recommend that you use the money saved to purchase a nice mike preamp or an optical comp strip. Please, do use the balanced inputs on this thing. For some reason, the 1/4" input doesn't track so well without hiss (however, still better than the old tascam reel to reel). I only knock a star off because you cant record with phantom power on just one mike.....Ribon and a condenser in stereo won't work without a nice mike preamp to provide the phantom power. Other than that, this thing is great! However, i still can"t get native instruments sounds/effects to work with it..........
I'm sure I'll figure it out..

Good luck!

title: Hot, but not too hot...
comment:
This review is from: Seymour Duncan SP90-1B Guitar Pickup, Black
As a dyed in the wool P-90 lover, I was a little hesitant to go with a P-90 quite this hot. However, I am quite pleased with the voice of this SP-902b in my exceptionally bright Faded SG Special(yes, it's heavily modified with P-90's instead of humbuckers).

The good: it definately adds muscle to a guitar with a tendency to be a little too bright and anemic in the bridge position. Mississippi Queen on this pickup kills, frankly.

The bad: this pickup does not clean up as well as a vintage voiced P-90. You sacrifice some of the old P-90 high end for warmth and muscle, though it blends quite well with the Gibson P-90 in the neck for some wonderful jazzy clean tones.

Also, as a plastics injection molder, I was a little miffed to see blushing on the pickup's cover. Not a big deal for a guitar that ain't so pretty to begin with, but production flaws like that should not be on a cosmetic part like a pickup cover. Need to check up on your Plastics and Quality Control departments, Seymour...

In short, this is overdrive loving rocker's P90, not a pickup to play rockabilly on. It will never take the place of the vintage P-90 in my '50's Les Paul Junior, but it's perfect for a modern SG that needed some girth in the bridge position.

Good pickup.

title: This amp is AMAZING
comment:
I love this amp! If you can get one on sale theyre a ridiculously good value, and even a good value at full price! I highly recommend spending the extra ~$15 and getting a usb to midi cable so you can update the firmware to 2.0; it then goes from having 4 different amp model choices to 30 and you can assign 8 at a time between the 4 slots on both channels A and B. It also adds 11 reverbs for a total of 12, adds a bunch of different mic emulations for direct out and lets you choose your cabinet emulations as well.

The fender cleans are awesome, sound just like a fender tube amp to my ears, and the Park-75 is the best sounding marshall ive ever heard (park amps were made by marshall they just branded and distributed as park in certain areas. Park is jim marshall's wife's maiden name i believe, but i digress). The JCM800 absolutely rips and theres several modern high gain options on tap as well. You can assign a twin reverb; bassman, plexi, jtm-45 bluesbreaker, Jcm800, vox ac15 or 30, supro, gibson gibtone, soldano, hiwatt, bogner(of course), dr z, /13, mesa boogie, and some others. Its so sweet being able to get the exact tone i want all the time. The only models its missing really that i can think of would be Orange amps.

title: Not ready for prime time.
comment:
Probably a good deal for the price, however the XLR microphone inputs are not wired for true balanced input. The schematic enclosed with mixer shows that only pins one and two are used on the XLR connectors. As pin three is not connected, only half the signal coming in through the XLR is used. This also defeats the electrical interference canceling benefits of using a balanced line input.

Other than that, the mixer will probably perform alright for most uses. But longer mic cable runs, especially in an environment with stage lighting and dimmers, are likely to suffer from hum and buzz.

Based on reading the schematic that came with it, I never unwrapped it. So I cannot speak to the rest of this mixer's performance.

title: Big Trouble Little Uke
comment:
It's good news bad news folks. Being the eternal optimist, I'll start with the good news.
The seller gave me what they promised, a little yellow Soprano Uke with a Dolphin bridge. No complaints with these guys, fellow Amazon buyers. I'd do business with this company again in a minute. That being said, let's talk for a minute about the Uke itself.
The look of the Uke is good. The paint job is good & is there anything cuter than a Dolphin bridge?! It's also more of a school bus yellow rather than the lighter yellow pictured on the website. Also, the little black ring circling the tone hole started flaking off almost immediately. Still not a problem I can't live with.
It's when we come to playing our little Uke where we find the major problems. THIS UKE WILL NOT STAY IN TUNE FOR ANYTHING! I play guitar & bass so I know a few tricks to keep strings in tune. I used 'em all folks & none of 'em work. It's frustrating not to get thru' more than one song without having to re-tune. Keep your tuners & patience handy- you're gonna' need 'em. For a few more dollars, Makala could put better tuning pegs on this little Uke & it would be awesome. MAKALA! Are you listening?
I'll keep experimenting with my Uke. Eventually, I'll get it to stay in tune. For those of you who aren't as patient I'll leave you with this- spend a couple of extra bucks for a step up in quality. The hair you keep from pulling out maybe your own! Peace thru' music!

title: Low Price, High Noise Floor
comment: As a guitarist for 50 years, I own multiple modelers, pedalboards, and amplifiers. This pedalboard is all the rage on YouTube right now and it seemed like it could be a backup for one of my current modelers. Low price, lots of features, and physically well built! Unfortunately, right out of the box the noise floor of the unit was really high (aka: you shouldn't need to use a noise gate to hide the hum and ambient noise of a device itself, with no effects turned on yet!). I purchased this unit with the intention of using it in one of my amplifier effects loops, but at performance level the ambient noise of this device is loud! In front of an amplifier, it has the same issue! Also, the menu is relatively easy to learn, but lacks depth and offers very few options. Finally, there are limited details in the american version of the manual and this product is brand new! So, a lot of searching through online guitar forums will be required (reading Chinese would be a big help). Also, I discovered the current manual is incorrect (regarding effects loop configurations and external pedal configuration, for example). For a beginning guitarist this product is an affordable alternative, but I would suggest saving up a little more, and spending your money on an entry level Headrush, or a Line6 Product. You'll have a much better designed product with a long track record, and something you won't want to replace in 6 months.
