title: Good deal that performs well.
comment:
Works very well. Added this light bar to a lighting system we’re already using consisting of several Chauvet Tri Bars controlled by a Chauvet DMX controller. For those of you who plan to use with a DMX controller, it utilizes the exact same 3 or 15 channel protocol so if you are controlling your Chauvet 4 Bar, Tri Bar, etc. with any DMX controller, this light bar will respond exactly the same. Nothing needed except to scroll through the menu to DMX address and set the same as your others.

If you’re not using a controller, the included two-button footswitch works well. Just be aware that all light scenes and built in programs require you to advance through each one to get the scene or program you want, unlike a DMX controller where you can directly select your scenes, this basic footswitch only advances forward through each of the scenes and programs. Other button is the blackout that works properly.

As others have commented on, it is a light-duty setup, not as sturdy as the Chauvet but if you take extra care using and transporting and zipping you’ll be fine. Others have commented on the tripod not fitting well into the bag but mine slips in easily with an inch or more to spare. Also, others have commented on the shortness of the tripod stand and only being about 6 feet tall. You can keep sliding the center tube further up and achieve over 7 feet of height and still have enough stability. All in all, it works as well as I expected and if it holds up over time, is a great deal. It is less than half the cost of the TriBar and smaller and lighter weight, both the bar and tripod. Like I said, not as beefy as the Chauvet bars and stands but as long as you are aware of this it will perform similarly and you should be satisfied.

title: Great Cymbal-Poor Shipping Method
comment:
4 Stars, this cymbal 19" A Custom, is by far one of the best cymbals I have owned throughout my years of playing. The cymbal has great attack, and feel. The bell gives you a lot to play with as well. Great over Cymbal. My only complaint is the method of shipping. The cymbal was delivered in a bag only. No cardboard, no Foam around the edges to prevent any damage. The cymbal was shipped to me via USPS, it did arrive quickly and on time, however I was disappointed in the lack of protection that was given to the item. It was left on my concrete porch. I opened the package holding my breath, praying it wasn't damaged. After a very thorough inspection, I did not see any signs of damage. Removed the bar code sticker and residue, put on my set, and it sounded great.

Just to summarize, I would caution whoever ships these items, and that they should maybe wrap them in cardboard at least to protect the product and its edges. This cymbal, and many like it are usually a couple hundred dollars, and a significant investment to many. Zildjian is a high quality company, and would hate to see its name tarnshed by shotty or cheap shipping methods.

title: expensive but worth the price for the spectral retouch alone
comment: i first took notice of the iZotope Rx when i researched spectral editing [IOW using DSP to spot-edit problem sounds such as coughs and chair squeaks in live performance recordings], and noticed that it was thousands cheaper than CEDAR ReTouch, so i bit and installed it on my puter, with my standard torture tests of severely noisy recordings- in terms of clicks/crackle, i found that it was average in terms of effectiveness but that at least it left musical timbres/transients alone [at settings no higher than the medium range], a big plus. i didn't notice that much of an improvement with Rx version 2, in terms of impulse noise reduction, but there was a slight but discernable improvement in the number of clicks removed. a much cheaper product called ClickRepair was more effective at declicking and decrackling my problem files, leaving them totally click-and-crackle-free [in one pass] except for the loudest pops [where there were missing bits of vinyl or shellac, from a broken record patched together]. this is where the iZotope Rx came into the picture, making short work [via the spectral retouching feature] of those nasty loud pops. i tried several other extraneous noises with it and it removed them all seamlessly, with a bit of parameter tweaking. next was hum and broadband noise, which both ClickRepair and iZotope handled equally well, with the latter having a bit of aliasing artifacts to adjust around. a RX plus is the "whitening" feature in the denoiser which compensates for the treble dulling that high settings of NR can introduce. the declipper worked well when i could get it to work, which was not all the time- this is probably a UI quibble and not so much a functional capacity issue of the software. the EQ function was good for adjusting final mastering balances, not so much for surgical corrections. all in all, Rx gives me a few new useful additional arrows in my audio restoration quiver.

I figured that because the AMP was built into the speaker, this was expected.  After one set of the speakers failed, I researched and purchased a new set of speakers.  I contemplated purchasing another set of these speakers, however, I decided to chose another brand and I'm glad that I did.  The new speakers have significantly better sound quality.  When powered on, no hum at all.  The handles on the side and top of the unit break easily and on several of the speakers, just pulling them out of the truck, the handles snapped off.  I gave these speakers a favorable rating only because of the value.  But I would suggest spending just a few dollars more and purchase a set of speakers that will last and will have better sound quality.

title: Works, but will wear out eventually
comment:
I used these to pull out nickel frets and cut stainless steel frets.
Cutting each fret left a noticeable divot in the edge of the nipper. Not a huge deal, because stainless steel frets will do that to pretty much any nipper anyway. But just so you know.
You could get about 2 stainless steel fretboards done with one of these before they just wear out and become unusable. I'm sure it's much better for nickel frets though.

title: 1100 Fog Machine is good.
comment:
I ordered the 1050 Chauvet Fog machine, and this 1100 model came instead? I was pissed because the one I ordered looks way better with a sleek silver coating. After bitching on the phone to customer service (Parts-Express was the dealer), I just decided to keep this 1100 Model and use it.

And I must say, there's nothing wrong with it. It warmed up in about two minutes (they say it takes 5 min), and after holding down the button for 3 seconds my room was THICK with fog. The remote works fine, and has a decent sized cord (about 8 feet). The blue LED is a cool feature as it lights up the fog juice letting you see the fluid level and also lets people see the unit in the dark... hopefully keeping them from tripping.

I've ONLY used this unit for one night, so I cannot say how it preforms over the years. But as of that one night, I'll give it 5 stars.

title: Superb cable for the $$
comment:
This is the second guitar cable I've reviewed from Amazon. The first one of a different brand and length didn't fare so well, but this one is a keeper. I tested it using both a Fender Telecaster and a Gibson Les Paul Standard through an Ethos pre-amp into a Behringer mixer with headphones, and compared it directly against a 10 ft Mogami Gold cable which is part of my standard rig. Incidentally, the Mogami cable is about 4X the price of this cable. The tone with this cable compared favorably with the Mogami......very similar sound, but perhaps had a teeny bit more high end evident when playing the telecaster. Honestly, they were so close that my ears started playing tricks on me trying to identify subtle differences as I switched back and forth. The connectors are of similar high quality to the Mogami. The cable itself is slighty thinner and considerably more supple than that of the Mogami, which I like. The built in mute switching is fantastic when swapping guitars....I've never had a cable with this feature. At this price point, this instrument cable is a winner and highly recommended.

I hope you find this review helpful.

title: Another Great Korg Tuner.
comment:
I have a Korg Pitchblack tuner pedal that has always worked well for me so when I decided to buy a clip-on tuner Korg was my preference.
After reading through numerous reviews I settled on the Sledgehammer Custom 100 and as luck would have it I found a new unit for half price.
Like the other two Sledgehammer models the Custom 100 offers three meter display modes- Regular, Strobe, and Half-Strobe.
Accuracy is +/- .1 cents. That's more fine than the Pitchblack pedal which is +/- 1 cent.
Another plus for this tuner is that it runs on one AAA battery which I prefer over the button batteries like those used in wrist watches.
Use is fairly simple, turning the left hand end of the barrel counter-clockwise powers the unit on or off. Turning it clockwise changes the meter display mode. The right hand end is where you can change calibration.
It doesn't take long to get used to operating the Custom 100.
For a clip-on unit it seems to be decent for durability. Nothing rattles or feels brittle. The clip is sturdy and stays in place. It is plastic so I wouldn't want to make a habit of dropping the tuner, but I think it will last quite a while of normal use.
I like this tuner and I haven't found a con for it yet.

However, my previous set of Evah Pirazzi strings lasted less than a year for me, causing me to buy new ones. Cleaning rosin off the strings with a soft cloth even started to make my A string unravel, and the E string's little nob on the bridge broke off so I had to make quick replacement before bridge damage.

Other than that, wonderful, but I just wish they were a little more in budget and stronger when it came to lasting long.

title: Low Sound, Low Quality
comment:
I was really excited for this mic. My Trans Continental Studios microphone cover broke (still works great with black tape lol). Ordered 2 Tonor's to use with my Fendi Passport PA system for Karaoke. The mic felt good in my hands, not cheap feeling and had a good weight to it.

Set up was a breeze, add batteries, plug and play. Plugged it into my PA to test and at first I thought it sounded good, if not a bit low. But then I found that I had to turn the volume up completely to even match the same sound as my TCS mic. I put both microphone volumes at the half way point and I could barely get any sound from the Tonor whereas my TCS mic sounded great. If I spoke into the Tonor from the side angle, I would get no sound at all. Only talking directly into the top of the mic will you get any sound. That sucked!

I did test how the mic worked if pressed against other mics or the speaker and there was no feedback which was good. However, the sound was just not up to par for my preference. We love to sing and hearing the singer vocals was too important for me to accept the sound quality of this mic. So disappointed!

title: Bull.
comment:
The colors are very nice. I got another led strip from some other manufacturer, and it was color change on each individual diode. It flickered while changing color, and the "white" was very blue. This led strip the diodes are separated, so to get white, the red green and blue lights are all on at once, which actually creates a much better looking shade of white. When transitioning colors it does it very smoothly. I'm very surprised at how good this looks. The only reason I did a 4 instead of 5 star was because if plug from the IR Remote box to the LED strip is very lose. I had to find the right spot and use electrical tape to stick it in place, other wise it occasionally flickered out. But I guess you get what you pay for. For what you're getting this is very cheap.

EDIT on 2/6/13: That has stopped working. Now whenever I have it at full brightness it will flicker out about once every five minutes. Not sure if it's a faulty power supply or what. Tried it on different outlets, same issue. Lowered to two star for the time being.

EDIT on 2/11/13: Seller has addressed the issue (very quickly might I add), sent me a new power supply, going to test tonight.

EDIT on 4/02/13: That didn't work. Contacted seller, no response to this one. I actually decided to buy another kit, trying to see if it was an issue with the some other piece. I was an optimist and I payed the price. It is also flickering. I plugged it in to different sources to see if that helped. Checked the cords on both units to see if there was any fraying on the wires. I can't find anything physically wrong. Now what do I do? Does anyone make an LED strip that works right?!?!

comment:
I've been playing acoustic guitar for more than 20 years now. The same one for the vast majority of that time. I've been through a lot of strings. A lot of Phosphor Bronze strings, in fact.

Phosphor Bronze strings are something of an enigma. They can be bright and punchy, but are typically too much for my taste. I put them on the guitar, try them for a few days and then swap them out again.

These are bright and punchy without being too bright or too punchy. The guitar has all of those sweet overtones that PhosBronze are known for, but I don't feel like they are overpowering, like they usually are.

I like these a lot and I plan to keep using them. They sound great and are impossible to beat for the price.

title: Worth the cost -- but not more
comment:
These rack rails are a mixed bag. The good:

- Heavy duty steel.
- Good paint coverage
- Includes the 'in between' hole positions sometimes used by 2U-3U products.

The bad:

- Hole lineup is so-so -- probably good enough to do what you need, but not dead-on.
- Holes are filled with paint, need cleaning with a 10-32NF tap before they work.

I've bought some of the higher-priced rails before, and while the hole lineup is sometimes there, standard rack screws turn straight out of the box/bag. I'd actually give these 3.5 stars if I could, but a product like this shouldn't require the user to own a tap-and-die set (I do).

title: Impressive guitar stand on a budget!

Other than that great stand most of them are really stable and if there a little bit off they can always be bent a little for flat concrete floors otherwise on carpet they're great! In other words if you want your guitar to be on the right stand you're going to have to order the type that has a buckle at the upper neck part. But stands like this seems to do the job great another thing you need to worry about is as soon as you pull this out the box the two pads that your guitar sits on they come out and at the end there are plastic inserts that go into hold the padding immediately take this out and put glue on the inside of the hole on the stand then reinsert turn clockwise then backwards a couple of times to make sure you have a coat of glue all around then do that to the other side now your pads that your guitar sits on or stable and will never come out. But do this as soon as you get it they do not glue those in definitely not a lot of work if you got the things laying around and for the price it's definitely worth it I finally got mine

title: Great pickups
comment:
I replaced the Fender Vintage Jazz Bass pickups in my Fender Jaguar bass with DiMarzio Area J pickups in an attempt to keep the Fender sound but eliminate the noise when playing just one pickup. I am very pleased with the Area J's; they sound VERY similar to the Fender Vintage pickups but with no noise! It's hard to describe sounds but the DiMarzios are a little smoother than the Fenders. I think this is because the Area J's use alnico 2 magnets instead of the alnico 5 the Fenders use. These pickups use alnico 2 magnets, not ceramic like the specs here show. The Area J's have raised pole pieces for the A and D strings and have excellent volume balance. The pole pieces are also grounded so there is no noise when fingers touch them.

One of my favorite features of these pickups is that they allow access to the individual coils via the five wires (EA coil is Red+ Black-, DG coil is White+ Green-, and the pole piece ground is gray). I used two DPDT on-on-on switches to allow me to select series/single coil/parallel wiring on each pickup. I use the DG coil on the neck and the EA coil on the bridge for the single coil selections so I can get an inverted/expanded P-bass sound. When the coils are wired in series (the suggested method in the instructions) the pickup has more bass and midrange and less treble; when wired in parallel it has less bass and midrange and more treble. If you want to be able to switch between series and parallel wiring, use a DPDT on-on switch for each pickup and wire them like this: looking at the rear of the switch, left to right on the top row the lugs will be 1, 2, and 3 and the second row left to right will be 4, 5, and 6. Wire red to 4, white to 5, black to 6, green to 2, run a wire from 1 to 6, use lug 4 for the positive lead and lug 2 for the negative lead of the pickup. If the switch is to the left the pickup will be wired in series and if the switch is to the right it will be in parallel. Run the gray wire straight to the shielding ground.

I replaced the pickups on my Ibanez PJ bass (GSR190) with Fender Vintage P-Bass pickups and a DiMarzio Area J bridge pickup. I wired the Area J for series/parallel and it blends very nicely with the P-Bass pickup.

I highly recommend the Area J pickups: they are quiet, versatile, and sound like Fenders.

title: Well built, quite cables
comment:
I needed another set of balanced cables to go from my studios monitor controller to a set of self amplified studio monitors. They are one of my main monitoring pairs when mixing, so I wanted to make sure that my signal being carried by the cable had minimal signal loss and and did not allow any noise or EFI in. I looked at several of the “big box” name brand cables that I’ve used in the past, and the prices had just become outrageous, if they even had them in stock.
When opening the packing I immediately noticed the caliber of the ends/connectors - though this didn’t have that industry known “name” on them, the appeared to be build extremely well. I unscrewed the covers to look at the solder connections and shielding; perfect. Done very well, no cold soldering and precisely done. I also like that these cables were very nice and flexible; they were easy to route thru my cable paths/treys, and laid nicely inbb my there. When I fired up my console, rack and workstation it was nice and quiet, no added hum, hiss/white noise. When cycling the volume/signal up thru the console to the monitors the noise floor remained consistent as to where it should be. When playing music thru the highs remained crisp and clear and the lows smooth and punchy. I saw substantial no roll off when metering either.
Very impressed with these cables and they came at a great price value. I would recommend them! And I have already ordered some other cables for my studio as well since these performed so well!

I think this may be why so many reviewers complained of breaking strings - esp the low G which is a little thick for the high G slot.  The strings also have a slight texture to them and feel a little stiffer than normal strings.  Not a negative, just different.  Really cool sound though! title: More than advertised
comment:
I have ten other guitars, most of which are very valuable. I play, but enjoy collecting more. I saw this Fender and knew it was a great deal, but still wondered how good it might really be at the price offered. When it came in, I was stunned. The mahogany was without blemish and did not have the darkish vertical lines shown in the photo. It appears to be old growth, and I realized the camera was doing what cameras do; it was emphasizing the darker lines within the wood. I checked it closely from top to bottom, inside and out, and found not a single flaw. The tone was rich, but classically a very bit tinny with the new strings. From long experience, I know this will mellow within a few weeks, and if not, can be fixed with some new heavier strings. All in all, I am very happy with the purchase. For $300 I got a $700 - $1000 guitar. It was black Friday after all, so it did not surprise me. (I bought my first good guitar over forty years ago. A Takamine accoustic-electric, which were all the rage than. The tone on that machine was amazing. The more I played the richer the tone got. I finally gave it to my grandson who was then learning to play. I paid $400 for it new.) I am very happy with this purchase.

I used Prime, so the instrument arrived right on time without shipping charges.

Works great ! especially for the price, one important thing is you MUST try the clip in a couple of different places on the headstock, mute all other strings except the one you are tuning and wait at least 5 seconds for the display to darken before going to the next string. IMHO it's better to start with the low E as it has the most tension and work your way up to the high E, then do it again.
Although it can be used to check intonation it's really not accurate enough to start screwing around with your bridge setup.
For $20 it can't be beat.

title: This is a fantastic piece of engineering
comment:
This is a fantastic piece of engineering. It holds our PreSonus StudioLive 16.4.2 perfectly. We paired it with the SKB 19 AV shelf for R1406 Gig Rig to hold a laptop for recording. The back rails hold our power conditioner and BBE Sonic Maximizer and the front rails hold the amps for our monitors. We keep two 8-channel snakes and all speaker cables permanently attached. The entire rig is self-contained and portable. We've even cable tied the laptop AC adapter and a wireless router to the back shelf.

- When expanded to full height, it is ergonomically placed well for me (6' tall). The laptop shelf is set a little too far back, but really all we're doing is clicking 'record', so it's tolerable
- The castors are outstanding. It even rolls on the grass.
- The handles are great. Thick grip, ergonomically spaced, and they swing away from the box well so you don't break your wrists

Things to watch out for:
- This thing is BIG so be sure to measure your vehicles.
- The fit/finish is solid, though very slightly off. The nature of the molded plastic means it may not fit together 'exactly'. If you're not careful, the hinges could require too much effort to close because of the misalignment. Luckily, the plastic can be 'moved' into position for easier close.
- We lost one of the wheel locks already. Not sure how it happened; don't know how to put it back on. We still have the other three, so no big deal.
- Once this thing is fully loaded with mixer, amps, cables, etc., it's HEAVY.

1. The price
2. The fit and finish are good quality
3. It has a spring tension adjustment
4. Swivel legs.
5. It has a unique bottom cymbal tilt mechanism. Turning a knob on the side pushes a wedge under the bottom washer. It’s easy to find and adjust.
My complaints:
1. The spring tension adjustment is a little clunky and requires a drum key and both hands to adjust.
Unless you're on the road a lot and abusing your kit every night, this is a nice piece of well designed hardware more than adequate for the weekend player.

title: NO DMX CHAINS
comment:
I'm surprised these lights have such a high review. I'm sure the people giving higher reviews didn't use them in a professional/church settin lighting rig as I am using them for, so it would make sense how this product is selling so well. However, if you are planning a lighting rig and hope to create an atmosphere that you can control via DMX cables and lighting boards, you are highly advised to check out any other product. There were three main problems I faced when trying to use two of these lights and I'll list them below.

1) First off, it should be noted that the UV output is basically non-existent. THERE IS LIKE NO UV IN THIS ADVERTISED UV LIGHT! That was frustrating. The pictures make it look like a main UV fixture but neither of them put off enough black light and even together they sucked. I will say that other light color output is really good for the price.

2) The light itself is extremely light and can't be used with dmx wires and sitting on the floor as freestanding lights should be able to. It is so light that it's impossible to get the light to stay because the cables are heavier than the light and always tip it over. It's almost like having a laptop where the screen is 5 times heavier than the base and it is always in need of support so it doesn't fall over.

3) Last but not least, in fact the most important flaw is that this light can't be dimmed. The dmx configuration works flawlessly but when trying to dim these lights in live worship, the lights don't dim, they skip and jump and it is very very distracting. For ex: when moving the red fader all the way to 255 lighting from 0, the light won't fade to 255. It will jump from 0 to 255 when you stop moving the fader. It doesn't change until the fader stops moving and then it jumps.

I hope this all made sense to everyone and again, I don't advise these lights. I would send them back but it would be too much of a hassle.

title: Massive Library
comment: I was expecting something that I'd only be able to really use on metal or heavy rock tracks, but this library comes with a massive selection of drums which can be engineered well to work with multiple genres. I've used it with rap, electronica, and even film scores (it mixes well with percussion sounds). The included presets and midi are very geared towards metal though, so if you are looking for versatility in those departments, I'd look elsewhere. Speaking of presets, I do believe this library has more presets than any other toontrack library, ranging from classic, death, nu, and just about any other kind of metal. In the midi section, there is 22 'songs' from which you can choose various grooves/sections. There are 3 different drummers, in both classic and contemporary metal styles. While the reverb of the room itself is very metal, if you give it a tighter feel by bringing down the ambient tracks, I think it works amazingly well with other genres. The real advantage is the ridiculously large snare and kick selections, with 14 and 12 different options to choose from, respectively. Some of the presets are huge and you can run into issues if trying to load the full kit in the 32 bit mode, but most of the time I do fine. If you aren't good at mixing or using things other than presets, unless you're doing metal, this may not be for you. But if you are up for exploring, engineering, and experimenting, this is a great well rounded set.

title: A compact audio interface with useful features
comment:
[[VIDEOID:1b90537274070583884a3649918ce3ae]] The interface is well designed, compact and lightweight, built in a sturdy metal case. The two inputs are dual XLR/TRS jacks; outputs are left and right TRS; computer input is through a USB connection. The device can supply 48vDC phantom power for condenser mics. There is also a headphone monitor jack on the front face. The input volume inobs light when an input signal is applied, and will vary in brightness according to the input signal level. A USB cable and adapter are included in the package.

My test was done using a Windows 10 laptop and a QSC K8 for an output speaker. The voice audio test was clean throughout the entire signal chain.

Note: This device requires a computer running Windows 10 or newer. It is possible to run audio through the interface directly to a powered output speaker (or an amp and passive speaker) without the computer -- as a simple 2 channel mixer.

title: Beautiful Concert Ukulele Package!
comment:
I am echoing many other people in expressing my appreciation for this fine instrument and it's surrounding accessories. I took this instrument out of its very secure packaging and was amazed. It far surpassed my expectations at this price range. It has closed Gotoh style tuners and an arched back for better sound projection. The finish is beautiful. The tone is beautiful. Ukuleles characteristically have to be tuned and retuned for the first week due to the strings stretching. Joseph Cheung wisely spent a bit more money to equip this instrument with not only Aquila strings but Aquila Super Nylagut strings to make that adjustment more rapid.These strings are formulated to not stretch as much as older models of string. I got my ukulele the evening of Friday, March 24 and tuned it with the accompanying high quality tuner. It actually settled down rather quickly. Little adjustment was needed after the initial tuning and this is the evening of Sunday, March 26, and it has required only minor adjustments. Due to the fact that ukuleles use nylon strings, they go out of tune more frequently than instruments that use steel strings, hence many ukulele players leave a tuner clipped to the headstock. (Do not put steel string on a uke: they put too much stress for the bridge to withstand, as ukuleles don't use a tailpiece which is a sturdier fixture to attach strings to.) The tuner included with this is quite excellent and easy to read, with a display that glows green for each string as it reaches optimal tuning. The craftsmanship is excellent. For example, I happen to own another basic make of concert ukulele at the same price point as this one. It happens to have fret wire that slightly sticks over the side of the fret-board creating a somewhat rough edge: the Lohanu's fret wires are carefully beveled at the edge of the fret-board and are totally smooth when you run your fingers along them, making handling the neck more comfortable for a child's delicate hands. The tone is absolutely wonderful. I believe, when comparing it to the other competitor's instrument, that the arched back has increased the projection and sustain. you can strum both instruments simultaneously and the Lohanu can still be heard several seconds later, while the other instrument had gone quiet! Additionally, I would be remiss if I did not mention the very nice strap and installed strap buttons on this instrument, which few ukes come with. This goes a long way in making the instrument more playable----a beginner has more on their mind learning chords, music theory and strumming and doesn't need to add worry about holding onto the instrument!

If all these other reviews and the above don't convince you that this instrument was astonishingly good, perhaps this will: after several hours of living with the Lohanu Concert Ukulele, I ordered the Lohanu Soprano to use at my job during lunch and before work to get in extra practice time! It is now on it's way to me and I will be more than happy to share my reaction to the Soprano as well! You will not make a mistake buying this and the high quality of this instrument at this price point may well make the difference between someone sticking with the instrument or giving it up. Joseph Cheung can be viewed on a YouTube video demonstrating this his product by Googling "Lohanu Concert Ukulele Bundle". He and his staff are very much concerned about consumer reaction to their merchandise and an included message from him states that if anything is amiss, to please notify him, and the problem will be rectified as soon as possible.

title: Easily the cheapest and best solution to play guitar through your PC.
comment:
I just sold my Tascam US-1641 audio interface as I lost my drums and didn't need multiple inputs. I was skeptical due to the reviews on this product but after the initial hassle of getting it to work, I'm very pleased with the results. I don't notice any difference with my guitar compared to the $300 Tascam I was using (I use Reaper and Guitar Rig mostly).

I'm on Windows 8.1 x64 and downloaded the drivers from behringer's website. I had to make sure everything was set to 48khz in order to get it to work without crackling but once I got it setup initially it has worked flawlessly. I didn't even have to disable the regular windows devices like others mentioned, I can actually play winamp and use my ASIO apps at the same time although I wouldn't rely on that as even the more expensive interfaces can have issues there (my tascam for one).

People who need phantom power for a mic should look into the cheap behringer USB mixers, I was on the fence between this product and that one but decided $10 was worth it to find out if this worked and I'm happy with my purchase.

title: A little smaller in size than american standard jazz bass pickguard screws

The black paint will scrape off quite easily to reveal (i think brass), so be careful when you put them on or they'll be all scrached up on the inside of the screwhead where the phillips screwdriver goes.

In short, get the originals if you really want to replicate the stock part.

title: Look KILLER play like BUTTER! Play as well as they feel and made in the USA.
comment:
These strings are awesome. I am happy. They feel good, are very fast and they look amazing.They stay in tune and were not a problem to put on.(Floyd Rose type system)

I had someone warn me that coated strings would sound like I had a sock on them, not the case. The sound through my Randall is amazing. These strings made me fall in love with my seven string. I am an experienced 6 string player and I have a nice Ibanez S7420 that has essentially sat in it's case for 10 years as I played my six strings and played bass doing shows etc. We decided to hang all my guitars on the wall, and since I needed new strings on the 7420, I decided to give these a shot, and I am so glad I did. They are made in the USA and honestly, when I pulled them out of the pack to string them, there were lots of "oohs" and "ahhs"

Dang, these things are BRIGHT green. I went with the 10's to keep that low end crunch. Now, the 7420 with these super bright green strings is my guitar of choice. Major compliments on it as well. It looks totally tricked out. When I played them, that sealed the deal. Now I want to get some for my Jackson and Charvel.

My normal choice for strings for 6 strings are GHS Boomers, 009-46. I have played guitar professionally on a local level since the early 90's. When I started playing bass for a band 6 years ago, I went to Cleartones for my Ibanez SR500. Those are coated strings that are amazing as well.

If I can figure out how to post a pic, you'll see what I am talking about.

After all, as art it may well be a better deal cost-wise than it represents as a guitar... maybe not, value is in the eye (fingers?) of the beholder. Most folks have never seen one, and it has a unique shape. Therefore I posted my review immediately after ordering, expecting delivery in a couple days.

I know my fellow guitar players are more interested in a player review, and many are available here, so please don't down-rate me on that account. I simply present another use for this distinctive instrument - as an expression of style, as well as one's love of music. The spot I have is just perfect for it. (Wife dubious until I showed her the images, then she readily agreed.)

[Edit - This little guy is addictive. Fun, and really quite playable when I'm laid back in my recliner. Tone obviously is compromised by the compact (and cool-looking) body, but surprisingly loud and pleasant. Its weakness becomes its strength on quiet nights when you want to play without disturbing others. Best of all, I managed to acquire another new guitar with complete wife approval due to its unique look pushing the instrument into the home décor category, so it has a high WAF.]

title: Nice Ukulele, Excellent price..
comment:
Overall this is a great little musical instrument..especially for the price. If your thinking about a Ukulele for the first time I would highly recommend this model. I Originally purchased the Kala KA-S (soprano) ukulele, but within a few hours realized that it was just too small for me to comfortably play. If your a full size adult I recommend going with this Concert sized Uk.. You get a larger body, longer neck with slightly wider spaced frets, and I really like the richer sound tone that the larger body gives.
The quality is high for this price range ($65). The fit and finish is great, no visible flaws, the finish is good, the glue inside is cleanly done without any mess.. a good sign of quality. The neck is clean and smooth, frets are well finished..nothing sharp..
This is the lower cost "Makala" MK-C version of the Kala KA-C (both by Kala).. The difference seems to be (other than $40) is that this Makala version has open tuners (you can see the gears on the back) and white tuner pegs, as opposed to closed, all metal tuners on the Kala version. And this version does not have white banding... But.. if your not worried about the nicer looks of the Kala version, this plays very well and saves you $40.
Set up and play-ability on my Uk was good.. no problem with fret buzz or hard to "note" strings.. the string height is just about where it should be.
The tone is great, and I've really enjoyed learning to play the Uk over the last few month.. very relaxing to just sit among the trees and strum away...

title: Perfect replacement for the Tascam DR-10
comment:
I've been a fan of the Tascam DR-10 for years. The Zoom F2 has just replaced it. Here's why:

I've been burned too many times using wireless lav mics on shoots. When I found the DR-10, it just made sense. However, the only way to monitor the DR-10 for audio levels was through the headphone jack. So, unless you wanted to run an 1/8" cable from the pack, you just had to trust the levels were good.

The F2 doesn't really need to monitoring, since it records in 32-float, but you can monitor it through the bluetooth connection if you need to. You can also start and stop recording remotely through the bluetooth connection. Convenient, and unlike the Tentaclesync pack, it doesn't disable the 1/8" out when recording. So you could still use a wireless pack in addition to this as well.

I use two of these for interviews. One goes on the subject as a lav and the other I connect to a Deity D3 Pro for a shotgun mic. I also run a 1/8" out from the shotgun pack into my camera for a backfeed.

My only con for this recorder is that the supplied mic is only so-so. It will get the job done, but replacing it with a Countryman or Sanken or even one of the Deity lav mics will make this unit that much better.

title: Get Slic Nuts and spend the extra money you save on something else
comment:
I bought these thinking they were going to be the best quick release system out there, offer the most durability, and be top of the line.

I have 4 DW 9000 cymbal stands and I bought 4 of these from guitar center which was around $72.

These were easy to install and easy to use. They worked pretty good for about a month. After a while of use, I noticed my crashes were swinging a little too out of control, so I screwed the bottom part up like I had done with China and Ride. Played them for a little bit and the screws on the bottom managed to come down again making it a pain and constant chore to get them right where I wanted them.

Oh and if you have a hardware case, expect to go diving for felts and toppers.

Then it happened, the middle of these are made of plastic, and there is no plastic cover I've found that will fit over them put extra shielding between the cymbal and stand. I saw metal shining through the plastic. I tried every solution I could think of without altering the the actual quick release shaft.

Then I realized my mistake. I spent $72 on a quick release system for a cymbal stand. I would have to spend $18 to replace each one as they do the same thing. If they only last me a month and all 4 need to be replaced, I would be spending $864 a year on a stupid quick release system! WHY WOULD I DO THIS!?!?

So Instead of sending a small chunk of my yearly salary to Trick Drums, I've decided to invest in Vater Slic Nuts instead. No sleeves, and the bottom part of the DW felts are perfect to accommodate them. I can tighten them with an Allen wrench as needed and all is right in the world again.

***EDIT***

I contacted Trick Drums about this issue and found that there are replacement sleeves on their website for $4.99 so I'll bump 1 star up for that. slick nuts still seem like a cheaper resolution though.

title: Far superior to all in one modelers if you are using with a real amp - not perfect but for the price its as good as it gets
comment:
If you are looking for an all around, reasonably priced multi-effect unit for a tube amp, this is in my opinion the best available option. Plenty of options for effects. I was able to figure out how to program it without the manual - the only thing I didn't get at first was how the patches are selected - you have to hold down the bank up/down button or hold down the "1,2,3" patch select buttons for a few seconds before they will activate - then when you actually release the switch, the bank number can be selected, or the patch will be engaged - this allows you to select presets, but at the same time if you just want to turn off individual effects in pedal board mode, you can just tap those and they are instantaneous "on/off" for the effect - just like old fashioned pedal boards.

Pros: nice sounds, sounds are real, reverb, delay, flange, chorus, compressor, and drives are good. Wasn't sure I liked the way the presets were changed but after understanding how they worked, I actually like it now. Drive sounds are very decent, not a huge range of styles like in a modeler, but just a solid warm crunchy OD and distortion.

Cons: Not huge fan of the phaser sounds - I actually had a G-Major a while back and thought the same thing, and this sounds the same as the G-Major to me. It is serviceable but not great. Also, there is a definite issue with the drive bleeding through when the drive is "off". If you have a patch programmed with drive on, and tap the drive off, you can faintly hear the drive still bleeding through the patch. Not the end of the world, but can be enough to mess up the pristine clean sound. So if you program truly clean patches, make sure you completely set the drive gain/levels to zero to eliminate the bleed issue. Not ideal, but can work around.

The Nova Systems compared to a POD 500HD and a Boss GT-100 - no comparison - yes they are different beasts, but I read many reviews of the modelers and many people claim that you can use these modelers with great success in front of an amp. So I tried them side by side with the Nova. I've been playing for over 30 years with all kinds of gear and I am not a tube snob, I truly wish that you could get inspiring real tones from the modelers, but alas, I think you need to go to the multi-thousand dollar units to get close to real tones and feel. The POD was ridiculously digital and compressed sounding - I know that many people can program them to sound better and the presets are supposedly no good yada yada, but come on, I had an older POD XT Live and it sounded way better in front of an amp with the presets (with the proper adjustments to remove cab sims, etc.) than this newer version. Boss GT-100 was a little better with my amp, but again, didn't sound real. Yes, I did turn off the cab simulations and I've owned these before so know how to program the tones, but it still just didn't sound good. I am not willing to accept the argument that you "have to really deep edit these modelers" to make them work with an amp. Why? Why should they be so hard to get to work with an amp? Amps have been around forever so why aren't these things compatible? The truth in my opinion is that is because the low end modelers (if you consider $500 low end) don't sound that good unless they are designed from the ground up to work with an amp, and these clearly are designed as modelers for PA systems. The modelers are fun and they can record well if you take some time, but they are not a substitute for a decent pedal board and amp. Are they fun and decent sounding for recording or maybe into a PA they are fine. But I keep getting reeled back in by the marketing hype for the modelers, own them for a few days, and then send them back every time.

So for me the all in one modeler just wasn't cutting it with an amp and the Nova sounds superior in every way, a little more basic, but just much higher quality, usable tones. So if you are looking for something that is good sounding, relatively easy to program, and good compromise between a bunch of pedals vs. a modeler, there really isn't much else out there that does what the Nova system does in my opinion.

title: Be aware that this is a *kit.*
comment:
The image shows a completed board and the title doesn't include the word "kit." It's in there as a note at the bottom of the description, but it should be more obvious -- you have to construct this one yourself. What makes this puzzling is that it's priced the same as a completed board. I just wanted the board, not the kit-building experience, so I returned it and bought the completed board from another vendor for the same price.

It's a simple fix, Amazons. Change the name to "MIDI Shield Kit" and clarify the description. Then people who want to build a kit will buy it and people (like me) who don't won't be disappointed.

title: Awesome Input Device For Podcast Webinar Zoom Skype And Screen Recording
comment: I'm not a recording artist or musician BUT I do record audio and video and Live Stream webinars off my Windows 10 PC.  I had been using a really nice Audio Technica AT 2005USB / XLR combo Dynamic mic for all my PC work and it has given me really nice results.  But there are times when I need to go wireless and I just had no way to get my Sennheiser G3 Body Pack into the PC, so tried this Steinberg and I'm really impressed.  The Drivers are super stable and my PC Instantly recognized the device.  I took my regular Mic which is normally connected to the PC and routed it into Input 1 and this thing took what was already really nice sound and just warmed it up and boosted it ever so slightly.  So Far so good, I got an improvement I wasn't even looking for.  Then I hooked up the EW100 G3 Receiver to Input 2.  And BINGO, wireless Mic straight into my PC!  I'm happy!  Just a couple of setting notes.  On my Dynamic Mic, I had to adjust the gain all the way to full 10 out of 10, but it didn't add any noise it just rounded out the sound of an already good mic.  For the Sennheiser wireless, I had to adjust the gain down to 2 out of 10 ....  It's a great Unit.  Solid Construction, looks good on the desk and travel friendly.  I can see taking this on the road with me.

title: When I tell You I have No Problem with This Thing...Until Now!  Read this Update Before Buying!!
comment:
Blue Yeti User:

Update: This is a good mic if you don't use it much or adjust your mic. Don't buy this mic. Just get the Logitech Blue Yeti stand. In the long run, the one fatal flaw of this design, which I initially wondered about, is why spend the money on the metal frame if the most important part, that part that holds the mic is plastic. I have had two of these now and they both failed in the same place. I bought this two years ago when updating my office to give lectures or to participate in webinars. I have been using the Logitech blue yeti boom arm for a while now; because there are no plastic parts except for the screw covers. This isn't a problem and I feel like it will last a very long time because the pieces that hold the mic are all metal and it's the one I have now is compatible with the current equipment I have listed below. The Inno is a solid stand, but if you use it weekly and need to handle your mic often, this IS NOT the mic for you. Pay the 100.00 for a long-lasting boom mic and don't pay 40 twice for this like did. Beginners mistake so let me save you the money.

I mean it, no problems. Sturdy, the plastic clamp is hard plastic. While I can see someone’s desire for a metal clamp, I don't have any reservations whether it will survive the life of the arm itself.

As a person who values cable management, I dont have a problem with how its set up. The velcro ties are helpful for storing the wires neatly.

Pro Tip: dont do as I had done initially and place the tie around the entire width of the arm, but between them.

The joints are stiff but that makes me confident that it will last a long time and that it will stay in place while in use. I am so glad that I did not buy the yeticaster (while cool) or the Rodes boom arms. This more than works for my purposes.

Use: I use my microphone for my WFH office to record lectures and just as a better mic for zoom meetings.

Equipment: I am using the InnoGear boom arm with my Mounkey metal pop filter (if you are thinking about it, get it, it’s worth it!) and a Auphonix pro blue yeti shock mount. I did not need an adapter and it fit right into my mount. Finally my desk is 5” long standing desk and it is two inches thick. It barely slid on but it did so if your desk is ANY thicker than that this WILL NOT work for you. However, if you bought a cheap boom arm first, like most of us, keep your clamp because it just might fit. I dont have a problem positioning my mic for comfort sitting or standing. I have it positioned at the side of my desk. If I got the jumbo version I would have the mic come from above my screens.

I would recommend! Enjoy!

title: Great sounding mic for an incredible price
comment:
I use these mics for live sound in place of the SM58s that they mimic. In the photo, they look they the same as the Shure version, but they are smaller in size in every way. They still have the heft of a 58 and they sound surprisingly similar. The metal windscreen is smaller and much tougher than a 58 - I use these mics when I'm running sound for bands that like to swings mics around and generally abuse them, but don't want to bring their own mics.

I bought 4 of these mics initially and will be buying more. A couple of them have been dropped, kicked, and thrown across the stage a number of times, but I can't tell them apart from the ones that have been treated better. With an SM58, I would definitely see some denting on the windscreen at least - not with these mics, though.

Having only used them for live sound, I haven't been able to tell the subtle differences between these mics and the Shure versions. They seem to have the exact same output and sound practically identical to my ear. I've tried other cheaper mics, but I generally stick with the more expensive brand name mics for my work - these mics are the exception and I would use them in place of a 58 without a second thought. The only downside I've found is that they pick up a little more handling noise when in a mic stand - touching or hitting the mic stand transfers more noise than with more expensive mics. Handholding the mic, or swinging and kicking the mic sounds the same.

A bargain at twice the price!
